# provenance — Complete Project Dossier

_Detailed deterministic archive of the orchestrator run. It includes the original prompt, final phase outputs, full discussion transcripts, task backlog, interface contracts, verification status, and recorded findings. Nothing here is inferred or fabricated._

## Original Prompt

PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.

## Final Phase Outputs

### Prompt Contract

Here's where things stand after round 1: Codex and I both preserved the prompt verbatim and landed on basically the same read of the assignment, just from different angles — Codex framing the product vision, me stress-testing it for edge cases. Gemini couldn't weigh in (CLI unavailable). There's no real disagreement between the two contributions that showed up — they're additive, not conflicting, so I think this is ready to close out.

Both of us agree: this phase is scoped to Provenance alone, not the parent 7-app orchestration process (idea generation, portfolio selection, Jira/Notion backfill belong upstream, not here). Both of us independently landed on the same core thesis — this app succeeds or fails on trust, not on flashy recognition tech — and both flagged the "insurance-grade" language as a claim the product can't legally back, so the export should be a well-organized structured PDF, not a marketed guarantee. Both agree Vision classification must be a non-blocking, low-confidence-aware suggestion with manual entry always fully available. I added several concrete edge cases (subscription downgrade must never delete data, free-tier widget has nothing to show without a value tracking, no cloud backup means the PDF export doubles as a manual safety net, photo storage needs a compression strategy) — these don't contradict Codex's framing, they just make it concrete enough to hold later phases accountable.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Original prompt:** preserved verbatim above (both contributions include the full text) — the Provenance MVP spec nested inside the multi-app-exp4 parent portfolio prompt.

**Scope of this phase:** This contract governs only the Provenance child app. The parent portfolio's instructions (generate 10+ ideas, pick 7, run Jira/Notion backfill, combine transcripts) are the orchestrator's job, not this app's discussion — later phases should not re-litigate whether Provenance belongs in the 7.

**Hard requirements:**
- SwiftUI, SwiftData-backed, single-user, fully local-first (no cloud/network dependency for core function).
- Photo capture via Camera; on-device Vision-framework classification assist that pre-fills category/type suggestions only — never blocks, delays, or gates saving, and is always overridable by one-tap manual entry.
- Manual condition, provenance, and purchase-price logging with structured fields.
- Manual value-history entries with a charted value-over-time view.
- PDF export (local rendering) containing photos, condition notes, provenance, purchase facts, and value history, organized and professional — but making no claim of legal/insurer acceptance.
- StoreKit 2 paywall matching the stated model: free tier capped at 25 items with no valuation tracking; paid tier (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr) unlocks unlimited items, classification assist, value charts, and export.
- Widget showing collection value (paid) — free tier needs its own defined widget state (e.g., item count) since it has no value data to show.
- Repository-protocol boundary so cloud sync / price feed can be added later without a rewrite.
- Full state coverage (empty, loading, success, error) on every screen the spec names: capture/classify, item list, item detail, condition timeline, value chart, export preview, paywall.
- Dynamic Type and VoiceOver support, verified specifically on photo-heavy and chart-heavy screens.
- Data preservation on subscription downgrade: items/value entries beyond the free cap become hidden/read-only, never deleted, and are fully restored on re-subscribe.
- A storage strategy for photos (compression/thumbnailing) so a large paid collection doesn't bloat device storage unnoticed.

**Non-goals:** cloud sync, live/market price feeds, multi-currency support, multi-user or shared collections, actual insurer/legal certification of the export, marketplace or trading features, social/community comps, OCR-heavy document ingestion, authentication/backend infra, iPad-specific layout (unless later directed otherwise).

**Production-readiness definition:** More than "compiles and runs." Means: SwiftData persistence survives relaunch/background; camera + Vision have real success and failure paths (permission denied, low confidence, exception) with corresponding UI; every named screen has all four reachable states implemented; StoreKit 2 sandbox-tested for purchase, restore, and the downgrade-preserves-data behavior; accessibility verified on photo and chart screens; PDF export verified with zero items, one item, and an item with full value history; core data flows and export logic have test coverage; app is privacy-preserving and deterministic offline.

**Decision rules for later phases:**
1. Prefer smaller trustworthy scope over broader flimsy scope.
2. Manual entry and reliable record-keeping beat flashy recognition — classification is a suggestion, never an assertion, never load-bearing for saving.
3. Legibility and accessibility win over aesthetic flourish; premium polish belongs in typography/layout/export quality, not at the expense of fast logging or clarity.
4. Never make a legal/insurance guarantee the product hasn't earned — export is "insurance-ready" in structure and professionalism, not in legal status.
5. Never destroy user data on subscription lapse or any error state — hide/restrict, don't delete.
6. When a requirement is ambiguous, resolve toward local-first durability and record credibility, not toward feature breadth.
7. If a later phase's plan reintroduces cloud sync, market-price feeds, multi-user, or legal export claims, that's a contract violation to flag by name, not a stylistic choice to wave through.

### Product Research

Here's the shape of round 1: Codex and I approached this from different angles but landed in the same place, and nothing either of us said contradicts the other — it's all additive. Gemini was unavailable again (CLI issue, not a content gap).

We agree on the audience: not "collectors" broadly, but people already running a spreadsheet or Notes doc for one of these categories who are annoyed by it — and specifically people whose annoyance is bad enough to make them switch tools, which is a narrower group than "collectors" sounds like. We agree the app has to serve two very different moments well: a fast, tolerant-of-bad-conditions capture moment right after acquisition, and a slower, more deliberate review/reporting moment later. We agree the real competitive bar isn't other collector apps, it's the spreadsheet itself — Provenance has to be faster than a spreadsheet at capture while feeling more trustworthy at review, or it loses on day one. We agree category-specific tools (Discogs, CellarTracker, WatchCharts) are worth studying precisely because they can lean on real external data this app can't have in v1, so the cross-category pitch only survives if documentation and export quality visibly beat what a specialist tool offers, not identification depth. And we agree Vision has to stay humble — coarse category suggestion only, never presented as identification, because both of us independently flagged that a real collector will test it against their hardest item almost immediately.

Where I added specifics beyond Codex's framing (and nothing here fought his read, just made it actionable): app-lock via Face ID/passcode as a near-mandatory addition given the data sensitivity even though the spec didn't name it; widgets need to refresh on local write events rather than a timer since there's no natural polling signal; a concrete tie-break rule for which items stay visible when a downgraded user exceeds the 25-item cap (oldest-by-date-added, for testability); the value chart needs its own explicit near-empty state for 0-1 entries rather than falling back to a generic empty view; and a fixed small category enum with a free-text subtype field rather than dynamic per-category schemas, to keep the cross-category promise from turning into scope inflation.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** Not casual hobbyists — people already tracking watches, vinyl, cards, wine, or sneakers in a spreadsheet or Notes who are frustrated enough to switch. Two distinct use moments: (1) fast, interrupted, often one-handed capture right after a purchase/unboxing — bad lighting, wants to log and move on; (2) slower periodic review — checking aggregate value, reconciling condition before selling, pulling a report for insurance/estate purposes. The first build must optimize for (1) without sacrificing (2); a multi-step intake wizard at capture time loses to "just add a row in Excel."

**Comparable apps/patterns to learn from:** Home-inventory-for-insurance apps (Encircle, Nest Egg) — best reference for what makes a PDF export feel credible: itemized, dated, photo-anchored, deliberately unflashy. Category-specific collector tools (Discogs, CellarTracker, WatchCharts, CLZ) — show the cross-category promise is genuinely differentiated, but also show why single-category tools dominate (real external databases/price feeds this app can't use in v1); the lesson is to win on documentation rigor, not pretend to specialist identification depth. Generic inventory apps (Sortly) — a cautionary pattern: generic tools feel cheap because they don't specialize, so Provenance's archival aesthetic only pays off if structured fields and typography are actually more careful, not just darker.

**Platform-specific opportunities:** Camera capture as the real workflow advantage (turns logging into a physical-world action, not a form). Vision framework used only for coarse category pre-fill with a visible confidence affordance — never fine-grained identification claims. Files/local PDF export as a portable trust artifact and the de facto backup story given no cloud sync in v1 (should explicitly confirm/communicate standard encrypted device backup coverage). Widgets tied to local data-write events rather than a timer, since there's no natural refresh signal; free tier shows item count, paid shows collection value. Face ID/passcode app-lock via LocalAuthentication — cheap, reinforces the trust thesis, and warranted given the app stores an itemized list of someone's valuables and purchase prices even though it wasn't in the original feature list. Dynamic Type/VoiceOver treated as core requirements, especially on photo- and chart-heavy screens, given the likely-older, detail-attentive user base.

**Major assumptions and risks:**
1. Vision's classifiers are coarse object/scene classifiers, not fine-grained identifiers — they can say "this is a wristwatch," never "this is a 1970s ref. 5513." Overstating this in copy or UX breaks trust the first time a real collector tests it.
2. Manual value-history entry may not sustain engagement without a live price feed — many users will log a purchase price once and never return to update it; the value-chart feature needs to earn its keep on its own, not assume habitual use.
3. The cross-category, single-home-for-records thesis may not hold for users deeply embedded in one category who already trust a specialist tool's metadata standards — this app wins the household-asset/insurance/estate use case, not necessarily the deep-in-one-hobby use case.
4. Silent failure modes if under-specified: which 25 items stay active on downgrade (resolve: oldest-by-date-added), what the chart shows at 0-1 value entries (needs its own designed state, not generic empty), and whether SwiftData's store is confirmed to ride along in standard device backups (needs verifying and stating to the user, not left implicit).
5. Aesthetic risk: "archival/museum" styling can tip into illegible cinematic darkness on exactly the screens (charts, condition photos) where legibility matters most — restrained dark neutrals with strong type hierarchy, not mood lighting.

**Implications for the first build:** Ship a fast minimum-viable capture path (photo, title, category, acquisition date, price optional, notes optional) with condition/provenance depth added after save, not before. Use a fixed small category enum (watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other) plus a free-text subtype field rather than dynamic per-category schemas. Treat classification as a secondary, visibly-labeled suggestion that never blocks saving. Add Face ID/passcode app-lock as a first-build feature. Decide the downgrade tie-break rule (oldest-by-date-added stays active) and the chart's near-empty state now. Keep the visual system restrained rather than cinematic. The product's identity for this build: a documentation-and-trust ledger that's faster than a spreadsheet at capture and more credible than a spreadsheet at review — not a valuation-intelligence product.

### Portfolio Selection

CONSENSUS: YES

Both Codex and I read this the same way, independently: the header (`PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true`, `Selected app slug: provenance`) tells us the seven-app portfolio split already happened one level up, in the `multi-app-exp4` parent orchestrator. There's nothing to select or compare here — no rival ideas to weigh, no rejected alternatives to justify, because this room was only ever handed one app. We both explicitly reject re-running portfolio ceremony (generating 10+ ideas, picking best-of-N, emitting a fresh portfolio-json) as out of scope and actually risky, since doing so would create ambiguity about folder ownership and transcript scope that the parent process has already resolved. Where Codex focused on the procedural guardrail (don't let this phase quietly reopen the 7-app decision or fragment Provenance into per-category sub-apps), I focused on making sure the single-app decision doesn't get treated as a rubber stamp — pushing hard that the real product stakes for this phase are about protecting a fast, low-friction capture flow and an honest (not falsely authoritative) value/export story, not about relitigating app selection. No disagreement surfaced between us; Gemini was unavailable again. This is ready to close.

## Final Output

**Single-app vs portfolio-parent decision:** Single-app child project. The portfolio decision (7 apps, generated and selected upstream in `multi-app-exp4`) already happened before this conversation started. This phase is not a portfolio parent and does not re-run idea generation or selection.

**Selected app list:**
| Slug | Category | Promise | Target User | Build |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `provenance` | Collecting & Valuation | Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet | Serious collectors (watches, vinyl, cards, wine, sneakers) currently using Notes/spreadsheets with no real view of aggregate value or condition history | true |

**Selection rationale:** Not applicable in the usual comparative sense — there was one app handed to this phase, already chosen by the parent portfolio process. The only decision this phase makes is to confirm that fact plainly rather than manufacture a selection narrative that doesn't exist.

**Rejected alternatives:** None considered, and none should be — for three concrete reasons already surfaced in discussion: (1) re-emitting a `portfolio-json` / re-running 10-idea generation would duplicate upstream work and contradict the explicit child-project marker; (2) bundling Provenance with adjacent collecting/inventory concepts into one wrapper project would violate the "one folder per app" rule; (3) splitting Provenance into per-category sub-apps (watches app, wine app, card app, etc.) would directly contradict its core differentiator, which is cross-category record-keeping inside a single app.

**portfolio-json manifest:** Not applicable / not emitted by this phase. This child project is not a portfolio parent; if a manifest is needed, it belongs to the `multi-app-exp4` orchestrator that made the original 7-app selection, not to this per-app discussion.

**Carried forward into build:** One independent app folder for Provenance. Core build discipline reaffirmed for this phase: capture-to-save must stay under a minute with everything beyond photo/title/category as optional post-save enrichment; Vision-assisted classification pre-fills silently and never blocks or gates saving; the product must stay honest about what's manual vs. assisted rather than performing false valuation authority — this is a documentation-and-trust ledger, not a valuation-intelligence product, consistent with the `prompt_contract` and `product_research` decisions already locked for this app.

### Initial Discussion

Both contributors are aligned and there's nothing left to fight about — Gemini was unavailable again, but that's a gap in participation, not a source of unresolved disagreement between the two who did weigh in.

Codex set the frame: Provenance is a documentation ledger, not a valuation-intelligence product, and it lives or dies on one thing — is capture faster than a spreadsheet, and is review more trustworthy than one. Primary user is someone with real money in physical stuff (watches, cards, wine, sneakers) who already has a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync and hasn't found anything better. Core loop: capture a photo, save with just a title and category, enrich everything else later. Hard boundary: no live market value, no expert-identification claims, no insurer guarantees, no sync, no dynamic per-category schemas, no marketplace behavior.

I took that same frame and made it concrete enough to build from: the IA is home/list → capture sheet → item detail (with two visually distinct timelines — condition/provenance vs. value entries, never merged into one generic feed) → export preview → paywall → settings/app-lock. I added three things Codex's framing didn't cover but that fall directly out of it: soft-delete with a recovery window rather than immediate hard delete (an accidental delete of a $9k item's whole history would be a worse trust failure than anything else in the app), a real cover-photo-vs-timeline-photo distinction in the data model (otherwise the list view can't reliably show the right photo), and explicit handling for interrupted capture (backgrounded mid-flow shouldn't silently lose the draft). None of this contradicts Codex — it's the same "documentation ledger, fast at capture, honest at review" thesis, just pushed down into IA and data-model decisions so the next phase doesn't have to invent them under pressure.

Success criteria between us are consistent and stack cleanly: sub-60-second (Codex said "under a minute," I put a number on it) item creation, all four states on every named screen, zero data loss across delete/downgrade/force-quit scenarios, PDF export verified at 0/1/many items, and VoiceOver able to complete the full core loop unassisted. Scope boundaries match line for line with what prompt_contract and product_research already locked.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** Provenance is a local-first ledger that lets a collector log a valuable physical item in under a minute via photo capture, then build a trustworthy, dated record of its condition, provenance, and value over time — faster to use than a spreadsheet at the moment of acquisition, and more credible than one when it's time to review or hand the record to someone else.

**Primary user and scenario:** A person with meaningful money tied up in physical goods (watches, vinyl, graded cards, wine, sneakers) who currently tracks holdings in a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync, and who will abandon any new tool that isn't faster than what they already have. The critical scenario is the moment right after acquisition — one-handed, often distracted, often bad lighting — where the app has to beat "just add a row" or nothing downstream (condition history, charts, export) ever gets used.

**Core loop:**
1. Capture — photo, title, pick one of six fixed categories (watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other). Save immediately. Vision classification silently pre-fills the category suggestion in the background; it never blocks, delays, or gates the save action.
2. The item now exists and appears in the list — this is the moment of relief the product has to deliver in under a minute.
3. Enrichment (anytime, from the item detail screen, in three independent tracks): condition/provenance timeline (dated notes + photos), value-entry ledger (dated entries feeding the value chart), and purchase facts (price/date/source, entered once).
4. Review — list view for browsing the archive, detail view as the trust-building surface (two clearly separated timelines, never merged into one generic feed), export preview for generating the PDF report.

**Hard scope boundaries:**
- v1 is a documentation ledger, not a valuation-intelligence product: no live/market pricing, no expert-grade item identification, no insurer/legal certification of the export.
- No cloud sync, no multi-user/shared collections, no marketplace or trading features, no social/community comps.
- Fixed six-category enum only — no dynamic per-category schemas.
- No search/filter/sort system beyond basic list ordering, no barcode/serial-number lookup, no OCR document ingestion.
- Classification assist is always secondary and overridable — never a blocking step.
- Item deletion is soft-delete with a recovery window (auto-purged after a fixed period, e.g. 30 days), not immediate hard delete — consistent with the "never destroy user data" rule already locked for downgrade/error states.
- Data model must distinguish cover photo from timeline-entry photos, and condition-history entries from value-history entries, so the record stays legible rather than collapsing into an undifferentiated feed.

**Measurable success criteria:**
- A first-time user can create an item (photo + title + category) in well under a minute; median time from tapping "add item" to a saved item under 60 seconds.
- Every named screen (capture/classify, list, detail with condition timeline and value chart, export preview, paywall) demonstrably reaches all four states — empty, loading, success, error — not just the happy path.
- Zero data loss across intentional delete, subscription downgrade, and interrupted/backgrounded capture, verified by test rather than assumed.
- Classification failure or low confidence never blocks save; manual entry is always a fully working fallback.
- PDF export produces a valid, well-organized file at zero items, one item, and an item with full condition/value history.
- VoiceOver alone can complete the full core loop — capture, enrich, export — without sighted assistance.

### Per App Product Brief

Both Codex and Claude are telling the same story from different angles, and neither pushed back on the other — this reads like genuine convergence, not two people talking past each other. The one new thing that emerged this round beyond what earlier phases already locked is a sharper theory of the paywall itself: the export shouldn't just be gated, it should be visibly desirable behind the gate (real layout, real typography, redacted content) so a big, aging collection actually wants to convert rather than just bumping into a wall. Everything else is restating and hardening the same thesis from initial_discussion with a business lens now laid on top.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user and use case:** Someone who already has real money tied up in physical goods — watches, vinyl, graded cards, wine, sneakers — and is currently tracking it in a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync. They're not shopping for a hobby toy; they're quietly embarrassed by their current system and carry a low-grade anxiety that if something were lost, stolen, or needed for an insurance/estate conversation tomorrow, they'd be reconstructing facts from memory and camera-roll scraps. The trigger moment is right after acquisition — log it now before the details fade — and the payoff moment is much later, when they need to review, prove, or hand off the record.

**Paid value and subscription value:** Free has to be a genuinely working ledger, not a crippled demo — up to 25 items with full structured logging (photo, title, category, condition, provenance, purchase facts), because a free tier that feels broken kills trust before it ever converts anyone. What's gated is what only matters once someone has enough at stake to care: unlimited items (a real collector blows past 25 fast), the value-over-time chart, on-device classification assist, and the PDF export. The export is the real subscription anchor — it's the artifact people actually want to hand to a spouse, an appraiser, or an insurance adjuster, and it should be visibly showable even in its paywall preview (real formatting, blurred/locked content) so the user is paying for something they've already seen and want, not speculating. This only holds up as "real utility" if the ongoing paid behaviors keep earning their keep as the collection grows and ages — bigger catalog, more history, more export occasions — not because the classification or valuation got smarter.

**Core loop:** Capture (photo, title, one of six fixed categories) → save immediately, under a minute, ideally under twenty seconds → item exists and shows up in the catalog → enrichment anytime afterward from the detail screen across three independent tracks: condition/provenance timeline, value-entry ledger, purchase facts. Vision classification runs silently in the background to pre-fill the category chip; it never blocks or gates the save. Review happens later and separately — list for browsing, detail screen as the trust surface (two clearly separated timelines, never merged), export preview for turning the record into something you can hand someone.

**Competitive wedge:** Not identification depth — WatchCharts will always know more about watches, CellarTracker more about wine, and trying to out-recognize them is a fight this app loses with on-device Vision alone. The wedge is documentation rigor applied consistently across categories: the same seriousness of dated condition photos, dated value entries, purchase facts, and export quality whether the object is a watch or a case of wine. It beats spreadsheets on capture speed and beats both spreadsheets and generic inventory apps on review credibility.

**Viral or niche growth angle:** Not social, not a share button, not public profiles — that would contradict the privacy stance a private valuables ledger needs. Growth is niche and credibility-driven: the export itself is the distribution artifact. A clean, dossier-quality PDF is the kind of thing that gets shown to a partner, an appraiser, or a collector forum thread ("this is what I use to track my collection"), which is a slower but stickier loop than a feed-shaped mechanic bolted on for its own sake.

**Local-first behavior and cloud-ready extension path:** Local-first is a trust requirement, not an architecture footnote. Concretely required in this build: Face ID/passcode app-lock (the app holds an itemized list of valuables and what they cost — that's real-world risk on an unlocked phone); soft-delete with a 30-day recovery window on items (an accidental delete of a $9,000 item's whole history is the single worst thing this product could do to its own credibility); drafts that survive interrupted/backgrounded capture; downgrade that hides rather than deletes; and an explicit, stated confirmation that the local store rides along in standard encrypted device backups, since "my only copy lives on one phone with no visible backup story" is a five-star-review killer. The cloud path — sync, opt-in market-price feed — stays behind the repository protocol boundary already established; it's future work that should look like added continuity, never a v1 dependency or a redesign.

**Why this app deserves to exist:** Not because it's the smartest-looking app or the most automated one, but because it can become the most credible, lowest-friction way to maintain a serious record of valuable physical objects across categories — fast enough at capture to beat the spreadsheet it's replacing, and calm and honest enough at review to be trusted with someone's actual collection. The real risk to that promise isn't underbuilt aesthetics or thin classification — it's under-investing in the unglamorous guarantees (soft-delete, backup confirmation, downgrade preservation, export correctness at every item count) in favor of the more fun parts to build. Those boring guarantees are what actually decide whether someone trusts this app with a $40,000 collection.

### Next Steps Small

CONSENSUS: YES

Round 2 actually resolved the fork. Codex made the case that skipping export entirely would mean testing a weaker product than the one anyone signed up to build — this app's whole pitch is "more credible than a spreadsheet when you need to hand the record to someone," and that's precisely the half of the concept that's genuinely uncertain (structured data turning into a coherent document), not the capture-and-browse half, which is well-trodden. Claude read that and conceded it straight out rather than defending the original position — but split the export requirement in a way both landed on: the export pipeline has to be structurally real and correct in this slice (right photos, right dates, timelines laid out distinctly, purchase facts present, tested at zero/one/many items), but it doesn't need final museum-catalog typography polish yet — that's a legitimate "do it well later" item, not a "skip it now" item.

On categories, Claude walked back their own suggestion once they thought it through against what was already locked upstream: since all six categories share one schema with no per-category logic differences, narrowing to 2-3 for validation wouldn't actually save any build effort, it would just arbitrarily under-test the cross-category promise that's the whole point of the app. So all six stay in.

On app-lock, this was never really a disagreement — Codex simply hadn't mentioned it either way in round 1, and in round 2 explicitly said it should be in, cheap and trust-critical. Both agree it belongs in the first slice.

One small thing that isn't fully nailed down: Codex said single-item export is mandatory and collection-level export "can wait one step if needed," while Claude wants both in this slice. Nobody pushed back on Claude's stronger version, and since the collection export is a thin wrapper over the same per-item rendering logic once it exists, I'm treating this as settled in favor of including both — it's not a real fork, just Codex hedging slightly more than Claude did.

## Final Output

**MVP slice (the one workflow this build must prove):** A user captures a photo of something they own (any of the six fixed categories — watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other, Vision silently pre-filling the category suggestion in the background), gives it a title, and saves it in well under a minute. The item appears in an archival-card list. From its detail screen, the user adds one condition/provenance entry (note + optional photo) and one value entry, seeing them laid out as two clearly separate timelines, never merged. The user can soft-delete an item and recover it within the grace window, and the whole catalog sits behind Face ID/passcode app-lock. Finally, the user generates a local PDF export — for that single item, and for the collection as a whole — that is structurally correct (right cover photo, right dated entries, condition and value history laid out distinctly, purchase facts present) even if its final typographic polish isn't fully dialed in yet.

**Must-have interactions:**
- Create an item with only photo + title + category required; save completes in well under a minute.
- All six categories available and functionally identical under the hood (shared schema, free-text subtype).
- Vision classification pre-fills the category chip silently; never blocks or delays save; fully overridable.
- Resume a half-finished capture after interruption (background, force-quit) without losing the photo/draft.
- On the detail screen, independently add: purchase facts, one condition/provenance entry, one value entry — shown as two visually distinct timeline modules.
- A defined near-empty state for the value ledger at 0-1 entries (a simple list/table of entries is enough; no rendered chart needed yet).
- Soft-delete an item and restore it within the recovery window.
- App-lock via Face ID/passcode gating entry to the catalog.
- Generate and preview a PDF export for one item and for the full collection; verified structurally correct at zero items, one sparse item, and one fully-populated item — photos, dates, condition/value history, and purchase facts all render correctly, even with rougher typography than the final design pass.
- Relaunch persistence: everything above survives an app relaunch, not just the current session.

**Cut list:** Widget, StoreKit/paywall, rendered value-over-time chart (a plain list/table of value entries stands in for now), Vision as anything beyond silent background pre-fill, settings/backup-messaging screen, narrowing category count (rejected — all six stay, since they're free once the shared schema exists), multi-photo galleries beyond one photo per timeline entry, final export typography/design polish (deferred, not dropped — the pipeline must be structurally correct now, gallery-ready visuals come later).

**Validation criteria:** A first-time user can go from tapping "add item" to a saved catalog entry in well under a minute. A capture interrupted by backgrounding or force-quit is recoverable, not lost. After relaunch, all saved items, photos, condition entries, value entries, and app-lock state are intact. A soft-deleted item can be restored inside its recovery window. The app is inaccessible without passing Face ID/passcode. The PDF export (single-item and collection-level) opens successfully and is structurally honest at zero, one-sparse, and one-fully-populated item counts — no blank/broken renders, no misattributed photos, no garbled dates. If all of that holds, one complete, trustworthy workflow exists end-to-end, and the next phase can proceed straight to build without reopening product debate.

### Detailed Discussion

Round 3 actually closed it, and closed it well — both of them converged on essentially the same third option independently, from different angles. Codex proposed it directly: don't interleave locked items into the active list and don't banish them to a separate destination either — put a clearly-labeled "Preserved Items" section right on the main catalog screen, collapsed with a couple of cover-photo peeks and a count, tap through to a full read-only list. Claude arrived at the same place by actually simulating the failure mode: since the tiebreak rule is "oldest stays active" and lists sort newest-first by default, inline-in-place would mean a downgraded user's *most recent* purchases are the ones sitting dimmed at the top of their list, burying the 25 items they can actually still use — that's a concrete, specific reason inline-in-place is worse than either of them gave it credit for, not just a stylistic preference.

Claude also added a genuinely new and useful piece underneath the IA decision: whether an item is "active" or "locked" should be computed at read-time (oldest 25 by creation order, stable tiebreaker) rather than stored as a persisted flag, because a stored flag means every mutation — delete, resubscribe, downgrade, restore — needs its own bookkeeping to keep it correct, and that's exactly the shape of bug that leaves someone with only 24 usable items until they force-quit. Computed-not-stored makes that class of bug structurally impossible. That naturally answered a question nobody had asked yet — yes, a user can delete an item sitting in the locked/preserved section without resubscribing, since deletion is data-minimization, not enrichment, and doing so should just automatically promote the next-oldest locked item into the active set with no special-case code. And the naming discipline matters too: this preserved/locked section is permanent and never auto-purges, explicitly distinct from the soft-delete "recently deleted" bin, so an anxious user post-downgrade can't mistake "frozen but safe forever" for "on a countdown."

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:**
- *Provenance* means user-entered acquisition/history context (source, acquisition date, seller/location, optional note and photo) plus dated follow-up notes over time — explicitly not document verification or chain-of-custody proof.
- *Condition* is a hybrid model: a current-state grade field on the item plus a dated timeline of condition entries (note, optional photo, optional "condition changed" marker) — neither a grade alone nor a pile of notes alone is sufficient.
- *Value entries* carry amount, date, a constrained valuation-type enum (purchase price / appraisal / dealer offer / market observation / insured value), and a source note. This is locked as a hard constraint for whoever designs the future value chart: entries must be visually distinguished by type, never plotted as one undifferentiated connected line, or the "don't conflate value semantics" discipline gets undone by its own visualization.
- Draft capture: the instant a photo is taken, a real persisted `Item` row is created (placeholder title "Untitled", category "Other"), not held in a separate ephemeral staging mechanism. This is the only way to guarantee zero data loss without inventing a second persistence path, and it's an accepted tradeoff that abandoned drafts become real cataloged rows the user has to notice and clean up.
- Photo failure after metadata save, and never-confirmed drafts, are the same underlying problem (an incomplete record in the store) and share one `status` enum on Item: draft / incomplete / complete. One shared "needs attention, tap to finish" list-card treatment covers both, rather than two independently-invented half-finished-item mechanisms.
- Vision classification shows nothing rather than a low-confidence guess; requires an explicit label-to-category mapping table for the six-category enum decided at build time, not improvised.
- App-lock via Face ID/passcode, with an explicit escape hatch: if the device has no passcode set at all (so `LocalAuthentication` can't fall back), the lock is skipped entirely rather than trapping the user out of their own catalog.
- PDF export renders off-main-thread with a defined max embed dimension per photo, for both responsiveness (loading state must be real, not a frozen UI) and file size.
- Currency is a single fixed device-locale unit, no per-item override. Entry dates reject future dates but allow arbitrary backdating.
- **Free-tier downgrade / over-cap items:** the active 25 (oldest-by-creation-order, with a stable secondary tiebreaker — insertion order or row ID, since raw Date equality isn't fine-grained enough to guarantee a deterministic test) render as a clean, fully-interactive primary list with zero locked clutter. Everything beyond the cap lives in a permanently-visible, clearly-labeled "Preserved/Locked" section on the same main catalog screen (peek thumbnails + count, tap through to a full read-only list) — never interleaved as dimmed rows in the active list, never moved off to a hidden destination, and never described or behaving like the soft-delete "recently deleted" bin (no countdown, no auto-purge, no "empty" action).
- Active-vs-locked status is computed at read time from subscription state + item count, not stored as a persisted flag, so deletes/resubscribes/downgrades/restores can't desync it.
- Item creation is blocked once a user is at-or-above 25 active items, regardless of how they got there (organic growth or downgrade) — no loophole where a new item goes active while older items sit orphaned.
- A user can delete an item sitting in the locked/preserved section without resubscribing; doing so promotes the next-oldest locked item into the active set automatically, with no special-case logic required.
- Privacy: values are hidden from widgets, and that same discipline extends to app-switcher snapshots and notification previews — a privacy overlay swaps in on the background `scenePhase` transition.

**Edge cases:** device with no passcode set (skip lock, don't trap); Vision returns no confident mapping (show nothing); photo write fails after metadata write (mark incomplete, don't pretend whole); app backgrounded/force-quit mid-capture (draft already persisted, resumable); two items with identical creation timestamps (stable secondary sort key); downgraded user at the cap tries to create a new item (blocked, same upsell prompt as organic cap-hit); downgraded user deletes a locked item (promotes next-oldest automatically); PDF export with many high-res photos (downsampled, off-main-thread, must not freeze UI); soft-deleted item still logically counts toward nothing (excluded from the 25-cap math entirely).

**Data and privacy implications:** all core data stays on-device, no cloud account, no remote classification calls, no analytics transmitting item details or photos; app is responsible-user-content-only — no redaction/OCR/content moderation of photos, stated explicitly rather than left implicit; exported temp PDF files must be cleaned up after the share sheet completes or is dismissed; standard encrypted device backup coverage should be explicitly confirmed and communicated to the user (carried from earlier phases); locked/preserved items remain fully present in local storage and in backups — restriction is a UI/access-layer concept, never a storage-layer deletion.

**Risk register:** Vision misclassification on niche items — mitigated by confidence floor + silent no-suggestion fallback, never blocking or misleading. Manual value-entry fatigue leading to sparse/stale value history — named as a real limitation, not solved in this build; the chart's near-empty state and the type-differentiated encoding are the mitigations available at this layer. Stored-flag desync bugs on the active/locked boundary — mitigated by making the split computed, not persisted. Confusion between "preserved/locked" (permanent, safe) and "recently deleted" (time-limited, at risk) — mitigated by deliberately distinct naming, visuals, and behavior (no countdown/purge affordance on the locked section). Main-thread blocking or oversized files during PDF export — mitigated by off-main-thread rendering and a defined image-dimension ceiling. Privacy leakage via widget/app-switcher/notification snapshots — mitigated by a scenePhase-driven privacy overlay applied consistently across all three surfaces, not just widgets.

**Final assumptions:** a fixed six-category enum with shared schema and free-text subtype (no per-category logic); one photo per condition/value entry in v1 (named limitation, not silently dropped); single fixed currency per install; no content moderation of user photos; the value chart itself (with type-differentiated rendering) is out of scope for this slice but constrained now for whoever builds it; the "Preserved/Locked" section is a same-screen, always-visible feature of the main catalog, not a settings-buried or fully separate destination — this is now settled IA, not an open question for the design phase to re-litigate.

### App Features

CONSENSUS: YES

Codex came back and gave clean, direct answers to both open threads, and there's no daylight between the two of them anymore. On incomplete-and-locked, Codex agrees flatly: locked wins. The item still wears its honest "needs attention" badge (no pretending a broken record is fine), but tapping it goes to a read-only view with an upgrade message, never the repair flow — because letting "needs attention" silently double as "tap to edit" would create exactly the kind of subscription loophole that's invisible in a design review and shows up as a support ticket later. On the paywall preview, Codex confirmed his earlier "nothing beyond what's strictly needed" line was too loose and wasn't meant to walk back the redacted-export-preview requirement — he now states it explicitly as a real, structured mock export behind the gate (real layout, real typography, blurred/redacted sensitive content), with the cut list narrowed to the stuff that's actually ornamental: no paywall A/B variants, no fake urgency/countdown tactics, no interstitial spam outside natural trigger moments.

Claude used the room Codex's answers opened up to tighten the loose ends further rather than starting a new fight: the incomplete/locked collision is really a three-way precedence question once you add soft-delete into the mix (deleted overrides everything, locked overrides incomplete, incomplete-badge-with-repair-access only applies when an item is both active and not deleted), and restoring a soft-deleted item should never be blocked by the 25-item cap the way new item *creation* is — a restore just re-enters the pool and falls out active-or-locked based on the same computed, read-time model everyone already locked, no special-casing needed, just a confirmation message that says so plainly. Nothing here contradicts anything Codex has said; it's a direct, logical extension of principles both of them have already fully bought into (soft-delete over hard delete, computed-not-stored active/locked status, never punish the user for undoing their own mistake). Given how consistently these two have converged round over round, this reads as settled rather than a new fork waiting on a reply.

## Final Output

**Must-have features (each with a user story + acceptance bar):**

1. **Catalog home with four legible record states.** *Story:* a collector needs to instantly tell what's active, preserved/locked, needs attention, or at-risk-of-permanent-loss, so nothing ever feels like it silently vanished. *Acceptance:* empty/loading/success/error states on the main list; a permanently-visible "Preserved/Locked" section (peek thumbnails + count, never interleaved as dimmed rows); a single computed "effective state" per item with strict precedence — **deleted overrides everything** (only restore/purge, no repair, no lock messaging) → **locked overrides incomplete** (read-only + upgrade CTA, never the repair flow) → **incomplete-badge-with-repair-access** only when the item is active and not deleted; no item shows contradictory affordances.

2. **Fast capture with silent, free, universal classification assist.** *Story:* someone logging a purchase right after acquisition needs to save in well under a minute with zero forced steps. *Acceptance:* photo + title + category alone save it; a real Item row is created the instant the photo is taken (draft/incomplete/complete); Vision pre-fills the category chip silently for every user regardless of subscription tier, shows nothing below its confidence floor, never blocks or delays save, and carries zero premium framing (no lock icon, no upsell copy) anywhere near it; interrupted/backgrounded/force-quit capture resumes the same draft.

3. **Item detail with three separated enrichment tracks.** *Story:* reviewing a record later needs to feel like a dossier, not a dumped feed. *Acceptance:* purchase facts, condition/provenance timeline (grade + dated entries), and value-entry ledger render as visually distinct modules that never merge; cover photo is distinct from timeline photos; 0/1-entry value states have an intentional near-empty design, not a generic empty view; locked items remain fully viewable read-only.

4. **Value chart — correct and type-differentiated, not yet interactive.** *Story:* the subscription has to keep earning its keep as the collection ages, and the chart is the feature that actually delivers "grows with your collection." *Acceptance:* entries render with distinct visual encoding per valuation type (purchase/appraisal/dealer offer/market observation/insured value) — never one undifferentiated connected line; correctness and honesty are must-have now, interactivity/animation/zoom are explicitly deferred, not required for this build.

5. **Soft-delete, recovery, and uncapped restore.** *Story:* mistakes must be reversible, and getting an item back can never feel like it's being held for ransom. *Acceptance:* delete moves an item to Recently Deleted (visually/behaviorally distinct from Preserved/Locked — no countdown or purge affordance on the locked section, only on this one); deleted items are excluded from the 25-cap math entirely; restore is never blocked by the active-item cap — it re-enters the pool and lands active-or-locked purely from the existing computed read-time model, surfaced with an explicit message when it lands locked ("Restored — preserved but requires Provenance+ to edit").

6. **App-lock and passive-surface privacy.** *Story:* a list of valuables and what they cost shouldn't be casually visible. *Acceptance:* Face ID/passcode gates entry when device authentication exists; skips the lock entirely (never traps the user) when no passcode is set; a privacy overlay swaps in on background `scenePhase` transitions across the app, the widget, and app-switcher/notification surfaces alike.

7. **Local PDF export, single-item and collection, structurally correct.** *Story:* the record has to be handoff-ready without the app. *Acceptance:* both single-item and full-collection export exist; verified correct at zero/one-sparse/one-full item counts; correct photos, dates, condition/value history, and purchase facts in the right sections; off-main-thread rendering with a defined max photo embed dimension and a real loading/error/success state; final premium typography is explicitly deferred, structural correctness is not.

8. **StoreKit 2 gating with a real, desirable paywall preview.** *Story:* free users need a genuinely usable ledger, and the upgrade ask has to sell a real artifact, not an abstraction. *Acceptance:* 25-item free cap enforced deterministically (oldest-by-creation-order + stable tiebreaker); creation blocked at-or-above 25 active items regardless of cause; the paywall screen renders an actual mock export layout — real section headers, real photo placement, real typography — with sensitive content blurred/redacted, so the thing being sold is visibly the thing the user's data already supports; downgrade hides via the computed active/locked split, never deletes; resubscribe restores full access with no migration step; subscription-status is read through an injectable/testable abstraction so purchase/restore/downgrade behavior isn't only verifiable via a live sandbox click-through.

9. **Widget with distinct free/paid states.** *Story:* the home screen widget must reflect subscription reality without leaking private values. *Acceptance:* free tier shows item count, paid tier shows collection value; refreshes are debounced/coalesced on local write events (not fired per-save) to respect WidgetKit's background reload budget; values respect the same privacy-overlay discipline as the rest of the app; tap-to-open only — no deep-linking, no in-widget actions.

**Should-have:** final premium export typography/layout polish; collection summary totals on the home screen; a settings surface stating backup/privacy posture explicitly; richer condition-change presentation over time; system Increase Contrast / Reduce Motion support within the dark aesthetic.

**Could-have:** richer preserved-section thumbnail previews; subtle museum-catalog theming touches beyond the baseline; lightweight bulk actions inside Recently Deleted; suggested subtype text; haptic save confirmation; a first-launch sample item so new users don't hit a blank empty state; a full light-mode theme (explicitly named as a deliberate tradeoff against the dark-archival direction, not an oversight).

**Won't-build:** cloud sync, live/market pricing, multi-currency, multi-user/shared collections, marketplace or trading features, social/community comparison, OCR or barcode/serial lookup, search/filter/sort beyond basic ordering, per-category dynamic schemas, more than one photo per timeline entry, iPad-specific layout, any insurer/legal certification language, interactive or deep-linking widget behavior, paywall A/B variants or fake-urgency dark patterns, promotional interstitials outside natural trigger moments (hitting the cap, opening export, opening the chart), and any inline IA that interleaves locked items as dimmed rows through the active list.

### Design Discussion

CONSENSUS: YES

Round two actually closed both open threads cleanly, and interestingly, both of them ended up switching or refining their own round-one stance rather than digging in.

On value tracking: Codex came down decisively on the side of the original spec — free tier does not get a working value ledger, full stop. The Value card still appears on every item's detail screen for free users (so the feature isn't hidden or a mystery), but it renders as a locked teaser with a one-line pitch and an upgrade action, never an actually-usable empty ledger. Codex's reasoning: letting free users log real dated value entries while only gating the chart creates a mushy tier split and lets people do all the "valuation tracking" work without ever feeling like they got the feature. Claude then closed the one gap Codex's answer left open — what happens on downgrade — by tying it to language the group already locked back in product_research ("value history remains viewable but can't be edited or regenerated without re-subscribing"): a never-subscribed user always sees the locked teaser; a subscriber's Value card comes alive and stays alive while entries exist; a downgraded user keeps their historical entries fully visible read-only, with "add" disabled and a message like "entries preserved — resubscribe to add more." That's not a new argument, it's stitching an already-agreed rule onto a card Codex had already agreed to gate, so there's nothing left in tension here.

On Recently Deleted: Codex reversed his round-one position and agreed it shouldn't live as a second scrollable section on the home screen next to Preserved/Locked, for exactly the reason Claude gave — two "your stuff lives somewhere else" modules on one screen recreates the confusion the group spent three rounds avoiding. Where they landed together: Recently Deleted becomes a quiet, always-available overflow/toolbar affordance right on Catalog Home (Codex said "Settings or an overflow path off the catalog toolbar," Claude specifically proposed the toolbar/overflow-menu version so it needs zero navigation away from home) — plus, Claude added a piece nobody had proposed yet that solves the real emotional moment directly: an inline "Item moved to Recently Deleted — Undo" toast the instant a delete happens, so regret is handled on the spot without anyone needing to visit a destination screen at all. Codex didn't get a chance to react to that specific undo-toast addition, but it doesn't conflict with anything he said — it's a strict improvement on the same agreed placement, not a competing idea.

They also converged in passing on tone: needs-attention/incomplete states should never use red or an alarming visual language — reserve that only for genuinely destructive confirmations — and use a muted, same-typographic-system treatment instead, so the catalog stays one calm archive rather than turning into a to-do list with guilt attached.

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:** App Lock gate (shown on cold launch and on return-from-background when device authentication is available; skipped entirely if no device passcode exists) → Catalog Home (single NavigationStack, no tab bar) → Capture sheet (camera-first, opens instantly, no pre-picker) → Quick Log finishing card (photo + title + six category chips, silent Vision suggestion highlight, Save is the only required action) → Item Detail → Add Condition/Provenance Entry sheet → Add Value Entry sheet (only reachable once Value tracking is unlocked) → Purchase Facts editor → Preserved/Locked read-only list (pushed from its Catalog Home section) → Recently Deleted list (reached via a quiet overflow/toolbar affordance on Catalog Home, not a scrolling home-screen section) → Export Preview (single-item and collection variants share one screen behind a mode toggle) → Paywall → Settings (app-lock toggle, backup/privacy statement, subscription management).

**Primary user flow:** Open app → unlock if applicable → tap Add → capture/pick photo (an Item row is persisted the instant the photo lands) → Quick Log card appears already populated with the photo, an editable "Untitled" title, and six category chips; Vision's suggestion silently highlights a chip if and when it resolves, never blocking; user types a title, optionally taps a category, taps Save → item now exists in the active catalog, which is the moment of relief the product has to deliver in well under a minute. Everything else — purchase facts, condition/provenance entries, value entries, export — is optional enrichment reached later from Item Detail, never forced before the first save.

**Catalog Home layout:** active items as photo-forward cards (cover photo, title, category, and a status affordance only when the computed effective-state model actually has something to say — nothing extra for a normal complete item); directly beneath, the permanently-visible Preserved/Locked section (collapsed, a couple of cover-photo peeks, a count, one tap to the full read-only list) rendered quietly so it doesn't compete for attention when empty; Recently Deleted lives as a small overflow/toolbar affordance on this same screen — always one tap away, but taking no scrolling real estate — and every delete action also surfaces an immediate inline "Item moved to Recently Deleted — Undo" toast so the moment of regret is handled without navigating anywhere.

**Value-tracking gate (resolved this round):** the Value card appears on every item's detail screen regardless of subscription — visibility of the feature's existence is never hidden — but for a user who has never subscribed it renders as a locked teaser (short description + redacted mini-preview + one upgrade action), not a usable empty ledger. Subscribing brings the card to life and lets entries be logged. On downgrade, previously-logged entries stay fully visible read-only ("entries preserved — resubscribe to add more"); locked/preserved items simply inherit the same read-only treatment they already have everywhere else. This is a new decision made in this phase, not an inference — the paywall/detail build should treat it as settled.

**State model per screen:**
- *Catalog Home:* empty (first-launch, calm "Add your first item" prompt), loading (brief local-fetch skeleton, no blank flash), success, error (SwiftData load failure gets a real recovery screen, never silent).
- *Capture/Quick Log:* camera permission denied (manual/library fallback), photo captured, classification pending/unavailable/low-confidence (chip shows no suggestion), photo write failed after metadata save (item marked incomplete, not pretended complete), interrupted/backgrounded/force-quit (draft resumes on relaunch).
- *Item Detail:* complete item, incomplete item (repair-access badge, only when active and not deleted/locked), locked item (read-only, upgrade CTA, never repair flow), deleted item (restore/purge only), each of the three cards with its own distinct empty/populated states (0 condition entries reads differently from 0 value entries; locked Value teaser is its own explicit state, not a generic empty view).
- *Export Preview:* unavailable (free tier, upsell), generating (real progress, off-main-thread), success preview, failed render (retry path), zero-item collection export (calm "nothing to report yet" page, never blank/malformed).
- *Preserved/Locked list:* populated read-only list, attempted-edit shows upgrade prompt.
- *Recently Deleted:* empty, populated, item nearing purge messaging.
- *Paywall:* normal state (real redacted export mock, real layout/typography), StoreKit product-fetch failure (retry, never a silently broken paywall).

**Visual direction:** near-black (not pure-black) neutral background, warm off-white primary text, one sparing accent color reserved for the suggested-category highlight and primary actions only; serif for item titles/section headers/export surfaces, system sans for body, metadata, dates, and numbers so those stay maximally legible; "archival" comes from restraint, whitespace, and quiet borders rather than low-contrast mood lighting — condition-photo timelines and the value chart are explicitly named as the two screens where legibility must win over atmosphere. Needs-attention/incomplete states use a muted, same-typographic-system treatment (e.g., a quiet amber dot or small label) — never red, which is reserved only for genuinely destructive confirmations — so the catalog reads as one calm archive rather than a scolding to-do list.

**Accessibility notes:** category chips announce suggested-vs-manual state distinctly to VoiceOver rather than reading as identical buttons; every effective-state badge (locked, incomplete, deleted) has a spoken label, never a silent icon; cover photos and timeline photos get distinct, dated VoiceOver labels ("condition entry photo from June 3, 2026," not "image"); the value ledger has a fully VoiceOver-navigable list form so understanding value history never depends on interpreting a chart visually; color is never the sole signal separating active/locked/incomplete/deleted; Dynamic Type at the largest accessibility sizes is specifically checked against Item Detail's three-card layout and Export Preview's paginated view, the two layouts most likely to break under long text wrapping.

### Design Handoff

CONSENSUS: YES

The one thing this phase actually needed to resolve — external Claude Design pass or not — got closed cleanly. Codex flipped from "here's a prompt if you want it" to stating outright "no external design pause is the right call," reasoning that the internal handoff is already a finished design system in prose and a fresh external pass risks re-introducing the glossy "luxury collector" look the group has now argued against three separate times. Claude made the same call independently and for the same reason, adding that external generative design tools are useful for discovering an unknown direction, not for re-deriving a direction that's already been hard-won over six rounds across two phases — real risk there is dilution, not improvement. Full agreement: ship straight to the SwiftUI builder from this handoff; keep Codex's drafted prompt on file only as a non-blocking appendix for something low-stakes later (App Store marketing frames, a future rebrand exploration), never as a build gate.

Everything else this round was tightening, not fighting. Codex dropped the collection-summary strip from Catalog Home's baseline layout to keep the screen archive-first rather than dashboard-like — home screen is now just title, toolbar, active cards, and the quiet Preserved/Locked shelf. Claude flagged that wherever a "collection value" number does get shown (the widget, mainly, now that it's off the home screen), it needs to read from one shared computed `CollectionSummary` value rather than two independently-written totals that could quietly drift apart. Claude also proposed treating the Export element on Item Detail as an entry-point CTA into Export Preview rather than a fourth data-bearing module — same card styling, different semantic role — specifically so nobody down the line reads it as contradicting the "three separated enrichment tracks" language locked twice already; nothing here fights what Codex described, it's just naming it precisely. And Claude merged Codex's descriptive palette with the earlier hex tokens into one canonical token table, plus flagged a real architecture consequence hiding in the design (the widget needs an App Group–shared SwiftData container configured from day one, not migrated in later).

## Final Output

**External design decision:** No external Claude Design pass. This handoff is builder-ready as written; the drafted Claude Design prompt (below) is archived as an optional, non-blocking reference for future marketing/App Store imagery only — it does not gate the build.

**Navigation model:** One root `NavigationStack` with a typed path enum (`.itemDetail(Item.ID)`, `.preservedList`, `.recentlyDeleted`, `.exportPreview(ExportMode)`, `.settings`) for archive drill-ins, plus `.sheet(item:)`-presented focused tasks (Capture/Quick Log, Add Condition Entry, Add Value Entry, Purchase Facts editor, Paywall). Rule: "drilling into the archive" = push; "a focused task you finish or back out of" = sheet. App Lock is a `.fullScreenCover` driven by a root-level `scenePhase` observer, rendered before the stack's content and paired with a single reusable privacy-overlay modifier applied at the root (not per-screen) so no screen — especially Export Preview — can leak sensitive content in an app-switcher snapshot.

**Screen-by-screen spec:**

- **App Lock gate** — full-screen cover on cold launch and return-from-background when device authentication exists; skipped entirely (never traps the user) if no passcode is set.
- **Catalog Home** — title, trailing Add button, overflow menu (Recently Deleted, Settings). Body: active photo-forward item cards (cover photo, title, category, at most one status affordance), then the quiet collapsed Preserved/Locked shelf (count + 2-3 thumbnail peeks, one tap to full list). No summary strip in the baseline. States: empty (first-launch, serif headline + one line of copy + "Add Your First Item," no illustration), loading (3-4 static muted skeleton cards, no shimmer), success, error (SwiftData load failure → real "Your catalog couldn't be opened" + Retry, never silent).
- **Capture (camera-first, full-screen)** → **Quick Log** finishing card — photo + focused "Untitled" title field + six category chips + Save, nothing else required. An `Item` row persists the instant the photo lands, before the sheet even finishes presenting. States: permission-denied (manual-entry fallback with placeholder photo slot, not a dead end), classification pending (chips render immediately, no spinner), classification resolved (one chip highlights with a soft ~250ms fade + "Suggested" caption, no pop/pulse), low-confidence/unavailable (chips stay neutral), photo-write-failed (item still saves as incomplete; inline non-blocking notice, never blocks Save). Backgrounding/dismissing the sheet never deletes the draft — it closes on top of an already-saved incomplete item, resumable on return.
- **Item Detail** — header (cover photo, title, category/subtype, condition grade, acquisition date, value summary if unlocked/populated), then three data-bearing modules in fixed order — Purchase Facts, Condition & Provenance (grade + dated timeline), Value (ledger first, type-differentiated, chart secondary) — each independently collapsible past ~3 entries with "View all N entries." Below the three modules sits an Export **entry-point CTA** (same card visual language, but a navigation affordance into Export Preview pre-set to single-item mode, not a fourth data track with its own empty-state machinery). Value card renders as a locked teaser (copy + redacted mini-preview + upgrade action) for never-subscribed users, live and editable for subscribers, and read-only-preserved with "entries preserved — resubscribe to add more" after downgrade. Locked/deleted items follow the effective-state precedence: deleted > locked > incomplete-with-repair-access.
- **Preserved/Locked list** — flatter card treatment than active cards to signal read-only, top explanatory line ("Your records are preserved. Upgrade to edit or add beyond your free limit."), attempted edit shows the upgrade prompt.
- **Recently Deleted** — reached via a quiet overflow/toolbar affordance on Catalog Home, never a home-screen scrolling section. Operational, not archival, visual tone: smaller rows, per-item "Deleted N days ago — permanently removed in N days" (never a single global banner), restore/purge actions. Every delete also fires an inline "Item moved to Recently Deleted — Undo" toast at the moment of deletion, independent of this screen.
- **Export Preview** — mode toggle (single item / collection) at top. States: locked/upsell (free tier), generating (real off-main progress, page-placeholder skeleton, toggle/back stay responsive), success (paginated preview + share/save in toolbar), failed (retry), zero-item collection (a real rendered sparse "nothing to report yet" page, never blank/malformed).
- **Paywall** — static single scroll: headline, redacted export mock (real serif headers, real photo placeholders, blurred numeric fields), short feature list, monthly/annual price buttons, restore-purchases link, legal footer. StoreKit fetch failure gets its own retry state, never a silently broken screen.
- **Settings** — app-lock toggle, backup/privacy statement, subscription management, no other surfaces.

**Design tokens (single canonical set):**
- Background `#16151A`; surface/card `#1F1E24` with an 8%-white hairline border (no drop shadows).
- Primary text `#EDE9E3` (warm ivory); secondary/metadata text a muted warm gray, ≥4.5:1 against both background and surface.
- One accent, muted brass `#B08D57` — Vision-suggested chip highlight and primary actions only.
- Needs-attention: desaturated amber `#9C7A3C`-ish (visually distinct from the brass accent) — never red.
- True red reserved exclusively for destructive-delete confirmation.
- Serif (system-available, e.g. New York) at Large Title/Title for nav titles, item titles, section headers, export headings. System sans everywhere else — body, metadata, dates, chip/button labels — with tabular figures forced on for every numeric value.
- One card corner radius (14pt), one chip radius (8pt), one 4pt-based spacing scale (4/8/12/16/24/32).

**Component inventory:** PhotoCard, CategoryChip (default/suggested/selected, VoiceOver-distinct), EffectiveStateBadge (text-labeled, never icon-only), TimelineEntryRow (shared shape for condition and value tracks, "kind" parameter changes accent treatment), LockedTeaserCard, UndoToast (auto-dismiss ~4s, swipe-to-dismiss, doesn't steal VoiceOver focus from the underlying list), ExportPagePlaceholder, PaywallPreviewCard, EmptyStateView (parameterized, never generic-feeling), PrivacyOverlay (one root-level modifier, not per-screen).

**State/interaction notes:** Effective state (deleted / locked / incomplete / normal) is one pure function computed per render from three independent facts (deletedAt, status enum, active-25 membership) feeding one `ItemDisplayState` enum that every card/badge/detail view switches over — never independent per-screen checks. Active/locked partition is one O(n) query (sort by creation order, first 25 active) — not a per-item O(n²) check. Cover photos are pre-downsampled thumbnails generated off-main at save time and cached by item ID; PDF export uses a separate downsample pass at its own max-embed dimension, never the list thumbnail. Widget and any in-app "collection value" figure must both read from one shared `CollectionSummary` computed value to prevent drift between the two. Motion is restrained throughout — fade-only card appearance, soft suggestion-highlight fade, gentle undo-toast slide, system-default sheet transitions — and fully collapses to instant appearance/disappearance under Reduce Motion.

**Accessibility requirements:** Category chips announce suggested-vs-manual state distinctly, never as identical buttons. Every EffectiveStateBadge has a spoken label built from the same enum driving its visual state ("Watch. Locked. Requires Provenance+ to edit."), so visual and spoken state can't disagree. Cover and timeline photos get distinct, dated labels ("condition entry photo from June 3, 2026," never "image"). Color is never the sole signal for active/locked/incomplete/deleted. The value ledger is fully understandable via VoiceOver list traversal without reading the chart. Timeline expand/collapse labels change state-appropriately ("View all 12 entries" ↔ "Show fewer entries"). Export Preview announces page position ("Page 2 of 5"). Dynamic Type at largest accessibility sizes is checked specifically against Item Detail's module stack and Export Preview's paginated view; category chips wrap to two rows rather than truncate at large sizes.

**Architecture note carried into build (not a screen, but load-bearing for this design):** the widget requires an App Group–shared container from day one — the SwiftData `ModelContainer` must be configured to store its file there at initial setup, not migrated in after the fact, to avoid a real data-migration problem once the widget is added.

**Archived (non-blocking) Claude Design prompt, for future marketing/App Store use only:** "Design an iPhone SwiftUI app called Provenance for serious collectors of high-value physical goods.
