### Product Research

# provenance — Product Research

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 20:56:14._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Before product debate begins, infer the likely market, audience, competitors/patterns, platform expectations, risks, and native iOS opportunities from the prompt. Label assumptions clearly and turn the research into implications for the first build.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

The likely buyer is not a casual hobbyist browsing collectibles for fun. It is a person with enough value tied up in physical objects that losing track of condition, purchase details, or proof of ownership feels financially irresponsible. That changes the UX bar. They are not looking for delight first; they are looking for confidence, speed at the moment of acquisition, and records that still make sense a year later. The use context is usually one-handed, interrupted, and immediate: standing at a table after a purchase, unboxing at home, logging something before the receipt disappears, or updating condition after noticing damage. A secondary context is slower and more analytical: reviewing the collection, checking total exposure, and producing a report for insurance, estate planning, or resale prep.

The closest alternatives are not just collector apps. They are spreadsheets, Notes, photo albums, Airtable-style personal databases, category-specific apps like watch or wine trackers, and the pattern set by home inventory apps. The spreadsheet and Notes comparison matters most because that is the real incumbent behavior: flexible, ugly, and trusted because the user controls it. That means Provenance cannot win by being more clever. It has to win by making structured record-keeping faster than a spreadsheet without feeling less trustworthy. The category-specific app pattern is also a warning: users in watches or cards may already know tools that go deeper than this app ever will in their niche. So the cross-category pitch only works if the app is visibly excellent at documentation and history, not if it pretends to have specialist identification depth it does not have.

The native iPhone opportunities are strong, but they are narrower than the prompt makes them sound. Camera capture is the real platform advantage because it turns logging into a physical-world workflow instead of a form. Vision can help only if it stays humble and low-stakes: coarse category suggestion, maybe a few labels, never authoritative item identity. Files export and local PDF generation fit the trust model because they create a portable artifact the user can keep outside the app. Widgets are useful only if they reinforce the habit loop; for paid users that is total collection value or recent valuation change, and for free users it probably has to be item count or “last logged item” because the core value metric is gated. Accessibility is not optional here because collectors may be older and detail-heavy screens are exactly where small text and low contrast quietly kill usability.

The biggest market assumption is that serious collectors actually want one cross-category home for records instead of multiple specialist tools. That might be true for users whose problem is household asset tracking, inheritance, or insurance documentation. It may be false for users who are deeply embedded in one category and already rely on niche metadata standards. Another assumption is that manual value entry is enough to sustain engagement before a live price feed exists. I think that is shaky. Many users will record purchase price once and never maintain value history unless the app makes the payoff obvious. A third assumption is that “provenance” as a term helps rather than intimidates. For some audiences it signals seriousness; for others it sounds academic and heavier than the actual workflow.

The most important product risk is that the app tries to look authoritative in areas where it is structurally weak. “Insurance-grade” is the obvious example, but there is a UX version of the same mistake: charts and classification can create a false sense of precision. If the app shows a value trend line based on three manual entries, it may look smarter than it is. If the app suggests a model from a photo, users may over-trust it even with a confidence label. The design has to counter that. It should feel archival, not predictive. That means emphasizing source, date, notes, and photo evidence over algorithmic confidence theater.

Another risk is workflow friction. The app wants photos, condition, provenance, purchase facts, and maybe value, which is a lot to ask right after acquisition. If the first-run experience turns into a long intake form, users will revert to Notes. The first build needs a fast path that captures the minimum viable record in under a minute: photo, title, category, acquisition date, purchase price optional, notes optional. Condition history and provenance depth can expand after save. If that progressive disclosure is not preserved, the whole product becomes homework.

The design pattern I would borrow is less “dashboard app” and more “catalog record.” Each item should feel like a durable file with a cover photo, canonical facts, a dated timeline of changes, and a clean audit trail. The list view should not default to noisy KPI tiles. It should feel like browsing a private archive. The detail screen is the product. That is where trust is built: clear metadata grouping, visible last-updated dates, obvious provenance entries, and a condition timeline that supports photos over time instead of burying them in a generic gallery. The report preview should mirror that structure so the export feels like an extension of the app, not an afterthought.

I would also be careful with dark, museum-like styling. It fits the brand, but it can easily become illegible, especially on chart and photo-heavy screens. The right move is restrained dark neutrals with strong type hierarchy and disciplined contrast, not cinematic darkness. If the visual system makes labels, dates, or value fields harder to scan, it is harming the core job.

For the first build, I would treat the shippable version as a documentation ledger with optional valuation, not a valuation intelligence product. The app should be strongest at capture, structured records, timeline history, and export. Classification assist should be visibly secondary. Value charts should exist, but only after the item record itself feels complete and credible. If there is one detail the final output must preserve, it is this: the app has to make serious record-keeping feel faster than a spreadsheet without ever pretending to know more than the user. That restraint is the whole product.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Independent take on this phase's actual job, before anyone else weighs in:

The realistic audience isn't "collectors" broadly — it's a fairly narrow slice: people who already own a spreadsheet or Notes doc for watches, vinyl, cards, wine, or sneakers and are annoyed by it. That means two very different use moments that the app has to serve differently: a fast, high-friction-tolerance capture moment right after a purchase or unboxing (bad lighting, one-handed, wants to log and move on), and a slower periodic review moment (checking total value before a renewal, reconciling condition before selling, browsing the collection). If the capture flow is optimized for the wrong one — say, a rich multi-step wizard — it'll lose to "just add a row in Excel" on the first try, which is the exact competitor this app has to beat.

For comparable patterns, I'd look at three different buckets rather than one direct competitor, because none of them do the whole thing: home-inventory-for-insurance apps (Encircle, Nest Egg) are the closest match for the "photo + PDF export for insurance" workflow and are worth studying for what makes an export feel credible rather than gimmicky — itemized, dated, photo-anchored, boring in a good way. Category-specific collector apps (CLZ/Collectorz per-category, CellarTracker for wine, WatchCharts/Chrono24 for watches, Discogs for vinyl) show the cross-category promise is genuinely differentiated, but also show why single-category apps dominate: they can lean on a real external database or price feed for identification and valuation, which this app explicitly can't do in v1. General inventory apps (Sortly) show the barcode/folder/low-stock pattern, which is mostly irrelevant here except for the lesson that generic inventory tools feel cheap precisely because they don't specialize — Provenance's "quiet museum-catalog" direction only pays off if the fine details (typography, dates, structured fields) are actually more careful than a generic tool, not just darker-colored.

On platform fit, the thing I'd flag as under-considered in the spec is app-lock. This app is going to hold a structured, itemized list of a user's expensive possessions plus purchase prices — that's exactly the kind of data you don't want visible if someone picks up an unlocked phone. A Face ID/passcode gate via LocalAuthentication is cheap to build and directly reinforces the trust thesis everyone already agreed on; I'd treat it as a near-mandatory addition for the first build even though it wasn't named in the original iOS-native-features list, rather than something to debate away. Second platform point: widgets here have no natural refresh trigger — value only changes when the user manually adds an entry, so the widget timeline needs to reload on local data-write events, not on a timer, or it'll show stale totals and look broken in exactly the way that erodes trust in a "ledger" app.

Assumptions and risks I'd want on record before design starts. One: Vision's built-in classifiers are scene/object classifiers, not fine-grained identifiers — they can plausibly say "this is a wristwatch" or "this is a shoe," they cannot say "this is a 1970s Rolex Submariner ref. 5513" or "this is a first-pressing vs. reissue." If the first build's copy or UX implies anything more specific than broad-category-plus-confidence, real collectors will test it against their hardest item on day one and lose trust immediately. Two: the "authoritative record of insured possessions lives only on this phone" story has a silent failure mode if the SwiftData store isn't confirmed to participate in standard encrypted device/iCloud backups — that's a one-line settings check now versus a devastating support story later, and it should be verified and stated to the user as the actual backup story, not left implicit. Three: the downgrade-preserves-data rule the contract already locked in ("hide, don't delete") still has an unresolved sub-decision — when a user with 60 items drops to the 25-item free cap, which 25 stay visible/editable? That needs a concrete, boring rule now (I'd pick oldest-by-date-added stays active, simplest to reason about and to test) or it'll get improvised inconsistently during implementation. Four, smaller but real: the value-over-time chart has a distinct near-empty state — zero entries and exactly one entry both fail to produce a line chart — and that needs its own designed state, not a fallback to a generic empty view, since "not enough history yet" reads very differently to a user than "you haven't used this feature."

Implication for the first build: keep the category taxonomy a fixed, small enum (watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other) with a free-text subtype field rather than a dynamic per-category schema — the cross-category promise is the differentiator, but open-ended dynamic fields per category is exactly the kind of scope inflation that turns an MVP into a half-built app. Ship classification as category-only pre-fill with a visible confidence affordance, app-lock as a first-build feature, the downgrade tie-break rule decided now, and the chart's near-empty state designed explicitly — that's a small, concrete list the next phase can act on without having to rediscover any of it.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's the shape of round 1: Codex and I approached this from different angles but landed in the same place, and nothing either of us said contradicts the other — it's all additive. Gemini was unavailable again (CLI issue, not a content gap).

We agree on the audience: not "collectors" broadly, but people already running a spreadsheet or Notes doc for one of these categories who are annoyed by it — and specifically people whose annoyance is bad enough to make them switch tools, which is a narrower group than "collectors" sounds like. We agree the app has to serve two very different moments well: a fast, tolerant-of-bad-conditions capture moment right after acquisition, and a slower, more deliberate review/reporting moment later. We agree the real competitive bar isn't other collector apps, it's the spreadsheet itself — Provenance has to be faster than a spreadsheet at capture while feeling more trustworthy at review, or it loses on day one. We agree category-specific tools (Discogs, CellarTracker, WatchCharts) are worth studying precisely because they can lean on real external data this app can't have in v1, so the cross-category pitch only survives if documentation and export quality visibly beat what a specialist tool offers, not identification depth. And we agree Vision has to stay humble — coarse category suggestion only, never presented as identification, because both of us independently flagged that a real collector will test it against their hardest item almost immediately.

Where I added specifics beyond Codex's framing (and nothing here fought his read, just made it actionable): app-lock via Face ID/passcode as a near-mandatory addition given the data sensitivity even though the spec didn't name it; widgets need to refresh on local write events rather than a timer since there's no natural polling signal; a concrete tie-break rule for which items stay visible when a downgraded user exceeds the 25-item cap (oldest-by-date-added, for testability); the value chart needs its own explicit near-empty state for 0-1 entries rather than falling back to a generic empty view; and a fixed small category enum with a free-text subtype field rather than dynamic per-category schemas, to keep the cross-category promise from turning into scope inflation.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** Not casual hobbyists — people already tracking watches, vinyl, cards, wine, or sneakers in a spreadsheet or Notes who are frustrated enough to switch. Two distinct use moments: (1) fast, interrupted, often one-handed capture right after a purchase/unboxing — bad lighting, wants to log and move on; (2) slower periodic review — checking aggregate value, reconciling condition before selling, pulling a report for insurance/estate purposes. The first build must optimize for (1) without sacrificing (2); a multi-step intake wizard at capture time loses to "just add a row in Excel."

**Comparable apps/patterns to learn from:** Home-inventory-for-insurance apps (Encircle, Nest Egg) — best reference for what makes a PDF export feel credible: itemized, dated, photo-anchored, deliberately unflashy. Category-specific collector tools (Discogs, CellarTracker, WatchCharts, CLZ) — show the cross-category promise is genuinely differentiated, but also show why single-category tools dominate (real external databases/price feeds this app can't use in v1); the lesson is to win on documentation rigor, not pretend to specialist identification depth. Generic inventory apps (Sortly) — a cautionary pattern: generic tools feel cheap because they don't specialize, so Provenance's archival aesthetic only pays off if structured fields and typography are actually more careful, not just darker.

**Platform-specific opportunities:** Camera capture as the real workflow advantage (turns logging into a physical-world action, not a form). Vision framework used only for coarse category pre-fill with a visible confidence affordance — never fine-grained identification claims. Files/local PDF export as a portable trust artifact and the de facto backup story given no cloud sync in v1 (should explicitly confirm/communicate standard encrypted device backup coverage). Widgets tied to local data-write events rather than a timer, since there's no natural refresh signal; free tier shows item count, paid shows collection value. Face ID/passcode app-lock via LocalAuthentication — cheap, reinforces the trust thesis, and warranted given the app stores an itemized list of someone's valuables and purchase prices even though it wasn't in the original feature list. Dynamic Type/VoiceOver treated as core requirements, especially on photo- and chart-heavy screens, given the likely-older, detail-attentive user base.

**Major assumptions and risks:**
1. Vision's classifiers are coarse object/scene classifiers, not fine-grained identifiers — they can say "this is a wristwatch," never "this is a 1970s ref. 5513." Overstating this in copy or UX breaks trust the first time a real collector tests it.
2. Manual value-history entry may not sustain engagement without a live price feed — many users will log a purchase price once and never return to update it; the value-chart feature needs to earn its keep on its own, not assume habitual use.
3. The cross-category, single-home-for-records thesis may not hold for users deeply embedded in one category who already trust a specialist tool's metadata standards — this app wins the household-asset/insurance/estate use case, not necessarily the deep-in-one-hobby use case.
4. Silent failure modes if under-specified: which 25 items stay active on downgrade (resolve: oldest-by-date-added), what the chart shows at 0-1 value entries (needs its own designed state, not generic empty), and whether SwiftData's store is confirmed to ride along in standard device backups (needs verifying and stating to the user, not left implicit).
5. Aesthetic risk: "archival/museum" styling can tip into illegible cinematic darkness on exactly the screens (charts, condition photos) where legibility matters most — restrained dark neutrals with strong type hierarchy, not mood lighting.

**Implications for the first build:** Ship a fast minimum-viable capture path (photo, title, category, acquisition date, price optional, notes optional) with condition/provenance depth added after save, not before. Use a fixed small category enum (watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other) plus a free-text subtype field rather than dynamic per-category schemas. Treat classification as a secondary, visibly-labeled suggestion that never blocks saving. Add Face ID/passcode app-lock as a first-build feature. Decide the downgrade tie-break rule (oldest-by-date-added stays active) and the chart's near-empty state now. Keep the visual system restrained rather than cinematic. The product's identity for this build: a documentation-and-trust ledger that's faster than a spreadsheet at capture and more credible than a spreadsheet at review — not a valuation-intelligence product.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This output clears the bar for product_research. It covers all five required sections concretely, ties back consistently to the prompt_contract decisions (non-blocking classification, no insurance/legal claims, data preservation on downgrade), and synthesizes both contributors' round 1 input additively rather than picking one over the other — Codex's "documentation ledger, not valuation intelligence" thesis and Claude's platform-specific edge cases (app-lock, widget refresh triggers, downgrade tie-break rule, chart near-empty state, fixed category enum) both survive into the final artifact without contradiction.

Strengths:
- Audience segmentation is specific and falsifiable (spreadsheet/Notes refugees, not "collectors" broadly), with two distinct use moments named and prioritized correctly (capture speed over review depth for v1).
- Comparable-pattern research names concrete apps in three distinct buckets (insurance-inventory, category-specialist, generic-inventory) with an explicit lesson drawn from each rather than just a list of names.
- Five risks are genuinely concrete and testable, not generic ("Vision misidentifies things" is generic; "Vision can say 'wristwatch,' never 'ref. 5513'" is concrete and checkable against the actual build).
- New platform opportunities introduced here (Face ID/passcode app-lock, event-driven widget refresh) are justified by reasoning tied to the app's own data sensitivity and architecture, not asserted from nowhere, and don't conflict with the locked prompt contract.
- Implications for the first build are a genuinely actionable punch list (fixed category enum, minimum-viable capture fields, specific downgrade/chart decisions) — the design/architecture phase can proceed without re-deriving any of this.

No repair needed. Proceed to the next phase.

We agree on the audience: not "collectors" broadly, but people already running a spreadsheet or Notes doc for one of these categories who are annoyed by it — and specifically people whose annoyance is bad enough to make them switch tools, which is a narrower group than "collectors" sounds like. We agree the app has to serve two very different moments well: a fast, tolerant-of-bad-conditions capture moment right after acquisition, and a slower, more deliberate review/reporting moment later. And we agree Vision has to stay humble — coarse category suggestion only, never presented as identification, because both of us independently flagged that a real collector will test it against their hardest item almost immediately.

### Portfolio Selection

# provenance — Portfolio Selection

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 20:58:12._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

If the prompt requests multiple apps, force a real independent selection process: one app per requested category or requested count, with no collapsed wrapper project. Produce the required portfolio-json manifest for sibling child projects. If the prompt is a single app, explicitly record that no portfolio split is needed.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

This is a single-app run, not a portfolio parent. The prompt already says `PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true`, names the parent portfolio as `multi-app-exp4`, and gives a specific selected app slug: `provenance`. So the portfolio split already happened upstream. This phase should explicitly record that no further child-project selection is needed here, no sibling apps are being chosen in this room, and no `portfolio-json` manifest should be emitted from this child app phase because that belongs to the parent portfolio-selection pass.

The selected app list is therefore just one app: `provenance`, in Collecting & Valuation, with the promise “Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet,” targeting serious collectors of high-value physical goods who currently use Notes or spreadsheets, and `build=true` because the parent prompt requires production-ready build effort for each chosen app and this child was already selected into that set.

The main reason to be strict about that is risk control. If this phase starts pretending it still has authority to compare Provenance against alternate apps, it breaks the orchestration model and creates ambiguity about folder structure, transcript ownership, and output scope. The smallest thing that ships here is one independent app folder for Provenance, with its own product direction and build output, not a wrapper project and not a re-opened seven-app decision. The biggest failure mode is silent scope drift: someone notices “portfolio_selection” in the phase name, reintroduces rejected or parallel app ideas, and collapses the child-app build into meta-planning instead of advancing Provenance.

Rejected alternatives, for this phase, are therefore procedural rather than product-based. Rejected option one: treating this as a new parent portfolio selection and generating `portfolio-json` again. That would duplicate upstream work and conflict with the explicit child-project marker. Rejected option two: bundling Provenance with adjacent collection or inventory concepts into one broader wrapper app. That violates the “one folder per app” rule and the child-project contract. Rejected option three: inventing additional child projects under Provenance by category like watches, wine, or cards. That directly contradicts the app’s own differentiation, which is cross-category record-keeping in a single app.

The detail the final phase output must preserve is simple: Provenance stands alone as one sibling app inside the already-chosen portfolio, and this phase must not collapse, merge, or re-select anything around it. If a later artifact needs a selection record, it should say plainly that the selection is already resolved: one app, slug `provenance`, separate folder, `build=true`, no further portfolio split required.

**Claude (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

This is a single-app run, not a portfolio parent, and I want to state that plainly before anything else: the header of this prompt literally tells us "PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true, Selected app slug: provenance." The portfolio decision — 7 apps, generate 10+ ideas, pick the best, one folder per app — already happened one level up, in the multi-app-exp4 orchestrator, before this conversation ever started. Our job here is not to re-run that selection or second-guess whether Provenance deserved its slot. It's to take the one already-selected spec and build it as a single, complete, production-quality app. So no portfolio-json from this phase — that manifest is either already emitted by the parent process or is not our job to produce. If this phase's playbook is checking for "does the selected app list match the prompt," the answer is: prompt asked for one specific selected app (Provenance), we're building exactly one, and that's a match, not a shortfall.

Now let me actually put on the hat that matters here — I'm going to argue for the real person this thing has to work for, because the earlier phases already did excellent work diagnosing the market and locking a contract, and I don't want to relitigate that so much as sharpen it into something a single stubborn user would actually adopt. Picture a specific person: someone with maybe 40-80 watches, or a wine cellar, or a few long boxes of graded cards, who has a Google Sheet with columns that have drifted out of sync for two years, who bought something last month and still hasn't logged it because opening the spreadsheet on their phone is miserable. That person doesn't want "insurance-grade valuation infrastructure." They want to take a photo in the checkout line, type three words, and have it filed correctly forever. If Provenance's first-run experience asks them for provenance details, condition grading, and a purchase price before it lets them save anything, that person closes the app and goes back to the spreadsheet within a week. That's my single biggest risk to my own direction: everything downstream — the timeline, the chart, the PDF — is worthless if item #1 never gets created because capture friction lost to "just add a row."

The corollary I want on record for this phase's output: the core loop this app lives or dies on is capture-to-save in under a minute, and every other named workflow (classify, log condition/provenance, track value, export) is something that happens to an item that already exists, not a gate in front of creating it. Concretely: photo, a title, pick from six categories (watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other), done — item exists, everything else is optional enrichment reachable from the detail screen afterward. Vision-assisted classification should feel like the app being helpful in the background (pre-filling the category chip), never like a step the user has to wait on or negotiate with. If classification takes more than an instant or shows a spinner that blocks the save button, that's a design failure regardless of how good the model is.

The second thing I want to defend, because it's the part most likely to get diluted later for the sake of "premium feel": this only works if it doesn't lie to the user about what it knows. It's tempting to make the value chart and the export look authoritative because that's what "premium" and "museum-catalog" suggest visually. But the actual person using this has spent years being annoyed by tools that either oversimplify their stuff (generic inventory apps) or overclaim precision they don't have (a slick chart built on two data points looks smarter than it is). The single detail that must survive into design and build: this is a documentation and provenance ledger the user trusts because it's honest about what's manual and what's assisted, not a valuation-intelligence product wearing an archival skin. If a later phase drifts toward making the app feel like it "knows" market value, or toward gating basic logging behind enrichment steps, that's the moment this product stops being better than a spreadsheet and just becomes a nicer-looking one.

Since this is a single, focused build rather than a portfolio decision, I don't think there's a rejected-alternatives list to produce here — there's one app, one slug, one folder, build flag true. The meaningful decision this phase needs to protect isn't "which apps do we build," it's "does this build stay disciplined about being fast at capture and honest at review," and I'd rather spend this phase's words defending that than manufacturing portfolio ceremony that doesn't apply to a child project.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

### Initial Discussion

# provenance — Initial Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 20:59:20._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Establish a shared, precise understanding of what this app is, who it is for, the core problem it solves, hard scope boundaries, and measurable success criteria.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Provenance should be defined much more narrowly than “a collector app.” The product promise I’d lock is: log a valuable physical item in under a minute, then maintain a trustworthy visual record of its condition, provenance, and value over time in one place. That is the thing a spreadsheet does badly and this app can do well. If that sentence gets diluted into “AI-powered valuation” or “insurance platform,” the product gets weaker, not stronger.

The primary user is not a broad “collector.” It is a person with enough money tied up in physical goods that bad record-keeping feels irresponsible, but who still hasn’t found a tool better than Notes or a spreadsheet. Concretely: someone who buys a watch, a few graded cards, a bottle case, or a pair of limited sneakers, means to log it immediately, and usually doesn’t because the process is annoying. The key scenario is right after acquisition, often on a phone, often distracted, often in mediocre lighting. If the app is not better than a spreadsheet in that exact moment, it loses before the later chart, export, and condition history features ever matter.

So the core loop should be brutally simple: capture photo, save item with title and category, then enrich later. Everything else belongs after save. Condition notes, provenance details, purchase facts, and value entries are important, but they should deepen an existing record, not block its creation. The user should feel “my item is safely logged” within the first minute. That is the real product win. Vision assist fits only as silent help on top of that flow, never as a required step and never as something the user has to wait on.

The hard scope boundary I’d set is this: v1 is a documentation ledger, not a valuation intelligence product. That means no pretending to know live market value, no claims of expert identification, no insurer acceptance guarantee, no cross-device sync, no collaborative collections, no dynamic schemas by category, and no marketplace behavior. One fixed set of categories, one local user, one durable item record, one clear export story. That is enough to build a finished app. Anything broader starts to eat the trust and speed that are supposed to differentiate it.

The measurable success criteria should stay user-behavior oriented, not feature-list oriented. A first-time user can create an item with photo, title, and category in under a minute. A saved item can later hold condition history, provenance notes, purchase details, and manual value entries without those concepts feeling buried or ambiguous. A paid user can generate a clear PDF report from local data that reads like a serious record, not a toy export. A free user understands exactly what is limited and never loses data on downgrade. Classification failure never blocks save. Every major screen state is understandable when empty or incomplete, especially the value history state when there is little or no data.

The biggest risk in this direction is that the app may feel too restrained and not “smart” enough to justify payment if value updates are manual and classification is intentionally humble. That is real. But the worse mistake would be compensating with fake sophistication. The first build has to earn trust through speed, clarity, and durable records. If users love it, richer valuation inputs can come later behind the repository boundary. If they do not trust it as a ledger, no amount of later intelligence will save it.

The detail I want preserved into the final output is simple: Provenance only works if capture is faster than a spreadsheet and review is more trustworthy than a spreadsheet. Every scope decision should be judged against that.

**Claude (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Let me give this a concrete shape before anyone else weighs in, thinking about it as both the person drawing the screens and the person who has to worry about what breaks six months later.

The one-sentence promise I'd lock in: Provenance is a local-first ledger that lets a collector photograph something the moment they get it and end up, over time, with a trustworthy dated record of what it is, what condition it's in, and what it's worth — faster to use than a spreadsheet and more credible to hand someone else than a spreadsheet ever could be. Primary user is the person we already converged on: has 40+ watches, or a wine cellar, or a stack of graded cards, currently tracked in a spreadsheet that's drifted out of sync, who will abandon this app in the first session if item #1 takes more than a minute to create.

Core loop, as I'd actually draw it: capture (photo + title + one of six categories, save immediately, Vision silently pre-fills the category chip in the background without blocking the button) → the item now exists and shows up in the list → enrichment happens whenever the user feels like it, from the detail screen, in three independent tracks that live side by side rather than as one long form: provenance/condition facts (a dated timeline of notes+photos), value entries (a dated ledger that feeds the chart), and purchase facts (price, date, source — entered once, rarely touched again). Review loop: list view for browsing the archive, detail view for trust-building on one object, export for handing the whole thing to someone else. That's the IA in full — home/list, capture sheet, item detail (with condition timeline and value chart as sub-sections, not separate top-level destinations), export preview, paywall, and a settings/app-lock screen. Nothing else needs to be a first-class destination for v1.

Here's the thing I don't think has been said yet and I want on record now because it's an IA decision, not an implementation detail: condition history and value history are two separate timelines that both attach to one item, and the detail screen has to make it visually obvious they're not the same thing — a condition entry ("small scratch on caseback, photo attached, 2026-03-01") is not a value entry ("appraised at $8,200, 2026-03-01"), even though they'll often happen on the same day. If the detail screen collapses these into one generic "activity feed," the record stops being legible, which directly undermines the trust thesis everyone's already agreed is the whole point. I'd design it as two distinct, clearly labeled timeline modules on the same screen, not a merged feed.

Second thing worth raising now: what happens when a user deletes an item. The prior phases locked "never destroy data" as a rule for subscription downgrade and error states, but nobody's addressed intentional user deletion — and for an app whose entire value is "this is the record I'd hand an insurer," an accidental delete of a $9,000 watch's entire provenance history is a catastrophic trust failure, arguably worse than a downgrade hiding it. I think item deletion needs to be a soft delete with a recovery window (a "recently deleted" holding area, purged automatically after some fixed period like 30 days) rather than an immediate hard delete, and that's cheap enough to build now that it shouldn't wait for a v2 nice-to-have list. The boundary has to be explicit too — this is not "trash forever," it auto-purges, so it doesn't become unbounded storage growth.

Third: photos need a defined role, not just a bag of images per item. The list view needs a stable cover photo per item; the condition timeline needs to attach photos to specific dated entries. If the data model treats all photos as one undifferentiated collection, the list view either shows the wrong photo after every condition update or the app has to guess, and guessing is exactly the kind of sloppiness this product can't afford. I'd bake "cover photo vs. timeline-entry photo" into the model now rather than retrofitting it.

Fourth, a smaller one but it'll bite if skipped: capture can get interrupted (phone call, app backgrounded, camera permission denial mid-flow). The scenario to design for explicitly is someone takes the photo, backgrounds the app before tapping save, and comes back later — the draft item shouldn't vanish, and it shouldn't half-save either. I'd rather this either autosaves the draft the instant the photo is taken (title/category still editable, defaulting to "Untitled" + "Other") or holds state reliably in-memory with an obvious resume point — but "silently lost" is not acceptable given the stated bar for production-readiness.

My biggest risk to my own direction: I'm asking for three parallel timelines-plus-a-trash-system on the detail screen of what's supposed to be a fast, minimal-friction app, and there's a real chance this reads as busy or over-engineered rather than "quiet and archival" if the visual hierarchy isn't disciplined. The corrective is that only the cover photo, title, and category need to be visible by default — the timelines and chart can be sections you scroll into, not things fighting for attention on first glance.

Hard scope boundaries I'd hold firm on, consistent with what's already locked: no cloud sync, no live pricing, no multi-user, no per-category dynamic schemas, no search/filter/sort system beyond basic list ordering (nobody's asked for it and it's easy to scope-creep into), no barcode/serial-number lookup, no sharing/collaboration on records, no legal export claims.

Measurable success criteria I'd actually hold this build to: median time from tapping "add item" to a saved item under 60 seconds; every named screen demonstrably reaches all four states (empty/loading/success/error) in testing, not just happy path; zero data loss across delete, downgrade, and force-quit-mid-capture scenarios, verified by test, not assumed; PDF export produces a valid file at 0, 1, and many-items-with-full-history; VoiceOver can complete the full core loop (capture → enrich → export) without a sighted assist. If those don't hold, the app isn't done regardless of how the screens look.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Both contributors are aligned and there's nothing left to fight about — Gemini was unavailable again, but that's a gap in participation, not a source of unresolved disagreement between the two who did weigh in.

Codex set the frame: Provenance is a documentation ledger, not a valuation-intelligence product, and it lives or dies on one thing — is capture faster than a spreadsheet, and is review more trustworthy than one. Primary user is someone with real money in physical stuff (watches, cards, wine, sneakers) who already has a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync and hasn't found anything better. Core loop: capture a photo, save with just a title and category, enrich everything else later. Hard boundary: no live market value, no expert-identification claims, no insurer guarantees, no sync, no dynamic per-category schemas, no marketplace behavior.

I took that same frame and made it concrete enough to build from: the IA is home/list → capture sheet → item detail (with two visually distinct timelines — condition/provenance vs. value entries, never merged into one generic feed) → export preview → paywall → settings/app-lock. I added three things Codex's framing didn't cover but that fall directly out of it: soft-delete with a recovery window rather than immediate hard delete (an accidental delete of a $9k item's whole history would be a worse trust failure than anything else in the app), a real cover-photo-vs-timeline-photo distinction in the data model (otherwise the list view can't reliably show the right photo), and explicit handling for interrupted capture (backgrounded mid-flow shouldn't silently lose the draft). None of this contradicts Codex — it's the same "documentation ledger, fast at capture, honest at review" thesis, just pushed down into IA and data-model decisions so the next phase doesn't have to invent them under pressure.

Success criteria between us are consistent and stack cleanly: sub-60-second (Codex said "under a minute," I put a number on it) item creation, all four states on every named screen, zero data loss across delete/downgrade/force-quit scenarios, PDF export verified at 0/1/many items, and VoiceOver able to complete the full core loop unassisted. Scope boundaries match line for line with what prompt_contract and product_research already locked.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** Provenance is a local-first ledger that lets a collector log a valuable physical item in under a minute via photo capture, then build a trustworthy, dated record of its condition, provenance, and value over time — faster to use than a spreadsheet at the moment of acquisition, and more credible than one when it's time to review or hand the record to someone else.

**Primary user and scenario:** A person with meaningful money tied up in physical goods (watches, vinyl, graded cards, wine, sneakers) who currently tracks holdings in a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync, and who will abandon any new tool that isn't faster than what they already have. The critical scenario is the moment right after acquisition — one-handed, often distracted, often bad lighting — where the app has to beat "just add a row" or nothing downstream (condition history, charts, export) ever gets used.

**Core loop:**
1. Capture — photo, title, pick one of six fixed categories (watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other). Save immediately. Vision classification silently pre-fills the category suggestion in the background; it never blocks, delays, or gates the save action.
2. The item now exists and appears in the list — this is the moment of relief the product has to deliver in under a minute.
3. Enrichment (anytime, from the item detail screen, in three independent tracks): condition/provenance timeline (dated notes + photos), value-entry ledger (dated entries feeding the value chart), and purchase facts (price/date/source, entered once).
4. Review — list view for browsing the archive, detail view as the trust-building surface (two clearly separated timelines, never merged into one generic feed), export preview for generating the PDF report.

**Hard scope boundaries:**
- v1 is a documentation ledger, not a valuation-intelligence product: no live/market pricing, no expert-grade item identification, no insurer/legal certification of the export.
- No cloud sync, no multi-user/shared collections, no marketplace or trading features, no social/community comps.
- Fixed six-category enum only — no dynamic per-category schemas.
- No search/filter/sort system beyond basic list ordering, no barcode/serial-number lookup, no OCR document ingestion.
- Classification assist is always secondary and overridable — never a blocking step.
- Item deletion is soft-delete with a recovery window (auto-purged after a fixed period, e.g. 30 days), not immediate hard delete — consistent with the "never destroy user data" rule already locked for downgrade/error states.
- Data model must distinguish cover photo from timeline-entry photos, and condition-history entries from value-history entries, so the record stays legible rather than collapsing into an undifferentiated feed.

**Measurable success criteria:**
- A first-time user can create an item (photo + title + category) in well under a minute; median time from tapping "add item" to a saved item under 60 seconds.
- Every named screen (capture/classify, list, detail with condition timeline and value chart, export preview, paywall) demonstrably reaches all four states — empty, loading, success, error — not just the happy path.
- Zero data loss across intentional delete, subscription downgrade, and interrupted/backgrounded capture, verified by test rather than assumed.
- Classification failure or low confidence never blocks save; manual entry is always a fully working fallback.
- PDF export produces a valid, well-organized file at zero items, one item, and an item with full condition/value history.
- VoiceOver alone can complete the full core loop — capture, enrich, export — without sighted assistance.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This output clears the bar for the initial_discussion phase. It delivers all five required elements concretely and ties them coherently to prior locked decisions (prompt_contract's non-blocking classification and no-legal-claims rules, product_research's fixed category enum and audience framing).

Strengths:
- The one-sentence promise is genuinely one sentence a developer could build from, and it's specific rather than generic marketing copy ("faster than a spreadsheet at capture, more credible than one at review" is a testable framing, not filler).
- Core loop correctly sequences capture-then-enrich, matching the "under a minute to save" discipline established in this phase's own discussion — enrichment (condition, value, purchase facts) is explicitly deferred to after save, not folded into a blocking intake form.
- Hard scope boundaries are a genuinely closed list (no live pricing, no sync, no multi-user, no dynamic schemas, no search/filter beyond basic ordering, no barcode lookup) — small enough to build in one pass, and each boundary is traceable to a reason surfaced in discussion rather than asserted arbitrarily.
- The two new decisions Claude introduced beyond Codex's framing — soft-delete with recovery window, and cover-photo vs. timeline-photo distinction in the data model — are additive, justified by the trust thesis, and don't contradict anything upstream. The coordinator correctly flagged these as extensions rather than disagreements, and there's no papered-over conflict since none existed.
- Success criteria are measurable and testable (sub-60-second median capture time, all four states per named screen, zero data loss verified by test across three specific scenarios, PDF export tested at 0/1/many items, VoiceOver-only completion of the full loop) — not vague aspirations.
- Consistent with prior phases: no scope reintroduction of cloud sync, price feeds, multi-user, or legal export claims — the exact violations prompt_contract told later phases to flag by name.

No repair needed. Gemini's continued unavailability is noted transparently as a participation gap, not hidden or treated as false consensus. Proceed to the next phase.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Both contributors are aligned and there's nothing left to fight about — Gemini was unavailable again, but that's a gap in participation, not a source of unresolved disagreement between the two who did weigh in.

Codex set the frame: Provenance is a documentation ledger, not a valuation-intelligence product, and it lives or dies on one thing — is capture faster than a spreadsheet, and is review more trustworthy than one. Primary user is someone with real money in physical stuff (watches, cards, wine, sneakers) who already has a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync and hasn't found anything better. Core loop: capture a photo, save with just a title and category, enrich everything else later. Hard boundary: no live market value, no expert-identification claims, no insurer guarantees, no sync, no dynamic per-category schemas, no marketplace behavior.

I took that same frame and made it concrete enough to build from: the IA is home/list → capture sheet → item detail (with two visually distinct timelines — condition/provenance vs. value entries, never merged into one generic feed) → export preview → paywall → settings/app-lock. I added three things Codex's framing didn't cover but that fall directly out of it: soft-delete with a recovery window rather than immediate hard delete (an accidental delete of a $9k item's whole history would be a worse trust failure than anything else in the app), a real cover-photo-vs-timeline-photo distinction in the data model (otherwise the list view can't reliably show the right photo), and explicit handling for interrupted capture (backgrounded mid-flow shouldn't silently lose the draft). None of this contradicts Codex — it's the same "documentation ledger, fast at capture, honest at review" thesis, just pushed down into IA and data-model decisions so the next phase doesn't have to invent them under pressure.

Success criteria between us are consistent and stack cleanly: sub-60-second (Codex said "under a minute," I put a number on it) item creation, all four states on every named screen, zero data loss across delete/downgrade/force-quit scenarios, PDF export verified at 0/1/many items, and VoiceOver able to complete the full core loop unassisted. Scope boundaries match line for line with what prompt_contract and product_research already locked.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** Provenance is a local-first ledger that lets a collector log a valuable physical item in under a minute via photo capture, then build a trustworthy, dated record of its condition, provenance, and value over time — faster to use than a spreadsheet at the moment of acquisition, and more credible than one when it's time to review or hand the record to someone else.

**Primary user and scenario:** A person with meaningful money tied up in physical goods (watches, vinyl, graded cards, wine, sneakers) who currently tracks holdings in a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync, and who will abandon any new tool that isn't faster than what they already have. The critical scenario is the moment right after acquisition — one-handed, often distracted, often bad lighting — where the app has to beat "just add a row" or nothing downstream (condition history, charts, export) ever gets used.

**Core loop:**
1. Capture — photo, title, pick one of six fixed categories (watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other). Save immediately. Vision classification silently pre-fills the category suggestion in the background; it never blocks, delays, or gates the save action.
2. The item now exists and appears in the list — this is the moment of relief the product has to deliver in under a minute.
3. Enrichment (anytime, from the item detail screen, in three independent tracks): condition/provenance timeline (dated notes + photos), value-entry ledger (dated entries feeding the value chart), and purchase facts (price/date/source, entered once).
4. Review — list view for browsing the archive, detail view as the trust-building surface (two clearly separated timelines, never merged into one generic feed), export preview for generating the PDF report.

**Hard scope boundaries:**
- v1 is a documentation ledger, not a valuation-intelligence product: no live/market pricing, no expert-grade item identification, no insurer/legal certification of the export.
- No cloud sync, no multi-user/shared collections, no marketplace or trading features, no social/community comps.
- Fixed six-category enum only — no dynamic per-category schemas.
- No search/filter/sort system beyond basic list ordering, no barcode/serial-number lookup, no OCR document ingestion.
- Classification assist is always secondary and overridable — never a blocking step.
- Item deletion is soft-delete with a recovery window (auto-purged after a fixed period, e.g. 30 days), not immediate hard delete — consistent with the "never destroy user data" rule already locked for downgrade/error states.
- Data model must distinguish cover photo from timeline-entry photos, and condition-history entries from value-history entries, so the record stays legible rather than collapsing into an undifferentiated feed.

**Measurable success criteria:**
- A first-time user can create an item (photo + title + category) in well under a minute; median time from tapping "add item" to a saved item under 60 seconds.
- Every named screen (capture/classify, list, detail with condition timeline and value chart, export preview, paywall) demonstrably reaches all four states — empty, loading, success, error — not just the happy path.
- Zero data loss across intentional delete, subscription downgrade, and interrupted/backgrounded capture, verified by test rather than assumed.
- Classification failure or low confidence never blocks save; manual entry is always a fully working fallback.
- PDF export produces a valid, well-organized file at zero items, one item, and an item with full condition/value history.
- VoiceOver alone can complete the full core loop — capture, enrich, export — without sighted assistance.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

### Per App Product Brief

# provenance — Per App Product Brief

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.
```
