## Phase Purpose

Create a production-grade product brief for this specific app: target user, paid value, core loop, subscription value, competitive wedge, viral or niche growth mechanism, local-first behavior, cloud-ready extension path, and the real reason the app deserves to exist.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Provenance should be built for the collector who has already crossed the line from hobby to responsibility. This is the person with real money tied up in objects, a spreadsheet that is always half out of date, and a recurring low-grade anxiety that if something were stolen, damaged, or needed to be documented tomorrow, they would be reconstructing facts from memory and camera roll scraps. The product is not for browsing collectibles, trading, or fandom. It is for fast capture at the moment of acquisition and credible review later. The key use case is simple and demanding: photograph the item, save it before the details disappear, and trust that the record can deepen over time without getting messy.

The paid value has to be operational, not decorative. Free needs to prove the basic ledger: up to 25 manually created items, structured condition and provenance fields, and a clean local catalog that already feels better than Notes. Paid should unlock the behaviors that make the app become a living record instead of a static list: unlimited items, on-device classification assist during capture, manual value history with charts, insurance-ready export, and the value widget. That is a defensible subscription because it maps to ongoing utility: larger collections, continued documentation, periodic valuation updates, and repeated export needs. If paid is just “nicer screens” or cosmetic organization, it will not hold. The reason to keep paying is that the app becomes the authoritative ledger for the collection.

The core loop should be designed around relief, not completeness. A user opens capture, takes or selects a photo, enters a title, confirms or overrides a category suggestion, and saves. At that point the item exists and shows up in the catalog. Everything else is enrichment after the fact: purchase facts, provenance notes, dated condition updates with photos, and dated value entries. That separation matters because it keeps the intake flow fast enough to beat a spreadsheet while still allowing the record to become serious over time. On the detail screen, the information architecture should make the record feel archival: a stable cover image and canonical facts at the top, then clearly separate modules for condition/provenance history and value history. If those histories collapse into one generic feed, the record stops feeling trustworthy.

The competitive wedge is not better identification than category-specific apps. That would be a false fight and the app would lose it. The wedge is that Provenance is the one place a serious collector can maintain a cross-category record that is photo-anchored, condition-aware, exportable, and legible later. Specialist tools go deeper in one domain; spreadsheets stay flexible but sloppy. Provenance wins by being faster than spreadsheets at capture and more credible than both spreadsheets and generic inventory apps at review. The museum-catalog aesthetic only matters if it reinforces that: restrained dark neutrals, strong typographic hierarchy, and layouts that make dates, notes, and evidence easy to scan. Premium here means orderly and serious, not dramatic.

The local-first behavior should be visible in the product, not just in the architecture. Item creation, editing, charting, browsing, classification fallback, and export all need to work offline with deterministic behavior. Photo storage should be managed locally with a clear strategy for cover images, timeline images, and thumbnailing so the app does not silently become a storage problem. The app should also act like a trustworthy local ledger: drafts survive interruption, downgrade never deletes data, and deletion is soft-delete with recovery rather than instant destruction. The future cloud path is important, but it should stay behind a repository boundary and never leak into v1 UX. Later sync should look like optional continuity and backup, not a redesign. The same goes for future market feeds: they should enrich the value ledger, not redefine the product around external intelligence.

The growth mechanism is niche and credibility-driven, not mass social. This app should spread through moments where collectors have to show their records to someone else: insurer conversations, estate planning, selling prep, collector group recommendations, and “what do you use to track all this?” The export is part of growth because it externalizes the product’s seriousness. If the PDF is clean enough that a broker, appraiser, spouse, or buyer can understand it immediately, the app advertises itself in exactly the environments that matter. The viral loop is not posting collections publicly. It is being the tool people recommend when another collector admits their inventory is trapped in Notes.

What will make people keep the app is not the first capture. It is the feeling that each update makes the record more defensible rather than more cluttered. Users will stay if opening an item six months later shows a clear chronology of what changed, what it cost, how its condition evolved, and what value entries were recorded, with photos that still make sense in context. They will stay if the app feels like a durable private archive, not a generic database. They will leave if valuation feels fake, classification feels overconfident, or the detail screen becomes a dumping ground.

The biggest risk in this direction is that the product may feel too restrained to justify a subscription if the user never builds the habit of adding value entries. That is a real objection. The answer is not to fake intelligence. The answer is to make the ongoing paid behaviors genuinely useful even when valuation is manual: unlimited catalog growth, fast assisted intake, periodic export, collection-level value visibility, and a record structure that is visibly better as the collection gets larger and older. If value fields remain sparse in real use, the app should be willing to lean harder into catalog plus condition history rather than pretending it is a market intelligence product.

The detail the final phase output must preserve is this: Provenance deserves to exist only if it becomes the most credible and lowest-friction way to maintain a serious record of valuable physical objects across categories. Not the smartest-looking app, not the most automated app, and not a thin spreadsheet replacement. It has to feel like a private archival ledger that is fast at capture, calm at review, and honest about what is manual versus assisted.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Here's the brief, written to be buildable, not debatable.

The target user is a specific person, not "a collector": someone with real money in physical goods across one or more of watches, vinyl, graded cards, wine, or sneakers, who is currently tracking holdings in a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync, and who has a distinct trigger moment — they just bought or received something and want it logged before the receipt disappears or the details fade. They are not shopping for a hobby-tracker toy; they are shopping for a replacement for a tool they're already embarrassed by. If this app can't beat "add a row in Excel" in the first ten seconds of the first session, it has already lost, regardless of what the detail screen looks like later.

Paid value has to be real utility, not a feature-count gate dressed up as a tier. Free gets you a fast, fully-functional catalog up to 25 items — photo, title, category, purchase facts, condition notes — with no artificial cripple on the core logging experience, because a free tier that feels broken generates one-star reviews before it generates conversions. What's actually gated is the stuff that only matters once someone has enough at stake to care: unlimited items (a serious collector blows past 25 within a month), the value-over-time chart, and the PDF export. That last one is the real subscription anchor — the export is the artifact people actually want to hand to a spouse, an estate lawyer, or an insurance adjuster, and it only has value once the catalog is big enough and old enough to be worth defending. A single item free-tier user has no reason to pay; a 60-item collector staring at an export wall does. That's the wedge, and it has to be sharp: the export should be visibly show-off quality even in the paywall preview — real layout, real typography, blurred or partially redacted content behind the lock — so the user is paying for something they've already seen and want, not speculating on vaporware.

Core loop, and I want this locked hard because it's the single thing every other decision in this build has to defend: tap add, take a photo, type a title, pick one of six fixed categories, save. That's it — under a minute, ideally under twenty seconds. Vision classification runs in the background and pre-fills the category chip if it's confident; if it's wrong or silent, nothing breaks, nothing blocks, the user just taps the right chip instead. Everything else — condition notes, provenance, purchase price, value entries — is enrichment reachable from the item's detail screen whenever the user feels like it, never a gate in front of the save button. The moment this turns into a five-field intake form before the item exists, it's dead on arrival against the spreadsheet it's replacing.

The competitive wedge is documentation rigor across categories, not identification depth in any one category. WatchCharts knows more about watches than this app ever will; CellarTracker knows more about wine. Provenance wins by being the one place that treats a watch, a case of wine, and a graded card with the same seriousness of record — dated condition photos, dated value entries, a purchase-fact record, and an export that reads like a dossier, not a printout of a spreadsheet. If a later design pass tries to compete on recognition accuracy instead of documentation quality, that's a strategic error worth stopping, because it's a fight this app can't win with on-device Vision alone.

Growth here is niche, not viral in the conventional sense, and I want to be honest about that rather than force a share button that doesn't fit. The realistic mechanism is the export itself as a distribution artifact — a well-made insurance/estate-style PDF report is the kind of thing a user shows a partner, a fellow collector, or a forum thread ("here's what I use to track my collection"), and that's a slower but more durable loop than anything social-feed-shaped. I would not build sharing, comments, or public profiles to manufacture virality; that would contradict the single-user privacy stance this app needs given it's cataloging someone's valuables and what they paid for them. If growth needs a boost, it comes from App Store positioning and word of mouth in collector communities that already exist, not from in-app social mechanics that don't belong in a private ledger.

Local-first is not a checkbox here, it's the trust foundation, and I want three specific things locked as build requirements rather than left as design nice-to-haves: Face ID/passcode app-lock, since this app holds an itemized list of expensive possessions and what the owner paid for them — that data being visible on an unlocked phone is a real-world risk, not a hypothetical; soft-delete with a 30-day recovery window on items, because a slip-of-the-thumb delete of a $9,000 item's entire history is the single worst thing that can happen to this product's credibility, worse than any bug; and a stated, verified position on device backup — SwiftData's default store rides along in standard encrypted iCloud/local backups, and the app should say so explicitly in settings, because "my only copy of my insurance record lives on one phone with no visible backup story" is a five-star-review-killer the first time someone loses a phone. The cloud-ready path is a repository protocol boundary now, with sync and an opt-in market-price feed as clearly-labeled future work behind that same interface — nothing about the MVP should require network access to function, and nothing about the architecture should make adding sync later require touching the view layer.

What actually keeps someone using this past the first week is not the classification assist and not even the chart — it's whether they trust the app enough to make it their only copy of the record. That trust is earned or lost in a small number of concrete moments: does save ever silently fail or lose a photo, does a subscription lapse ever delete rather than hide data, does deleting an item ever feel irreversible without a safety net, does the export actually open and look correct on the first try with zero, one, and many items. Those are the moments I'd test explicitly before calling this done, because a beautiful capture flow sitting on top of an untrustworthy save/export path is a worse product than a plain one that never loses data. The biggest risk to this whole direction is that the team over-invests in the archival aesthetic and Vision assist because they're the fun parts to build, and under-invests in the boring parts — soft-delete, backup confirmation, downgrade data preservation, export correctness at edge-case item counts — which are the parts that actually determine whether a real collector trusts this app with their $40,000 watch collection. I'd rather ship a visually plainer app where every one of those boring guarantees is verified than a gorgeous one where any of them is assumed.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Both Codex and Claude are telling the same story from different angles, and neither pushed back on the other — this reads like genuine convergence, not two people talking past each other. The one new thing that emerged this round beyond what earlier phases already locked is a sharper theory of the paywall itself: the export shouldn't just be gated, it should be visibly desirable behind the gate (real layout, real typography, redacted content) so a big, aging collection actually wants to convert rather than just bumping into a wall. Everything else is restating and hardening the same thesis from initial_discussion with a business lens now laid on top.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user and use case:** Someone who already has real money tied up in physical goods — watches, vinyl, graded cards, wine, sneakers — and is currently tracking it in a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync. They're not shopping for a hobby toy; they're quietly embarrassed by their current system and carry a low-grade anxiety that if something were lost, stolen, or needed for an insurance/estate conversation tomorrow, they'd be reconstructing facts from memory and camera-roll scraps. The trigger moment is right after acquisition — log it now before the details fade — and the payoff moment is much later, when they need to review, prove, or hand off the record.

**Paid value and subscription value:** Free has to be a genuinely working ledger, not a crippled demo — up to 25 items with full structured logging (photo, title, category, condition, provenance, purchase facts), because a free tier that feels broken kills trust before it ever converts anyone. What's gated is what only matters once someone has enough at stake to care: unlimited items (a real collector blows past 25 fast), the value-over-time chart, on-device classification assist, and the PDF export. The export is the real subscription anchor — it's the artifact people actually want to hand to a spouse, an appraiser, or an insurance adjuster, and it should be visibly showable even in its paywall preview (real formatting, blurred/locked content) so the user is paying for something they've already seen and want, not speculating. This only holds up as "real utility" if the ongoing paid behaviors keep earning their keep as the collection grows and ages — bigger catalog, more history, more export occasions — not because the classification or valuation got smarter.

**Core loop:** Capture (photo, title, one of six fixed categories) → save immediately, under a minute, ideally under twenty seconds → item exists and shows up in the catalog → enrichment anytime afterward from the detail screen across three independent tracks: condition/provenance timeline, value-entry ledger, purchase facts. Vision classification runs silently in the background to pre-fill the category chip; it never blocks or gates the save. Review happens later and separately — list for browsing, detail screen as the trust surface (two clearly separated timelines, never merged), export preview for turning the record into something you can hand someone.

**Competitive wedge:** Not identification depth — WatchCharts will always know more about watches, CellarTracker more about wine, and trying to out-recognize them is a fight this app loses with on-device Vision alone. The wedge is documentation rigor applied consistently across categories: the same seriousness of dated condition photos, dated value entries, purchase facts, and export quality whether the object is a watch or a case of wine. It beats spreadsheets on capture speed and beats both spreadsheets and generic inventory apps on review credibility.

**Viral or niche growth angle:** Not social, not a share button, not public profiles — that would contradict the privacy stance a private valuables ledger needs. Growth is niche and credibility-driven: the export itself is the distribution artifact. A clean, dossier-quality PDF is the kind of thing that gets shown to a partner, an appraiser, or a collector forum thread ("this is what I use to track my collection"), which is a slower but stickier loop than a feed-shaped mechanic bolted on for its own sake.

**Local-first behavior and cloud-ready extension path:** Local-first is a trust requirement, not an architecture footnote. Concretely required in this build: Face ID/passcode app-lock (the app holds an itemized list of valuables and what they cost — that's real-world risk on an unlocked phone); soft-delete with a 30-day recovery window on items (an accidental delete of a $9,000 item's whole history is the single worst thing this product could do to its own credibility); drafts that survive interrupted/backgrounded capture; downgrade that hides rather than deletes; and an explicit, stated confirmation that the local store rides along in standard encrypted device backups, since "my only copy lives on one phone with no visible backup story" is a five-star-review killer. The cloud path — sync, opt-in market-price feed — stays behind the repository protocol boundary already established; it's future work that should look like added continuity, never a v1 dependency or a redesign.

**Why this app deserves to exist:** Not because it's the smartest-looking app or the most automated one, but because it can become the most credible, lowest-friction way to maintain a serious record of valuable physical objects across categories — fast enough at capture to beat the spreadsheet it's replacing, and calm and honest enough at review to be trusted with someone's actual collection. The real risk to that promise isn't underbuilt aesthetics or thin classification — it's under-investing in the unglamorous guarantees (soft-delete, backup confirmation, downgrade preservation, export correctness at every item count) in favor of the more fun parts to build. Those boring guarantees are what actually decide whether someone trusts this app with a $40,000 collection.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This clears the bar. All six required outputs are present and concrete: target user (spreadsheet-embarrassed collector with a specific acquisition-moment trigger), paid value (free tier is a genuinely working ledger, not a demo; gated items — unlimited items, chart, classification assist, export — all justify themselves by growing in value as the collection ages, not by being cosmetically nicer), core loop (photo/title/category → save under a minute → enrichment later across three separated tracks), competitive wedge (documentation rigor across categories vs. specialist identification depth — explicitly names why it can't win on recognition), growth angle (export-as-distribution-artifact, explicitly rejecting social/share mechanics as inconsistent with the privacy stance), and local-first/cloud-ready plan (app-lock, soft-delete window, interrupted-capture safety, downgrade-hides-not-deletes, backup confirmation, repository-boundary for future sync/price-feed).

It's specific enough to build from without further ideation — concrete numbers (25 items, 30-day recovery, under-a-minute capture), concrete mechanisms (paywall preview shows real redacted formatting, not just a lock icon), and concrete non-goals reaffirmed (no social features). Subscription value is functional, not cosmetic — every gated item is tied to a real behavior change, not a skin.

It stays consistent with every prior locked phase (prompt_contract's non-blocking classification and no-legal-claims rule, product_research's audience framing, initial_discussion's soft-delete/cover-photo/interrupted-capture decisions) and doesn't reintroduce any of the barred scope (no cloud dependency, no live pricing claims, no social virality). The coordinator's synthesis note correctly identifies the one genuinely new idea this round (paywall preview should show real, desirable formatting rather than a generic lock) as additive rather than papering over any actual disagreement — there wasn't one to paper over, and that's stated plainly rather than manufactured.

No repair needed. Ready for the design phase.

Both Codex and Claude are telling the same story from different angles, and neither pushed back on the other — this reads like genuine convergence, not two people talking past each other. The one new thing that emerged this round beyond what earlier phases already locked is a sharper theory of the paywall itself: the export shouldn't just be gated, it should be visibly desirable behind the gate (real layout, real typography, redacted content) so a big, aging collection actually wants to convert rather than just bumping into a wall. Everything else is restating and hardening the same thesis from initial_discussion with a business lens now laid on top.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user and use case:** Someone who already has real money tied up in physical goods — watches, vinyl, graded cards, wine, sneakers — and is currently tracking it in a spreadsheet or Notes doc that's drifted out of sync. They're not shopping for a hobby toy; they're quietly embarrassed by their current system and carry a low-grade anxiety that if something were lost, stolen, or needed for an insurance/estate conversation tomorrow, they'd be reconstructing facts from memory and camera-roll scraps. The trigger moment is right after acquisition — log it now before the details fade — and the payoff moment is much later, when they need to review, prove, or hand off the record.

**Paid value and subscription value:** Free has to be a genuinely working ledger, not a crippled demo — up to 25 items with full structured logging (photo, title, category, condition, provenance, purchase facts), because a free tier that feels broken kills trust before it ever converts anyone. What's gated is what only matters once someone has enough at stake to care: unlimited items (a real collector blows past 25 fast), the value-over-time chart, on-device classification assist, and the PDF export. The export is the real subscription anchor — it's the artifact people actually want to hand to a spouse, an appraiser, or an insurance adjuster, and it should be visibly showable even in its paywall preview (real formatting, blurred/locked content) so the user is paying for something they've already seen and want, not speculating. This only holds up as "real utility" if the ongoing paid behaviors keep earning their keep as the collection grows and ages — bigger catalog, more history, more export occasions — not because the classification or valuation got smarter.

**Core loop:** Capture (photo, title, one of six fixed categories) → save immediately, under a minute, ideally under twenty seconds → item exists and shows up in the catalog → enrichment anytime afterward from the detail screen across three independent tracks: condition/provenance timeline, value-entry ledger, purchase facts. Vision classification runs silently in the background to pre-fill the category chip; it never blocks or gates the save. Review happens later and separately — list for browsing, detail screen as the trust surface (two clearly separated timelines, never merged), export preview for turning the record into something you can hand someone.

**Competitive wedge:** Not identification depth — WatchCharts will always know more about watches, CellarTracker more about wine, and trying to out-recognize them is a fight this app loses with on-device Vision alone. The wedge is documentation rigor applied consistently across categories: the same seriousness of dated condition photos, dated value entries, purchase facts, and export quality whether the object is a watch or a case of wine. It beats spreadsheets on capture speed and beats both spreadsheets and generic inventory apps on review credibility.

**Viral or niche growth angle:** Not social, not a share button, not public profiles — that would contradict the privacy stance a private valuables ledger needs. Growth is niche and credibility-driven: the export itself is the distribution artifact. A clean, dossier-quality PDF is the kind of thing that gets shown to a partner, an appraiser, or a collector forum thread ("this is what I use to track my collection"), which is a slower but stickier loop than a feed-shaped mechanic bolted on for its own sake.

**Local-first behavior and cloud-ready extension path:** Local-first is a trust requirement, not an architecture footnote. Concretely required in this build: Face ID/passcode app-lock (the app holds an itemized list of valuables and what they cost — that's real-world risk on an unlocked phone); soft-delete with a 30-day recovery window on items (an accidental delete of a $9,000 item's whole history is the single worst thing this product could do to its own credibility); drafts that survive interrupted/backgrounded capture; downgrade that hides rather than deletes; and an explicit, stated confirmation that the local store rides along in standard encrypted device backups, since "my only copy lives on one phone with no visible backup story" is a five-star-review killer. The cloud path — sync, opt-in market-price feed — stays behind the repository protocol boundary already established; it's future work that should look like added continuity, never a v1 dependency or a redesign.

**Why this app deserves to exist:** Not because it's the smartest-looking app or the most automated one, but because it can become the most credible, lowest-friction way to maintain a serious record of valuable physical objects across categories — fast enough at capture to beat the spreadsheet it's replacing, and calm and honest enough at review to be trusted with someone's actual collection. The real risk to that promise isn't underbuilt aesthetics or thin classification — it's under-investing in the unglamorous guarantees (soft-delete, backup confirmation, downgrade preservation, export correctness at every item count) in favor of the more fun parts to build. Those boring guarantees are what actually decide whether someone trusts this app with a $40,000 collection.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

## Original Prompt

```

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.
```

## Phase Purpose

Define the smallest valuable slice / immediate next steps that de-risk and validate the concept with minimal effort.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

The smallest valuable slice is not “all the tabs, minus polish.” It is one complete trust loop: create an item from a photo, enrich it enough to feel like a real record, and export a professional-looking PDF from local data. If that loop works offline, survives relaunch, and feels faster than the user’s spreadsheet, the concept is validated. If it does not, widgets, StoreKit, and fancy classification are just noise on top of a weak product.

So the MVP slice I would lock is this: local catalog with a list screen, add-item flow, item detail, value-history entry, and PDF export preview/share for a single item and for the collection. Capture must be fast: photo, title, category, save. Detail must immediately support the second half of the promise: add purchase facts, add one condition/provenance note with optional photo, add one manual value entry, then see those reflected in the record and in the export. That is enough to prove the app is a trustworthy ledger instead of a dark-themed note app. I would include the fixed category enum, photo storage strategy, soft-delete, and interrupted-draft recovery in this slice because those are not polish; they are part of whether the user trusts the app with expensive objects.

I would not put Vision assist, StoreKit paywall, widgets, or app-lock in the first proving slice. They matter for the product, but they are not the minimum concept proof. Vision is especially easy to overvalue here: if manual capture plus export is not compelling, classification suggestions will not rescue it. Same for the paywall: monetization can only be validated after the underlying record feels worth protecting. If this phase pretends the “smallest slice” includes every premium surface, it is failing its own job.

The must-have interactions are concrete and few. User can create an item in under a minute with only photo, title, and category required. User can resume a half-finished capture after interruption without losing the photo. User can open the saved item and separately add purchase facts, a dated condition/provenance entry, and a dated value entry. User can see a clear near-empty value state with one entry instead of a broken chart. User can soft-delete an item and recover it. User can generate and preview a PDF that includes the cover photo, core facts, condition/provenance history, and value history, and that export works for zero items, one sparse item, and one fully populated item.

The cut list should be aggressive. Cut collection-level dashboards beyond a simple list summary. Cut widgets. Cut StoreKit. Cut Vision classification. Cut charts beyond a very simple value-history visualization if needed; the actual must-have is that dated value entries exist and appear correctly in the export. Cut multiple sort/filter systems. Cut category-specific schemas. Cut any insurer-language that implies certification. Cut multi-item batch tools. Cut cloud hooks in the UI; keep the repository boundary in code, not product surface.

The main risk in my own direction is that it may feel too plain and may underrepresent the “premium collector app” promise if the first slice does not include classification assist or the paywall shape. But that is a better risk than shipping a wider slice that cannot prove trust. The failure mode to avoid is a beautiful shell with no evidence that records are durable, legible, and exportable. If the export looks credible and the record survives bad conditions, the premium layer can come next. If not, the concept is still unproven.

Validation has to be mercilessly practical. A new user can save an item from camera to catalog in under 60 seconds. After force-quit or background interruption during capture, the draft is still recoverable. After relaunch, saved items, photos, condition entries, and value entries are intact. Soft-deleted items can be restored. The export opens successfully and reflects sparse and full data honestly without layout failure. Empty, loading, success, and error states exist on list, capture, detail, and export surfaces where they are reachable. If those are true, the app has one complete workflow and the next phase does not need new product debate.

The detail that must survive into the final output is this: the first slice proves trust, not breadth. If the slice does not end with “I logged an item, enriched it, and produced a dossier-quality local export without losing data,” then it is not the right slice.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

Picking the smallest slice that's still emotionally satisfying is the whole game here, and I think the temptation in this room will be to define "MVP" as "every named workflow, but shallow." I want to resist that. The smallest slice that actually proves this concept is: a user opens the app, captures a photo of something they own, gives it a title and a category, and the moment it lands in the catalog it already looks like it belongs in a private museum ledger — not a placeholder row, a finished-feeling artifact. Then they tap into it and see one thing that couldn't exist in a spreadsheet: a condition/provenance timeline with a single dated entry and a photo, and a value ledger with a single dated entry, visually distinct from each other. That's it. That's the whole slice. If that loop feels premium and fast, the concept is proven. If it feels like a form with a nice font, it isn't, no matter how much else we bolt on.

The core interactions I'd hold as non-negotiable for this slice: photo capture (camera or library), title entry, category chip selection with Vision silently suggesting one in the background, immediate save with no intermediate screens, the item appearing in a list styled as archival cards (cover photo, title, category, maybe a small "logged X ago" marker), a detail screen that shows canonical facts up top and the two separated timeline modules below, and the ability to add one condition entry and one value entry so a user can feel the shape of "this deepens over time" without needing the chart or export yet. I'd also keep app-lock in this slice, not defer it — it's cheap, and a $9,000 watch catalog behind no lock at all undercuts the trust story on day one, before the user has even had a chance to add a second item. Soft-delete (or even just a confirmation + short grace period) belongs here too, for the same reason: the first time someone fat-fingers a delete on their one logged item, if it's gone forever, the app has already broken its own promise.

Here's where I'd push harder than "safe MVP" thinking: I don't think the value chart, the PDF export, or the paywall need to be in this smallest slice at all, and I'd resist the urge to include a thin version of each just to say every workflow exists. A chart with one or two data points is not a real feature, it's a stub pretending to be one — better to not show it yet than to show a flat line that undersells the idea. Same with export: a rough PDF that doesn't yet look dossier-quality would actively damage the "insurance-ready" positioning the whole product is betting on, worse than not having it yet. And the paywall has nothing to gate meaningfully until there's a real 25-item cap being approached, which won't happen in a validation slice anyway. Those three are real, necessary, and already scoped by earlier phases — they just don't need to exist for us to know whether the core thesis (faster than a spreadsheet at capture, more credible than one at review) is true.

What I'd cut entirely from this immediate next step, not just defer: the widget (it has nothing meaningful to show until there's a real collection and a value story), StoreKit integration, the full six-category breadth (I'd rather nail two or three categories — watch, vinyl, card, say — end to end than spread thin across six untested category-specific nuances that don't actually differ from each other in the data model anyway), multi-photo timeline galleries beyond one photo per entry, and any settings/backup-messaging screen. None of that teaches us anything about whether the core loop is emotionally right; it's all work that can bolt on after the slice proves itself.

The biggest risk to my own framing: by deliberately leaving out chart, export, and paywall, I'm proposing a slice that doesn't demonstrate the money-making mechanism or the "insurance-grade" promise at all — someone skimming this could reasonably ask "where's the actual product." My answer is that this phase isn't asking us to ship the product, it's asking for the smallest slice that de-risks the concept, and the actual concept at risk isn't "can we build a PDF exporter" (we can, that's execution risk, not concept risk) — it's "does logging something this way feel better than a spreadsheet, and does the resulting record feel trustworthy enough to want to keep." Those are the two things genuinely in doubt, and they're fully testable with just capture + list + detail + two timelines + lock + soft-delete.

The detail I want preserved no matter how this gets debated down: the validation criterion for this slice isn't "does it compile" or "are all screens present," it's felt speed and felt credibility — can a real user create an item in well under a minute without thinking about it, and does looking at the detail screen afterward feel like something worth trusting with a second, then a tenth, then a fortieth item. If the answer to either is "kind of," the slice isn't done, regardless of what else got built around it.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things actually stand: both of them agree on a lot — cut the widget, cut StoreKit, keep the fixed category enum and soft-delete/interrupted-capture recovery as trust-critical rather than "polish," and don't let this slice pretend Vision or the paywall are load-bearing for concept validation. That's solid common ground.

But there's a real fork that hasn't been resolved, not just different emphasis: Codex says the minimum slice has to end with a working PDF export (single item and full collection, tested at zero/one/many items) — his argument is that "insurance-grade record-keeping" is the actual product claim, and if you can't produce a credible export, you haven't proven the concept no matter how nice capture feels. Claude argues the opposite: leave the export (and the value chart, and the paywall) out of this smallest slice entirely, on the theory that a rushed, not-yet-dossier-quality PDF would actively undersell the "insurance-ready" positioning, and that the two things genuinely in doubt — is capture faster than a spreadsheet, does the record feel trustworthy enough to keep adding to — are fully testable with just capture + list + detail + two separated timelines + app-lock + soft-delete, no export needed.

There's a second, smaller disagreement riding along with it: Claude wants to narrow the validation slice to two or three categories (watch/vinyl/card) rather than all six, to avoid spreading thin; Codex didn't address category breadth at all, so it's unclear if he'd agree to that cut. And Claude explicitly wants app-lock in this first slice; Codex's list of must-haves doesn't mention app-lock one way or the other.

So next round needs to actually argue out: is "produces a real PDF export" part of proving the concept, or is it downstream execution risk that can wait? And, secondarily, does the smallest slice need all six categories or is 2-3 enough to validate, and is app-lock in or out of round one. Those need a real answer, not just two people talking past each other, before this phase can close.

### Round 2


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

Claude is right about one thing and wrong about the most important thing. He’s right that we should not stuff this slice with widget, StoreKit, or a fake chart just to say the app has “more product.” He’s also right that trust beats breadth. But I think he’s underestimating how much of this product’s trust claim lives in the export, not just the detail screen. If Provenance were only trying to prove “this feels nicer than a spreadsheet,” then capture + detail + two timelines would be enough. But that is not the claim we’ve been carrying forward. The claim is that this becomes a record you can hand to someone else. The moment you remove export from the proving slice, you stop testing the hardest part of the concept and start testing a weaker product: a private catalog. That might still be useful, but it’s not the thing we’ve been building toward.

The risk case is straightforward. A beautiful detail screen can absolutely fool us. Internally it may feel archival and credible, but the first time you render that same data into a PDF you find out whether the model is actually coherent: do cover photos and timeline photos resolve correctly, do sparse items read honestly instead of looking broken, do dates and purchase facts group cleanly, does a one-entry value history degrade gracefully, does zero-item export have a real empty report instead of an embarrassing blank file. Those are not “later polish” questions. They are the fastest way to expose whether the record structure is real or just UI theater. I’d rather ship a narrow but real export now than defer it and accidentally validate the wrong product. So my position is: include export, but keep it brutally scoped. One dossier-quality local PDF for a single item is mandatory. Collection export can wait one step if needed. The concept proof is “I logged an item, added one condition entry and one value entry, and generated a credible document from it offline.” Without that, we have not de-risked the insurance/estate/reporting half of the promise.

On the other two disagreements, I’d split the difference cleanly. App-lock should be in. Claude is right there. It’s cheap, it reinforces trust immediately, and it tests a real product assumption: this is sensitive inventory, not casual hobby data. Leaving it out saves almost nothing and weakens the emotional contract on day one. But I would not narrow the taxonomy to 2-3 categories. That is the wrong optimization. The fixed six-category enum is already cheap because it does not change schema complexity, and cutting categories buys almost no engineering relief while creating a false validation environment around a product whose wedge is cross-category seriousness. We do not need category-specific depth, but we do need all six chips present so we’re validating the actual positioning, not a narrowed watch-and-cards pilot pretending to be the same app. So the concrete slice I’d close on is: list, capture, detail, soft-delete/recover, interrupted draft recovery, app-lock, fixed six-category enum, one condition/provenance entry flow, one value entry flow with a proper one-entry empty/near-empty state instead of a fake chart, and single-item PDF export that works offline and survives relaunch. That is one complete workflow, it proves trust instead of breadth, and it ends the debate cleanly.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

Codex's point about export actually lands harder than I gave it credit for, and I want to say that plainly rather than dig in on my prior position out of stubbornness. Here's the thing I glossed over: the core loop we already locked in per_app_product_brief and initial_discussion isn't "capture and enrich," it's "capture, enrich, *and review/hand off*." The whole competitive wedge is "more credible than a spreadsheet when it's time to review or hand the record to someone." If the smallest slice stops at a nice detail screen and never produces the artifact, we haven't actually tested the second half of the thesis at all — we've only tested the half that was always the easier half to get right. A pretty capture flow was never really in doubt; SwiftUI camera capture and a card list are well-trodden ground. What's actually uncertain is whether structured local data (photos + timeline entries + purchase facts) can turn into something that reads as a real document instead of a printout of a form. That's a genuine unknown, and Codex is right that skipping it means the slice doesn't prove the riskier half of the concept.

But I don't think that means I was wrong to worry about a rushed export undercutting the "insurance-ready" positioning — I think the fix is to separate two things I was conflating. There's "does the export pipeline work and produce correct, well-organized content" (photos render, dates are right, condition and value timelines are laid out distinctly, purchase facts are present) — that's a structural proof, and it absolutely belongs in this slice, full stop, no negotiation, because it's the only way to know the data model actually supports the product's real promise. And there's "does the export have final premium typography, serif display treatment, museum-catalog polish" — that's a design-quality bar, not a concept-validation bar, and I think that part is fair to leave rougher in this pass without threatening the positioning, as long as it's clearly understood as v1-of-the-export rather than the final export. So: export stays in, but the acceptance bar for this slice is "structurally correct and legible," not "gallery-ready." I'll concede the point — Codex is right that leaving it out entirely was too conservative, and I was wrong to treat "not yet polished" and "not present at all" as the same risk. They're not; only one of them actually leaves the concept unproven.

On categories, I want to walk back my own suggestion too, now that I think about it next to what we already locked upstream. The fixed six-category enum was explicitly designed so there's no per-category schema difference — same fields, just a different label and a free-text subtype. That means shipping all six costs basically nothing extra once the form and model support the enum at all; there's no "watch flow" versus "vinyl flow" to separately build and validate. My "only do 2-3 categories" idea doesn't actually save real effort, it just arbitrarily restricts coverage of something that's already unified under the hood. I'd rather we keep all six in the slice — it's free, and "does this feel like it actually handles my whole cross-category collection" is part of what we're trying to validate, per the very audience definition we all agreed on (someone with watches *and* wine *and* cards, not just one).

Where I'll hold my ground is app-lock, and I noticed Codex didn't argue against it, he just didn't mention it either way — I don't think that's a real disagreement, more an omission, and I want to make sure it doesn't quietly fall out of scope by default. This is genuinely cheap (LocalAuthentication is a few lines against a system API, not a feature to design from scratch), and it's not polish — it's the one thing that makes the "we can trust this app with an itemized list of our valuables" story actually true the moment there's a second item in the catalog, which will happen the same day someone starts testing this. I'd rather over-include this now than have it get pushed to "later" the way non-urgent security features always do.

So here's where I've landed, updated from round 1: the slice is capture (all six categories, photo/title/category, Vision pre-fill silent, save under a minute) → list as archival cards → detail with the two separated timelines (one condition entry, one value entry to prove the shape) → soft-delete with recovery → app-lock → and a structurally-real, not-yet-typography-polished PDF export for one item and for the collection, tested at zero/one/many items like Codex said. What stays cut, and I think we're fully aligned on this now: widgets, StoreKit/paywall, the value-over-time chart (a data table or simple list of value entries can back the export without needing a rendered chart component yet — that's a separate, deferrable piece of work from "value entries exist and export correctly"), Vision as anything beyond silent background pre-fill, and any settings/backup messaging screen. That gives us one real, complete workflow — the acceptance check this phase actually requires — instead of a half-proof that dodges the harder question.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

Round 2 actually resolved the fork. Codex made the case that skipping export entirely would mean testing a weaker product than the one anyone signed up to build — this app's whole pitch is "more credible than a spreadsheet when you need to hand the record to someone," and that's precisely the half of the concept that's genuinely uncertain (structured data turning into a coherent document), not the capture-and-browse half, which is well-trodden. Claude read that and conceded it straight out rather than defending the original position — but split the export requirement in a way both landed on: the export pipeline has to be structurally real and correct in this slice (right photos, right dates, timelines laid out distinctly, purchase facts present, tested at zero/one/many items), but it doesn't need final museum-catalog typography polish yet — that's a legitimate "do it well later" item, not a "skip it now" item.

On categories, Claude walked back their own suggestion once they thought it through against what was already locked upstream: since all six categories share one schema with no per-category logic differences, narrowing to 2-3 for validation wouldn't actually save any build effort, it would just arbitrarily under-test the cross-category promise that's the whole point of the app. So all six stay in.

On app-lock, this was never really a disagreement — Codex simply hadn't mentioned it either way in round 1, and in round 2 explicitly said it should be in, cheap and trust-critical. Both agree it belongs in the first slice.

One small thing that isn't fully nailed down: Codex said single-item export is mandatory and collection-level export "can wait one step if needed," while Claude wants both in this slice. Nobody pushed back on Claude's stronger version, and since the collection export is a thin wrapper over the same per-item rendering logic once it exists, I'm treating this as settled in favor of including both — it's not a real fork, just Codex hedging slightly more than Claude did.

## Final Output

**MVP slice (the one workflow this build must prove):** A user captures a photo of something they own (any of the six fixed categories — watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other, Vision silently pre-filling the category suggestion in the background), gives it a title, and saves it in well under a minute. The item appears in an archival-card list. From its detail screen, the user adds one condition/provenance entry (note + optional photo) and one value entry, seeing them laid out as two clearly separate timelines, never merged. The user can soft-delete an item and recover it within the grace window, and the whole catalog sits behind Face ID/passcode app-lock. Finally, the user generates a local PDF export — for that single item, and for the collection as a whole — that is structurally correct (right cover photo, right dated entries, condition and value history laid out distinctly, purchase facts present) even if its final typographic polish isn't fully dialed in yet.

**Must-have interactions:**
- Create an item with only photo + title + category required; save completes in well under a minute.
- All six categories available and functionally identical under the hood (shared schema, free-text subtype).
- Vision classification pre-fills the category chip silently; never blocks or delays save; fully overridable.
- Resume a half-finished capture after interruption (background, force-quit) without losing the photo/draft.
- On the detail screen, independently add: purchase facts, one condition/provenance entry, one value entry — shown as two visually distinct timeline modules.
- A defined near-empty state for the value ledger at 0-1 entries (a simple list/table of entries is enough; no rendered chart needed yet).
- Soft-delete an item and restore it within the recovery window.
- App-lock via Face ID/passcode gating entry to the catalog.
- Generate and preview a PDF export for one item and for the full collection; verified structurally correct at zero items, one sparse item, and one fully-populated item — photos, dates, condition/value history, and purchase facts all render correctly, even with rougher typography than the final design pass.
- Relaunch persistence: everything above survives an app relaunch, not just the current session.

**Cut list:** Widget, StoreKit/paywall, rendered value-over-time chart (a plain list/table of value entries stands in for now), Vision as anything beyond silent background pre-fill, settings/backup-messaging screen, narrowing category count (rejected — all six stay, since they're free once the shared schema exists), multi-photo galleries beyond one photo per timeline entry, final export typography/design polish (deferred, not dropped — the pipeline must be structurally correct now, gallery-ready visuals come later).

**Validation criteria:** A first-time user can go from tapping "add item" to a saved catalog entry in well under a minute. A capture interrupted by backgrounding or force-quit is recoverable, not lost. After relaunch, all saved items, photos, condition entries, value entries, and app-lock state are intact. A soft-deleted item can be restored inside its recovery window. The app is inaccessible without passing Face ID/passcode. The PDF export (single-item and collection-level) opens successfully and is structurally honest at zero, one-sparse, and one-fully-populated item counts — no blank/broken renders, no misattributed photos, no garbled dates. If all of that holds, one complete, trustworthy workflow exists end-to-end, and the next phase can proceed straight to build without reopening product debate.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This clears the bar. The round 2 fork (export in vs. out) was genuinely argued rather than smoothed over, and the resolution is coherent: export stays in as a structural-correctness requirement (right photos, right dates, distinct timelines, purchase facts, tested at zero/one/many items) while final typographic polish is explicitly deferred, not dropped. That distinction is load-bearing and correctly reflected in both the MVP slice description and the must-have list. The categories walk-back is honest about being a concession rather than hiding it, and the reasoning (shared schema means narrowing buys no build-effort savings) is sound. App-lock convergence is accurately characterized as an omission-turned-agreement, not a manufactured consensus. The one loose thread (single-item vs. collection-level export) is flagged transparently and resolved with a defensible tiebreak rationale rather than silently picking one side.

Required output coverage is complete: MVP slice, must-have interactions, cut list, and validation criteria are all present and concrete. Acceptance checks pass — this is one complete workflow (capture → enrich → soft-delete/lock → export) specified concretely enough that a build phase could start immediately without reopening product debate. It stays consistent with all upstream locked decisions (non-blocking classification, soft-delete over hard delete, six-category enum, no paywall/widget/cloud in v1, structural-not-legal export framing). No repair needed — proceed to the next phase.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Round 2 actually resolved the fork. Codex made the case that skipping export entirely would mean testing a weaker product than the one anyone signed up to build — this app's whole pitch is "more credible than a spreadsheet when you need to hand the record to someone," and that's precisely the half of the concept that's genuinely uncertain (structured data turning into a coherent document), not the capture-and-browse half, which is well-trodden. Claude read that and conceded it straight out rather than defending the original position — but split the export requirement in a way both landed on: the export pipeline has to be structurally real and correct in this slice (right photos, right dates, timelines laid out distinctly, purchase facts present, tested at zero/one/many items), but it doesn't need final museum-catalog typography polish yet — that's a legitimate "do it well later" item, not a "skip it now" item.

On categories, Claude walked back their own suggestion once they thought it through against what was already locked upstream: since all six categories share one schema with no per-category logic differences, narrowing to 2-3 for validation wouldn't actually save any build effort, it would just arbitrarily under-test the cross-category promise that's the whole point of the app. So all six stay in.

On app-lock, this was never really a disagreement — Codex simply hadn't mentioned it either way in round 1, and in round 2 explicitly said it should be in, cheap and trust-critical. Both agree it belongs in the first slice.

One small thing that isn't fully nailed down: Codex said single-item export is mandatory and collection-level export "can wait one step if needed," while Claude wants both in this slice. Nobody pushed back on Claude's stronger version, and since the collection export is a thin wrapper over the same per-item rendering logic once it exists, I'm treating this as settled in favor of including both — it's not a real fork, just Codex hedging slightly more than Claude did.

## Final Output

**MVP slice (the one workflow this build must prove):** A user captures a photo of something they own (any of the six fixed categories — watch/vinyl/card/wine/sneaker/other, Vision silently pre-filling the category suggestion in the background), gives it a title, and saves it in well under a minute. The item appears in an archival-card list. From its detail screen, the user adds one condition/provenance entry (note + optional photo) and one value entry, seeing them laid out as two clearly separate timelines, never merged. The user can soft-delete an item and recover it within the grace window, and the whole catalog sits behind Face ID/passcode app-lock. Finally, the user generates a local PDF export — for that single item, and for the collection as a whole — that is structurally correct (right cover photo, right dated entries, condition and value history laid out distinctly, purchase facts present) even if its final typographic polish isn't fully dialed in yet.

**Must-have interactions:**
- Create an item with only photo + title + category required; save completes in well under a minute.
- All six categories available and functionally identical under the hood (shared schema, free-text subtype).
- Vision classification pre-fills the category chip silently; never blocks or delays save; fully overridable.
- Resume a half-finished capture after interruption (background, force-quit) without losing the photo/draft.
- On the detail screen, independently add: purchase facts, one condition/provenance entry, one value entry — shown as two visually distinct timeline modules.
- A defined near-empty state for the value ledger at 0-1 entries (a simple list/table of entries is enough; no rendered chart needed yet).
- Soft-delete an item and restore it within the recovery window.
- App-lock via Face ID/passcode gating entry to the catalog.
- Generate and preview a PDF export for one item and for the full collection; verified structurally correct at zero items, one sparse item, and one fully-populated item — photos, dates, condition/value history, and purchase facts all render correctly, even with rougher typography than the final design pass.
- Relaunch persistence: everything above survives an app relaunch, not just the current session.

**Cut list:** Widget, StoreKit/paywall, rendered value-over-time chart (a plain list/table of value entries stands in for now), Vision as anything beyond silent background pre-fill, settings/backup-messaging screen, narrowing category count (rejected — all six stay, since they're free once the shared schema exists), multi-photo galleries beyond one photo per timeline entry, final export typography/design polish (deferred, not dropped — the pipeline must be structurally correct now, gallery-ready visuals come later).

**Validation criteria:** A first-time user can go from tapping "add item" to a saved catalog entry in well under a minute. A capture interrupted by backgrounding or force-quit is recoverable, not lost. After relaunch, all saved items, photos, condition entries, value entries, and app-lock state are intact. A soft-deleted item can be restored inside its recovery window. The app is inaccessible without passing Face ID/passcode. The PDF export (single-item and collection-level) opens successfully and is structurally honest at zero, one-sparse, and one-fully-populated item counts — no blank/broken renders, no misattributed photos, no garbled dates. If all of that holds, one complete, trustworthy workflow exists end-to-end, and the next phase can proceed straight to build without reopening product debate.
