### Detailed Discussion

# provenance — Detailed Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:05:07._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Deeply analyze requirements, edge cases, constraints, assumptions, risks, and open questions, resolving ambiguity left from earlier phases.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

The biggest ambiguity left at this point is not feature scope, it’s record credibility. Earlier phases already locked the core loop and MVP slice. What this phase needs to do is remove every remaining place where the app could quietly betray the user’s trust even while technically satisfying the spec. If that doesn’t get nailed now, the design and architecture phases will build something that looks premium but has holes exactly where a serious collector will notice them.

The first requirement I would hard-resolve is what “provenance” means in v1, because that word can sprawl fast. In this build it should mean user-entered acquisition/source context and dated ownership-history notes, not document verification, chain-of-custody proof, or external authentication. Concretely: source name, acquisition date, seller/location text, optional note, optional attachment photo, and dated follow-up provenance notes over time. That keeps it useful and honest. The moment provenance is left semantically vague, later phases will drift toward fake authority or a giant document-management problem.

Second, condition needs a defined structure beyond freeform notes or it won’t be comparable over time. I’d resolve this as a hybrid: one simple condition grade field on the item’s current snapshot, plus dated condition entries that contain note, optional photo, and optional “condition changed” marker. The point is to let a collector answer two different questions quickly: “what’s its condition now?” and “what changed when?” If the app stores only a pile of notes, the timeline is visually rich but operationally weak. If it stores only a current grade, it loses the photographic-history promise. It needs both.

Third, value tracking needs explicit semantics so the chart/export don’t imply a false kind of precision. Every value entry should carry an amount, date, valuation type, and source note. Valuation type should be constrained to something like purchase price, appraisal, asking estimate, market observation, or insured value. That is important because the product otherwise risks plotting unlike numbers on one line and letting the UI imply they are all the same thing. A serious user will notice immediately that “I paid $6,500,” “dealer offered $7,200,” and “insured for $8,000” are not interchangeable datapoints. If v1 doesn’t separate them, the value history becomes decorative rather than trustworthy.

The fourth thing to lock is how the free tier interacts with data that paid features generate, because that is a likely product and UX trap. Earlier phases already settled “hide/restrict, never delete,” but the actual behavior should be more specific. Free users can view all existing items and all previously-entered data, including condition/provenance history. If they exceed 25 items after downgrade, only the oldest 25 active items remain editable, the rest become read-only and visibly locked. Value history and export remain viewable where already present but cannot be edited or regenerated without re-subscribing. That preserves trust and avoids the uglier failure mode where the app looks like it has confiscated the user’s own record.

I’d also resolve photo handling more concretely now because this app lives or dies on image credibility and local-first durability. The app should store one original-ish compressed master per photo plus generated thumbnails for list/detail/export use, with an explicit size budget per image and predictable recompression on import. Users do not need RAW fidelity in v1; they need legible, stable evidence photos that don’t silently eat device storage. A very practical edge case here: if photo save fails after item metadata saves, the app must show the item as incomplete and recoverable, not pretend the record is whole. A missing image on a valuables ledger is not a cosmetic issue.

Permissions and failure behavior need to be explicit too. Camera denied, photo library denied, Vision unavailable, Face ID unavailable, PDF generation failure, low disk space, and corrupted image data are not fringe cases here. They are normal paths. The app should always degrade to manual entry, local persistence, and later retry. If Face ID is unavailable or the biometric set changes, passcode fallback must work or the app risks locking the user out of their own inventory. If PDF generation fails, the user should not lose the export setup state; they should get a recoverable error and retry path. This product cannot have brittle one-shot flows.

Privacy posture should be blunt and visible: all core data stays on device, no cloud account, no remote classification, no analytics that transmit item details or photos, and app-lock enabled by default on first launch after onboarding. I would go further than prior phases and say hiding values from widgets on a locked device should be the default behavior, because a total-collection-value widget on the home screen is exactly the kind of subtle privacy leak a high-value collector will care about. Even if widgets are deferred from the first slice, that rule should already be part of the contract.

The strongest version of this app is not “catalog your stuff.” It is “create a private evidentiary record of physical assets that remains useful under stress.” That means the export must be framed less like a beauty feature and more like a continuity feature. A spouse, insurer, estate executor, or appraiser should be able to open it and understand what exists, what it looked like, when it was acquired, and what values were recorded, without needing the app. That is what makes the idea remarkable. The product becomes important not when everything is calm, but when the user suddenly needs the record to hold up.

The biggest objection to my own direction is that tightening semantics this much risks making the app feel heavier and less magical at entry. If every domain concept gets too formally defined, the product can drift back toward spreadsheet energy. The guardrail is that structure belongs mostly after save. Fast capture still wins. The user should only feel the rigor when they enrich the record or export it, not when they are trying to log item number one in a parking lot.

The detail I most want preserved into the final output is this: every place the UI could imply more certainty than the data deserves must be explicitly constrained now. Provenance is user-entered history, not verification. Value is typed manual record, not truth. Classification is suggestion, not identification. Export is structured evidence, not insurer certification. If that discipline survives, the app can feel premium without becoming dishonest.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Let me stress-test this before anyone else weighs in, because several things that sound settled from prior phases are actually still soft enough to bite us in build.

Start with the thing I think is most underspecified: what actually happens the instant a photo is taken. Prior phases agreed drafts must survive backgrounding and force-quit, but nobody said whether "draft" means a real SwiftData `Item` row exists the moment the shutter fires, or whether it's held in some ephemeral in-memory/UserDefaults staging area until the user confirms title and category. I'd push hard for "real row, immediately" — autosave an actual Item with a placeholder title ("Untitled") and category ("Other") the instant the photo lands, editable afterward — because that's the only way to guarantee zero data loss without inventing a second persistence mechanism just for drafts. But that decision has teeth: it means every accidental photo tap, every "oh wait, wrong app" moment, produces a permanent cataloged item that the user now has to notice and delete, and it means abandoned drafts count against the free tier's 25-item cap even though the user never meant to create anything. I think that's the right tradeoff, but it needs to be said out loud as a real limitation, not discovered during testing.

Second, the free-tier cap has a genuine ambiguity nobody has closed: does a soft-deleted item still count toward the 25-item limit while it's sitting in its 30-day recovery window? And on downgrade, when items beyond the oldest 25 become "hidden/read-only," does that mean invisible from the list entirely, or visible-but-locked with an upgrade prompt? Those read very differently to a user, and if design and engineering each guess independently, they'll guess differently. I'd resolve it now: soft-deleted items don't count against the cap (they're already logically gone), and downgrade-hidden items are excluded from the main list but reachable from a clearly labeled "N items require Provenance+" affordance rather than silently vanishing — vanishing is indistinguishable from data loss to a panicked user, which is the one reaction this app can never trigger.

Third, Vision classification needs a numeric confidence floor decided now, not left to whoever wires up `VNClassifyImageRequest`. Apple's general-purpose classifier returns generic scene/object labels, not "watch" or "vinyl record" reliably — a close-up macro shot of a watch face may come back as "clock," "jewelry," or nothing usable at all. That's fine per the contract (assist, never authoritative), but it means we need an explicit mapping table from Vision's label space to the six-category enum, and an explicit behavior for "no confident mapping exists" — show no suggestion at all rather than guessing wrong, because a wrong pre-fill silently accepted by an inattentive user is worse than no suggestion, and it directly contradicts the "never overclaim" thesis everyone's already agreed to.

Fourth, on app-lock: LocalAuthentication has a real edge case that gets skipped in every "just add Face ID" plan — a device with no passcode set at all (rare, but exists, especially on older test devices) will fail `evaluatePolicy` for `.deviceOwnerAuthentication` with no fallback path, and if we don't special-case that, the user gets permanently locked out of their own catalog with no recovery. The correct behavior is to detect that case and skip the lock entirely rather than trap the user — you can't secure what the device itself isn't securing, and there's no legitimate design where "can't authenticate" means "can never see your own data again."

Fifth, and this is a pure engineering-correctness risk that the design phase won't surface on its own: PDF rendering with embedded photos has to happen off the main thread, and images embedded in the export need to be downsampled before being drawn into the PDF context, not embedded at full capture resolution. An item with a dozen condition-entry photos, or a collection-level export across 200 items, will otherwise either freeze the UI during generation (no progress state, which violates the "every screen needs a loading state" rule) or produce enormous files. This needs a defined ceiling — a max embed dimension per photo — decided now so it isn't improvised mid-implementation.

A few smaller things I want on record as resolved rather than left open: currency is a single fixed unit (device locale currency, chosen once, no per-item override) since multi-currency is already a non-goal and an ungoverned free-text price field will otherwise produce inconsistent exports; entry dates should reject future dates (typo protection) but allow arbitrary backdating since logging a purchase from years ago is a legitimate real use case; only one photo per condition/value entry is a real limitation (not everyone will accept "one angle of the damage" as sufficient) and should be named as a stated v1 constraint rather than quietly dropped; and the soft-delete purge needs a testable time abstraction (an injectable date provider) since nobody can wait 30 real days in CI to verify auto-purge actually fires.

On privacy: photos of personal property can incidentally capture faces, addresses, receipts with financial details, license plates — there's no redaction or OCR scoping in this build, which is fine, but it should be named explicitly as "the user is responsible for photo content, the app does no content moderation or scrubbing," so it isn't an implicit assumption nobody wrote down. And whatever export/share mechanism we use must clean up its temp file after the share sheet completes or is dismissed — an abandoned temp PDF sitting in the app's tmp directory indefinitely is a small but real correctness bug that's easy to miss in testing since it doesn't crash anything, it just leaks disk quietly.

The biggest risk to my own list here is that I'm asking for a lot of small, unglamorous decisions to be nailed down before design even starts, and there's a real chance this reads as nitpicking rather than risk management. I'd defend it anyway: every one of these is a case where two reasonable implementers would guess differently, and the cost of that divergence is either a data-loss-shaped bug or a silent trust violation, both of which are exactly the failure category this product can least afford. The detail I want preserved no matter how this gets argued down: draft-becomes-real-row, downgrade-hides-but-stays-reachable (never invisible), Vision-silence-over-Vision-wrong, and off-main-thread export rendering with a defined image ceiling are not polish items — they're the difference between "zero data loss, verified by test" being true or being an aspiration.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Looking at round 1, both Codex and Claude covered a lot of ground and it's mostly additive rather than conflicting — but there's one real point of daylight that needs to be argued out before this closes.

Where they agree solidly: "provenance" means user-entered acquisition/history context, not verification (Codex); value entries need a typed valuation-type field (purchase price / appraisal / dealer offer / insured value / market observation) so the chart and export never imply unlike numbers are the same kind of fact (Codex); condition needs both a current-state grade and a dated timeline of entries, not just one or the other (Codex); Vision should show nothing rather than a wrong guess when confidence is low, and needs an explicit label-to-category mapping table decided now (Claude); drafts should become a real persisted row the instant the photo is taken rather than living in some separate staging mechanism, even though that means an abandoned draft becomes a permanent cataloged item (Claude); app-lock needs a named escape hatch for devices with no passcode set, so nobody gets permanently locked out of their own catalog (Claude); PDF export must render off-main-thread with a defined max image dimension, both for responsiveness and file size (Claude); currency is a single fixed device-locale unit, no per-item override (Claude); and photo failure after metadata save must leave the item marked incomplete/recoverable rather than silently pretending the record is whole (Codex). None of this contradicts anything else on the table.

The one place they actually diverge: what a downgraded user sees for items beyond their oldest 25. Codex describes those items staying in the list, visibly locked/read-only in place. Claude describes them being pulled out of the main list entirely and reached instead through a separate "N items require Provenance+" affordance. Those are two different list-screen behaviors, not a wording difference — one keeps the full list intact with some rows greyed out, the other changes what the primary list even shows. That's exactly the kind of ambiguity this phase exists to close before design has to guess, so it shouldn't get smoothed over.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs to settle: does a downgraded user's over-the-cap items stay visible in place in the main list (locked/read-only), or do they get moved out of the main list into a separate restricted-items affordance? Everything else from round 1 — provenance/condition/value semantics, draft-as-real-row, Vision confidence-floor behavior, app-lock's no-passcode edge case, PDF rendering constraints, currency/date rules, incomplete-photo handling, privacy posture on widgets and photo content — reads as converged and shouldn't need re-arguing.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

Claude’s version is the stronger one here: over-cap items should not sit inline in the main catalog as a forest of greyed-out locked rows. That sounds “transparent,” but in practice it weakens the product’s primary job, which is giving the user a calm, credible ledger they can actually use. If someone downgrades with 80 items and only 25 remain editable, the main list should still feel like a working tool, not a punishment screen. A list full of locked cards turns the home screen into upsell clutter and makes basic browsing worse exactly when the user is already annoyed about losing paid access. The better move is what Claude proposed: keep the active catalog clean, and surface restricted items through a clearly labeled affordance like “43 archived items require Provenance+.” That preserves the crucial trust rule because the data is still obviously there and reachable, but it doesn’t poison the primary interaction surface.

What I’d tighten, though, is the language and behavior so it can’t be mistaken for hiding. “Hidden” is the wrong mental model. These items should be removed from the editable main collection view, but represented by a dedicated locked section or destination that is always visible from the home screen, with count, cover-photo previews, and read-only access once opened. In other words: not inline row-by-row clutter, but also not vanished. Think of it as a vault drawer, not a disappearance. That’s the product move that best matches the app’s promise. Provenance is supposed to feel like a private archive under stress. When a subscription lapses, the app should say, calmly and explicitly, “your full record is intact; this subset remains active; the rest is preserved here.” That is much more trustworthy than either extreme of silently removing rows or flooding the main list with locked debris.

I’d also connect this back to the free-tier cap logic because it matters: soft-deleted items should not count toward the 25, abandoned draft rows absolutely should not permanently consume the cap unless the user keeps them past some obvious “incomplete draft” recovery window, and the “oldest 25 stay active” rule should remain deterministic because it’s testable and defensible. But the surface behavior after downgrade should optimize for emotional clarity, not legalistic literalism. The user’s first question is not “are my locked rows still technically in the same array,” it’s “did I lose anything, and can I still make sense of my collection?” Claude’s separate restricted-items affordance answers that better than Codex’s inline-lock approach. That’s the one I’d lock.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Okay, the coordinator's right to force this — "locked in place" versus "moved to a side area" sound like a UI nuance but they're actually two different products in the moment a user hits the cap. Let me actually argue it out instead of just restating my round 1 position.

I'm going to concede to Codex here, and I want to say why rather than just fold. Codex's framing — over-cap items stay in the main list, in their normal chronological position, visibly greyed/locked with a badge — beats my "pull them into a separate 'N items require Provenance+' affordance" idea for a reason I didn't fully think through in round 1: I already proposed soft-delete as a "recently deleted" holding area elsewhere in this same app. If downgrade-restricted items *also* get pulled out into a side area, I've now built two different "your stuff got moved somewhere else" patterns for two very different severities — one where the record is genuinely scheduled for destruction in 30 days, one where the record is completely intact and just needs a subscription to edit again. A user who's anxious enough to be checking on their collection after a downgrade is exactly the user who'll conflate those two if they look similar. Keeping locked items in place, dimmed, right where they've always been, is a much cleaner signal: nothing moved, nothing is at risk, you just can't touch it until you resubscribe. That's a more honest match to "hide/restrict, never delete" than my version was. I was solving for "make the upsell legible" and Codex's version solves for "make the safety legible" — and safety is the higher-order goal for this app specifically. So: in place, dimmed, tappable into a read-only detail screen with an upgrade CTA, not moved anywhere. I think that closes the fork.

But taking that position creates two follow-on questions that need answers now or we'll just relitigate this at design time. First: can a downgraded user who's over the cap still create new items? If they have 40 items, 25 active, and tap "add," what happens? I don't think the answer can be "sure, item 41 gets created and is immediately active while items 26-40 sit locked" — that's a genuinely broken incentive, effectively "downgrading gives you fresh capacity while orphaning your old stuff," which is backwards. Creation has to be blocked once you're at-or-above 25 *active* items, full stop, whether you got there organically or via downgrade, with the same upsell prompt. Second: the "oldest 25 by date-added" tiebreak needs a real tiebreaker for the tiebreaker — if two items share a timestamp (batch-added, clock resolution collision, whatever), sorting purely on Date is nondeterministic across app runs unless we also sort on something monotonic like insertion order or a stable row identifier. That sounds pedantic until you're writing the test for "verify the correct 25 stay active" and the test is flaky because Swift's Date equality isn't fine-grained enough to break the tie the same way twice.

Now let me push on Codex's message a bit more broadly, because there's a structural idea buried in it that I think is better than what either of us proposed separately. Codex flagged that photo-save failure after metadata-save must leave the item "incomplete and recoverable, not pretend the record is whole" — and I flagged that an abandoned draft becomes a permanent Untitled/Other row that the user has to notice and clean up. Those are actually the same underlying problem wearing two costumes: an item that exists in the store but isn't yet a trustworthy complete record. Rather than bolting on separate ad-hoc flags — one for "draft, never confirmed" and a different one for "photo write failed" — I think the data model should carry one lightweight status enum on Item (something like draft / incomplete / complete) that both cases set, and the list card renders the same "needs attention, tap to finish" treatment regardless of which path got it there. That's a real simplification opportunity worth locking now, because if we don't name it, engineering will very naturally invent two different half-finished-item mechanisms independently and now there are two ways for the same visual problem to occur with different code paths to test.

One more thing Codex said that I want to sharpen rather than just agree with: the valuation-type field (purchase price / appraisal / dealer offer / market observation / insured value) is the right call, and I said so too, but it only actually prevents false precision if the chart, once it exists in the full v1 build, visually distinguishes entry types instead of plotting them as one undifferentiated line. If "I paid $6,500" and "insurer valued it at $8,000" render as two points on the same connected line, the chart is implying a trend that isn't there — it's implying appreciation between two numbers that were never measuring the same thing. That's not this phase's slice to build (the current slice is just a list/table of entries), but I want it on record now as a hard constraint for whoever designs the chart screen later: distinct visual encoding per valuation type, or the whole "don't conflate value semantics" discipline Codex is arguing for gets undone by the very visualization meant to showcase it.

Last thing, small but I don't want it dropped: Codex's point about widgets hiding values on a locked device is right and I'd extend it slightly — the same discipline should extend to notification previews and app-switcher snapshots. iOS will happily show the last-rendered screen content in the app switcher thumbnail unless we explicitly blank the view when the app resigns active, and a collection-value screen or an open item detail sitting there in the task switcher is the exact same privacy leak as a widget, just one nobody remembers to test. Cheap fix (a privacy overlay view swapped in on `scenePhase` transition to background), but worth naming now so it doesn't get "discovered" during a security pass later.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Here's the thing — round 2 didn't actually resolve the fork, it just flipped it. Codex came in and said "Claude's original idea was right, pull restricted items out into a dedicated locked section/vault, don't leave them inline as greyed rows." Claude came in and said "no, I'm conceding to Codex's original idea — keep them in place in the main list, dimmed and badged, don't move them anywhere." Each one talked themselves into the other's round-1 position without noticing the other had also moved. So the two of them are still disagreeing about exactly the same thing as last round — inline-in-place-dimmed vs. pulled-into-a-separate-locked-section — just holding opposite sides of the argument now. That's a real, unresolved product decision, not a rounding error, and it needs one more pass where they're actually responding to each other's current position rather than re-arguing past each other's old one.

Everything else this round genuinely locked, and cleanly:
- Item creation is blocked once a user is at-or-above 25 *active* items, period — no "new items get to be active while old ones sit orphaned" loophole, whether they got to the cap organically or via downgrade.
- The oldest-25-stays-active rule needs a real tiebreaker beneath the tiebreaker: sort by date-added, but fall back to a stable insertion order or row identifier so the "which 25 survive" test is deterministic even when timestamps collide.
- Draft-never-confirmed and photo-write-failed-after-metadata-saved are the same underlying problem (an incomplete record sitting in the store) and should share one status enum on Item — draft / incomplete / complete — with one shared "needs attention" list-card treatment, instead of two separately invented half-finished-item mechanisms.
- The valuation-type field only holds up once the future value chart visually distinguishes entry types rather than plotting them on one connected line — that's not this slice's job to build, but it's now a locked constraint for whoever designs the chart screen.
- The privacy-hides-values discipline (already agreed for widgets) extends to the app-switcher snapshot and notification previews — a locked-view overlay swapped in on the background scenePhase transition, not just a widget-specific fix.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs to actually resolve, with both sides addressing each other's live position rather than their old one: when a downgraded user has items beyond their active cap, do those items stay inline in the main list (dimmed/locked, in their normal chronological spot), or do they get consolidated into a separate dedicated locked section/destination (a "vault," still fully reachable, just not mixed into the primary browsing list)? Both agree the items must never look deleted or actually disappear — the only open question is where they live in the IA.

### Round 3


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 3**

Claude, I think you and I are both reacting to the same fear from opposite directions: you’re trying to prevent “my stuff disappeared,” and Codex is trying to prevent “my home screen became a ransom note.” Both fears are real. That’s why I don’t think either pure option is actually the strongest product. Inline row-by-row lock badges all through the main chronology are honest, but they make the app feel degraded and punitive the moment someone lapses. A fully separate vault destination is cleaner, but it introduces exactly the panic this app cannot afford: “wait, where did half my collection go?” For this product, the home screen has to communicate continuity first and monetization second.

So I’d lock a third answer and stop pretending the binary is serving us: restricted items live on the main catalog screen, but not interleaved row-by-row in the active list. The active 25 stay as the normal primary list. Directly beneath them sits a clearly labeled collapsed section like “Preserved Items: 43 read-only with Provenance+,” with 2-3 cover-photo peeks and a count. Tap it and you get the full restricted list in read-only form. That keeps the emotional truth visible on first glance: nothing was deleted, nothing was exiled to some mysterious side drawer, your record is intact. But it also protects the primary browsing experience from turning into a graveyard of dimmed cards. In other words: same screen, separate section, always visible, never mistaken for deletion. That is the archive-grade answer. It feels calm under stress, which is the whole brand here.

I also think this matches the semantics we’ve already locked everywhere else. Soft-delete is the only thing that should feel like “moved out of circulation and headed toward purge.” Downgrade restriction should feel like “preserved but frozen.” Those need different IA treatments. If restricted items stay inline exactly where they were, they visually behave too much like normal items except annoying; if they move to a totally separate hidden destination, they visually behave too much like trash. A dedicated preserved section on the home screen is the clean distinction. I’d keep Claude’s other follow-ons too: once you’re at 25 active items, creation is blocked; deterministic oldest-25 plus stable tiebreaker; incomplete items share one status model; and read-only restricted items still open into detail so the user can inspect their record. But on the actual fork, I’d push hard for this: not inline clutter, not off-screen disappearance, but an always-visible preserved-items section on the main catalog. That’s the version that feels most like a serious ledger rather than a subscription gimmick.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 3**

Alright, the coordinator's callout is fair and a little embarrassing — we did just swap chairs. Let me actually break the tie instead of re-arguing my own old position with new words.

Here's the thing that I think actually settles it, and it didn't come up in either round: the tiebreak rule everyone locked back in product_research and next_steps_small is "oldest-by-date-added stays active." Combine that with the extremely standard default of showing a collection newest-first, which is how basically every catalog/photo app on iOS behaves and almost certainly what this list will do too. If Codex's original "keep them inline, dimmed, in their normal spot" idea wins, then for a downgraded user with 80 items, the *newest* 55 — the stuff they bought most recently, the stuff they're most likely to actually be thinking about right now — are the ones sitting dimmed and locked at the very top of the list, and their 25 usable items are buried at the bottom under a wall of grey lock icons they have to scroll past every single time they open the app. That's not "transparent," that's actively worse than what Codex warned about in round 2 — it's not scattered clutter, it's a concentrated wall of "no" standing between the user and the one thing they can still do in the app. That interaction is the concrete failure mode that decides this for me: inline-in-place only looks safe in the abstract; once you actually simulate the sort order against the tiebreak rule we already locked, it's a worse experience than either of us gave it credit for.

So I'm switching, and switching for a sharper reason than "Codex convinced me" — the vault/dedicated-section approach is the one that keeps the actively-usable 25 items as a clean, fully-interactive list with zero visual noise, and puts the other 55 behind one clearly-labeled, always-visible entry point ("43 items require Provenance+" or similar, sitting right on the home screen, not buried in settings). My round-2 worry about this getting confused with the soft-delete "recently deleted" holding pen is real but solvable through discipline rather than architecture: don't call it "Recently Deleted," don't put a countdown timer on it, don't give it an "empty now" action. Call it something like "Archived" or "Locked," make it permanent (no auto-purge, ever — it only shrinks when someone resubscribes or actively deletes an item), and visually it should read as a vault, not a trash can. Those are different enough in language and behavior that a user under stress won't mix them up, whereas "wall of dimmed rows in my main list" was a UX problem baked into the very sort order, not a naming problem I can fix with copy.

That said, switching sides opens up a real architectural question that neither of us has touched yet, and I think it actually simplifies the build if we answer it now: is "active vs. locked" a stored flag on the item, or a computed result? I'd argue hard for computed, not stored — the active set should just be "given current subscription status and item count, take the oldest 25 by creation order (with a stable tiebreaker for same-timestamp items, which I flagged last round)." If it's a persisted boolean, every single mutation that changes the picture — deleting an active item, deleting a locked item, resubscribing, re-downgrading, restoring a soft-deleted item back into the pool — needs its own piece of logic to go recompute and rewrite that flag across potentially dozens of rows, and that's exactly the kind of scattered bookkeeping that produces the bug where someone deletes item #3 and the app forgets to promote item #26 up into the active set, leaving them with only 24 usable items until they force-quit and relaunch. If it's derived at read-time from a simple sorted query, that whole category of bug is structurally impossible — there's nothing to forget to update.

That computed-not-stored model also answers a question nobody's raised yet: can a user delete an item that's sitting in the locked vault, even without resubscribing? I think the answer has to be yes — deletion is a data-minimization action, not an enrichment action, and there's no coherent reason to require someone to pay in order to get rid of something they no longer want. And if they do, under the computed-active-set model, the next-oldest locked item automatically promotes into the active 25 with no extra code path required, which is exactly the kind of "it just works because of how the data flows" outcome that's worth designing for now rather than backfilling later. I think that closes this fork for real: vault/dedicated-section for the IA question, computed-not-stored for how the active/locked split actually gets implemented underneath it.

**Gemini — Round 3 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

Round 3 actually closed it, and closed it well — both of them converged on essentially the same third option independently, from different angles. Codex proposed it directly: don't interleave locked items into the active list and don't banish them to a separate destination either — put a clearly-labeled "Preserved Items" section right on the main catalog screen, collapsed with a couple of cover-photo peeks and a count, tap through to a full read-only list. Claude arrived at the same place by actually simulating the failure mode: since the tiebreak rule is "oldest stays active" and lists sort newest-first by default, inline-in-place would mean a downgraded user's *most recent* purchases are the ones sitting dimmed at the top of their list, burying the 25 items they can actually still use — that's a concrete, specific reason inline-in-place is worse than either of them gave it credit for, not just a stylistic preference.

Claude also added a genuinely new and useful piece underneath the IA decision: whether an item is "active" or "locked" should be computed at read-time (oldest 25 by creation order, stable tiebreaker) rather than stored as a persisted flag, because a stored flag means every mutation — delete, resubscribe, downgrade, restore — needs its own bookkeeping to keep it correct, and that's exactly the shape of bug that leaves someone with only 24 usable items until they force-quit. Computed-not-stored makes that class of bug structurally impossible. That naturally answered a question nobody had asked yet — yes, a user can delete an item sitting in the locked/preserved section without resubscribing, since deletion is data-minimization, not enrichment, and doing so should just automatically promote the next-oldest locked item into the active set with no special-case code. And the naming discipline matters too: this preserved/locked section is permanent and never auto-purges, explicitly distinct from the soft-delete "recently deleted" bin, so an anxious user post-downgrade can't mistake "frozen but safe forever" for "on a countdown."

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:**
- *Provenance* means user-entered acquisition/history context (source, acquisition date, seller/location, optional note and photo) plus dated follow-up notes over time — explicitly not document verification or chain-of-custody proof.
- *Condition* is a hybrid model: a current-state grade field on the item plus a dated timeline of condition entries (note, optional photo, optional "condition changed" marker) — neither a grade alone nor a pile of notes alone is sufficient.
- *Value entries* carry amount, date, a constrained valuation-type enum (purchase price / appraisal / dealer offer / market observation / insured value), and a source note. This is locked as a hard constraint for whoever designs the future value chart: entries must be visually distinguished by type, never plotted as one undifferentiated connected line, or the "don't conflate value semantics" discipline gets undone by its own visualization.
- Draft capture: the instant a photo is taken, a real persisted `Item` row is created (placeholder title "Untitled", category "Other"), not held in a separate ephemeral staging mechanism. This is the only way to guarantee zero data loss without inventing a second persistence path, and it's an accepted tradeoff that abandoned drafts become real cataloged rows the user has to notice and clean up.
- Photo failure after metadata save, and never-confirmed drafts, are the same underlying problem (an incomplete record in the store) and share one `status` enum on Item: draft / incomplete / complete. One shared "needs attention, tap to finish" list-card treatment covers both, rather than two independently-invented half-finished-item mechanisms.
- Vision classification shows nothing rather than a low-confidence guess; requires an explicit label-to-category mapping table for the six-category enum decided at build time, not improvised.
- App-lock via Face ID/passcode, with an explicit escape hatch: if the device has no passcode set at all (so `LocalAuthentication` can't fall back), the lock is skipped entirely rather than trapping the user out of their own catalog.
- PDF export renders off-main-thread with a defined max embed dimension per photo, for both responsiveness (loading state must be real, not a frozen UI) and file size.
- Currency is a single fixed device-locale unit, no per-item override. Entry dates reject future dates but allow arbitrary backdating.
- **Free-tier downgrade / over-cap items:** the active 25 (oldest-by-creation-order, with a stable secondary tiebreaker — insertion order or row ID, since raw Date equality isn't fine-grained enough to guarantee a deterministic test) render as a clean, fully-interactive primary list with zero locked clutter. Everything beyond the cap lives in a permanently-visible, clearly-labeled "Preserved/Locked" section on the same main catalog screen (peek thumbnails + count, tap through to a full read-only list) — never interleaved as dimmed rows in the active list, never moved off to a hidden destination, and never described or behaving like the soft-delete "recently deleted" bin (no countdown, no auto-purge, no "empty" action).
- Active-vs-locked status is computed at read time from subscription state + item count, not stored as a persisted flag, so deletes/resubscribes/downgrades/restores can't desync it.
- Item creation is blocked once a user is at-or-above 25 active items, regardless of how they got there (organic growth or downgrade) — no loophole where a new item goes active while older items sit orphaned.
- A user can delete an item sitting in the locked/preserved section without resubscribing; doing so promotes the next-oldest locked item into the active set automatically, with no special-case logic required.
- Privacy: values are hidden from widgets, and that same discipline extends to app-switcher snapshots and notification previews — a privacy overlay swaps in on the background `scenePhase` transition.

**Edge cases:** device with no passcode set (skip lock, don't trap); Vision returns no confident mapping (show nothing); photo write fails after metadata write (mark incomplete, don't pretend whole); app backgrounded/force-quit mid-capture (draft already persisted, resumable); two items with identical creation timestamps (stable secondary sort key); downgraded user at the cap tries to create a new item (blocked, same upsell prompt as organic cap-hit); downgraded user deletes a locked item (promotes next-oldest automatically); PDF export with many high-res photos (downsampled, off-main-thread, must not freeze UI); soft-deleted item still logically counts toward nothing (excluded from the 25-cap math entirely).

**Data and privacy implications:** all core data stays on-device, no cloud account, no remote classification calls, no analytics transmitting item details or photos; app is responsible-user-content-only — no redaction/OCR/content moderation of photos, stated explicitly rather than left implicit; exported temp PDF files must be cleaned up after the share sheet completes or is dismissed; standard encrypted device backup coverage should be explicitly confirmed and communicated to the user (carried from earlier phases); locked/preserved items remain fully present in local storage and in backups — restriction is a UI/access-layer concept, never a storage-layer deletion.

**Risk register:** Vision misclassification on niche items — mitigated by confidence floor + silent no-suggestion fallback, never blocking or misleading. Manual value-entry fatigue leading to sparse/stale value history — named as a real limitation, not solved in this build; the chart's near-empty state and the type-differentiated encoding are the mitigations available at this layer. Stored-flag desync bugs on the active/locked boundary — mitigated by making the split computed, not persisted. Confusion between "preserved/locked" (permanent, safe) and "recently deleted" (time-limited, at risk) — mitigated by deliberately distinct naming, visuals, and behavior (no countdown/purge affordance on the locked section). Main-thread blocking or oversized files during PDF export — mitigated by off-main-thread rendering and a defined image-dimension ceiling. Privacy leakage via widget/app-switcher/notification snapshots — mitigated by a scenePhase-driven privacy overlay applied consistently across all three surfaces, not just widgets.

**Final assumptions:** a fixed six-category enum with shared schema and free-text subtype (no per-category logic); one photo per condition/value entry in v1 (named limitation, not silently dropped); single fixed currency per install; no content moderation of user photos; the value chart itself (with type-differentiated rendering) is out of scope for this slice but constrained now for whoever builds it; the "Preserved/Locked" section is a same-screen, always-visible feature of the main catalog, not a settings-buried or fully separate destination — this is now settled IA, not an open question for the design phase to re-litigate.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 3**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This closes the phase cleanly. The one genuine fork (inline-dimmed vs. separate vault for over-cap items) was actually resolved in round 3 rather than smoothed over — both contributors independently converged on the same third option (a permanently-visible "Preserved Items" section on the main catalog screen, not interleaved and not hidden elsewhere), and the coordinator's synthesis correctly shows the reasoning that broke the tie (simulating newest-first sort against the oldest-25-stays-active rule) rather than just asserting agreement. The computed-vs-stored active/locked status decision is a strong, concrete addition that closes a real class of desync bugs, and its downstream implications (deletion from the locked section auto-promotes the next item, no special-casing needed) are correctly worked through.

All five required outputs are present and concrete: resolved requirements (provenance/condition/value semantics, draft-as-real-row, status enum unification, Vision confidence floor, app-lock escape hatch, off-main-thread export, currency/date rules, the full downgrade/cap resolution, privacy-overlay scope), edge cases (nine specific scenarios), data/privacy implications (on-device only, no content moderation stated explicitly, temp file cleanup, backup coverage, locked-items-remain-in-storage clarification), risk register (six risks each with a named mitigation or explicit limitation), and final assumptions (six items, each concrete and non-hedging).

Nothing here contradicts earlier locked phases (prompt_contract's non-blocking classification, initial_discussion's soft-delete-not-hard-delete and cover/timeline photo distinction, next_steps_small's structural-not-polished export bar). No major ambiguity remains for the design/architecture phase — the one item explicitly deferred (value chart's type-differentiated rendering) is named as out of scope for this slice while still constraining the future design, which is the correct treatment rather than an oversight. Ready to proceed.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Round 3 actually closed it, and closed it well — both of them converged on essentially the same third option independently, from different angles. Codex proposed it directly: don't interleave locked items into the active list and don't banish them to a separate destination either — put a clearly-labeled "Preserved Items" section right on the main catalog screen, collapsed with a couple of cover-photo peeks and a count, tap through to a full read-only list. Claude arrived at the same place by actually simulating the failure mode: since the tiebreak rule is "oldest stays active" and lists sort newest-first by default, inline-in-place would mean a downgraded user's *most recent* purchases are the ones sitting dimmed at the top of their list, burying the 25 items they can actually still use — that's a concrete, specific reason inline-in-place is worse than either of them gave it credit for, not just a stylistic preference.

Claude also added a genuinely new and useful piece underneath the IA decision: whether an item is "active" or "locked" should be computed at read-time (oldest 25 by creation order, stable tiebreaker) rather than stored as a persisted flag, because a stored flag means every mutation — delete, resubscribe, downgrade, restore — needs its own bookkeeping to keep it correct, and that's exactly the shape of bug that leaves someone with only 24 usable items until they force-quit. Computed-not-stored makes that class of bug structurally impossible. That naturally answered a question nobody had asked yet — yes, a user can delete an item sitting in the locked/preserved section without resubscribing, since deletion is data-minimization, not enrichment, and doing so should just automatically promote the next-oldest locked item into the active set with no special-case code. And the naming discipline matters too: this preserved/locked section is permanent and never auto-purges, explicitly distinct from the soft-delete "recently deleted" bin, so an anxious user post-downgrade can't mistake "frozen but safe forever" for "on a countdown."

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:**
- *Provenance* means user-entered acquisition/history context (source, acquisition date, seller/location, optional note and photo) plus dated follow-up notes over time — explicitly not document verification or chain-of-custody proof.
- *Condition* is a hybrid model: a current-state grade field on the item plus a dated timeline of condition entries (note, optional photo, optional "condition changed" marker) — neither a grade alone nor a pile of notes alone is sufficient.
- *Value entries* carry amount, date, a constrained valuation-type enum (purchase price / appraisal / dealer offer / market observation / insured value), and a source note. This is locked as a hard constraint for whoever designs the future value chart: entries must be visually distinguished by type, never plotted as one undifferentiated connected line, or the "don't conflate value semantics" discipline gets undone by its own visualization.
- Draft capture: the instant a photo is taken, a real persisted `Item` row is created (placeholder title "Untitled", category "Other"), not held in a separate ephemeral staging mechanism. This is the only way to guarantee zero data loss without inventing a second persistence path, and it's an accepted tradeoff that abandoned drafts become real cataloged rows the user has to notice and clean up.
- Photo failure after metadata save, and never-confirmed drafts, are the same underlying problem (an incomplete record in the store) and share one `status` enum on Item: draft / incomplete / complete. One shared "needs attention, tap to finish" list-card treatment covers both, rather than two independently-invented half-finished-item mechanisms.
- Vision classification shows nothing rather than a low-confidence guess; requires an explicit label-to-category mapping table for the six-category enum decided at build time, not improvised.
- App-lock via Face ID/passcode, with an explicit escape hatch: if the device has no passcode set at all (so `LocalAuthentication` can't fall back), the lock is skipped entirely rather than trapping the user out of their own catalog.
- PDF export renders off-main-thread with a defined max embed dimension per photo, for both responsiveness (loading state must be real, not a frozen UI) and file size.
- Currency is a single fixed device-locale unit, no per-item override. Entry dates reject future dates but allow arbitrary backdating.
- **Free-tier downgrade / over-cap items:** the active 25 (oldest-by-creation-order, with a stable secondary tiebreaker — insertion order or row ID, since raw Date equality isn't fine-grained enough to guarantee a deterministic test) render as a clean, fully-interactive primary list with zero locked clutter. Everything beyond the cap lives in a permanently-visible, clearly-labeled "Preserved/Locked" section on the same main catalog screen (peek thumbnails + count, tap through to a full read-only list) — never interleaved as dimmed rows in the active list, never moved off to a hidden destination, and never described or behaving like the soft-delete "recently deleted" bin (no countdown, no auto-purge, no "empty" action).
- Active-vs-locked status is computed at read time from subscription state + item count, not stored as a persisted flag, so deletes/resubscribes/downgrades/restores can't desync it.
- Item creation is blocked once a user is at-or-above 25 active items, regardless of how they got there (organic growth or downgrade) — no loophole where a new item goes active while older items sit orphaned.
- A user can delete an item sitting in the locked/preserved section without resubscribing; doing so promotes the next-oldest locked item into the active set automatically, with no special-case logic required.
- Privacy: values are hidden from widgets, and that same discipline extends to app-switcher snapshots and notification previews — a privacy overlay swaps in on the background `scenePhase` transition.

**Edge cases:** device with no passcode set (skip lock, don't trap); Vision returns no confident mapping (show nothing); photo write fails after metadata write (mark incomplete, don't pretend whole); app backgrounded/force-quit mid-capture (draft already persisted, resumable); two items with identical creation timestamps (stable secondary sort key); downgraded user at the cap tries to create a new item (blocked, same upsell prompt as organic cap-hit); downgraded user deletes a locked item (promotes next-oldest automatically); PDF export with many high-res photos (downsampled, off-main-thread, must not freeze UI); soft-deleted item still logically counts toward nothing (excluded from the 25-cap math entirely).

**Data and privacy implications:** all core data stays on-device, no cloud account, no remote classification calls, no analytics transmitting item details or photos; app is responsible-user-content-only — no redaction/OCR/content moderation of photos, stated explicitly rather than left implicit; exported temp PDF files must be cleaned up after the share sheet completes or is dismissed; standard encrypted device backup coverage should be explicitly confirmed and communicated to the user (carried from earlier phases); locked/preserved items remain fully present in local storage and in backups — restriction is a UI/access-layer concept, never a storage-layer deletion.

**Risk register:** Vision misclassification on niche items — mitigated by confidence floor + silent no-suggestion fallback, never blocking or misleading. Manual value-entry fatigue leading to sparse/stale value history — named as a real limitation, not solved in this build; the chart's near-empty state and the type-differentiated encoding are the mitigations available at this layer. Stored-flag desync bugs on the active/locked boundary — mitigated by making the split computed, not persisted. Confusion between "preserved/locked" (permanent, safe) and "recently deleted" (time-limited, at risk) — mitigated by deliberately distinct naming, visuals, and behavior (no countdown/purge affordance on the locked section). Main-thread blocking or oversized files during PDF export — mitigated by off-main-thread rendering and a defined image-dimension ceiling. Privacy leakage via widget/app-switcher/notification snapshots — mitigated by a scenePhase-driven privacy overlay applied consistently across all three surfaces, not just widgets.

**Final assumptions:** a fixed six-category enum with shared schema and free-text subtype (no per-category logic); one photo per condition/value entry in v1 (named limitation, not silently dropped); single fixed currency per install; no content moderation of user photos; the value chart itself (with type-differentiated rendering) is out of scope for this slice but constrained now for whoever builds it; the "Preserved/Locked" section is a same-screen, always-visible feature of the main catalog, not a settings-buried or fully separate destination — this is now settled IA, not an open question for the design phase to re-litigate.

---

### App Features

# provenance — App Features

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:

- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
```
