## Phase Purpose

Enumerate and prioritize the full feature set with an explicit scoring / MoSCoW model and clear in-scope vs out-of-scope lines.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

The feature list needs to stay brutally aligned to the promise, and that promise is narrower than it sounds. This app is not “all collector tooling.” It is “I can log a valuable physical item quickly, trust the record later, and hand over a credible report without embarrassment.” Anything that does not directly strengthen speed of capture, trust of record, or clarity of handoff should be demoted immediately, even if it sounds premium.

Must-have means the workflow breaks without it. The musts I would lock are these: a catalog home that makes active items and preserved/locked items legible without mixing them; fast add-item capture with photo, title, category, and silent classification assist that never blocks save; an item detail screen with clearly separated facts, condition/provenance history, and value history; recovery states for drafts/incomplete items so half-finished records never masquerade as complete ones; soft-delete plus restore; local PDF export for single-item and full-collection reports; app-lock/privacy behavior; and subscription gating that enforces the 25-item free cap plus read-only preserved items without deleting anything. If any one of those drops, the app stops feeling like a trustworthy ledger and becomes either a note app or a paywall shell.

Each must needs a real user story and an acceptance bar. The catalog story is: “As a collector, I need to immediately understand what is active, what is preserved, and what needs attention so I never think data vanished.” Acceptance means the main screen can show empty, loading, success, and error; incomplete items have a visibly different treatment; preserved/locked items appear in their own always-visible section with count and previews; soft-deleted items are nowhere in active counts; and a downgraded user never sees locked clutter interleaved through the active list. The capture story is: “As a user logging something right after acquisition, I need to save an item in under a minute without being forced through a wizard.” Acceptance means photo, title, and category are sufficient; save works with manual entry even if Vision returns nothing; interruption recovery brings me back to the draft; permission-denied states have manual fallback; and low-confidence classification shows no suggestion rather than a bad one. The detail-screen story is: “As a user reviewing a record later, I need the item to read like a dossier, not a dumping ground.” Acceptance means purchase facts are distinct from condition/provenance entries and distinct from value entries; cover photo is visually separate from timeline photos; condition and value modules never collapse into one feed; 0-entry and 1-entry value states are designed intentionally; and read-only locked items remain fully viewable. The incomplete/recovery story is: “As a user, I need unfinished or partially failed records to be obvious and fixable, not silently wrong.” Acceptance means draft/incomplete/complete status is reflected in list and detail; photo-save failures do not create fake-complete records; and tapping an incomplete item resumes the right repair flow. The deletion story is: “As a user, I need mistakes to be reversible.” Acceptance means delete moves items to Recently Deleted, restore works, deleted items do not count toward caps, and Recently Deleted is visually and behaviorally distinct from Preserved/Locked. The export story is: “As a user, I need a report I can hand to someone else and have it make sense without the app.” Acceptance means single-item and collection export both exist; exports handle zero items, sparse items, and fully populated items; photos, dates, provenance, purchase facts, and value entries render in the correct sections; generation shows real loading/error/success states; and the result is legible even if final premium typography is deferred. The privacy story is: “As a user storing a list of valuables, I need the app to avoid casual exposure.” Acceptance means app-lock gates entry when available, skips trapping the user if device authentication is unavailable, and sensitive content is obscured in app-switcher snapshots and similar passive surfaces. The subscription story is: “As a free or downgraded user, I need to understand what I can still use and trust that nothing is being taken from me.” Acceptance means the 25 active items are deterministic, new item creation blocks at the cap, preserved items stay read-only and reachable, resubscription restores access without data migration, and no user data is deleted on downgrade.

Should-have features are the ones that materially improve the product but are not load-bearing for this build slice: richer export typography and layout polish, collection summary totals, better filtering or sorting inside the catalog, a clearer settings surface for backup/privacy messaging, and a more expressive detail presentation for condition changes over time. I would also put the future chart surface here if the build is tight, provided the underlying typed value entries already exist and export correctly. The reason is simple: the chart is only valuable if the underlying semantics are trustworthy first.

Could-have features are nice if nearly free and only if the musts are already solid: richer thumbnail peeks in the preserved section, subtle museum-catalog theming touches, lightweight bulk actions inside Recently Deleted, and slightly smarter capture affordances like suggested subtype text. These are the first things I would cut if timing slips, because none of them rescue a weak core flow.

Won’t-build for this phase should be explicit so the team stops secretly designing them: widgets, StoreKit paywall surfaces beyond what is strictly needed to express gating behavior, live market pricing, cloud sync, multi-currency handling, marketplace/social features, OCR document ingestion, category-specific schemas, multiple photos per value/condition entry, and any export language that implies insurer certification or appraiser validation. Also out: any inline IA that floods the main list with locked rows. That would be a design regression, not a simplification.

The biggest risk in my own direction is that I’m pushing for a very disciplined IA with several distinct states and sections, and that can make the app feel heavier if the visual design is not ruthless about calm hierarchy. “Active,” “Preserved,” “Incomplete,” and “Recently Deleted” are all necessary distinctions, but if they are not visually obvious on first glance, the app will feel bureaucratic instead of archival. The answer is not to remove the distinctions; it is to make them unmistakable and low-friction.

The detail the final output absolutely has to preserve is this: the main catalog cannot be a generic scrolling list of rows with mixed-status clutter. The whole trust model depends on four states being visually and behaviorally different at a glance: active items you can use, preserved items that are safe but locked, incomplete items that need attention, and deleted items that are actually at risk. If that separation blurs, the app’s premium aesthetic will not save it.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Coming into this fresh, I want to separate two things that are easy to conflate: what's already locked as a hard requirement from prior phases, and what's actually a genuine prioritization decision this phase still needs to make. A lot of what looks like "must-have feature scoping" here was already settled in prompt_contract and detailed_discussion — I don't think this phase should relitigate whether StoreKit, the widget, or the value chart belong in the build; they're locked. What this phase should actually do is turn those locked commitments into testable acceptance criteria, catch the places where locked decisions still contradict each other, and be honest about which of the "must" items carry the most build risk so nobody discovers that risk three weeks in.

The one real contradiction I want flagged before anything else gets prioritized: whether Vision classification assist is free-tier or paid-gated. The Subscription Model section of the original spec puts "on-device photo-based item ID assist" behind the paid tier. But every downstream phase — initial_discussion, per_app_product_brief's core loop, next_steps_small's must-have list, detailed_discussion's edge-case treatment — describes it as a silent, always-on part of the capture flow with no subscription check anywhere near it. Those can't both be true. My recommendation: classification assist is free-tier and universal. Gating it means either free users get zero assist and manually pick a category every single time (which fights the entire "faster than a spreadsheet" thesis for the tier most likely to be evaluating whether to trust the app at all), or engineering has to thread a paywall check into the one flow that's supposed to be foolproof and fast — that's added complexity in exactly the wrong place. The real paid gates should be unlimited items, the value chart, and export, which is also the framing per_app_product_brief leans on hardest for "ongoing value that grows with the collection." This needs to be locked explicitly in this phase's output, not left ambiguous for whoever writes the paywall gating code to guess at.

For the must-have list itself: local SwiftData catalog with the six-category draft-on-photo capture flow (a real Item row exists the instant the photo is taken, draft/incomplete/complete status, save completes with only photo+title+category in well under a minute) is must-have with acceptance criteria that are now easy to state precisely because detailed_discussion already did the hard thinking — a first-run user can go from tapping capture to a saved list entry in under a minute, and killing the app mid-capture leaves a recoverable draft, not a lost photo. The list view with the Preserved/Locked section (computed at read time, never a stored flag) is must-have, and its acceptance criteria should include the specific regression detailed_discussion was worried about: deleting an active item promotes the next visible item without any app relaunch required, and deleting a locked item promotes the next-oldest locked item automatically. Item detail with the three enrichment tracks (purchase facts, condition timeline with grade + dated entries, provenance timeline, typed value-entry ledger) is must-have, and the acceptance criterion I'd insist on is that condition and value entries never render in a merged feed — that's a UI regression that would quietly undo a decision the group spent real effort locking. Soft-delete with a real recovery window, app-lock with the no-passcode escape hatch, the value chart with type-differentiated rendering (not one connected line across incompatible valuation types), PDF export for single-item and collection at 0/1/many items rendered off-main-thread, StoreKit 2 with the paywall preview showing real redacted layout, the widget with distinct free (count) and paid (value) states, and full four-state coverage on every named screen — all must-have, all already committed to by earlier phases, so my job here is mostly to attach acceptance criteria sharp enough that "done" isn't debatable later.

Where I'd push back on my own inclination to just rubber-stamp everything as must-have: this is a lot of surface area for one MVP build — capture, six categories, Vision, two timelines, a typed value ledger, a rendered chart, PDF export, StoreKit, a widget, app-lock, soft-delete, and full accessibility coverage, all in one pass. The pragmatist in me wants to cut something. But I don't think this phase has the authority to cut hard requirements that were already through a full multi-round consensus in prompt_contract — that would be re-opening a closed decision, not prioritizing. The actual risk-management move available to this phase is sequencing and de-scoping the acceptance bar within each must, the way next_steps_small already did for export ("structurally correct now, typography polish later"). I'd apply that same discipline to the chart: must-have is a correct, type-differentiated, legible chart; must-not-have-yet is any interactive/zoomable/animated chart behavior — that's Could, not Must. Similarly widget correctness (right value, right privacy state) is Must; widget deep-linking into a specific item is Could.

Two concrete platform risks I want on record now because they're the kind of thing that gets "discovered" during QA otherwise: WidgetKit has a background reload budget — if the widget refreshes on every local write event as already decided, that needs to be debounced/coalesced, not fired on every single save, or iOS will start silently dropping reload requests and the widget will just look stale with no error surfaced anywhere. And StoreKit 2 testing (purchase, restore, downgrade-preserves-data) needs an injectable subscription-status abstraction behind the repository boundary, the same way soft-delete purge needs an injectable clock — otherwise every test of the downgrade/restore path depends on a live sandbox transaction, which is slow and flaky, and "verified" ends up meaning "someone clicked through it once manually."

For should/could: richer per-category placeholder art when no photo exists yet, haptic confirmation on save, a first-launch sample item so the empty state isn't the very first thing a new user sees, and support for system-level Increase Contrast / Reduce Motion within the dark aesthetic are all reasonable should-haves that don't threaten the build. A full light-mode theme I'd put as could-have at best — the design direction is explicitly dark archival, and forcing that everywhere is a legitimate product choice, but I want it named explicitly as a deliberate tradeoff against some users' accessibility contrast preferences, not something nobody noticed.

Won't-build should restate, not silently drop, everything already locked as out of scope: cloud sync, live market pricing, multi-currency, multi-user/shared collections, legal/insurer certification language, marketplace or trading features, social/community comparison, OCR or barcode/serial lookup, search/filter/sort beyond basic list ordering, per-category dynamic schemas, more than one photo per timeline entry, and iPad-specific layout. I'd add one more to that list explicitly: no interactive or actionable widget (tap-to-open-app only) — an editable or deep-linking widget is scope creep dressed up as a small addition, and it's exactly the kind of "just one more thing" that turns a finished MVP into a half-built one.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where the room landed after round one: Codex and Claude actually agree on almost the entire shape of the must/should/could/won't list, and it's clearly built on top of everything already locked in earlier phases rather than re-litigating it. Both of them independently anchor on the same four-state legibility idea for the catalog (active / preserved-locked / incomplete / recently-deleted) as the thing the whole trust story depends on, both attach real user-story-plus-acceptance-criteria to every must-have the way the playbook asks for, and both draw the same should/could/won't lines — richer export typography, collection totals, and non-interactive niceties are should/could, while cloud sync, live pricing, social features, OCR, multi-currency, and any "insurer-certified" language stay firmly in won't-build. Claude's additions (an injectable subscription-status abstraction so StoreKit downgrade/restore tests aren't flaky, WidgetKit's background-reload budget needing debouncing rather than firing on every save, cutting interactive/animated chart behavior down to Could while the type-differentiated correctness stays Must) are all things Codex's list doesn't contradict, just didn't get to.

But there's one real, unresolved fork, and it matters enough that this phase shouldn't close without it: the original spec text puts "on-device photo-based item ID assist" behind the paid tier, while every downstream locked phase (initial_discussion, per_app_product_brief, next_steps_small, detailed_discussion) describes Vision classification as a silent, always-on, non-blocking part of capture with no subscription gate anywhere near it. Claude flagged this directly and proposed resolving it in favor of "free and universal, because gating it either kills the free tier's capture speed or means threading a paywall check into the one flow that's supposed to be foolproof" — but Codex's round-1 message doesn't address this contradiction at all, so there's no actual agreement yet, just one person's proposed resolution sitting unchallenged. That needs Codex (and ideally Gemini, if available) to explicitly weigh in next round — either concur with "Vision assist is free-tier" or make a case for the paid-gate reading — before this phase can say the must-have list is actually settled, since it directly affects what "must-have" acceptance criteria look like for both the capture flow and the paywall-gating logic.

## Final Output
Not final yet — round 2 needs to explicitly resolve whether on-device Vision classification assist is free-tier (as every downstream product phase implies) or paid-gated (as the original spec's subscription-model line states), since the must-have acceptance criteria for both capture and paywall gating depend on that answer. Everything else in this phase's must/should/could/won't list reads as converged and shouldn't need re-arguing next round.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Claude is right to force this into the open, because the current state is incoherent in a way that will absolutely leak into the UX if we pretend it isn’t. The original subscription line says classification assist is paid. Every actual workflow decision we’ve made since then treats it like ambient infrastructure inside capture: silent, low-confidence-aware, non-blocking, always there if it works, invisible if it doesn’t. Those are not compatible product shapes. If we keep the paid-gate reading, the capture screen now has to know subscription status before it can decide whether to even run classification, which means the one flow we’ve spent the entire project protecting from friction suddenly gets a business-rule branch inside it. That’s bad architecture, but more importantly it’s bad product feel. Free users are exactly the people deciding whether this app is faster than their spreadsheet. If the answer is “not really, because we withheld the tiny bit of assist that makes category selection feel effortless,” then we’re sabotaging evaluation at the front door.

So I’d lock the contradiction now and resolve it in favor of what the rest of the product already wants: classification assist is free-tier and universal. Not because it’s generous, but because it’s the wrong thing to monetize. It’s not where the enduring value lives. The enduring value is scale, continuity, and handoff: unlimited items, value-over-time tracking, export, maybe alerts later. Those are the things that get more useful as the collection grows. Silent category suggestion is capture polish. Good polish matters, but gating capture polish creates the exact wrong emotional contour: the free tier feels slower and dumber at the moment the user is asking “should I switch from Notes?” while the paid tier gets an advantage most visible only during initial entry. That’s upside-down monetization. I’d go further and say the UX should never even hint that classification is a premium feature. No lock icon near it, no “upgrade for smarter detection,” nothing. It either quietly suggests a category or it doesn’t. The paid wall should appear around features that naturally read as premium surfaces: export preview, value chart, unlimited collection growth, maybe the collection-value widget state. Claude’s point about not threading paywall logic into the capture flow is exactly right, and from a design perspective I think that principle is bigger than this one feature: business gating should happen where the user is asking for more capability, not where they’re trying to complete the core save action without losing momentum.

The other thing I want preserved from this phase is that “must-have” now needs two different levels of honesty. There’s “must exist because the product promise depends on it,” and there’s “must be polished to the final premium bar.” We keep muddying those. Export must exist; it does not need final museum typography in the first build. The chart must exist if it’s still in scope from prior phases; it does not need interaction flourishes, but it does need type-differentiated encoding or it becomes misleading. The widget must exist if we’re honoring the earlier contract; it does not need clever deep links or fancy refresh behavior, but it does need a free-state and a paid-state and a privacy-safe reload model. Same pattern with Vision: it must exist as silent assist, but only in the humblest possible form. If we phrase the final output that way, the build phase gets a real target instead of a fake binary where every must sounds equally polished. But on the actual unresolved fork, I don’t think there’s a serious case left for paid-gating classification. It weakens the free-tier first-run experience, complicates the cleanest flow in the app, and monetizes the wrong moment. Free and universal is the stronger product and the cleaner IA.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Codex, your four-bucket framing (active / preserved-locked / incomplete / recently-deleted) is the right shape and I want to build on it rather than add a fifth thing, but I don't think it's actually four flat, mutually-exclusive states, and that gap matters for acceptance criteria. Status (draft/incomplete/complete) and active-vs-locked (computed from subscription + item count) are two independent axes, not one enum. That means an item can absolutely be incomplete-and-locked: a downgraded user has an old, half-finished draft sitting past their 25-item cap. Your incomplete story says "tapping an incomplete item resumes the right repair flow," but your subscription story says locked items stay read-only. Those two acceptance criteria collide the moment both are true of the same item, and if we don't pick a winner now, someone builds the "tap to finish" affordance generically and it accidentally lets a downgraded user edit a locked record, which breaks the entire "hide, never allow mutation without resubscribing" guarantee we spent three rounds locking in detailed_discussion. My answer: locked wins. An incomplete-and-locked item still gets the visual "needs attention" treatment so it's honestly represented, but tapping it goes to a read-only view with an upgrade CTA, not the repair flow. I'd want that written down explicitly rather than left as an inference, because it's exactly the kind of interaction nobody notices is undefined until a tester stumbles into it.

On the classification-assist gating question the coordinator flagged — Codex, you didn't take a side on it directly, but I'd point out your own capture-story acceptance criteria already answer it without saying so: "save works with manual entry even if Vision returns nothing" and "low-confidence classification shows no suggestion rather than a bad one" are both written with zero mention of subscription state. If classification were actually paid-gated the way the original spec's Subscription Model line says, your acceptance bar would need a third branch — "free users see no suggestion regardless of confidence, full stop" — and that's a meaningfully different, more restrictive behavior than "shows no suggestion when confidence is low." Since you didn't write that branch, I'm reading your silence as tacit agreement with free-tier-universal, but I'd rather you say so explicitly than have me infer it, because this is exactly the kind of ambiguity that turns into a real disagreement between whoever builds the capture screen and whoever builds the paywall-gating logic. I'm holding my position: classification assist is free and universal, gated features are unlimited items, the chart, and export.

Where I actually want to push back on you directly is demoting the chart to should-have "if the build is tight." I get the instinct — it's true that the chart is only trustworthy once the underlying typed value entries already work, so it's downstream of other musts. But think about what the paid tier is actually selling if the chart slips to should-have and gets cut under time pressure: unlimited items, classification assist (which I just argued should be free anyway), and export. That's it. Per_app_product_brief leaned hard on "ongoing paid behaviors keep earning their keep as the collection grows" as the whole justification for a recurring subscription instead of a one-time unlock, and a value chart that shows appreciation/depreciation over time is the one feature that actually delivers that "grows with your collection" feeling — export doesn't grow, it's episodic, you generate it and you're done until the next time you need it. If the chart quietly becomes a should-have and gets cut when the schedule slips, we ship a subscription whose real ongoing value is "you can add a 26th item," which is a much weaker pitch than what four separate consensus phases already agreed to build. So I'd keep chart as must-have, but exactly the way I said last round: must-have is correct and type-differentiated, must-not-have-yet is any interactive/animated/zoomable behavior. That protects the schedule without gutting what the subscription is actually for.

Last thing — your won't-build list includes "StoreKit paywall surfaces beyond what is strictly needed to express gating behavior," and I want to make sure that doesn't accidentally swallow something that already went through a quality gate. Per_app_product_brief explicitly locked that the paywall preview should show real, desirable formatting — actual export layout with blurred/redacted content — specifically so a user is "paying for something they've already seen and want, not speculating." If "strictly needed to express gating" means a plain lock icon and a price button, that's a regression from what was already approved and passed quality review. If it just means "no A/B-tested paywall variants, no promotional banner carousel, no upsell modal spam," then we agree completely and it's just a phrasing risk. I'd rather we say it precisely in the final output — the paywall preview showing real redacted export content is a must-have, not a "nice surface" that got swept into won't-build by accident.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: NO

Good news: the big fork from last round is actually closed. Codex came in and explicitly sided with Claude — Vision classification assist is free-tier and universal, full stop, no lock icon anywhere near it, because gating it would make the free tier feel dumber at exactly the moment someone's deciding whether to ditch their spreadsheet, and it would force a business-logic branch into the one flow the whole project has been protecting from friction. Claude noted that Codex's own round-1 acceptance criteria (manual entry always works, low-confidence shows nothing) never mentioned subscription state at all, which was already a tell that this was the real intent — good to have it said outright now instead of left as an inference. That question is settled: gating lives on unlimited items, the chart, and export — never on capture-time assist.

The chart debate also resolved itself without needing a real fight — Codex had floated "should-have if the build is tight" in round 1, but by round 2 had already moved to "must exist... needs type-differentiated encoding, not interaction flourishes," which is exactly the must-have-but-scoped-down position Claude was arguing for. So chart-as-must-have (correct and type-differentiated, no animation/zoom/interactivity required) looks like genuine agreement now, not a lingering disagreement.

Two things are still open, both raised only by Claude in round 2 with no response yet from Codex:

1. **Incomplete-and-locked collision.** Claude pointed out that "status" (draft/incomplete/complete) and "active vs. locked" (subscription + cap) are independent axes, so an item can be both incomplete *and* locked — an old half-finished draft that's fallen past the 25-item cap after a downgrade. Codex's incomplete-item story says tapping it resumes the repair flow; the subscription story says locked items are read-only. Those collide on that specific item, and Claude proposed "locked wins" (show the needs-attention visual, but tapping it opens read-only + upgrade CTA, not the repair flow) as the tiebreak. Codex hasn't weighed in on this yet.

2. **Paywall preview fidelity.** Claude flagged that Codex's won't-build line ("StoreKit paywall surfaces beyond what is strictly needed to express gating behavior") could accidentally walk back something per_app_product_brief already locked and passed quality review on — that the paywall preview must show real, desirable export formatting with blurred/redacted content, not just a lock icon and a price button. Claude wants this stated explicitly as a must-have rather than left ambiguous inside a won't-build bullet. Needs Codex to confirm that's not what he meant to exclude.

Next round just needs Codex to react to those two specific points — they're narrow and probably quick to close, not new fights.

## Final Output
Not final yet. Locked from this phase: classification assist is free-tier and universal with zero premium framing; the value chart is must-have but scoped to correctness/type-differentiated rendering only (no interactive/animated polish required); export and widget both must-exist-but-not-be-fully-polished the same way. Still needs round 3: (1) confirm "locked wins" as the tiebreak when an item is simultaneously incomplete and over-cap/locked, and (2) confirm the paywall preview showing real redacted export content is an explicit must-have, not something the won't-build list is meant to cut.

### Round 3


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 3**

Claude’s right on both open points, and I don’t think either one is optional cleanup. On the incomplete-and-locked collision, locked has to win. If we let “needs attention” implicitly mean “tappable into repair flow” in every case, we’ve just created a subscription leak through the messiest edge case in the app: an old draft or photo-failed record that slipped past the active 25 after downgrade. That’s exactly the kind of loophole that sounds tiny in product discussion and becomes a permanent support headache later. The UX answer is not to hide the incompleteness marker; it’s to separate “honest diagnosis” from “permission to edit.” So yes, an incomplete-and-locked item should still wear the visual badge that says this record is unfinished, because pretending it’s complete would be dishonest. But tapping it should land in a read-only detail state with a very explicit message: this item is preserved, incomplete, and requires Provenance+ to finish. That’s the calmest, least surprising behavior. It preserves the truth of the record, preserves the paywall boundary, and avoids the much worse failure mode where two identical-looking incomplete cards behave differently depending on some invisible subscription math. If we want to be extra disciplined, the main catalog should probably never promise “tap to finish” in the generic copy at all; it should say something like “Needs attention” and let the destination determine whether the user can repair or only inspect.

And on the paywall preview, Claude is also right that my earlier phrasing was too loose. When I said “no StoreKit surfaces beyond what’s strictly needed,” I did not mean “strip the paywall down to a lock icon and a buy button.” That would be a direct regression from what the group already locked, and worse, it would weaken the premium story at exactly the moment the product needs to feel most concrete. The redacted export preview is not fluff. It’s the single best way to make the subscription feel tied to a real artifact instead of an abstract entitlement. This app is selling handoff credibility. If the user can see an actually beautiful, actually structured report sitting just behind the gate, with real layout and blurred content, the paywall makes sense. If all they see is “upgrade to export,” they’re being asked to imagine value, which is always weaker. So I’d lock that explicitly: the paywall must preview the real export format with redacted or blurred content, not a placeholder mock, and not just generic marketing copy. The thing I still want cut is all the ornamental monetization junk around it: no carousel of upsell cards, no multiple paywall modes, no interruption spam inside capture. One serious paywall, shown at the moments where the user hits a real boundary, and anchored by a visible, desirable export artifact. That’s the clean answer.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 3**

Codex, glad we're aligned on classification — "no lock icon anywhere near it, ever" is exactly right, and I like that you tied it to a bigger design principle: gate where the user is asking for more capability, not where they're trying to finish the core save action. That's a good rule to carry forward into every future feature debate on this app, not just this one. And the "must exist vs. must be polished" split you drew is the right way to keep this list honest — I'd bake that distinction directly into how we write up the must-have list rather than let it stay implicit, because otherwise the build phase reads "PDF export: must-have" and someone burns a week on kerning before the core rendering pipeline is even correct.

Since you haven't weighed in yet on the two things I flagged, let me sharpen both so there's nothing left to interpret when you do. On incomplete-and-locked: I'd go further than just "locked wins" and actually define the full precedence order, because there's a third axis I skipped past too quickly — deletion. An item can be incomplete, then get soft-deleted (someone abandons a bad draft and swipes to delete it), and that item is now simultaneously "incomplete" and "recently deleted." Does it show the needs-attention treatment inside Recently Deleted? Obviously not — once something's in the deleted bin, its incompleteness is irrelevant, the only thing that matters is restore-or-purge. So the real rule is a strict precedence: deleted overrides everything (only restore/purge affordances, no repair, no lock messaging), locked overrides incomplete (read-only + upgrade CTA, no repair flow), and incomplete only gets its "tap to finish" treatment when the item is both active and not deleted. That's three axes collapsing into one deterministic display rule, and it's cheap to implement as a single computed "effective state" function per item rather than a tangle of if-statements scattered across the list and detail views — which matters because scattered conditionals are exactly how this kind of bug survives code review and gets caught by a user instead.

That precedence question actually surfaces a real edge case nobody's named yet: what happens when someone restores a soft-deleted item and they're currently at or over the 25-item active cap? We already locked that new item *creation* is blocked at the cap, no exceptions. But restore isn't creation — it's un-doing a deletion, and I don't think it should be blocked the same way, because blocking a restore would mean "you can't have your own previously-existing item back unless you pay," which is a much uglier trust violation than anything else we've discussed — worse than locking, because it's actively refusing to return something the user already had. My proposed rule: restore is never blocked by the cap. The item comes back into the pool, and because active/locked is computed fresh from creation-order at read time — the same design decision that already saves us from a dozen other desync bugs — the restored item just lands wherever it falls: active if it's within the oldest 25, locked if it isn't. No special-casing needed, it falls straight out of the model we already agreed on. I want this written down explicitly though, because "restore always succeeds, but might immediately show up as locked" is a real product behavior someone needs to design a confirmation message around ("Restored — this item is preserved but requires Provenance+ to edit, since you're over your free-tier limit"), not something we want engineering to discover mid-sprint and improvise copy for.

On the paywall preview — I'll wait to hear how Codex actually meant that won't-build line, but I want to propose the specific wording now so we're not just gesturing at "it's fine, right?" back and forth. I'd write it as: must-have — the paywall screen renders an actual mock export layout (real section headers, real photo placement, real typography treatment) with sensitive content (item values, possibly item photos) blurred or redacted, so the thing being sold is visibly the thing the user already has data for. Won't-build, separately and non-conflictingly — multiple paywall variants/A-B tests, time-pressure dark patterns like fake countdowns or "3 people upgraded in the last hour," and promotional interstitials outside the natural moments (hitting the cap, tapping export, tapping the chart). That phrasing keeps Codex's instinct — don't overbuild paywall infrastructure — fully intact while making sure the one specific paywall requirement that already passed a quality gate doesn't get read as collateral damage. If Codex agrees with that split, I think both open threads are actually closed and this phase is done arguing.

**Gemini — Round 3 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

CONSENSUS: YES

Codex came back and gave clean, direct answers to both open threads, and there's no daylight between the two of them anymore. On incomplete-and-locked, Codex agrees flatly: locked wins. The item still wears its honest "needs attention" badge (no pretending a broken record is fine), but tapping it goes to a read-only view with an upgrade message, never the repair flow — because letting "needs attention" silently double as "tap to edit" would create exactly the kind of subscription loophole that's invisible in a design review and shows up as a support ticket later. On the paywall preview, Codex confirmed his earlier "nothing beyond what's strictly needed" line was too loose and wasn't meant to walk back the redacted-export-preview requirement — he now states it explicitly as a real, structured mock export behind the gate (real layout, real typography, blurred/redacted sensitive content), with the cut list narrowed to the stuff that's actually ornamental: no paywall A/B variants, no fake urgency/countdown tactics, no interstitial spam outside natural trigger moments.

Claude used the room Codex's answers opened up to tighten the loose ends further rather than starting a new fight: the incomplete/locked collision is really a three-way precedence question once you add soft-delete into the mix (deleted overrides everything, locked overrides incomplete, incomplete-badge-with-repair-access only applies when an item is both active and not deleted), and restoring a soft-deleted item should never be blocked by the 25-item cap the way new item *creation* is — a restore just re-enters the pool and falls out active-or-locked based on the same computed, read-time model everyone already locked, no special-casing needed, just a confirmation message that says so plainly. Nothing here contradicts anything Codex has said; it's a direct, logical extension of principles both of them have already fully bought into (soft-delete over hard delete, computed-not-stored active/locked status, never punish the user for undoing their own mistake). Given how consistently these two have converged round over round, this reads as settled rather than a new fork waiting on a reply.

## Final Output

**Must-have features (each with a user story + acceptance bar):**

1. **Catalog home with four legible record states.** *Story:* a collector needs to instantly tell what's active, preserved/locked, needs attention, or at-risk-of-permanent-loss, so nothing ever feels like it silently vanished. *Acceptance:* empty/loading/success/error states on the main list; a permanently-visible "Preserved/Locked" section (peek thumbnails + count, never interleaved as dimmed rows); a single computed "effective state" per item with strict precedence — **deleted overrides everything** (only restore/purge, no repair, no lock messaging) → **locked overrides incomplete** (read-only + upgrade CTA, never the repair flow) → **incomplete-badge-with-repair-access** only when the item is active and not deleted; no item shows contradictory affordances.

2. **Fast capture with silent, free, universal classification assist.** *Story:* someone logging a purchase right after acquisition needs to save in well under a minute with zero forced steps. *Acceptance:* photo + title + category alone save it; a real Item row is created the instant the photo is taken (draft/incomplete/complete); Vision pre-fills the category chip silently for every user regardless of subscription tier, shows nothing below its confidence floor, never blocks or delays save, and carries zero premium framing (no lock icon, no upsell copy) anywhere near it; interrupted/backgrounded/force-quit capture resumes the same draft.

3. **Item detail with three separated enrichment tracks.** *Story:* reviewing a record later needs to feel like a dossier, not a dumped feed. *Acceptance:* purchase facts, condition/provenance timeline (grade + dated entries), and value-entry ledger render as visually distinct modules that never merge; cover photo is distinct from timeline photos; 0/1-entry value states have an intentional near-empty design, not a generic empty view; locked items remain fully viewable read-only.

4. **Value chart — correct and type-differentiated, not yet interactive.** *Story:* the subscription has to keep earning its keep as the collection ages, and the chart is the feature that actually delivers "grows with your collection." *Acceptance:* entries render with distinct visual encoding per valuation type (purchase/appraisal/dealer offer/market observation/insured value) — never one undifferentiated connected line; correctness and honesty are must-have now, interactivity/animation/zoom are explicitly deferred, not required for this build.

5. **Soft-delete, recovery, and uncapped restore.** *Story:* mistakes must be reversible, and getting an item back can never feel like it's being held for ransom. *Acceptance:* delete moves an item to Recently Deleted (visually/behaviorally distinct from Preserved/Locked — no countdown or purge affordance on the locked section, only on this one); deleted items are excluded from the 25-cap math entirely; restore is never blocked by the active-item cap — it re-enters the pool and lands active-or-locked purely from the existing computed read-time model, surfaced with an explicit message when it lands locked ("Restored — preserved but requires Provenance+ to edit").

6. **App-lock and passive-surface privacy.** *Story:* a list of valuables and what they cost shouldn't be casually visible. *Acceptance:* Face ID/passcode gates entry when device authentication exists; skips the lock entirely (never traps the user) when no passcode is set; a privacy overlay swaps in on background `scenePhase` transitions across the app, the widget, and app-switcher/notification surfaces alike.

7. **Local PDF export, single-item and collection, structurally correct.** *Story:* the record has to be handoff-ready without the app. *Acceptance:* both single-item and full-collection export exist; verified correct at zero/one-sparse/one-full item counts; correct photos, dates, condition/value history, and purchase facts in the right sections; off-main-thread rendering with a defined max photo embed dimension and a real loading/error/success state; final premium typography is explicitly deferred, structural correctness is not.

8. **StoreKit 2 gating with a real, desirable paywall preview.** *Story:* free users need a genuinely usable ledger, and the upgrade ask has to sell a real artifact, not an abstraction. *Acceptance:* 25-item free cap enforced deterministically (oldest-by-creation-order + stable tiebreaker); creation blocked at-or-above 25 active items regardless of cause; the paywall screen renders an actual mock export layout — real section headers, real photo placement, real typography — with sensitive content blurred/redacted, so the thing being sold is visibly the thing the user's data already supports; downgrade hides via the computed active/locked split, never deletes; resubscribe restores full access with no migration step; subscription-status is read through an injectable/testable abstraction so purchase/restore/downgrade behavior isn't only verifiable via a live sandbox click-through.

9. **Widget with distinct free/paid states.** *Story:* the home screen widget must reflect subscription reality without leaking private values. *Acceptance:* free tier shows item count, paid tier shows collection value; refreshes are debounced/coalesced on local write events (not fired per-save) to respect WidgetKit's background reload budget; values respect the same privacy-overlay discipline as the rest of the app; tap-to-open only — no deep-linking, no in-widget actions.

**Should-have:** final premium export typography/layout polish; collection summary totals on the home screen; a settings surface stating backup/privacy posture explicitly; richer condition-change presentation over time; system Increase Contrast / Reduce Motion support within the dark aesthetic.

**Could-have:** richer preserved-section thumbnail previews; subtle museum-catalog theming touches beyond the baseline; lightweight bulk actions inside Recently Deleted; suggested subtype text; haptic save confirmation; a first-launch sample item so new users don't hit a blank empty state; a full light-mode theme (explicitly named as a deliberate tradeoff against the dark-archival direction, not an oversight).

**Won't-build:** cloud sync, live/market pricing, multi-currency, multi-user/shared collections, marketplace or trading features, social/community comparison, OCR or barcode/serial lookup, search/filter/sort beyond basic ordering, per-category dynamic schemas, more than one photo per timeline entry, iPad-specific layout, any insurer/legal certification language, interactive or deep-linking widget behavior, paywall A/B variants or fake-urgency dark patterns, promotional interstitials outside natural trigger moments (hitting the cap, opening export, opening the chart), and any inline IA that interleaves locked items as dimmed rows through the active list.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 3**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This closes the phase cleanly. Round 3 resolved both open threads from round 2 with genuine agreement rather than a rubber stamp: Codex explicitly confirmed "locked wins" for the incomplete-and-locked collision (with Claude's precedence refinement — deleted overrides locked overrides incomplete — extending it coherently rather than reopening it), and Codex explicitly walked back the ambiguous won't-build phrasing to confirm the redacted mock-export paywall preview is a must-have, not something the cut list swallows. Both resolutions are concrete and testable, not hand-waved.

All four required outputs are present: 9 must-have features each with a user story and specific acceptance criteria, a should-have list, a could-have list (with the light-mode tradeoff explicitly named as deliberate rather than an oversight), and a won't-build list that correctly restates every previously-locked non-goal plus the newly-surfaced ones (no interactive widget, no inline dimmed-row IA, no paywall dark patterns).

Acceptance checks hold: every must-have ties directly back to the product promise locked in per_app_product_brief and detailed_discussion (documentation-and-trust ledger, non-blocking classification, computed-not-stored active/locked state, soft-delete-never-hard-delete, structural-before-polish export/chart/widget). The must-have list is scoped in a way the build phase can actually complete — each item has an explicit "must exist" vs. "must be fully polished" split (chart: correctness yes, interactivity no; export: structural correctness yes, final typography no), which is exactly the discipline the playbook asks for to keep build size realistic. No contradictions with earlier locked phases were introduced. Ready to proceed to design/architecture.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.
