### Design Discussion

# provenance — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:16:54._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide UX flows, information architecture, key screens/states, and the design principles that govern them.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

The design needs to protect one thing above all: the app has to feel faster than a spreadsheet at capture and more trustworthy than one at review. If the design gets too cinematic, too form-heavy, or too clever about gating, it misses the whole product. The right UX shape is a very short path into a saved record, then a slower dossier-style detail view where rigor shows up after the fact.

I would keep the navigation extremely flat. The primary tab is the catalog. From there you can add an item, open an item, open the preserved/locked section, open recently deleted, or open settings. I would not bury preserved items in settings or a secondary tab. They belong on the main catalog screen because the downgrade story has to communicate “your record is still here” immediately. I also would not add a separate “capture flow” stack that feels like onboarding every time. Tapping add should open a camera-first sheet, take the photo, create the draft item immediately, and then drop the user into a compact finishing screen with title, category, and save. Everything else is optional after save.

The screen inventory should be small and concrete: launch lock screen if app-lock is enabled; catalog home; camera/capture sheet; quick-finish draft screen; item detail; add condition/provenance entry; add value entry; purchase facts editor; preserved/locked list; recently deleted; export preview; paywall; settings. That is enough to cover every required workflow without inventing ornamental surfaces. The widget and chart are extensions of the core data model, not excuses to create more product.

The primary user flow should be brutally short: open app, unlock if needed, tap add, capture photo, review silent category suggestion if one exists, enter title, save. Now the item exists in the catalog. From the detail view, the user can later add purchase facts, add a condition/provenance entry, add a value entry, or export. That order matters. The app should not try to make the user “finish the record properly” during capture. The entire design should communicate that a saved draft is a success, not an incomplete failure.

The catalog home should have a very clear top-to-bottom structure: active collection first, preserved/locked section second, and an affordance to recently deleted that is visually separate and lower emphasis. Incomplete active items should appear inside the active collection with a visible needs-attention treatment, but not as alarming red errors. This app stores expensive things; the tone should be calm, not scolding. If the user is over the free cap after downgrade, the active list still needs to look like a functioning product. The preserved section should show a count and a few photo peeks, enough to reassure without flooding the main list.

The item detail screen is the trust surface, so it needs stronger information architecture than a generic form. I would make the top area a large cover photo, item title, category, subtype, current condition grade, and current value summary if the user has paid access and at least one value entry. Under that, three distinct modules: purchase facts, condition/provenance history, and value history. Condition/provenance should read like a dated archive with photos and notes. Value should read like a ledger first and a chart second, because the data is manual and typed. The user has to understand what kind of value each entry represents before they see any trend line. If that module becomes a pretty chart with weak semantics, the app starts overstating certainty.

The export preview should feel like a document viewer, not a marketing mockup. For the real export flow, the user should see a clear loading state, a preview, and then share/save actions. For the paywall preview, show the same layout but redacted. That preserves the product truth: the subscription is selling a real artifact, not an abstract unlock. The paywall itself should appear only at natural boundaries: trying to open the chart, trying to export, trying to add item 26, or trying to edit a preserved item. Never during capture before the first save.

The visual direction should stay restrained. Dark neutral background, warm paper-like cards or panels, serif only where it adds hierarchy, not everywhere. The mistake to avoid is “luxury app” theatrics. This product should feel archival, not glossy. Photos should do most of the emotional work. Typography should do the credibility work. I would use a quiet sans for UI controls and a restrained serif for titles, section headers, and export surfaces. Condition and value modules should have distinct visual signatures so they are instantly separable without needing heavy labels.

State coverage needs to be explicit because this app has more edge states than a normal CRUD app. Catalog: empty, loading, success, error. Capture: camera permission denied, photo taken, classification unavailable, classification low confidence, photo save failed, resumed draft. Item detail: complete item, incomplete item, locked item, missing photo, no value entries, one value entry, multiple value entries. Export: unavailable for free tier, preparing, success preview, failed render. Preserved section: empty because user is under cap, populated read-only list, upgrade prompt on edit attempt. Recently deleted: empty, populated, purge countdown messaging. If these states are not designed up front, they will show up later as ad hoc alerts and broken transitions.

Accessibility should follow the product’s trust thesis. Dynamic Type must not break the catalog cards or detail modules. VoiceOver order on detail should read like a record, not like a random pile of controls. Photos need meaningful labels like “cover photo” or “condition entry photo from June 3, 2026,” not just “image.” Color cannot be the only signal separating active, locked, incomplete, and deleted states. The value chart cannot be the only way to understand value history; the ledger entries must remain fully readable and understandable without interpreting the chart visually.

The biggest risk in this direction is that the app could still feel heavy if every state is made too visible at once. Catalog, preserved, incomplete, deleted, locked, draft, export, paywall, chart, app-lock: that is a lot of concepts for an MVP. The way to keep it shippable is not to remove necessary states, but to make the normal path almost insultingly simple and let complexity appear only when the user enters it. Most users most days should see a list, an add button, and a detail screen that feels calm.

The detail the final output has to preserve is this: the design should treat “saved quickly” as the primary success and “completed richly” as later enrichment. If the screens start forcing full provenance, condition, purchase facts, and value entry before the first save, the product loses the one moment it needs to win. Everything else can be sophisticated. Capture cannot.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Let me ground this in a concrete person: someone with forty-odd watches or a wine cellar's worth of bottles, currently tracking it all in a Notes app that's three years out of sync, opening this app for the first time skeptical that it'll actually be faster than what they already do. Every screen decision below is aimed at not losing that person in the first ninety seconds.

I'd build this as a single NavigationStack, no tab bar. This isn't a multi-domain app — it's one archive with drill-ins, and a tab bar would fragment that feeling and add chrome the museum-catalog aesthetic doesn't want. Screen inventory: Catalog Home (the only real "home"), a Quick Log capture sheet, Item Detail, an Add Condition Entry sheet, an Add Value Entry sheet, a Purchase Facts edit sheet, a Preserved/Locked read-only list (pushed from the home-screen section), a Recently Deleted list (reached from a settings/overflow surface, not the home screen directly — it's not something you browse casually), an Export Preview screen (single-item and collection variants share one screen with a mode toggle rather than two separate destinations), a Paywall screen, a Settings screen (app-lock toggle, backup/privacy statement, subscription management, link to Recently Deleted), and an App Lock gate that sits in front of all of it on cold launch/foreground-from-background.

The capture flow is the single most important interaction in the app and I want to describe it precisely because "under a minute" is easy to say and easy to accidentally violate with one extra tap. Tapping Add opens a picker immediately — camera by default, library as a visible alternative, no intermediate "choose category first" screen. The moment a photo is captured or picked, the Item row is created and persisted (this is already locked, but it has a direct UI consequence: the sheet doesn't wait for anything, it transitions instantly into what I'd call the Quick Log card — the photo thumbnail, a title field pre-filled "Untitled" with the cursor already active so typing just works, and a row of six category chips). Vision classification runs in the background and may take a moment to resolve; the chips must render immediately with nothing pre-selected, and if a suggestion arrives before the user taps Save, the suggested chip highlights itself with a small "suggested" affordance — never a lock icon, never premium framing, exactly as the group already locked. Save is the only required action beyond photo and title. Everything else — condition, provenance, purchase facts, value entries — is reached later from Item Detail, not stuffed into this sheet. I'd resist any temptation to add a "quick condition note" field to this sheet even though it seems convenient, because every optional field on the fast path is a tax on the moment this app has to win outright.

Item Detail is where the "dossier, not a feed" promise has to be visually obvious at a glance, not just true in the data model. I'd lay it out as a cover-photo header (distinct treatment from any timeline photo — larger, no timestamp badge on it, unlike timeline photos which always carry their date), then three cards stacked vertically that never visually blend into each other: Purchase Facts (price, date, source — entered once, edited in place), Condition & Provenance (grade chip plus a dated timeline of entries, photos included inline), and Value (the ledger, type-differentiated). Each card has its own "add entry" affordance local to it, so the user's mental model is "three logs, three plus buttons," never one generic "add" button that then asks what kind of thing you're adding.

Here's a real product question hiding inside what's supposed to be a design phase, and I don't think it's been settled yet: is the Value ledger itself a paid feature, or is only the chart/visualization paid while free users can log dated value entries? The original spec says free tier is "manual catalog... no valuation tracking," which reads as the whole ledger being gated. But app_features only names "unlimited items, the chart, and export" as the paid gates, without saying the ledger itself is locked. This isn't a wording nit — it changes what the Item Detail screen looks like for 90% of first-time users, since most people trying this app before paying will be on the free tier. I'd resolve it as: the Value card itself is the gated surface, not just its chart. Free users see the card present (so they know the feature exists and isn't hidden) but in a locked/teaser state — "Track value over time with Provenance+" with a single unlock action — rather than being allowed to log entries that then get hidden or can't be charted until they pay. The alternative — let anyone log value entries, only gate the chart — creates an ugly moment where a free user builds up two years of dated appraisal entries and then discovers, only when they try to visualize them, that they were never really getting the feature. Gating the whole card upfront is more honest, even though it's a smaller free tier. I want this decided now, not discovered by whoever builds the paywall logic.

Catalog Home itself needs restraint. Active items render as a clean vertical list of photo-forward cards — cover photo, title, category, and a small status affordance only when the effective-state model says something needs surfacing (needs-attention badge, or nothing at all for a normal complete item; never a badge for badge's sake). Directly below the active list sits the Preserved/Locked section exactly as already locked — collapsed, a couple of cover-photo peeks, a count, one tap to the full read-only list. I would not add a persistent "X locked" banner or anything shouty; it should read as a quiet library shelf, not a warning. The add button lives in the toolbar, always reachable, never buried. Recently Deleted deliberately does not get a home-screen presence — it lives one level down in Settings — because giving it a peer-level visual slot next to Preserved/Locked on the home screen risks exactly the confusion the group already spent three rounds avoiding: two "some of your stuff is over here" affordances sitting side by side on the same screen invites a user to conflate "frozen but safe forever" with "on a 30-day countdown." Preserved/Locked earns a home-screen presence because it's permanent and large-scale (potentially most of a downgraded user's collection); Recently Deleted is transient and usually small, and burying it one tap deeper actually reinforces that it's the less consequential, more temporary bin.

State coverage, screen by screen, because this is where "full state coverage" claims usually turn out to be theater: Catalog Home has real empty (first-launch, zero items, a calm "Add your first item" prompt, not a sad-illustration graphic that undercuts the archival tone), loading (should be near-instant since it's local, but the first frame after a cold launch still needs a defined skeleton rather than a flash of blank white/black), success, and error (SwiftData store failed to load — this should never happen silently; show a real recovery screen, not a crash). Item Detail needs its own empty sub-states per card, not one generic empty view borrowed three times — "no condition entries yet" reads differently and needs different copy than "no value entries yet." Export Preview needs a real generating state with progress, not a spinner over a frozen screen, since rendering is off-main-thread and could take a few seconds for a fully-populated item; and it needs to handle the zero-item collection export gracefully — a single calm page stating there's nothing to report yet, never a blank or malformed PDF, since that's one of the three item-counts this build is contractually required to verify. Paywall needs a StoreKit-fetch-failed state with retry — if the product list fails to load, the user should see "couldn't load pricing, try again," never a paywall that just looks broken.

On visual direction, the phrase "dark neutral, archival, museum-catalog" is doing a lot of work and I want to push back preemptively on the version of this that would look best in a portfolio shot and worst in actual use. Museums light their cases brightly precisely so you can see the object — the "archival" feeling should come from restraint, serif display type for titles, generous whitespace, and quiet borders, not from low-contrast mood lighting. Concretely: near-black but not pure-black background, a warm off-white for primary text, a single accent color used sparingly (maybe for the suggested-category highlight and the primary action button, nowhere else), serif for item titles and section headers, system sans for body/metadata/timestamps so dates and numbers stay maximally legible. The chart and the condition-photo timeline are exactly the two places this aesthetic is most tempting to over-darken, and exactly the two places legibility matters most — I'd treat those as the screens where restraint gets tested, not where atmosphere gets to win.

Accessibility notes I want on record now rather than retrofitted: every category chip needs a VoiceOver label that distinguishes "Watch, suggested" from a manually-tapped selection, so a screen-reader user isn't just hearing six identical button names; every effective-state badge needs a spoken label, not just an icon — "Locked, requires Provenance+ to edit" or "Needs attention, incomplete record," never a silent lock glyph; the value chart needs an accessible non-visual equivalent (a VoiceOver-navigable list of the same typed entries) since Swift Charts doesn't get this for free — a chart nobody using VoiceOver can read is a chart that's failed the "review has to feel trustworthy" promise for a whole class of users; and Dynamic Type at the largest accessibility sizes needs to be checked specifically on Item Detail's three-card layout and the Export Preview's paginated view, since those are the two screens most likely to break under long text wrapping.

The biggest risk in what I'm proposing is the Catalog Home doing too much in one screen — active list, preserved teaser, and the add action all competing for attention on first launch. The mitigation is hierarchy, not more screens: the preserved section should be visually quiet (smaller type, muted tone) precisely so it doesn't compete with the active list for a user who has zero locked items and will never see it. The single detail I most want preserved out of this pass: the Value ledger card being the actual paid-gated surface (not just its chart) is a real decision this phase is making, not a restatement of something already settled, and whoever builds the paywall logic needs to see it argued and decided here, not inferred later.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things stand after round one: Codex and Claude are largely painting the same picture — flat navigation, camera-first capture that saves in seconds with everything else deferred to enrichment, a catalog home where Preserved/Locked lives quietly and permanently rather than being buried or shouted about, an Item Detail screen built as three visually distinct cards (purchase facts, condition/provenance, value), a redacted-but-real export preview, and a restrained "archival means legible, not dark and moody" visual take. Both also independently flagged the same risk: this app has a lot of legitimate states (active/locked/incomplete/deleted, draft resumption, chart near-empty, export at 0/1/many), and the fix is to keep the default path insultingly simple and let complexity only surface when a user actually walks into it.

But Claude raised two concrete IA questions in round one that Codex's message (written first, before Claude's) never addresses, so they're genuinely open, not just unstated agreement:

1. **Is the Value ledger itself a paid feature, or only its chart/visualization?** Claude points out the original spec's "no valuation tracking" language on free tier conflicts with app_features' "gates are unlimited items, chart, and export" — and argues the whole Value card should show as locked/teaser for free users (not letting them log entries that only become visible/chartable later, which would feel like a bait-and-switch). This is a real product decision the group hasn't actually debated yet, just noticed needs deciding.

2. **Where does Recently Deleted live?** Codex's catalog home includes "an affordance to recently deleted that is visually separate and lower emphasis" — implying it's still visible on the home screen, just quieter than Preserved/Locked. Claude instead argues it should not appear on the home screen at all, and should be reached one level down through Settings, specifically to avoid two "some of your stuff lives elsewhere" affordances sitting side-by-side and inviting confusion between "frozen forever" and "on a 30-day countdown." That's a real difference in the screen inventory and home-screen layout, not a wording gap.

## Final Output
Not final yet. Next round needs Codex to respond directly to both open questions: (1) take a position on whether the Value/ledger card itself is gated for free users or just its chart, and (2) agree or push back on pulling Recently Deleted off the catalog home screen entirely into Settings, versus keeping a quiet affordance for it on the home screen. Everything else from round one — flat navigation, the screen inventory, the capture flow being save-first-enrich-later, the three-card detail layout, export preview sharing redacted layout with the paywall, and the "legible archival, not moody" visual direction — reads as converged and shouldn't need re-arguing.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Claude’s right to force both of these, because they’re not cosmetic IA choices, they change what the product is actually selling and what the home screen is optimizing for.

On value tracking: I think the original spec wins here, not the looser wording that crept in later. Free tier should not get the value ledger. If the promise of paid is “ongoing market-value tracking,” then letting free users create dated value entries but hiding the chart/export is a mushy middle that weakens the upgrade line and creates the wrong habit. They’d be doing the work of valuation tracking without getting the part that makes it feel coherent. Worse, it muddies the free product’s identity. Free Provenance should be a genuinely useful catalog and condition/provenance ledger up to 25 items. Paid Provenance is where it becomes a valuation ledger. That split is clean, teachable, and honest. So I’d make the Value card visible on detail for free users, but locked as a teaser surface from day one: maybe one sentence about tracking purchase price, appraisals, dealer offers, and insured value over time, plus the redacted miniature chart/report treatment and a single upgrade action. That keeps the IA stable across tiers, but it doesn’t pretend free users already “have” valuation tracking. I don’t buy the argument that gating the whole card makes free too weak, because the free tier still has the core trust loop intact: photo, title, category, condition, provenance, purchase facts, up to 25 items. That is a real product. And strategically, it protects the paid tier from collapsing into “you can own more than 25 things,” which is too thin.

On Recently Deleted, I’m with Claude, not my own round-one phrasing. It should not live on the catalog home. Preserved/Locked earns permanent home-screen presence because downgrade anxiety is central to the product and those items may represent most of someone’s collection. Recently Deleted is different: it’s transient, lower-frequency, and conceptually dangerous to place adjacent to Preserved because the whole app has already spent multiple phases distinguishing “safe but frozen” from “counting down toward purge.” Putting both on the main screen reintroduces exactly that confusion. So I’d move Recently Deleted into Settings or an overflow path off the catalog toolbar, with a small enough amount of friction that recovery is still easy, but not so prominent that it competes with the main job of the screen. The catalog home should say: here is your active collection, here is the preserved portion of your archive if subscription state matters. Not: here are two separate side-bins for unusual states, good luck remembering which one means what. If we keep home that disciplined, the app stays calm. If we let every legitimate state demand equal surface area, it turns into state-management UI instead of a collector’s ledger.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Codex, I want to pull on something you said almost in passing, because I think it actually resolves the value-ledger question before we even have to argue it fresh: you wrote that the detail screen's top area should show "current value summary if the user has paid access and at least one value entry." That's already assuming paid access gates the value summary — you just didn't say it out loud as a rule. So let me make it explicit and then sharpen it with something I dug up from our own earlier transcript that I think settles this cleanly rather than us re-litigating from scratch: back in product_research, the group already wrote "Value history and export remain viewable where already present but cannot be edited or regenerated without re-subscribing." That's about the downgrade case specifically, but it tells us something important — value data that already exists stays visible read-only even without an active subscription. It doesn't tell us whether a user who has *never* subscribed can originate new value entries in the first place.

So here's the version I'd actually lock, which is a refinement of what I said in round one, not a restatement: think of Value-tracking capability as its own gate, completely separate from the active/locked cap math. A user who has never subscribed sees the Value card on every item as a locked teaser — "Unlock value tracking with Provenance+" — because giving them an empty ledger they can't meaningfully use is worse than being upfront that it's a paid capability, and it matches "no valuation tracking" in the original spec almost word for word. The moment they subscribe, that card comes alive on every active item and starts accepting entries. If they later downgrade, their active items' Value cards don't vanish or re-lock into a teaser — they flip into the state we already have a name for: historical entries stay fully visible, "add" is disabled, with a message that matches the precedent language, something like "entries preserved — resubscribe to add more." And locked/preserved items just inherit whatever their Value card state already was, because locked items are read-only across the board anyway, nothing new needed there. I like this because it's not a new invention, it's stitching together a rule we'd already written down with the thing you and I were both dancing around without saying directly. Codex, if you agree this is what you meant by "if the user has paid access," say so plainly so it's not something design has to reverse-engineer from a parenthetical.

On Recently Deleted — you're pushing for it to stay visible on the home screen, just quieter than Preserved/Locked, and I think you've actually got a real point I dismissed too fast in round one: if someone just deleted a $9,000 item's whole history and immediately regrets it, telling them "go find it in Settings" adds a genuine beat of anxiety at exactly the wrong moment, and that's the scenario this whole soft-delete mechanism exists to protect against. I don't want to lose that. But I still think a second persistent section on the home screen, sitting near Preserved/Locked, recreates the exact confusion we spent three rounds in app_features avoiding — two "some of your stuff lives somewhere else" modules stacked on the same screen is genuinely harder to parse than one, no matter how different their copy is, because the visual pattern (a collapsed section with a count and peek thumbnails) is identical even if the words underneath it aren't.

So let me propose a third option instead of just picking a side: put Recently Deleted in the navigation bar itself — a quiet icon or an item in an overflow menu right on Catalog Home, one tap away, always there, but taking zero vertical space in the scrolling content where Preserved/Locked lives. That gets you the proximity you're arguing for, Codex — nobody has to leave the home screen or go digging through a Settings hierarchy — without creating a second scrollable "your stuff is elsewhere" module competing for the same visual attention as Preserved/Locked. And I'd pair it with something neither of us mentioned yet: the instant someone deletes an item, show an inline undo affordance right there — a toast or an in-place "Item moved to Recently Deleted, Undo" row — so the actual moment of regret is handled without navigating anywhere at all. That covers the real emotional case (I just did this and I want it back right now) with zero navigation, and it means Recently Deleted-as-a-destination becomes the calmer, more deliberate "I want to check what's in my trash from a few days ago" visit, which doesn't need home-screen billing to feel safe. I think that's a stronger answer than either "buried in Settings" or "a second home-screen section," and it directly answers your worry without recreating mine.

One more thing I want to react to directly: your line that "incomplete active items should appear inside the active collection with a visible needs-attention treatment, but not as alarming red errors... the tone should be calm, not scolding." Completely agree, and I'd go one step further on execution — I'd avoid red entirely for needs-attention states and reserve red (or whatever our one accent gesture is) for something the user actually has to act on urgently, like a destructive confirmation. A muted amber dot or a small "unfinished" label in the same serif/sans system as everything else keeps the whole app in one emotional register. If incomplete items get a different color language than everything else, the catalog stops feeling like one calm archive and starts feeling like a to-do list with guilt attached, which is exactly the "scolding" tone you're right to want to avoid.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

Round two actually closed both open threads cleanly, and interestingly, both of them ended up switching or refining their own round-one stance rather than digging in.

On value tracking: Codex came down decisively on the side of the original spec — free tier does not get a working value ledger, full stop. The Value card still appears on every item's detail screen for free users (so the feature isn't hidden or a mystery), but it renders as a locked teaser with a one-line pitch and an upgrade action, never an actually-usable empty ledger. Codex's reasoning: letting free users log real dated value entries while only gating the chart creates a mushy tier split and lets people do all the "valuation tracking" work without ever feeling like they got the feature. Claude then closed the one gap Codex's answer left open — what happens on downgrade — by tying it to language the group already locked back in product_research ("value history remains viewable but can't be edited or regenerated without re-subscribing"): a never-subscribed user always sees the locked teaser; a subscriber's Value card comes alive and stays alive while entries exist; a downgraded user keeps their historical entries fully visible read-only, with "add" disabled and a message like "entries preserved — resubscribe to add more." That's not a new argument, it's stitching an already-agreed rule onto a card Codex had already agreed to gate, so there's nothing left in tension here.

On Recently Deleted: Codex reversed his round-one position and agreed it shouldn't live as a second scrollable section on the home screen next to Preserved/Locked, for exactly the reason Claude gave — two "your stuff lives somewhere else" modules on one screen recreates the confusion the group spent three rounds avoiding. Where they landed together: Recently Deleted becomes a quiet, always-available overflow/toolbar affordance right on Catalog Home (Codex said "Settings or an overflow path off the catalog toolbar," Claude specifically proposed the toolbar/overflow-menu version so it needs zero navigation away from home) — plus, Claude added a piece nobody had proposed yet that solves the real emotional moment directly: an inline "Item moved to Recently Deleted — Undo" toast the instant a delete happens, so regret is handled on the spot without anyone needing to visit a destination screen at all. Codex didn't get a chance to react to that specific undo-toast addition, but it doesn't conflict with anything he said — it's a strict improvement on the same agreed placement, not a competing idea.

They also converged in passing on tone: needs-attention/incomplete states should never use red or an alarming visual language — reserve that only for genuinely destructive confirmations — and use a muted, same-typographic-system treatment instead, so the catalog stays one calm archive rather than turning into a to-do list with guilt attached.

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:** App Lock gate (shown on cold launch and on return-from-background when device authentication is available; skipped entirely if no device passcode exists) → Catalog Home (single NavigationStack, no tab bar) → Capture sheet (camera-first, opens instantly, no pre-picker) → Quick Log finishing card (photo + title + six category chips, silent Vision suggestion highlight, Save is the only required action) → Item Detail → Add Condition/Provenance Entry sheet → Add Value Entry sheet (only reachable once Value tracking is unlocked) → Purchase Facts editor → Preserved/Locked read-only list (pushed from its Catalog Home section) → Recently Deleted list (reached via a quiet overflow/toolbar affordance on Catalog Home, not a scrolling home-screen section) → Export Preview (single-item and collection variants share one screen behind a mode toggle) → Paywall → Settings (app-lock toggle, backup/privacy statement, subscription management).

**Primary user flow:** Open app → unlock if applicable → tap Add → capture/pick photo (an Item row is persisted the instant the photo lands) → Quick Log card appears already populated with the photo, an editable "Untitled" title, and six category chips; Vision's suggestion silently highlights a chip if and when it resolves, never blocking; user types a title, optionally taps a category, taps Save → item now exists in the active catalog, which is the moment of relief the product has to deliver in well under a minute. Everything else — purchase facts, condition/provenance entries, value entries, export — is optional enrichment reached later from Item Detail, never forced before the first save.

**Catalog Home layout:** active items as photo-forward cards (cover photo, title, category, and a status affordance only when the computed effective-state model actually has something to say — nothing extra for a normal complete item); directly beneath, the permanently-visible Preserved/Locked section (collapsed, a couple of cover-photo peeks, a count, one tap to the full read-only list) rendered quietly so it doesn't compete for attention when empty; Recently Deleted lives as a small overflow/toolbar affordance on this same screen — always one tap away, but taking no scrolling real estate — and every delete action also surfaces an immediate inline "Item moved to Recently Deleted — Undo" toast so the moment of regret is handled without navigating anywhere.

**Value-tracking gate (resolved this round):** the Value card appears on every item's detail screen regardless of subscription — visibility of the feature's existence is never hidden — but for a user who has never subscribed it renders as a locked teaser (short description + redacted mini-preview + one upgrade action), not a usable empty ledger. Subscribing brings the card to life and lets entries be logged. On downgrade, previously-logged entries stay fully visible read-only ("entries preserved — resubscribe to add more"); locked/preserved items simply inherit the same read-only treatment they already have everywhere else. This is a new decision made in this phase, not an inference — the paywall/detail build should treat it as settled.

**State model per screen:**
- *Catalog Home:* empty (first-launch, calm "Add your first item" prompt), loading (brief local-fetch skeleton, no blank flash), success, error (SwiftData load failure gets a real recovery screen, never silent).
- *Capture/Quick Log:* camera permission denied (manual/library fallback), photo captured, classification pending/unavailable/low-confidence (chip shows no suggestion), photo write failed after metadata save (item marked incomplete, not pretended complete), interrupted/backgrounded/force-quit (draft resumes on relaunch).
- *Item Detail:* complete item, incomplete item (repair-access badge, only when active and not deleted/locked), locked item (read-only, upgrade CTA, never repair flow), deleted item (restore/purge only), each of the three cards with its own distinct empty/populated states (0 condition entries reads differently from 0 value entries; locked Value teaser is its own explicit state, not a generic empty view).
- *Export Preview:* unavailable (free tier, upsell), generating (real progress, off-main-thread), success preview, failed render (retry path), zero-item collection export (calm "nothing to report yet" page, never blank/malformed).
- *Preserved/Locked list:* populated read-only list, attempted-edit shows upgrade prompt.
- *Recently Deleted:* empty, populated, item nearing purge messaging.
- *Paywall:* normal state (real redacted export mock, real layout/typography), StoreKit product-fetch failure (retry, never a silently broken paywall).

**Visual direction:** near-black (not pure-black) neutral background, warm off-white primary text, one sparing accent color reserved for the suggested-category highlight and primary actions only; serif for item titles/section headers/export surfaces, system sans for body, metadata, dates, and numbers so those stay maximally legible; "archival" comes from restraint, whitespace, and quiet borders rather than low-contrast mood lighting — condition-photo timelines and the value chart are explicitly named as the two screens where legibility must win over atmosphere. Needs-attention/incomplete states use a muted, same-typographic-system treatment (e.g., a quiet amber dot or small label) — never red, which is reserved only for genuinely destructive confirmations — so the catalog reads as one calm archive rather than a scolding to-do list.

**Accessibility notes:** category chips announce suggested-vs-manual state distinctly to VoiceOver rather than reading as identical buttons; every effective-state badge (locked, incomplete, deleted) has a spoken label, never a silent icon; cover photos and timeline photos get distinct, dated VoiceOver labels ("condition entry photo from June 3, 2026," not "image"); the value ledger has a fully VoiceOver-navigable list form so understanding value history never depends on interpreting a chart visually; color is never the sole signal separating active/locked/incomplete/deleted; Dynamic Type at the largest accessibility sizes is specifically checked against Item Detail's three-card layout and Export Preview's paginated view, the two layouts most likely to break under long text wrapping.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This closes the phase well. All five required outputs are present and concrete: screen inventory (with a full navigation graph, not just a list), primary user flow (capture-to-save under a minute, enrichment deferred), per-screen state models (empty/loading/success/error mapped explicitly for Catalog Home, Capture, Item Detail, Export, Preserved/Locked, Recently Deleted, Paywall), visual direction (specific palette/typography rules with an explicit anti-pattern called out — legibility over mood lighting on the two riskiest screens), and accessibility notes (concrete VoiceOver label content, not just "support VoiceOver").

Both round-1 forks were genuinely argued to resolution rather than smoothed over: the value-ledger gating question is resolved with textual grounding in the original spec ("no valuation tracking" on free tier) and reconciled with the previously-locked downgrade language from product_research, producing a clean three-state rule (never-subscribed teaser / active subscriber live / downgraded read-only-preserved) that doesn't contradict prompt_contract's non-goals. The Recently Deleted placement fork resolved with both parties updating their own position and landing on a placement (toolbar affordance + inline undo toast) that is a strict improvement over either original proposal, not a compromise that reintroduces the very confusion (Preserved vs. Recently-Deleted conflation) the group spent three rounds in app_features avoiding.

Acceptance checks hold: a SwiftUI developer has enough here to build the view hierarchy (NavigationStack, sheet list, per-screen state enums implied), and every required workflow (capture/classify, condition/provenance, value tracking, export, paywall, app-lock) has an explicit screen/state path. No contradictions with prompt_contract, app_features, or detailed_discussion were introduced — the one apparent tension (prompt_contract listing classification assist as paid) was already superseded by app_features' explicit, consensus-reached reversal, and this phase correctly builds on that rather than re-litigating it. Ready to proceed to architecture/implementation planning.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Round two actually closed both open threads cleanly, and interestingly, both of them ended up switching or refining their own round-one stance rather than digging in.

On value tracking: Codex came down decisively on the side of the original spec — free tier does not get a working value ledger, full stop. The Value card still appears on every item's detail screen for free users (so the feature isn't hidden or a mystery), but it renders as a locked teaser with a one-line pitch and an upgrade action, never an actually-usable empty ledger. Codex's reasoning: letting free users log real dated value entries while only gating the chart creates a mushy tier split and lets people do all the "valuation tracking" work without ever feeling like they got the feature. Claude then closed the one gap Codex's answer left open — what happens on downgrade — by tying it to language the group already locked back in product_research ("value history remains viewable but can't be edited or regenerated without re-subscribing"): a never-subscribed user always sees the locked teaser; a subscriber's Value card comes alive and stays alive while entries exist; a downgraded user keeps their historical entries fully visible read-only, with "add" disabled and a message like "entries preserved — resubscribe to add more." That's not a new argument, it's stitching an already-agreed rule onto a card Codex had already agreed to gate, so there's nothing left in tension here.

On Recently Deleted: Codex reversed his round-one position and agreed it shouldn't live as a second scrollable section on the home screen next to Preserved/Locked, for exactly the reason Claude gave — two "your stuff lives somewhere else" modules on one screen recreates the confusion the group spent three rounds avoiding. Where they landed together: Recently Deleted becomes a quiet, always-available overflow/toolbar affordance right on Catalog Home (Codex said "Settings or an overflow path off the catalog toolbar," Claude specifically proposed the toolbar/overflow-menu version so it needs zero navigation away from home) — plus, Claude added a piece nobody had proposed yet that solves the real emotional moment directly: an inline "Item moved to Recently Deleted — Undo" toast the instant a delete happens, so regret is handled on the spot without anyone needing to visit a destination screen at all. Codex didn't get a chance to react to that specific undo-toast addition, but it doesn't conflict with anything he said — it's a strict improvement on the same agreed placement, not a competing idea.

They also converged in passing on tone: needs-attention/incomplete states should never use red or an alarming visual language — reserve that only for genuinely destructive confirmations — and use a muted, same-typographic-system treatment instead, so the catalog stays one calm archive rather than turning into a to-do list with guilt attached.

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:** App Lock gate (shown on cold launch and on return-from-background when device authentication is available; skipped entirely if no device passcode exists) → Catalog Home (single NavigationStack, no tab bar) → Capture sheet (camera-first, opens instantly, no pre-picker) → Quick Log finishing card (photo + title + six category chips, silent Vision suggestion highlight, Save is the only required action) → Item Detail → Add Condition/Provenance Entry sheet → Add Value Entry sheet (only reachable once Value tracking is unlocked) → Purchase Facts editor → Preserved/Locked read-only list (pushed from its Catalog Home section) → Recently Deleted list (reached via a quiet overflow/toolbar affordance on Catalog Home, not a scrolling home-screen section) → Export Preview (single-item and collection variants share one screen behind a mode toggle) → Paywall → Settings (app-lock toggle, backup/privacy statement, subscription management).

**Primary user flow:** Open app → unlock if applicable → tap Add → capture/pick photo (an Item row is persisted the instant the photo lands) → Quick Log card appears already populated with the photo, an editable "Untitled" title, and six category chips; Vision's suggestion silently highlights a chip if and when it resolves, never blocking; user types a title, optionally taps a category, taps Save → item now exists in the active catalog, which is the moment of relief the product has to deliver in well under a minute. Everything else — purchase facts, condition/provenance entries, value entries, export — is optional enrichment reached later from Item Detail, never forced before the first save.

**Value-tracking gate (resolved this round):** the Value card appears on every item's detail screen regardless of subscription — visibility of the feature's existence is never hidden — but for a user who has never subscribed it renders as a locked teaser (short description + redacted mini-preview + one upgrade action), not a usable empty ledger. Subscribing brings the card to life and lets entries be logged. On downgrade, previously-logged entries stay fully visible read-only ("entries preserved — resubscribe to add more"); locked/preserved items simply inherit the same read-only treatment they already have everywhere else. This is a new decision made in this phase, not an inference — the paywall/detail build should treat it as settled.

**State model per screen:**
- *Catalog Home:* empty (first-launch, calm "Add your first item" prompt), loading (brief local-fetch skeleton, no blank flash), success, error (SwiftData load failure gets a real recovery screen, never silent).
- *Capture/Quick Log:* camera permission denied (manual/library fallback), photo captured, classification pending/unavailable/low-confidence (chip shows no suggestion), photo write failed after metadata save (item marked incomplete, not pretended complete), interrupted/backgrounded/force-quit (draft resumes on relaunch).
- *Item Detail:* complete item, incomplete item (repair-access badge, only when active and not deleted/locked), locked item (read-only, upgrade CTA, never repair flow), deleted item (restore/purge only), each of the three cards with its own distinct empty/populated states (0 condition entries reads differently from 0 value entries; locked Value teaser is its own explicit state, not a generic empty view).
- *Export Preview:* unavailable (free tier, upsell), generating (real progress, off-main-thread), success preview, failed render (retry path), zero-item collection export (calm "nothing to report yet" page, never blank/malformed).
- *Preserved/Locked list:* populated read-only list, attempted-edit shows upgrade prompt.
- *Recently Deleted:* empty, populated, item nearing purge messaging.
- *Paywall:* normal state (real redacted export mock, real layout/typography), StoreKit product-fetch failure (retry, never a silently broken paywall).

**Visual direction:** near-black (not pure-black) neutral background, warm off-white primary text, one sparing accent color reserved for the suggested-category highlight and primary actions only; serif for item titles/section headers/export surfaces, system sans for body, metadata, dates, and numbers so those stay maximally legible; "archival" comes from restraint, whitespace, and quiet borders rather than low-contrast mood lighting — condition-photo timelines and the value chart are explicitly named as the two screens where legibility must win over atmosphere. CONSENSUS: YES

### Design Handoff

# provenance — Design Handoff

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:22:24._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce a complete design handoff: screen-by-screen specs, interaction states, motion notes, design tokens, accessibility expectations, and a professional Claude Design prompt or import plan if the run is configured to pause for external design.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

The design has to make one very particular person feel smart and safe: someone standing over a newly acquired watch, bottle, record, or card, knowing they should log it now before details evaporate, and fearing this app is about to make them do more work than a spreadsheet row. If the first-save path feels ceremonious, the product loses. If the later review path feels vague or decorative, the product also loses. So the interface should be split emotionally into two modes: immediate capture relief, then deliberate archival review.

I’d keep the app as a single `NavigationStack` with sheets for add/edit moments. No tab bar. No secondary “analytics” or “profile” surfaces. The top-level destinations should be App Lock, Catalog Home, Capture, Quick Log, Item Detail, Add Condition/Provenance Entry, Add Value Entry, Purchase Facts Editor, Preserved/Locked List, Recently Deleted, Export Preview, Paywall, and Settings. That’s already a lot, so the rule should be that only Catalog Home feels persistent. Everything else is entered for a job and then dismissed cleanly.

Catalog Home should feel like a private archive table, not a dashboard. Top chrome is minimal: title, overflow menu, add button. The main body is three zones in order: collection summary strip, active items list, preserved/locked teaser. The summary strip should be compact and calm, showing either item count for free users or total collection value for paid users, plus maybe a tiny delta indicator only if the user has enough value data for it to mean something. The active list is the dominant element: large photo-forward cards with cover image, item title, category chip, optional subtype, and exactly one secondary status cue when needed. Never stack multiple badges on a card. The preserved/locked section sits below as a quiet shelf: section title, count, two or three thumbnail peeks, one chevron into the full read-only list. Recently Deleted should not be a content section at all; it belongs in overflow, with undo handled inline after delete. If the home screen starts listing every exception state, it becomes records-management software instead of a premium collector tool.

The add flow should be camera-first and fast enough to feel inevitable. Tap add, present full-screen capture immediately with a library fallback button and a cancel affordance. The moment a photo is captured or chosen, create the draft item and transition to Quick Log. Quick Log should be a compact card over the app background, not a full endless form. It needs the photo, a title field already focused, a row of six category chips, the save button, and nothing else required. Vision suggestion should appear as a subtle highlight on one chip with “Suggested” text if confidence clears the threshold; if not, there is no suggestion UI at all. No spinner asking the user to wait. No “analyzing” takeover state. Save should always be enabled once there is a photo and non-empty title. If the user backgrounds mid-flow, reopening should take them back into that exact draft, not to home with a vague banner.

Item Detail is the trust surface and needs a very explicit hierarchy. Header first: cover photo, title, category/subtype, current condition grade, acquisition date if present, current value summary if that feature is unlocked and populated. Under that, four modules in this order: Purchase Facts, Condition & Provenance, Value, Export. Purchase Facts is the simplest card and should read like a factual label sheet: purchase price, date, source, location, notes. Condition & Provenance is the deepest module and should feel archival: current grade at top, then a dated timeline with entries that can include note, photo, and provenance context. Value is a separate module with its own identity; it should never look like a continuation of the condition timeline. Ledger entries come first because users need to understand what each number means. The chart can sit under the ledger preview or on a linked detail screen within the same module, but type distinctions must be legible in both places. Export is a smaller action-oriented card near the bottom that explains what the generated report contains and leads into preview.

The free-versus-paid behavior should be visually honest and stable. A free user should still see the Value card, but in a locked teaser state if they’ve never subscribed: short explanatory copy, a small redacted chart/ledger preview, one upgrade action. If they had value history before downgrading, the card becomes read-only preserved, not teaser. Preserved/locked items across the app should still look like real records, just with edit affordances removed and a clear upgrade explanation at the point of attempted mutation. The app should never feel like it hid their data; it should feel like it froze it carefully.

The Preserved/Locked list itself should mirror Catalog Home’s item card language but slightly flatter and more restrained to signal read-only status. Users need to browse it without confusion, confirm their items still exist, and understand why they can’t edit. That screen should include a concise explanation block at top: “Your records are preserved. Upgrade to edit or add beyond your free limit.” Nothing more dramatic than that. Recently Deleted, by contrast, should feel operational rather than archival: smaller rows, clear deletion date, days remaining, restore and purge actions. These two destinations must not share visual patterns too closely, or users will conflate “safe forever” with “counting down.”

Export Preview should look like a document viewer, not a modal gimmick. The screen needs a mode switch at top for single item versus collection, then a rendering state area. While generating, show skeleton pages or page placeholders with a progress message, not a generic spinner. On success, show paged previews with share/save actions pinned in the toolbar. For zero-item collection export, render a deliberately sparse title page that says there are currently no items to report. The paywall should reuse this document language: the hero content is a redacted export preview with real hierarchy, not generic feature marketing. If the subscription is selling credibility, the paywall needs to show credibility.

Visually, I’d keep the palette tight: charcoal background, slightly warmer panel color, ivory text, subdued separators, one muted accent that can carry primary actions and Vision suggestion state. Serif only for titles, section names, and the export itself. Sans for controls, metadata, numbers, and long-form notes. The cards should have soft edges but not look plush; this is museum-catalog, not luxury-commerce. Photos should be the most vivid element in the app. The quickest way to ruin this direction is low contrast in the name of mood. Dates, values, and field labels must stay immediately readable.

Component-wise, the builder needs a fixed set of reusable pieces: photo-forward item card, preserved teaser shelf, status badge, category chip with suggested/manual/selected states, summary strip, archival module card, timeline entry row, value ledger row, locked teaser card, restore toast with undo, export page placeholder, paywall preview card, privacy overlay, and a consistent empty-state block. The component rule should be that every state change is expressed through these primitives rather than one-off alert styling.

Motion should be restrained and purposeful. Capture to Quick Log should feel immediate, almost like placing a card on the table. Saving an item should dismiss with a quick continuity animation back to the new card in the catalog. Expanding into Preserved/Locked or Export Preview can use a slightly slower push to suggest depth. Undo toast should slide up gently and disappear without drama. No decorative parallax, no bouncy oversell. The app should feel composed.

Accessibility has to be explicit because the photo-heavy, dark UI makes it easy to get wrong. Every card needs a single coherent VoiceOver summary before exposing sub-elements. Category chips must announce selected and suggested states distinctly. Status needs text, not color alone. The value module must be fully understandable from the ledger without reading the chart. Dynamic Type must be tested hardest on Catalog cards, the Item Detail header, and export preview controls. The dark theme needs enough contrast in both default and Increase Contrast modes. If light mode is omitted, that should be a deliberate product decision with compensating accessibility rigor, not accidental.

The biggest risk in this direction is that the app could become too elegant about complexity and hide important system status. A downgraded user, an incomplete draft, a photo save failure, and a read-only value history are all meaningful states; if the archival aesthetic sands them down too far, the user won’t know what’s happening. So the design needs quiet clarity, not quiet ambiguity.

The detail the final output absolutely needs to preserve is this: first save must feel like success, not like the start of a form. Every screen and motion decision should protect that. If the design turns capture into an intake wizard, no amount of premium typography later will save the product.

If external design is desired, I’d hand Claude Design a very concrete prompt: design an iPhone SwiftUI app called Provenance for serious collectors of high-value physical goods. The product is a local-first archival ledger, not a marketplace or social app. Prioritize a camera-first quick-log flow that saves an item in under a minute, then a dossier-style item detail view with four distinct modules: purchase facts, condition/provenance timeline, value tracking, and export. Use a quiet museum-catalog aesthetic: charcoal and warm ivory, serif only for titles/section headers, system sans for data and controls, photo-forward cards, restrained borders, no glossy luxury tropes. Show these screens in full: Catalog Home with active items and a quiet preserved/locked section; Capture camera screen; Quick Log card after photo capture with title field and six category chips; Item Detail with distinct archival modules; locked Value card teaser for free users; Preserved/Locked read-only list; Recently Deleted utility screen; Export Preview with paged document view; Paywall featuring a real redacted export preview. Include empty, loading, success, locked, incomplete, and error variants where relevant. Include motion notes, accessibility notes, and a component inventory suitable for SwiftUI implementation.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Let me think through this as the person who actually has to make the SwiftUI view hierarchy and state machine work, not just describe how it should look.

Start with navigation, because it determines everything else. This should be one `NavigationStack` at the app root with a typed path (`[CatalogRoute]` enum: `.itemDetail(Item.ID)`, `.preservedList`, `.recentlyDeleted`, `.exportPreview(ExportMode)`, `.settings`), plus a small number of sheets presented via `.sheet(item:)` bindings rather than path pushes: Capture/Quick Log, Add Condition Entry, Add Value Entry, Purchase Facts editor, and Paywall. The rule I'd lock in: anything that represents "drilling into the archive" is a push (detail, preserved list, recently deleted, export, settings); anything that's "a focused task you either finish or back out of" is a sheet. This matters because the Quick Log card has a real persistence side effect the moment the photo lands — if it's a push, backgrounding or swiping back mid-flow behaves differently than a sheet's interactive-dismiss gesture, and we already committed to "backgrounded mid-capture must resume the draft," so the sheet needs `.interactiveDismissDisabled(false)` (let them dismiss, the row already exists as a draft) but the dismiss action must not delete anything — it just closes the sheet on top of an already-saved incomplete item. I'd make this explicit in the handoff because it's exactly the kind of thing that's invisible in a Figma frame and only shows up as a bug in QA.

The App Lock gate is a full-screen cover, not a view in the stack — `.fullScreenCover(isPresented:)` driven by a scenePhase observer at the App level, evaluated before the NavigationStack ever renders its content, and the same scenePhase transition needs to drive the privacy-overlay obfuscation (a blur/redaction view) that sits even above the lock gate's dismissal animation, so there's no single frame where a screenshot mid-transition could leak a value or photo in the app switcher. I'd treat "privacy overlay" as its own tiny reusable view modifier applied at the root, not something each screen implements independently — if it's per-screen, someone will forget it on the Export Preview screen, which is exactly the screen most likely to have sensitive numbers visible when the user backgrounds mid-review.

On the effective-state model: this has to be a single pure function, `func effectiveState(for item: Item, subscriptionActive: Bool, activeItemIDs: Set<PersistentIdentifier>) -> ItemState`, computed at render time from three independent facts (deletedAt != nil, status enum, membership in the active-25 set), never stored. The dangerous version of this feature is one where each screen (list row, detail header, badge) independently checks "is this locked" and "is this incomplete" with its own if/else, because that's how the precedence rule (deleted > locked > incomplete) drifts out of sync between screens over one release cycle. I'd make this a single `ItemDisplayState` enum with associated data, computed once per item per render pass, and every view — list card, detail header, badge — switches over that one enum. The active/locked partition itself needs to be one query, not one check per item: fetch all non-deleted items sorted by creation order, take the first 25 as active, the rest as locked. If this gets implemented as "for each item, count how many earlier items exist" that's O(n²) on a catalog someone's spent years building, and it's the kind of perf cliff that only shows up after the app's already shipped and someone has 400 sneakers logged.

On the catalog list rendering itself: cover photos need to be pre-downsampled thumbnails written to disk at save time (via `byPreparingThumbnail`, off-main, per the async-image guidance), never the full-res capture decoded live in a `List` — with photo-forward cards as the primary content, a scroll-heavy catalog of 300+ items decoding full-res JPEGs on scroll will visibly stutter, and that failure mode directly undermines the "calm archive" feeling this whole design is built around. Thumbnail generation should happen once, get cached to a predictable file URL keyed by item ID, and the full-res original stays only for the cover-photo hero on Item Detail and for PDF export (where it needs its own separate downsample pass at export-time to the "max embed dimension" already locked in earlier phases — don't reuse the list thumbnail for export, it'll look soft blown up to a report page).

For the Quick Log card, the six category chips need to be laid out so Dynamic Type doesn't break them into an ugly wrap — I'd spec them as a horizontally-scrolling chip row at default sizes but explicitly allow (and design for) wrapping into two rows at accessibility text sizes, never truncation, since a truncated category name ("Sneak…") next to five others is worse than a two-row layout. Vision's suggested chip highlight should be a state on the chip itself (background tint + a small "Suggested" caption beneath, both removed the instant the user taps any chip manually), and that suggestion arriving asynchronously after the sheet is already visible means the chip row needs to animate the highlight in with a soft fade rather than an abrupt pop — jarring changes here read as "the app just did something behind my back," bad in a trust-first app.

Screen-by-screen, building directly on the locked screen inventory:

**Catalog Home** — states: first-launch empty (centered serif headline "Your Collection," a short line of body copy, one primary "Add Your First Item" button, no illustration — an empty state that's mostly whitespace fits the archival tone better than art); loading (a skeleton of 3-4 placeholder cards with shimmer suppressed — just static muted rectangles, since shimmer reads as "flashy" against this aesthetic); success (active cards + collapsed Preserved/Locked module beneath, toolbar with Add (leading… actually trailing, primary action) and an overflow menu holding Recently Deleted + Settings); error (SwiftData load failure — a centered icon-free message, "Your catalog couldn't be opened" plus a Retry button, and this state needs to be reachable in a preview/test harness deliberately, not just theoretical). Content hierarchy top to bottom: nav title "Provenance" (serif, large title style), active item cards, Preserved/Locked collapsed module, done — no collection-value summary text on this screen for now unless should-have summary numbers land later, since raw values here fight the privacy overlay's job.

**Capture/Quick Log** — states: permission-denied (a manual-entry fallback that skips straight to the finishing card with a placeholder photo icon slot, not a dead end); photo captured (finishing card populated); classification pending (chips render immediately, no suggestion yet — no spinner on the chips themselves, that's overkill for something silent); classification resolved (one chip highlights); low-confidence/unavailable (chips stay neutral, nothing implies failure); photo-write-failed (the item still saves as "incomplete," and the sheet shows a small inline notice — not a blocking alert — "Photo couldn't be saved, you can retry from the item" so Save is never blocked by a storage-layer failure).
