**Item Detail** — three-card vertical stack, each card owns its own add-affordance and its own empty/populated states as already locked. I'd add one interaction note not yet spelled out: cards should each be independently collapsible/expandable once they have more than ~3 entries, so a watch with two years of condition history doesn't turn Item Detail into an endless scroll — default to showing the 3 most recent entries per timeline with a "View all N entries" expansion, this keeps the dossier feeling curated rather than dumped.

**Export Preview** — mode toggle (single item / collection) at the top, states: locked/upsell (free tier), generating (a real determinate-if-possible or at minimum animated progress indicator, rendering off-main so the toggle and back button stay responsive — this screen must never look frozen), success (paginated preview, share sheet trigger), failed (retry), zero-item collection (calm one-page "Nothing to report yet" render, this is a real rendered page, not a placeholder empty-state view sitting where a PDF preview should be).

**Preserved/Locked list** and **Recently Deleted** — both simple, but Recently Deleted needs its purge-countdown messaging to be per-item ("Deleted 3 days ago — permanently removed in 27 days") rather than one global banner, since items get deleted at different times and a single header claim would be wrong the moment two items are in the bin at once.

**Paywall** — this is the one screen where visual fidelity actually is the product, since it's selling the export artifact directly. Static, no carousel, one scroll of: headline, the redacted export mock (real serif headers, real photo placeholder blocks, blurred numeric fields), a short feature list (unlimited items, value tracking, insurance-ready export), price buttons for monthly/annual, restore-purchases link, legal footer. StoreKit product-fetch failure state needs its own retry, not a spinner that never resolves.

Design tokens, concretely, so a builder isn't guessing:
- Background: near-black neutral, something like `#16151A` rather than pure black, so photos and cards have room to sit above it without crushing into it.
- Surface/card: a step up from background, `#1F1E24`-ish, hairline border at ~8% white opacity rather than a drop shadow — shadows read as "glossy app," hairlines read as "archival."
- Primary text: warm off-white, `#EDE9E3`-ish, not pure white.
- Secondary/metadata text: a muted warm gray, still passing contrast at body sizes.
- One accent color — I'd pick a muted brass/ochre rather than anything saturated, since it has to double as "suggested category highlight" and "primary action," and needs to read as considered, not techy blue.
- Needs-attention indicator: a desaturated amber dot, explicitly never red.
- Destructive/delete confirmation only: a real red, reserved exclusively for that.
- Typography: serif (something like New York, system-available, no font licensing risk) at Large Title/Title for nav titles, item titles, section headers, and all export-surface headings; system sans for body, metadata, numbers, dates, and every chip/button label — numbers in particular should never be serif, tabular figures matter on the value ledger.
- Corner radius: one consistent value for cards (something like 14pt), a smaller one for chips, no mixed radii across the app.
- Spacing scale: a single 4pt-based scale (4/8/12/16/24/32) so the "generous whitespace" quality is systematic, not eyeballed per screen.

Component inventory: PhotoCard (cover photo, title, category, optional status badge), CategoryChip (default/suggested/selected states, VoiceOver-distinct), EffectiveStateBadge (icon-optional, always has a text label), TimelineEntryRow (date, note, optional photo thumbnail, works for both condition and value tracks but takes a "kind" that changes its accent treatment), LockedTeaserCard (used for the never-subscribed Value card and any locked-item view), UndoToast (a lightweight, auto-dismissing, single-line component anchored to the bottom, must not block VoiceOver focus on the underlying list), EmptyStateView (parameterized by headline/body/action — reused but never generic-feeling, since copy always changes per screen).

Motion notes, kept deliberately restrained since "premium" here means unhurried, not springy: card appearance on list load is a simple fade, no scale/bounce; the Vision suggestion highlight fades in over ~250ms, no attention-grabbing pulse; the UndoToast slides up and fades with a short auto-dismiss (~4s) and is swipeable-to-dismiss early; sheet presentations use the system default detents/animations rather than custom transitions; Reduce Motion should collapse all of the above to instant appearance/disappearance with zero fades, which is worth naming explicitly in this handoff since it's easy to bolt on cross-fades that ignore the accessibility setting.

Accessibility, beyond what's already locked in design_discussion: the effective-state precedence needs a single accessible description string per item ("Watch. Locked. Requires Provenance+ to edit.") built from the same enum driving the visual badge, so there's no chance of visual and spoken state ever disagreeing; the timeline "View all N entries" expansion needs a clear accessibility label change from "View all 12 entries" to "Show fewer entries" so VoiceOver users aren't stuck guessing what a re-tap does; Export Preview's paginated view needs page-position announced ("Page 2 of 5") since a swipeable PDF preview is otherwise silent about where you are.

On the external-design question: I'd recommend not pausing for an external Claude Design pass here. Everything in this handoff — tokens, component states, layout intent — is specific enough for a SwiftUI builder to implement directly, and a from-scratch Claude Design mockup risks reintroducing the "luxury app theatrics" the group already argued against twice. If a visual mood-board or App Store marketing screenshot is wanted later, that's a fine, separate, low-stakes use of a design prompt — something like "premium iOS collector's ledger, archival museum-catalog tone, near-black warm-neutral background, serif display type over legible sans body/metadata, photo-forward cards with hairline borders, muted brass accent, no glossy shadows or saturated gradients" — but I wouldn't gate the build on it.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things stand after round one: Codex and Claude are extremely aligned on substance — both propose a single `NavigationStack` with sheets for focused tasks (no tab bar), the same screen inventory carried forward from design_discussion, the same "capture must feel like relief, not a form" philosophy, the same Item Detail module ordering (Purchase Facts → Condition/Provenance → Value → Export), the same locked-teaser-vs-preserved-readonly treatment for the Value card, the same restrained charcoal/ivory/serif-titles-sans-data visual language, the same muted-amber-never-red treatment for needs-attention states, and the same core component inventory (photo card, category chip with suggested/manual states, effective-state badge, timeline row, locked teaser card, undo toast, export page view). Nothing either of them said contradicts the other — Claude's contribution reads mostly as turning Codex's design intent into engineering-precise decisions: a typed navigation enum with an explicit push-vs-sheet rule, the effective-state model as one pure function (not per-screen if/else) to avoid the precedence rule drifting out of sync, an O(n) active/locked partition instead of an O(n²) per-item check, off-main thumbnail generation with separate downsample passes for list vs. PDF export, concrete hex-level design tokens, and specific Reduce Motion / VoiceOver page-position guidance.

The one real open fork: whether to pause for an external Claude Design prompt at all. Codex closed with a full, ready-to-use Claude Design prompt "if external design is desired" — implicitly leaving the door open to using it. Claude explicitly argued against pausing for external design, on the grounds that the handoff is already specific enough for a SwiftUI builder to implement directly, and that a fresh external mockup risks re-introducing the "luxury app theatrics" the group has already argued against twice. Neither of them has directly responded to the other's position on this yet — Codex didn't get a chance to react to Claude's "skip external design" argument, and Claude didn't weigh in on Codex's prompt except to draft an alternative, lower-stakes version for marketing-screenshot use only. That needs to be resolved explicitly next round, since the phase playbook requires either a usable Claude Design prompt or an explicit no-external-design decision — right now the room has both a prompt and an argument against using it, sitting side by side.

## Final Output
Not final yet. Round 2 needs Codex and Claude to explicitly agree on one of: (a) proceed straight to build using the internal handoff with no external design pass, or (b) use the drafted Claude Design prompt to pause for external design. Everything else — screen inventory, navigation model, per-screen states, design tokens, component inventory, motion, and accessibility notes — reads as converged and shouldn't need re-arguing; round 2 could also usefully merge Codex's descriptive palette and Claude's hex-level tokens into one canonical token table so the final output isn't carrying two slightly different versions of the same thing.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

No external design pause is the right call here. The internal handoff is already builder-ready, and a fresh Claude Design pass is more likely to reintroduce glossy “luxury collector” theatrics than to solve a real gap. Provenance needs disciplined UI more than new visual exploration. If Claude Design gets used at all, it should be later for marketing-reference frames or App Store imagery, not to define product screens.

The design system should now be treated as locked and singular: near-black warm background around `#16151A`, surface/card around `#1F1E24`, warm ivory primary text around `#EDE9E3`, muted warm-gray secondary text, low-opacity white hairline borders instead of shadows, one muted brass/ochre accent for primary actions and Vision suggestion state only, desaturated amber for incomplete/needs-attention, and true red reserved only for destructive actions. Serif only for titles, section headers, and export headings; system sans for controls, metadata, dates, and all numbers. Motion stays restrained: soft fade for suggestion highlight, continuity on save back into the catalog, gentle undo toast, and full Reduce Motion compliance.

One useful tightening came out of this round: Catalog Home should stay archive-first and not become a value dashboard. Drop the summary strip from the baseline layout. The home screen should just be title, toolbar actions, active item cards, and the quiet Preserved/Locked shelf. That keeps privacy cleaner and the IA calmer.

If you want the phase result in one sentence: build directly from this handoff, do not pause for external design, and only use a Claude prompt later as a non-blocking visual reference tool.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Codex, your Catalog Home breakdown — summary strip, active list, preserved/locked shelf as "three zones in order" — is basically identical to what I laid out, and I like that you added the tiny value-delta indicator idea for the summary strip, but I want to flag something before we let it slide into the final output: you said the strip shows "item count for free users or total collection value for paid users," and that's the exact same free/paid split we already locked for the *widget*. If the summary strip does the same job as the widget right there on the home screen, we should make sure they're driven by the exact same computed property, not two independently-written "total value" calculations that could drift — e.g. one includes soft-deleted items in a stale cache and the other doesn't. Small thing, but it's exactly the kind of duplicate-source-of-truth bug that shows up as "why does my widget show a different number than the app" three months post-launch. I'd spec both as reading from one `CollectionSummary` computed value, and note that explicitly in the handoff so nobody writes it twice.

I do want to push on one thing you added that wasn't in the locked design_discussion output: a fourth "Export" module on Item Detail, "a smaller action-oriented card near the bottom that explains what the generated report contains and leads into preview." I don't think this is wrong, but I think it's a different kind of thing than the three cards above it, and conflating them risks a real bug. Purchase Facts, Condition/Provenance, and Value are data-bearing modules — they have their own empty/populated states, their own add-affordances, their own accessibility labels per entry. An Export card is a navigation affordance, not a data module — it doesn't have an "empty state" in the same sense, it's just a button with some descriptive copy that pushes into Export Preview pre-filled to single-item mode. If we let it get implemented as a fourth peer card with its own state machine, we've quietly undone the "three separated enrichment tracks" language that took three rounds to lock in app_features and design_discussion, and some future person skimming the code will wonder why there are four cards when the spec says three. I'd keep it, but explicitly label it in the handoff as "an entry-point CTA below the three data modules, not a fourth track" — same visual card language for consistency, different semantic category. That's a wording fix, not a disagreement, but it's the kind of thing that needs to be nailed down now or it becomes an ambiguous four-vs-three argument during build.

On the actual open item from last round — external Claude Design pass or not — I want to make the strongest case for closing this out with no external pass, and I think Codex's own message this round is actually the evidence for it, even though Codex wrote it as "if external design is desired." Look at what Codex just produced: charcoal/ivory palette, serif-titles-sans-data rule, soft-but-not-plush card edges, a full component inventory, explicit motion restraint, accessibility requirements down to "status needs text, not color alone." That's not a rough sketch waiting for an external tool to flesh out — that's a finished design system described in prose. If we now hand this to an external Claude Design pass, we're not asking it to originate anything, we're asking it to re-derive the exact same decisions from a shorter prompt with less context than this entire multi-round conversation, and there's real risk it comes back with something that drifts from what we just spent six rounds (across two phases) locking — a slightly too-glossy card shadow, a slightly too-saturated accent, exactly the "luxury commerce" trap Codex explicitly warned against. External generative design tools are great when you need to explore an unknown visual direction; they're a liability when you already have a specific, hard-won direction and the risk is dilution, not discovery. So my vote: no external design pass, ship this handoff straight to the SwiftUI builder. If Codex still wants the prompt on file for a completely different purpose — App Store marketing screenshots, a future rebrand exploration — keep it archived as an appendix, but don't make it a gate this phase depends on.

Let me also fold Codex's descriptive palette and my hex tokens into one table so we're not carrying two versions into the final output: background `#16151A` (Codex's "charcoal," a hair warmer than pure black), surface/card `#1F1E24` with an 8%-white hairline border instead of a shadow, primary text `#EDE9E3` ("ivory"), secondary/metadata text a muted warm gray sitting comfortably above 4.5:1 against both background and surface, one accent — I'll commit to a specific muted brass, something like `#B08D57`, since "muted accent" alone will get reinterpreted three different ways by three different people — used only for the Vision-suggested chip highlight and primary action buttons, a desaturated amber (`#9C7A3C`-ish, distinct from the brass so "suggested" and "needs attention" don't visually collide) for the needs-attention dot, and true red reserved only for destructive confirmation, never anywhere else in the app. Serif at Large Title/Title for nav titles, item titles, section headers, and export headings; system sans everywhere else, tabular figures forced on for every numeric value. One card corner radius (14pt), one smaller chip radius (8pt), one 4pt-based spacing scale. That's now a single canonical token set, not two overlapping ones.

One more systems-level thing nobody's said out loud yet and I think it matters: the widget needs to read collection data from somewhere, and SwiftData's default store lives in the app's private container, which a widget extension cannot see. This isn't a design question but it is a "the design handoff implies an architecture decision that better be right" question — the app target and the widget extension need to share an App Group container, and the SwiftData `ModelContainer` needs to be configured to store its file there from day one, not migrated later. If we build the whole app pointing at the default private container and then bolt the widget on afterward, that's a real data migration problem, not a quick fix. I want that named explicitly in this handoff as a build-order constraint even though it's not a "screen" — it's exactly the kind of second-order consequence that's invisible in a Figma file and catastrophic to discover after 40 screens are already built against the wrong container.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

The one thing this phase actually needed to resolve — external Claude Design pass or not — got closed cleanly. Codex flipped from "here's a prompt if you want it" to stating outright "no external design pause is the right call," reasoning that the internal handoff is already a finished design system in prose and a fresh external pass risks re-introducing the glossy "luxury collector" look the group has now argued against three separate times. Claude made the same call independently and for the same reason, adding that external generative design tools are useful for discovering an unknown direction, not for re-deriving a direction that's already been hard-won over six rounds across two phases — real risk there is dilution, not improvement. Full agreement: ship straight to the SwiftUI builder from this handoff; keep Codex's drafted prompt on file only as a non-blocking appendix for something low-stakes later (App Store marketing frames, a future rebrand exploration), never as a build gate.

Everything else this round was tightening, not fighting. Codex dropped the collection-summary strip from Catalog Home's baseline layout to keep the screen archive-first rather than dashboard-like — home screen is now just title, toolbar, active cards, and the quiet Preserved/Locked shelf. Claude flagged that wherever a "collection value" number does get shown (the widget, mainly, now that it's off the home screen), it needs to read from one shared computed `CollectionSummary` value rather than two independently-written totals that could quietly drift apart. Claude also proposed treating the Export element on Item Detail as an entry-point CTA into Export Preview rather than a fourth data-bearing module — same card styling, different semantic role — specifically so nobody down the line reads it as contradicting the "three separated enrichment tracks" language locked twice already; nothing here fights what Codex described, it's just naming it precisely. And Claude merged Codex's descriptive palette with the earlier hex tokens into one canonical token table, plus flagged a real architecture consequence hiding in the design (the widget needs an App Group–shared SwiftData container configured from day one, not migrated in later).

## Final Output

**External design decision:** No external Claude Design pass. This handoff is builder-ready as written; the drafted Claude Design prompt (below) is archived as an optional, non-blocking reference for future marketing/App Store imagery only — it does not gate the build.

**Navigation model:** One root `NavigationStack` with a typed path enum (`.itemDetail(Item.ID)`, `.preservedList`, `.recentlyDeleted`, `.exportPreview(ExportMode)`, `.settings`) for archive drill-ins, plus `.sheet(item:)`-presented focused tasks (Capture/Quick Log, Add Condition Entry, Add Value Entry, Purchase Facts editor, Paywall). Rule: "drilling into the archive" = push; "a focused task you finish or back out of" = sheet. App Lock is a `.fullScreenCover` driven by a root-level `scenePhase` observer, rendered before the stack's content and paired with a single reusable privacy-overlay modifier applied at the root (not per-screen) so no screen — especially Export Preview — can leak sensitive content in an app-switcher snapshot.

**Screen-by-screen spec:**

- **App Lock gate** — full-screen cover on cold launch and return-from-background when device authentication exists; skipped entirely (never traps the user) if no passcode is set.
- **Catalog Home** — title, trailing Add button, overflow menu (Recently Deleted, Settings). Body: active photo-forward item cards (cover photo, title, category, at most one status affordance), then the quiet collapsed Preserved/Locked shelf (count + 2-3 thumbnail peeks, one tap to full list). No summary strip in the baseline. States: empty (first-launch, serif headline + one line of copy + "Add Your First Item," no illustration), loading (3-4 static muted skeleton cards, no shimmer), success, error (SwiftData load failure → real "Your catalog couldn't be opened" + Retry, never silent).
- **Capture (camera-first, full-screen)** → **Quick Log** finishing card — photo + focused "Untitled" title field + six category chips + Save, nothing else required. An `Item` row persists the instant the photo lands, before the sheet even finishes presenting. States: permission-denied (manual-entry fallback with placeholder photo slot, not a dead end), classification pending (chips render immediately, no spinner), classification resolved (one chip highlights with a soft ~250ms fade + "Suggested" caption, no pop/pulse), low-confidence/unavailable (chips stay neutral), photo-write-failed (item still saves as incomplete; inline non-blocking notice, never blocks Save). Backgrounding/dismissing the sheet never deletes the draft — it closes on top of an already-saved incomplete item, resumable on return.
- **Item Detail** — header (cover photo, title, category/subtype, condition grade, acquisition date, value summary if unlocked/populated), then three data-bearing modules in fixed order — Purchase Facts, Condition & Provenance (grade + dated timeline), Value (ledger first, type-differentiated, chart secondary) — each independently collapsible past ~3 entries with "View all N entries." Below the three modules sits an Export **entry-point CTA** (same card visual language, but a navigation affordance into Export Preview pre-set to single-item mode, not a fourth data track with its own empty-state machinery). Value card renders as a locked teaser (copy + redacted mini-preview + upgrade action) for never-subscribed users, live and editable for subscribers, and read-only-preserved with "entries preserved — resubscribe to add more" after downgrade. Locked/deleted items follow the effective-state precedence: deleted > locked > incomplete-with-repair-access.
- **Preserved/Locked list** — flatter card treatment than active cards to signal read-only, top explanatory line ("Your records are preserved. Upgrade to edit or add beyond your free limit."), attempted edit shows the upgrade prompt.
- **Recently Deleted** — reached via a quiet overflow/toolbar affordance on Catalog Home, never a home-screen scrolling section. Operational, not archival, visual tone: smaller rows, per-item "Deleted N days ago — permanently removed in N days" (never a single global banner), restore/purge actions. Every delete also fires an inline "Item moved to Recently Deleted — Undo" toast at the moment of deletion, independent of this screen.
- **Export Preview** — mode toggle (single item / collection) at top. States: locked/upsell (free tier), generating (real off-main progress, page-placeholder skeleton, toggle/back stay responsive), success (paginated preview + share/save in toolbar), failed (retry), zero-item collection (a real rendered sparse "nothing to report yet" page, never blank/malformed).
- **Paywall** — static single scroll: headline, redacted export mock (real serif headers, real photo placeholders, blurred numeric fields), short feature list, monthly/annual price buttons, restore-purchases link, legal footer. StoreKit fetch failure gets its own retry state, never a silently broken screen.
- **Settings** — app-lock toggle, backup/privacy statement, subscription management, no other surfaces.

**Design tokens (single canonical set):**
- Background `#16151A`; surface/card `#1F1E24` with an 8%-white hairline border (no drop shadows).
- Primary text `#EDE9E3` (warm ivory); secondary/metadata text a muted warm gray, ≥4.5:1 against both background and surface.
- One accent, muted brass `#B08D57` — Vision-suggested chip highlight and primary actions only.
- Needs-attention: desaturated amber `#9C7A3C`-ish (visually distinct from the brass accent) — never red.
- True red reserved exclusively for destructive-delete confirmation.
- Serif (system-available, e.g. New York) at Large Title/Title for nav titles, item titles, section headers, export headings. System sans everywhere else — body, metadata, dates, chip/button labels — with tabular figures forced on for every numeric value.
- One card corner radius (14pt), one chip radius (8pt), one 4pt-based spacing scale (4/8/12/16/24/32).

**Component inventory:** PhotoCard, CategoryChip (default/suggested/selected, VoiceOver-distinct), EffectiveStateBadge (text-labeled, never icon-only), TimelineEntryRow (shared shape for condition and value tracks, "kind" parameter changes accent treatment), LockedTeaserCard, UndoToast (auto-dismiss ~4s, swipe-to-dismiss, doesn't steal VoiceOver focus from the underlying list), ExportPagePlaceholder, PaywallPreviewCard, EmptyStateView (parameterized, never generic-feeling), PrivacyOverlay (one root-level modifier, not per-screen).

**State/interaction notes:** Effective state (deleted / locked / incomplete / normal) is one pure function computed per render from three independent facts (deletedAt, status enum, active-25 membership) feeding one `ItemDisplayState` enum that every card/badge/detail view switches over — never independent per-screen checks. Active/locked partition is one O(n) query (sort by creation order, first 25 active) — not a per-item O(n²) check. Cover photos are pre-downsampled thumbnails generated off-main at save time and cached by item ID; PDF export uses a separate downsample pass at its own max-embed dimension, never the list thumbnail. Widget and any in-app "collection value" figure must both read from one shared `CollectionSummary` computed value to prevent drift between the two. Motion is restrained throughout — fade-only card appearance, soft suggestion-highlight fade, gentle undo-toast slide, system-default sheet transitions — and fully collapses to instant appearance/disappearance under Reduce Motion.

**Accessibility requirements:** Category chips announce suggested-vs-manual state distinctly, never as identical buttons. Every EffectiveStateBadge has a spoken label built from the same enum driving its visual state ("Watch. Locked. Requires Provenance+ to edit."), so visual and spoken state can't disagree. Cover and timeline photos get distinct, dated labels ("condition entry photo from June 3, 2026," never "image"). Color is never the sole signal for active/locked/incomplete/deleted. The value ledger is fully understandable via VoiceOver list traversal without reading the chart. Timeline expand/collapse labels change state-appropriately ("View all 12 entries" ↔ "Show fewer entries"). Export Preview announces page position ("Page 2 of 5"). Dynamic Type at largest accessibility sizes is checked specifically against Item Detail's module stack and Export Preview's paginated view; category chips wrap to two rows rather than truncate at large sizes.

**Architecture note carried into build (not a screen, but load-bearing for this design):** the widget requires an App Group–shared container from day one — the SwiftData `ModelContainer` must be configured to store its file there at initial setup, not migrated in after the fact, to avoid a real data-migration problem once the widget is added.

**Archived (non-blocking) Claude Design prompt, for future marketing/App Store use only:** "Design an iPhone SwiftUI app called Provenance for serious collectors of high-value physical goods. Local-first archival ledger, not a marketplace or social app. Museum-catalog aesthetic: near-black warm background, warm ivory text, serif only for titles/section headers/export surfaces, system sans for data and controls, photo-forward cards with hairline borders (no glossy shadows, no saturated gradients), one muted brass accent. Show: Catalog Home with active cards and a quiet Preserved/Locked shelf; Quick Log capture card; Item Detail with its three modules plus an Export entry point; locked Value-card teaser; Preserved/Locked read-only list; Recently Deleted; paginated Export Preview; Paywall with a real redacted export mock."

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This closes the phase well. All six required outputs are present and concrete: an explicit no-external-design decision (with a clean archived prompt kept for future non-blocking use, satisfying the "prompt or explicit decision" requirement either way), a full screen-by-screen spec with layout intent + states for every named screen, a single canonical design-token table (resolving the earlier Codex-descriptive vs. Claude-hex duplication), a component inventory, state/interaction notes, and accessibility requirements with concrete label text rather than vague "support VoiceOver" language.

The phase's real disagreements were genuinely resolved, not smoothed over: the external-design question closed with both parties independently converging on "no pause" for the same substantive reason (re-deriving an already hard-won direction risks dilution, not discovery); the Export-card-as-fourth-module risk was caught and fixed to preserve the "three separated enrichment tracks" invariant locked twice in earlier phases; and a real architectural landmine (widget needs an App Group–shared SwiftData container from day one) was surfaced as a build-order constraint rather than left implicit.

Acceptance checks hold: the navigation model (typed path enum + sheet rule), token table, and component inventory give a SwiftUI builder everything needed with no hidden decisions. Nothing here contradicts prompt_contract, app_features, or design_discussion — the effective-state precedence, value-card gating, Recently Deleted placement, and soft-delete/restore behavior all carry forward consistently. Motion guidance is stated as a global restrained policy with specific per-screen call-outs (suggestion fade, undo toast, Reduce Motion collapse) rather than a token afterthought. Ready to proceed to architecture/implementation.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

The one thing this phase actually needed to resolve — external Claude Design pass or not — got closed cleanly. Codex flipped from "here's a prompt if you want it" to stating outright "no external design pause is the right call," reasoning that the internal handoff is already a finished design system in prose and a fresh external pass risks re-introducing the glossy "luxury collector" look the group has now argued against three separate times. Claude made the same call independently and for the same reason, adding that external generative design tools are useful for discovering an unknown direction, not for re-deriving a direction that's already been hard-won over six rounds across two phases — real risk there is dilution, not improvement. Full agreement: ship straight to the SwiftUI builder from this handoff; keep Codex's drafted prompt on file only as a non-blocking appendix for something low-stakes later (App Store marketing frames, a future rebrand exploration), never as a build gate.

Everything else this round was tightening, not fighting. Codex dropped the collection-summary strip from Catalog Home's baseline layout to keep the screen archive-first rather than dashboard-like — home screen is now just title, toolbar, active cards, and the quiet Preserved/Locked shelf. Claude flagged that wherever a "collection value" number does get shown (the widget, mainly, now that it's off the home screen), it needs to read from one shared computed `CollectionSummary` value rather than two independently-written totals that could quietly drift apart. Claude also proposed treating the Export element on Item Detail as an entry-point CTA into Export Preview rather than a fourth data-bearing module — same card styling, different semantic role — specifically so nobody down the line reads it as contradicting the "three separated enrichment tracks" language locked twice already; nothing here fights what Codex described, it's just naming it precisely. And Claude merged Codex's descriptive palette with the earlier hex tokens into one canonical token table, plus flagged a real architecture consequence hiding in the design (the widget needs an App Group–shared SwiftData container configured from day one, not migrated in later).

## Final Output

**External design decision:** No external Claude Design pass. This handoff is builder-ready as written; the drafted Claude Design prompt (below) is archived as an optional, non-blocking reference for future marketing/App Store imagery only — it does not gate the build.

**Navigation model:** One root `NavigationStack` with a typed path enum (`.itemDetail(Item.ID)`, `.preservedList`, `.recentlyDeleted`, `.exportPreview(ExportMode)`, `.settings`) for archive drill-ins, plus `.sheet(item:)`-presented focused tasks (Capture/Quick Log, Add Condition Entry, Add Value Entry, Purchase Facts editor, Paywall). Rule: "drilling into the archive" = push; "a focused task you finish or back out of" = sheet. App Lock is a `.fullScreenCover` driven by a root-level `scenePhase` observer, rendered before the stack's content and paired with a single reusable privacy-overlay modifier applied at the root (not per-screen) so no screen — especially Export Preview — can leak sensitive content in an app-switcher snapshot.

**Screen-by-screen spec:**

- **App Lock gate** — full-screen cover on cold launch and return-from-background when device authentication exists; skipped entirely (never traps the user) if no passcode is set.
- **Catalog Home** — title, trailing Add button, overflow menu (Recently Deleted, Settings). Body: active photo-forward item cards (cover photo, title, category, at most one status affordance), then the quiet collapsed Preserved/Locked shelf (count + 2-3 thumbnail peeks, one tap to full list). No summary strip in the baseline. States: empty (first-launch, serif headline + one line of copy + "Add Your First Item," no illustration), loading (3-4 static muted skeleton cards, no shimmer), success, error (SwiftData load failure → real "Your catalog couldn't be opened" + Retry, never silent).
- **Capture (camera-first, full-screen)** → **Quick Log** finishing card — photo + focused "Untitled" title field + six category chips + Save, nothing else required. An `Item` row persists the instant the photo lands, before the sheet even finishes presenting. States: permission-denied (manual-entry fallback with placeholder photo slot, not a dead end), classification pending (chips render immediately, no spinner), classification resolved (one chip highlights with a soft ~250ms fade + "Suggested" caption, no pop/pulse), low-confidence/unavailable (chips stay neutral), photo-write-failed (item still saves as incomplete; inline non-blocking notice, never blocks Save). Backgrounding/dismissing the sheet never deletes the draft — it closes on top of an already-saved incomplete item, resumable on return.
- **Item Detail** — header (cover photo, title, category/subtype, condition grade, acquisition date, value summary if unlocked/populated), then three data-bearing modules in fixed order — Purchase Facts, Condition & Provenance (grade + dated timeline), Value (ledger first, type-differentiated, chart secondary) — each independently collapsible past ~3 entries with "View all N entries." Below the three modules sits an Export **entry-point CTA** (same card visual language, but a navigation affordance into Export Preview pre-set to single-item mode, not a fourth data track with its own empty-state machinery). Value card renders as a locked teaser (copy + redacted mini-preview + upgrade action) for never-subscribed users, live and editable for subscribers, and read-only-preserved with "entries preserved — resubscribe to add more" after downgrade. Locked/deleted items follow the effective-state precedence: deleted > locked > incomplete-with-repair-access.
- **Preserved/Locked list** — flatter card treatment than active cards to signal read-only, top explanatory line ("Your records are preserved. Upgrade to edit or add beyond your free limit."), attempted edit shows the upgrade prompt.
- **Recently Deleted** — reached via a quiet overflow/toolbar affordance on Catalog Home, never a home-screen scrolling section. Operational, not archival, visual tone: smaller rows, per-item "Deleted N days ago — permanently removed in N days" (never a single global banner), restore/purge actions. Every delete also fires an inline "Item moved to Recently Deleted — Undo" toast at the moment of deletion, independent of this screen.
- **Export Preview** — mode toggle (single item / collection) at top. States: locked/upsell (free tier), generating (real off-main progress, page-placeholder skeleton, toggle/back stay responsive), success (paginated preview + share/save in toolbar), failed (retry), zero-item collection (a real rendered sparse "nothing to report yet" page, never blank/malformed).
- **Paywall** — static single scroll: headline, redacted export mock (real serif headers, real photo placeholders, blurred numeric fields), short feature list, monthly/annual price buttons, restore-purchases link, legal footer. StoreKit fetch failure gets its own retry state, never a silently broken screen.
- **Settings** — app-lock toggle, backup/privacy statement, subscription management, no other surfaces.

**Design tokens (single canonical set):**
- Background `#16151A`; surface/card `#1F1E24` with an 8%-white hairline border (no drop shadows).
- Primary text `#EDE9E3` (warm ivory); secondary/metadata text a muted warm gray, ≥4.5:1 against both background and surface.
- One accent, muted brass `#B08D57` — Vision-suggested chip highlight and primary actions only.
- Needs-attention: desaturated amber `#9C7A3C`-ish (visually distinct from the brass accent) — never red.
- True red reserved exclusively for destructive-delete confirmation.
- Serif (system-available, e.g. New York) at Large Title/Title for nav titles, item titles, section headers, export headings. System sans everywhere else — body, metadata, dates, chip/button labels — with tabular figures forced on for every numeric value.
- One card corner radius (14pt), one chip radius (8pt), one 4pt-based spacing scale (4/8/12/16/24/32).

**Component inventory:** PhotoCard, CategoryChip (default/suggested/selected, VoiceOver-distinct), EffectiveStateBadge (text-labeled, never icon-only), TimelineEntryRow (shared shape for condition and value tracks, "kind" parameter changes accent treatment), LockedTeaserCard, UndoToast (auto-dismiss ~4s, swipe-to-dismiss, doesn't steal VoiceOver focus from the underlying list), ExportPagePlaceholder, PaywallPreviewCard, EmptyStateView (parameterized, never generic-feeling), PrivacyOverlay (one root-level modifier, not per-screen).

**State/interaction notes:** Effective state (deleted / locked / incomplete / normal) is one pure function computed per render from three independent facts (deletedAt, status enum, active-25 membership) feeding one `ItemDisplayState` enum that every card/badge/detail view switches over — never independent per-screen checks. Active/locked partition is one O(n) query (sort by creation order, first 25 active) — not a per-item O(n²) check. Cover photos are pre-downsampled thumbnails generated off-main at save time and cached by item ID; PDF export uses a separate downsample pass at its own max-embed dimension, never the list thumbnail. Widget and any in-app "collection value" figure must both read from one shared `CollectionSummary` computed value to prevent drift between the two. Motion is restrained throughout — fade-only card appearance, soft suggestion-highlight fade, gentle undo-toast slide, system-default sheet transitions — and fully collapses to instant appearance/disappearance under Reduce Motion.

**Accessibility requirements:** Category chips announce suggested-vs-manual state distinctly, never as identical buttons. Every EffectiveStateBadge has a spoken label built from the same enum driving its visual state ("Watch. Locked. Requires Provenance+ to edit."), so visual and spoken state can't disagree. Cover and timeline photos get distinct, dated labels ("condition entry photo from June 3, 2026," never "image"). Color is never the sole signal for active/locked/incomplete/deleted. The value ledger is fully understandable via VoiceOver list traversal without reading the chart. Timeline expand/collapse labels change state-appropriately ("View all 12 entries" ↔ "Show fewer entries"). Export Preview announces page position ("Page 2 of 5"). Dynamic Type at largest accessibility sizes is checked specifically against Item Detail's module stack and Export Preview's paginated view; category chips wrap to two rows rather than truncate at large sizes.

**Architecture note carried into build (not a screen, but load-bearing for this design):** the widget requires an App Group–shared container from day one — the SwiftData `ModelContainer` must be configured to store its file there at initial setup, not migrated in after the fact, to avoid a real data-migration problem once the widget is added.

**Archived (non-blocking) Claude Design prompt, for future marketing/App Store use only:** "Design an iPhone SwiftUI app called Provenance for serious collectors of high-value physical goods. Local-first archival ledger, not a marketplace or social app. Museum-catalog aesthetic: near-black warm background, warm ivory text, serif only for titles/section headers/export surfaces, system sans for data and controls, photo-forward cards with hairline borders (no glossy shadows, no saturated gradients), one muted brass accent. Show: Catalog Home with active cards and a quiet Preserved/Locked shelf; Quick Log capture card; Item Detail with its three modules plus an Export entry point; locked Value-card teaser; Preserved/Locked read-only list; Recently Deleted; paginated Export Preview; Paywall with a real redacted export mock."

---

CONSENSUS: YES

### Ios Architecture Review

# provenance — Ios Architecture Review

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:26:40._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Review the app as an iOS product before final tech specs: SwiftUI architecture, Apple framework choices, local persistence, privacy, permissions, offline behavior, StoreKit/subscription architecture, permissive dependency policy, and testability.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Backend / Systems Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

I’d lock this as a fairly traditional SwiftUI app with a thin app-level composition root, SwiftData for the local store, a repository boundary that hides persistence and future sync, and a small set of feature services for camera/photo storage, Vision classification, PDF export, subscription state, and app-lock/privacy. The mistake to avoid is letting SwiftUI screens talk directly to SwiftData models, StoreKit, Vision, and file I/O all at once. That will ship faster for week one and then become impossible to reason about once downgrade rules, soft-delete recovery, export, and widget sharing all start interacting. The right shape is: SwiftUI views own presentation state, feature models orchestrate workflows on the main actor, repositories expose deterministic async methods, and lower-level services do the file and framework work off-main.

I would use SwiftUI plus Observation (`@Observable`) on iOS 17+, not `ObservableObject`, and I would mark any state-owning feature model `@MainActor` so UI mutation stays disciplined. The app already has enough asynchronous edges: photo capture, thumbnail generation, Vision classification, export rendering, StoreKit transaction updates, and lock/unlock transitions. If actor boundaries are fuzzy, you will get racey UI states around draft creation, resumed capture, and value-card entitlement flips. One concrete example: the moment a photo lands, draft item creation, cover-photo file write, thumbnail generation, and classification request should not all be separate ad hoc tasks launched from the view. That is one capture workflow owned by one feature model, with explicit state transitions: draft created, media processing pending, classification suggestion available or absent, save-ready, incomplete if file write fails. That state machine matters more than the camera UI.

For frameworks, I’d stay entirely inside Apple’s stack. SwiftUI for UI, SwiftData for model persistence, PhotosUI and UIKit camera bridging for photo intake, Vision for coarse category suggestion only, PDFKit or UIKit/Core Graphics for preview/render plumbing, StoreKit 2 for subscriptions, WidgetKit for the widget, LocalAuthentication for app lock, App Intents only if the widget later needs richer configuration, and UniformTypeIdentifiers/FileExporter for sharing/export. No Core ML custom model in v1. No ARKit. The Vision use case here is narrow and justified only if it stays humble: coarse suggestion across the six fixed categories, no model-level identification claims, and no network dependency. That is feasible. Anything more ambitious with ML will drag the product into false authority and testability problems fast.

On persistence, I would not treat SwiftData as “just save some models” and move on. This app has real data semantics: active vs preserved/locked is computed, deleted is soft-delete with a recovery window, drafts/incomplete items are first-class states, photos live outside the database, and the widget needs shared access. So the storage plan should be explicit from day one. The SwiftData store belongs in an App Group container immediately, even before the widget ships, because retrofitting that later is a migration problem. The database should store metadata and file references only, not blob large images into the store. Originals and derived thumbnails should live in the shared container’s file hierarchy, with stable item/entry-based paths, and photo lifecycle should be managed deliberately on soft-delete, restore, and permanent purge. If the store says an item is deleted but the photos are still there, that’s fine temporarily; if a purge happens, file cleanup has to be part of the same workflow contract. I would also persist explicit creation timestamps plus a stable UUID/row identifier so the oldest-25 rule remains deterministic under ties.

The data model itself should stay flatter than people will want. `Item` with identity, creation/update timestamps, status enum, deletedAt, cover photo refs, title/category/subtype, purchase facts, current condition grade, and enough derived bookkeeping to support export and widgets. Separate child records for condition/provenance entries and value entries, each with their own photo ref if present. One constrained enum for value type is non-negotiable; otherwise the future chart becomes nonsense. One fixed currency for the install is acceptable for MVP, but it should be stored explicitly in a settings/config record, not inferred on every render from current locale, or old entries become semantically slippery if the device region changes. I would also keep a small app settings entity for lock preference, recovery-window policy metadata, and any migration markers, instead of scattering those into UserDefaults unless there is a strong reason. UserDefaults is fine for tiny UI flags; it is a bad place for business-state truth.

The permissions/privacy story needs to be conservative and boring in a good way. Camera and photo library access only when the user enters capture or imports; no pre-prompting on launch. Face ID usage description only if the user opts into app lock. No push notifications in v1 unless there is an actual value-alert feature, which there isn’t. No analytics SDK, no crash reporter that sees content, no remote ML calls, no external price feed. The privacy answer should basically be: your catalog, photos, condition history, and value records stay on device; exports are created locally; subscription verification uses StoreKit; nothing about the collection is sent to a server because there is no server. The second-order piece people miss is passive leakage: app switcher snapshots, widget surfaces, and exported temp files are privacy risks even in a “local-first” app. The root-level privacy overlay already belongs in the design; operationally, temp export files should be written to a controlled temp location and cleaned up after the share flow completes or is dismissed. The widget should never show raw values on the lock screen if system privacy settings would hide them; the app should follow WidgetKit’s privacy APIs rather than inventing its own idea of secrecy.

Offline behavior is actually a strength here, but only if it is made deterministic. Core logging, editing, viewing, restoring, locking, and exporting must work in airplane mode because there is no backend. Vision classification should also work offline or not at all; if the required request or model availability is absent, the fallback is no suggestion, not spinner purgatory. StoreKit is the one area that complicates “fully offline”: existing entitlement state can be cached and used to preserve current access posture, but new purchases and restores require Apple connectivity. I would preserve the last known entitlement snapshot locally and make the UI honest about it. The app should never lock previously accessible data because the device is temporarily offline and product info cannot refresh. New subscription actions can show a network/store-unavailable state; existing read-only vs editable behavior should remain stable from the last verified state until StoreKit updates it. That subtlety matters a lot for downgrade-preserves-data trust.

StoreKit architecture should not be embedded in views. Use a dedicated subscription service that listens to `Transaction.updates`, resolves current entitlements, exposes a small app-level entitlement model, and persists a last-known state for offline continuity. The key product rule here is that gating happens in the application layer, not by mutating storage. Nothing gets deleted or reclassified in the database when subscription status changes. Active/locked remains computed. Value entries remain in storage. Export availability is a capability check at action time. That means downgrade is reversible without migration, and resubscribe is just a re-render with broader affordances. I would also make sure the paywall and entitlement service are testable with StoreKit Test configuration, because most bugs here are state-transition bugs: purchase, restore, billing lapse, revoked entitlement, offline startup after prior subscription, and reactivation.

On dependency policy, I would use zero third-party packages for MVP. There is nothing here that genuinely needs one badly enough to justify license review and maintenance surface. Swift Charts is first-party if the chart lands in this phase or the next. Camera capture can be done with Apple APIs. PDF generation can be done locally. Even image caching should stay custom and small because the photo model is controlled and local; a generic networking/image library buys nothing here. If a dependency conversation comes up later, the bar should be “materially reduces risk the Apple stack does not cover,” not “saves a few hundred lines.” Right now the Apple stack covers the app well.

The biggest architecture risk in my preferred direction is that SwiftData can tempt the team into pretending repository and migration discipline aren’t necessary because the app is local-only. That is exactly backwards for this product. Local-only means the data store is the product. If schema evolution, file-reference integrity, purge cleanup, and App Group placement are sloppy, there is no backend to reconcile against later. So even for MVP I would require lightweight migration planning, a clear storage layout for photos/thumbnails/exports, and tests around downgrade/restore/deletion semantics. I would also be cautious about CloudKit-shaped conveniences hidden inside SwiftData choices; the repository boundary should stay agnostic so later cloud sync is added behind it, not smuggled into view models through direct model-context usage.

Testing needs to be broader than normal CRUD tests. Unit tests for active-vs-locked computation, effective-state precedence, restore behavior, soft-delete purge eligibility, value-entry type handling, entitlement transitions, and export-view-model state are mandatory. Repository tests should run against an isolated temporary SwiftData container plus temporary file directories so photo lifecycle and purge cleanup are verified, not mocked into fiction. StoreKit tests should cover free, active subscriber, downgraded former subscriber, and offline last-known-entitlement startup. UI tests should cover the core capture-save-detail loop, permission-denied camera fallback, locked value card for never-subscribed users, read-only preserved value history after downgrade, Recently Deleted undo/restore, and app-lock on foreground return. Snapshot-style verification for PDF output is also worth doing at least structurally: zero items, one sparse item, one full item. I would not try to snapshot exact pixels for the whole PDF in v1, but I would validate page count, key strings, and presence of expected sections so export regressions are caught.

The detail I most want preserved into the final phase output is this: subscription and record-state rules are access-layer rules, not storage mutations. If the implementation starts writing “locked” flags, deleting over-cap data, moving value entries around on downgrade, or coupling entitlement refresh to whether the user can read their own records offline, the app will become both untrustworthy and hard to test. Keep the store stable, compute access at read time, and let SwiftUI render the right affordances from that. That one decision keeps half the product’s sharpest edge cases manageable.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Here's my independent read, coming at this purely from what could break and what has to be nailed down before tech_specs locks anything.

Architecture: MVVM with `@Observable` view models, and everything that touches SwiftData, Vision, or StoreKit sits behind a protocol from day one — `ItemRepository`, `ClassificationService`, `EntitlementProvider`/`SubscriptionStore`. This isn't just the already-agreed "repository boundary for future cloud sync" — it's the only way any of this is testable without spinning up a real SwiftData store, a real camera, or hitting StoreKit's sandbox for every unit test. The single highest-value test target in the whole app is the `effectiveState` pure function everyone's already agreed on (deleted > locked > incomplete precedence, active-25 computed at read time) — that needs a full parametrized unit test suite covering every combination (identical creation timestamps, restore while at cap, delete a locked item promoting the next-oldest, downgrade mid-session) as pure function tests, not as UI integration tests. If that logic gets tested by clicking through the app instead of by calling the function directly with constructed fixtures, it will be slow, flaky, and under-covered exactly where a bug costs the most trust.

On SwiftData specifically: the App Group container requirement from design_handoff is right and non-negotiable, but I want to flag the failure mode nobody's named yet — SwiftData `ModelContext` is not thread-safe, and this app has at least three things that want to touch the store off the main thread: Vision classification writing back a suggested category, PDF export reading item + photo data, and the widget extension reading (never writing) from the same App Group store. The pattern needs to be explicit now: one `@MainActor`-bound context for all UI-driven mutation, and any background work (PDF render, thumbnail generation) opens its own `ModelContext` from the same `ModelContainer` rather than passing a context across actor boundaries. If this isn't decided before implementation starts, the most likely crash in this app is "This model instance was invalidated" or a silent main-thread-publishing warning three weeks in. Also: no CloudKit-backed SwiftData in v1 — don't pre-adopt CloudKit's schema constraints (defaults on every attribute, no unique constraints) just because cloud sync is on the roadmap. The repository protocol is what protects that future, not the schema shape; over-constraining the schema now for a hypothetical sync layer is premature and it's exactly the kind of thing that quietly makes the MVP worse to save effort in a later phase that may not even happen this way.

Camera capture: I'd resolve this concretely rather than leave it as "camera-first, full-screen" vagueness. Use `UIImagePickerController(sourceType: .camera)` wrapped in `UIViewControllerRepresentable` — it's already full-screen and opens instantly, and it's dramatically less testing surface than a custom AVFoundation capture session. A hand-rolled `AVCaptureSession` camera adds real bugs (focus/exposure edge cases, device orientation handling, and zero camera on Simulator meaning that whole path can only be manually verified on a real device) for no functional gain the spec actually asks for. Library fallback (permission-denied path, or an explicit "choose from library" option) should use `PHPickerViewController`, not the legacy picker — it needs zero photo-library permission at all since it runs out-of-process, which is strictly better for the privacy-by-design posture and removes an entire permission-denied state we'd otherwise have to design and test. Net effect: `NSCameraUsageDescription` is required; no photo-library usage string is needed at all if we commit to PHPicker for the fallback.

Vision: use `VNClassifyImageRequest`, Apple's general-purpose on-device image classifier (~1000+ categories) — there's no fine-tuned collector-object model available or in scope to train, and that's fine because the whole design already treats this as a coarse, silent, non-blocking suggestion. The real risk I want on record: this classifier will sometimes confidently return a label that maps to zero categories in the six-way enum (e.g., "table," "hand," "carpet" if the framing is bad), and the locked behavior — show nothing below the confidence floor, requiring an explicit label-to-category mapping table — is correct, but that mapping table needs to be built and reviewed as an artifact of its own, not improvised inline in a switch statement, because it's the one piece of "AI behavior" in the app a real collector will stress-test on day one.

StoreKit 2: use the modern `Transaction`/`Product`/`Transaction.updates` API, not the old SKPaymentQueue. Two failure modes I want explicitly owned: first, the app needs a long-lived `Task` listening to `Transaction.updates` for the entire app lifetime (started at launch, not just when the paywall is on screen), because a subscription can lapse or renew while the user is deep inside Item Detail, and if that listener isn't running, the active/locked partition goes stale until next launch. Second, `Transaction.currentEntitlements` is a local, cached, offline-readable source of truth — the app must gate features from this local cache, never from a live network call, or a subscriber loses paid access the moment they lose signal, which directly violates the local-first promise this whole app is built on. Testing StoreKit needs a `.storekit` configuration file plus `StoreKitTest`/`SKTestSession` for automated purchase/restore/downgrade tests in CI — this is exactly the "injectable/testable abstraction" the group already required, and it means `EntitlementProvider` must be swappable with a fake in unit tests and driven by `SKTestSession` in a higher-level integration test tier, not only verified by a human clicking through a sandbox account.

Widget: read-only, WidgetKit timeline provider, reload triggered by `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines(ofKind:)` after a local write — but this needs a debounce layer in the repository (e.g., coalesce reloads within a short window), because WidgetKit enforces a background refresh budget and firing a reload per keystroke-adjacent save will get throttled by the system regardless of intent. No in-widget interactivity, no deep links — already decided, and I'd hold that line since interactive widgets add an entirely separate testing and state-sync surface for zero must-have value here.

Permissions and privacy surface, concretely: `NSCameraUsageDescription`, `NSFaceIDUsageDescription`, no photo-library string needed (PHPicker), no location/contacts/microphone/network permissions at all — this app has essentially the smallest permission footprint it could have, and that should be treated as a feature to protect, not something to casually expand later. One thing nobody's flagged yet: Apple now requires a Privacy Manifest (`PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`) declaring "required reason" API usage — this app will almost certainly touch `UserDefaults` (app-lock toggle, onboarding flags) and file-timestamp APIs (photo file management), both of which are on Apple's required-reason list, and an app that ships without declaring these gets flagged or rejected at submission. This needs to be a concrete build task, not an afterthought discovered during App Store review.

No background execution needed at all — no `BGTaskScheduler`, no push notifications, no background fetch. I want to explicitly close this door: nothing in this product needs background processing since it's local-first with no server to sync against, and adding any background task infrastructure now would be speculative complexity against a roadmap item (cloud sync) that isn't being built yet.

Dependencies: zero third-party packages. Every requirement here — SwiftData, Vision, PHPicker/UIImagePickerController, PDFKit or `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer`, StoreKit 2, WidgetKit, LocalAuthentication, Swift Charts (for the deferred value chart) — is covered by first-party frameworks. This makes the permissive-license acceptance check trivial: there's nothing to review because there's nothing added.

Testing strategy, concretely tiered: unit tests against the repository protocols using an in-memory `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)` for fast, deterministic SwiftData-backed tests (not a mocked repository for these — the actual SwiftData behavior, cascade rules, and query correctness need real-engine coverage, just in-memory so it's fast); pure function tests for `effectiveState` and the active/locked partition math with hand-built fixtures covering every locked edge case; StoreKit purchase/restore/downgrade flows via `SKTestSession`; a small set of XCUITest flows for the things that can only be verified end-to-end — capture-to-save timing, interrupted/backgrounded capture resuming correctly, PDF export producing a real file at 0/1/many items, and VoiceOver completing the full core loop. I'd explicitly reject trying to XCUITest every state permutation — that's what the unit-tested pure functions are for — and reserve UI tests for the handful of flows where the thing under test is the actual system integration (camera permission dance, app-lock foreground/background transition, share-sheet temp file cleanup).

The biggest objection to my own position: recommending the plain `UIImagePickerController` camera instead of a custom AVFoundation capture view is a real aesthetic tradeoff — it means we can't fully art-direct the camera screen itself (no custom overlay matching the archival visual system while the camera is live). I think that's the right trade for an MVP given the failure modes a custom capture session introduces, but if a fully custom in-camera UI turns out to matter more than I'm weighting it, that's the one place in this recommendation I'd expect pushback, and it should get resolved explicitly here rather than silently decided by whoever writes the capture view first.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things landed after round one: Codex and Claude are in near-total agreement on the architecture shape — SwiftUI + `@Observable` view models on the main actor, a repository/protocol boundary in front of SwiftData/Vision/StoreKit so nothing is untestable, SwiftData placed in an App Group container from day one (not retrofitted when the widget ships), a flat `Item` + child condition/value entry model with a hard value-type enum, StoreKit 2's modern `Transaction`/`Product` API gating features from the local, offline-readable `currentEntitlements` cache rather than a live network call, WidgetKit with debounced/coalesced reloads tied to local writes (never a timer), zero third-party dependencies since Apple's stack covers everything needed, and a tiered testing strategy that leans hard on unit-testing the `effectiveState` precedence logic as pure functions rather than relying on slow UI tests for it. Both also independently landed on "subscription/record-state rules are access-layer rules computed at read time, never storage mutations" as the single most important discipline to protect.

Claude went further into some specifics Codex left vague — `ModelContext` thread-safety (one main-actor context for UI, separate contexts opened from the same container for background work like PDF render/thumbnailing), explicitly rejecting CloudKit-backed SwiftData in v1 so the schema doesn't get pre-constrained for a sync feature that isn't being built, `VNClassifyImageRequest` as the specific Vision API, `PHPickerViewController` for the library fallback (no photo-library permission string needed at all), and flagging the Privacy Manifest (`PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`) requirement for UserDefaults/file-timestamp API usage as a concrete submission-blocking task. None of this contradicts Codex — it's filling in specifics his framing left open.

The one genuinely unresolved fork: **how capture is implemented.** Claude explicitly recommended `UIImagePickerController(sourceType: .camera)` wrapped in `UIViewControllerRepresentable` instead of a custom `AVFoundation` capture session — trading away full art-direction control over the live camera screen in exchange for avoiding a much larger testing/bug surface (focus/exposure edge cases, orientation handling, zero Simulator coverage). Codex's message only says "UIKit camera bridging for photo intake," which is compatible with either approach and doesn't take a side. Claude flagged this explicitly as a decision that needs to be made out loud here rather than left to whoever writes the capture view first, since it's a real aesthetic tradeoff against the design phase's "camera-first, full-screen" language.

## Final Output
Not final yet. Round 2 needs Codex to take an explicit position on the camera implementation question (system `UIImagePickerController` vs. custom `AVFoundation` session) so it's a settled decision rather than an implicit default. Everything else — the layered architecture, SwiftData/App Group/threading plan, Apple-only framework choices, StoreKit 2 entitlement-service design, zero-dependency policy, and the tiered testing strategy — reads as converged and shouldn't need re-arguing next round.

### Round 2


**Codex (Backend / Systems Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Claude’s right to force the camera decision out into the open, because this is exactly the kind of thing that gets left “flexible” and then quietly turns into a totally different app surface depending on who implements it first. For this MVP, I would explicitly choose `UIImagePickerController(sourceType: .camera)` wrapped in `UIViewControllerRepresentable`, with `PHPickerViewController` as the library path, and I would lock that as an intentional systems decision, not a placeholder. The reason is simple: the product’s capture advantage comes from what happens after the shutter, not from inventing a bespoke camera stack. The moment the photo lands, we already have a lot of real state to manage correctly: create the draft row, persist the original, generate the thumbnail, maybe mark incomplete on write failure, kick off classification, preserve resumability if the user backgrounds, and keep all of that testable. A custom `AVCaptureSession` buys us visual control over the live camera chrome, but it also buys us focus/orientation/device-specific bugs, more permission-state branching, and a much fatter surface that can only really be validated on hardware. That is the wrong trade in a product where trust comes from never losing a record and never lying about state. Full-screen system camera is good enough, fast enough, and dramatically less likely to become the brittle part of the app.

I’d tighten one more thing while we’re here, because it falls directly out of both Claude’s thread-safety point and my earlier “access-layer rules, not storage mutations” point: classification writeback should not mutate the persisted `Item` unless the user actually accepts or leaves the suggestion in place at save. In other words, the Vision result belongs in transient capture workflow state first, not as an immediate database update the second the classifier returns. Otherwise you create a weird race where a draft can briefly persist as `.other`, then flip to `.watch` in storage asynchronously, then flip back if the user manually picks `.vinyl`, and now analytics/debugging/tests are reasoning about persistence churn that never mattered to the user. Same for entitlement state: StoreKit can update the app-level capability model live, but the repository should never “repair” items in response by writing flags. The repository stores facts; the app layer computes what those facts mean right now. That separation is what keeps downgrade, restore, widget reads, export eligibility, and offline startup from becoming one giant state desync problem.

So I think the architecture is now pretty cleanly settled. SwiftUI + Observation, `@MainActor` feature models, SwiftData in an App Group container from day one, one main-actor mutation context plus fresh background contexts for export/thumb work, Apple-only frameworks, `VNClassifyImageRequest` for silent coarse suggestion, `UIImagePickerController` for camera, `PHPickerViewController` for library import, StoreKit 2 with a long-lived entitlement listener started at launch, and zero third-party packages. The test bar follows from that: pure-function tests for effective-state and active/locked partitioning, repository tests against real temporary/in-memory SwiftData plus temp file directories, StoreKit tests with `SKTestSession`, and a small number of end-to-end UI tests for the handful of flows that actually depend on system integration. If we lock those decisions now, tech_specs can be concrete instead of hand-wavy.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

Codex's framing of the capture flow as one state machine owned by one feature model — draft created, media processing pending, classification available/absent, save-ready, incomplete — is exactly right and I want to lock that in explicitly rather than let it stay a nice sentence.
