The failure mode if this isn't one owned state machine is a real one: if draft creation, cover-photo write, thumbnail generation, and the Vision request all get fired as independent tasks from the view (which is the natural thing to do if you're moving fast), you get races where, say, the thumbnail finishes after the user has already tapped Save and navigated away, or the classification result arrives and tries to mutate a chip on a view that's already been dismissed. One feature model, one set of explicit transitions, is the only way "resumed draft after force-quit" behaves the same whether the interruption happened before or after any of those sub-tasks completed. I'd want this called out as a concrete deliverable in tech_specs — a diagram or enum of that state machine — not left as an implied consequence of "use good architecture."

I also want to explicitly agree with two things Codex added that I didn't cover in round one and think are load-bearing: photos living in the file system with stable item/entry-keyed paths rather than blobbed into SwiftData, and a small dedicated settings/config entity for things like fixed currency and lock preference instead of letting business-state truth leak into UserDefaults. On the currency point specifically — if we re-derive currency from `Locale.current` on every render instead of stamping it once at install into a settings record, a value entry logged before a user travels or changes their region setting becomes ambiguous after the fact ("was this $400 or £400 when it was logged?"). That's a real trust bug in a product whose entire pitch is dated, defensible records. Storing it once, explicitly, closes that off completely instead of leaving it as an inference every time the screen renders.

Now, the thing the coordinator flagged as the actual open item: camera capture implementation. Codex, your message says "UIKit camera bridging for photo intake," which is genuinely compatible with either `UIImagePickerController` or a custom `AVCaptureSession`, so it hasn't actually taken a side — and I think this needs to close this round, not slide into tech_specs as an implicit default that whoever writes the capture view decides unilaterally. I'm going to push hard for `UIImagePickerController(sourceType: .camera)` and explain why I don't think the counterargument holds up under real scrutiny. The only real reason to build a custom `AVCaptureSession` view here is to art-direct the live camera screen itself — a custom overlay, brass-accent framing guides, a styled capture button matching the archival system. But the design_handoff language is "camera-first, full-screen," and `UIImagePickerController`'s camera mode already satisfies that literally — it's already full-screen, already opens instantly, already handles flash, HEIC, orientation, and low-light behavior that Apple has spent a decade tuning. Every one of those things becomes our bug surface the moment we roll our own capture session, and the cruelest part is that a good chunk of it — orientation handling in particular — is only testable on a real device, since Simulator has no camera at all either way. So the "we can't fully test AVFoundation on Simulator" problem doesn't go away by choosing AVFoundation, it just moves the burden of getting orientation/exposure/focus right from Apple's decade of tuning onto our few weeks of MVP build time. Given the global rule to prefer a smaller finished app over a larger half-built one, and given that nothing in the locked product spec asks for in-viewfinder decoration, I think this is a clean call: system picker for camera, `PHPickerViewController` for library fallback, and I'd like Codex to say so explicitly so this doesn't quietly get re-litigated by whoever writes ContentView.

One more concrete risk I want to put on the table before this phase closes, because it directly threatens a requirement we already locked in design_handoff: "Export Preview announces page position ('Page 2 of 5')." The natural instinct is to generate the PDF with `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer` (right choice, off-main, gives full control over layout and the max-embed photo dimension) and then just drop the resulting file into a `PDFKit` `PDFView` for the in-app preview, since `PDFView` gives you paging for free. But `PDFView`'s built-in accessibility support for announcing page position to VoiceOver is notoriously unreliable — it's a UIKit-bridged scroll view under the hood and doesn't reliably expose "page 2 of 5" semantics the way a native SwiftUI paged view would. If we build the preview screen assuming `PDFView` just handles this, we'll ship an Export Preview that fails the exact accessibility requirement the design phase called out by name, and it'll only surface during a VoiceOver pass late in the build. I'd resolve this now: use `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer` to generate the actual shareable/saveable PDF file (that's the real export artifact, unaffected by how we preview it), but render the in-app preview as a paged SwiftUI view over rasterized page images (or `PDFDocument.page(at:)` thumbnails) inside something like a `TabView(.page)` or a custom paged scroll view where we control the accessibility announcement directly, rather than embedding `PDFView` and hoping its VoiceOver behavior is good enough. That's a small implementation decision with an outsized consequence if it's left ambiguous, so I want it written down as a decision now rather than discovered as a bug later.

Everything else here reads fully converged to me — the App Group/SwiftData threading model, the repository/service boundary, StoreKit 2's offline-safe entitlement caching, zero third-party dependencies, WidgetKit's debounced reload, and the tiered testing strategy all match between Codex and me with no daylight left. If Codex takes the camera-picker position explicitly and doesn't object to the PDF-generation-vs-preview split, I think this phase is done.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

Round two closed the loop cleanly. Codex took an explicit, unhedged position on the camera question — system `UIImagePickerController(sourceType: .camera)` for capture, `PHPickerViewController` for the library fallback — for the same reason Claude argued: the product's edge is in what happens after the shutter fires, not in owning a bespoke camera stack, and a custom `AVCaptureSession` trades away Apple's decade of tuned focus/exposure/orientation handling for bugs that are only testable on real hardware anyway (Simulator has no camera either way). Both landed in the same place independently, so this is now a settled, named decision rather than something left for whoever writes the capture view first.

Codex also tightened something that falls directly out of his own "access-layer rules, not storage mutations" principle: Vision's classification result should live in transient capture-workflow state, not get written to the persisted `Item` the instant the classifier returns — otherwise a draft could flicker through multiple stored categories before the user ever taps Save, turning something that should be ephemeral UI state into noisy, hard-to-reason-about persistence churn. Claude explicitly agreed and folded it into the same "one feature model owns the whole capture state machine" discipline already on the table, adding that this state machine (draft created → media pending → suggestion available/absent → save-ready → incomplete) should be a named, diagrammed deliverable in tech_specs, not just an implied consequence of "good architecture."

Claude raised one new risk this round that Codex didn't get a chance to react to, but it doesn't conflict with anything he said — it's a specific implementation call inside territory both already agreed on (off-main PDF rendering, accessible export preview): don't use `PDFKit`'s `PDFView` for the in-app export preview, because its VoiceOver page-position announcement is unreliable, which would quietly violate the "Page 2 of 5" accessibility requirement locked back in design_handoff. The fix — generate the real shareable file with `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer`, but render the in-app preview as a paged native SwiftUI view (e.g. `TabView(.page)` over rasterized page images) where the accessibility announcement is fully controlled — is a refinement of an already-agreed plan, not a new fork.

Everything else — the App Group/SwiftData container plan with per-context thread separation, the repository/service protocol boundary, StoreKit 2's offline-safe entitlement caching with a launch-time listener, zero third-party dependencies, WidgetKit's debounced reload, photos-on-disk with a dedicated settings entity for currency/lock-preference, and the tiered testing strategy — was already aligned and nothing here reopened it.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture recommendation:** MVVM-style, with `@Observable` (Observation framework) feature models marked `@MainActor` owning all UI-facing state — no `ObservableObject`/`@Published`. Views never talk directly to SwiftData, Vision, StoreKit, or the file system; everything routes through a small set of protocol-defined boundaries (`ItemRepository`, `ClassificationService`, `EntitlementProvider`) so every layer above storage/frameworks is testable with fakes. Capture is modeled as one explicit state machine owned by a single feature model — draft created → media processing pending → classification suggestion available/absent → save-ready → incomplete-on-failure — rather than several independent tasks fired from the view; this must be a named, diagrammed deliverable in tech_specs, not an implied side effect. Vision's classification result lives in transient capture-workflow state and is only written to the persisted `Item` if it's still the selected category at save time — it never causes an interim database write-then-flip. Subscription/record-state rules (active/locked/incomplete/deleted) are computed at read time from stored facts; nothing about entitlement or cap status is ever written back into the store as a mutation.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI + Observation, SwiftData (App Group–backed container from day one, not retrofitted for the widget later), `UIImagePickerController(sourceType: .camera)` wrapped in `UIViewControllerRepresentable` for capture (explicitly chosen over a custom `AVCaptureSession` — the product's advantage is in what happens after the photo lands, not in art-directing the live camera chrome, and a custom capture session trades Apple's tuned focus/exposure/orientation handling for a bug surface only verifiable on real hardware), `PHPickerViewController` for library fallback (no photo-library permission needed at all), `VNClassifyImageRequest` for silent, coarse, confidence-floored category suggestion, `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer` for generating the actual shareable/saveable PDF (off-main, with a defined max photo-embed dimension), a native SwiftUI paged view (e.g. `TabView(.page)` over rasterized page images, not embedded `PDFKit`/`PDFView`) for the in-app Export Preview so VoiceOver page-position announcements ("Page 2 of 5") are fully controllable, StoreKit 2's modern `Transaction`/`Product` API with a long-lived `Transaction.updates` listener task started at app launch, WidgetKit with debounced/coalesced reload triggers on local writes, LocalAuthentication for app-lock, and Swift Charts reserved for the (deferred) value chart. No ARKit, no custom/trained Core ML model, no background execution APIs (no `BGTaskScheduler`, no push) — none of this product's scope needs them.

**Persistence and local data plan:** SwiftData store lives in an App Group container from initial setup. One main-actor `ModelContext` handles all UI-driven mutation; background work (PDF export, thumbnail generation) opens its own fresh `ModelContext` from the same `ModelContainer` rather than sharing a context across actor boundaries. No CloudKit-backed SwiftData in v1 — the repository protocol, not a CloudKit-shaped schema, is what protects the future sync roadmap. Data model stays flat: `Item` (identity, timestamps, status enum, deletedAt, cover-photo reference, title/category/subtype, purchase facts, current condition grade) plus child condition/provenance entries and value entries (each with a hard, constrained value-type enum and their own optional photo reference). Photos and thumbnails live on disk with stable item/entry-keyed paths, never blobbed into the database; a dedicated small settings/config entity stores fixed currency and lock preference explicitly at install time rather than re-deriving from `Locale.current` on every render, since re-derivation would make historical value entries ambiguous after a region change.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** `NSCameraUsageDescription` and `NSFaceIDUsageDescription` only — no photo-library usage string needed given the PHPicker choice. No location, contacts, microphone, or network permissions. A Privacy Manifest (`PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`) declaring required-reason API usage (UserDefaults, file-timestamp APIs) is a concrete, tracked build task, not an afterthought discovered at submission. Root-level privacy overlay on `scenePhase` transitions, temp export files cleaned up after the share flow completes/dismisses, and widget/app-switcher surfaces never show raw values outside the same privacy discipline as the rest of the app.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Every requirement in this build is covered by first-party Apple frameworks, which makes the permissive-license check trivial — there's nothing added to review.

**Testing implications:** Pure-function unit tests (hand-built fixtures, no UI) for `effectiveState` precedence and active/locked partitioning, covering every locked edge case (tied timestamps, restore-while-at-cap, deleting a locked item promoting the next-oldest). Repository tests run against real SwiftData with `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)` plus temp file directories, so photo lifecycle and purge cleanup are verified against real engine behavior, not mocked into fiction. StoreKit purchase/restore/downgrade/offline-startup flows tested via `SKTestSession` against an injectable `EntitlementProvider`. A small set of end-to-end UI tests reserved only for genuine system-integration flows: capture-to-save timing, interrupted/backgrounded capture resuming correctly, PDF export producing a valid file at 0/1/many items, VoiceOver completing the full core loop, and Export Preview's page-position announcement specifically (since that's the one place a plausible-but-wrong implementation choice — `PDFView` — would silently fail an already-locked accessibility requirement).

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This closes the phase well. All six required outputs are present and concrete: the SwiftUI architecture recommendation names the actual pattern (`@Observable`/`@MainActor` feature models, protocol boundaries, the capture state machine as a named deliverable, and the access-layer-vs-storage-mutation discipline that resolves a real class of bugs from earlier phases). Apple framework choices are specific and justified per-choice (system camera picker over custom `AVCaptureSession` with the reasoning stated, `PHPickerViewController` to eliminate a permission surface entirely, `VNClassifyImageRequest`, `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer` + native paged SwiftUI preview instead of `PDFView` to protect a previously-locked accessibility requirement). Persistence plan is concrete (App Group container from day one, main-actor vs. background-context split, explicit rejection of CloudKit-shaped schema pre-adoption, flat data model, photos on disk not blobbed). Permissions/privacy plan enumerates the actual entitlement strings, correctly derives zero photo-library permission from the PHPicker choice, and catches a real submission-blocking requirement (Privacy Manifest) nobody had named before this phase. Dependency decision is a clean zero-third-party call with justification. Testing implications are tiered and concrete (pure-function tests, in-memory SwiftData, `SKTestSession`, a narrow, deliberately-scoped UI test set).

The phase's one genuine open fork (camera implementation) was actually resolved with both parties converging independently and explicitly, not smoothed over — and the round surfaced two more real, non-trivial risks (Vision writeback timing, `PDFView` VoiceOver unreliability) that were caught and resolved rather than left implicit. Nothing here contradicts prompt_contract, design_handoff, or the detailed_discussion/app_features precedence rules — the effective-state computed-at-read-time discipline, the App Group requirement, and the three-track detail screen model all carry forward consistently. Acceptance checks hold: no vague architecture gaps, Vision use is scoped and justified (silent, non-blocking, confidence-floored), and the zero-dependency decision trivially satisfies the license policy. Ready to proceed to tech_specs.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Round two closed the loop cleanly. Codex took an explicit, unhedged position on the camera question — system `UIImagePickerController(sourceType: .camera)` for capture, `PHPickerViewController` for the library fallback — for the same reason Claude argued: the product's edge is in what happens after the shutter fires, not in owning a bespoke camera stack, and a custom `AVCaptureSession` trades away Apple's decade of tuned focus/exposure/orientation handling for bugs that are only testable on real hardware anyway (Simulator has no camera either way). Both landed in the same place independently, so this is now a settled, named decision rather than something left for whoever writes the capture view first.

Codex also tightened something that falls directly out of his own "access-layer rules, not storage mutations" principle: Vision's classification result should live in transient capture-workflow state, not get written to the persisted `Item` the instant the classifier returns — otherwise a draft could flicker through multiple stored categories before the user ever taps Save, turning something that should be ephemeral UI state into noisy, hard-to-reason-about persistence churn. Claude explicitly agreed and folded it into the same "one feature model owns the whole capture state machine" discipline already on the table, adding that this state machine (draft created → media pending → suggestion available/absent → save-ready → incomplete) should be a named, diagrammed deliverable in tech_specs, not just an implied consequence of "good architecture."

Claude raised one new risk this round that Codex didn't get a chance to react to, but it doesn't conflict with anything he said — it's a specific implementation call inside territory both already agreed on (off-main PDF rendering, accessible export preview): don't use `PDFKit`'s `PDFView` for the in-app export preview, because its VoiceOver page-position announcement is unreliable, which would quietly violate the "Page 2 of 5" accessibility requirement locked back in design_handoff. The fix — generate the real shareable file with `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer`, but render the in-app preview as a paged native SwiftUI view (e.g. `TabView(.page)` over rasterized page images) where the accessibility announcement is fully controlled — is a refinement of an already-agreed plan, not a new fork.

Everything else — the App Group/SwiftData container plan with per-context thread separation, the repository/service protocol boundary, StoreKit 2's offline-safe entitlement caching with a launch-time listener, zero third-party dependencies, WidgetKit's debounced reload, photos-on-disk with a dedicated settings entity for currency/lock-preference, and the tiered testing strategy — was already aligned and nothing here reopened it.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture recommendation:** MVVM-style, with `@Observable` (Observation framework) feature models marked `@MainActor` owning all UI-facing state — no `ObservableObject`/`@Published`. Views never talk directly to SwiftData, Vision, StoreKit, or the file system; everything routes through a small set of protocol-defined boundaries (`ItemRepository`, `ClassificationService`, `EntitlementProvider`) so every layer above storage/frameworks is testable with fakes. Capture is modeled as one explicit state machine owned by a single feature model — draft created → media processing pending → classification suggestion available/absent → save-ready → incomplete-on-failure — rather than several independent tasks fired from the view; this must be a named, diagrammed deliverable in tech_specs, not an implied side effect. Vision's classification result lives in transient capture-workflow state and is only written to the persisted `Item` if it's still the selected category at save time — it never causes an interim database write-then-flip. Subscription/record-state rules (active/locked/incomplete/deleted) are computed at read time from stored facts; nothing about entitlement or cap status is ever written back into the store as a mutation.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI + Observation, SwiftData (App Group–backed container from day one, not retrofitted for the widget later), `UIImagePickerController(sourceType: .camera)` wrapped in `UIViewControllerRepresentable` for capture (explicitly chosen over a custom `AVCaptureSession` — the product's advantage is in what happens after the photo lands, not in art-directing the live camera chrome, and a custom capture session trades Apple's tuned focus/exposure/orientation handling for a bug surface only verifiable on real hardware), `PHPickerViewController` for library fallback (no photo-library permission needed at all), `VNClassifyImageRequest` for silent, coarse, confidence-floored category suggestion, `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer` for generating the actual shareable/saveable PDF (off-main, with a defined max photo-embed dimension), a native SwiftUI paged view (e.g. `TabView(.page)` over rasterized page images, not embedded `PDFKit`/`PDFView`) for the in-app Export Preview so VoiceOver page-position announcements ("Page 2 of 5") are fully controllable, StoreKit 2's modern `Transaction`/`Product` API with a long-lived `Transaction.updates` listener task started at app launch, WidgetKit with debounced/coalesced reload triggers on local writes, LocalAuthentication for app-lock, and Swift Charts reserved for the (deferred) value chart. No ARKit, no custom/trained Core ML model, no background execution APIs (no `BGTaskScheduler`, no push) — none of this product's scope needs them.

**Persistence and local data plan:** SwiftData store lives in an App Group container from initial setup. One main-actor `ModelContext` handles all UI-driven mutation; background work (PDF export, thumbnail generation) opens its own fresh `ModelContext` from the same `ModelContainer` rather than sharing a context across actor boundaries. No CloudKit-backed SwiftData in v1 — the repository protocol, not a CloudKit-shaped schema, is what protects the future sync roadmap. Data model stays flat: `Item` (identity, timestamps, status enum, deletedAt, cover-photo reference, title/category/subtype, purchase facts, current condition grade) plus child condition/provenance entries and value entries (each with a hard, constrained value-type enum and their own optional photo reference). Photos and thumbnails live on disk with stable item/entry-keyed paths, never blobbed into the database; a dedicated small settings/config entity stores fixed currency and lock preference explicitly at install time rather than re-deriving from `Locale.current` on every render, since re-derivation would make historical value entries ambiguous after a region change.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** `NSCameraUsageDescription` and `NSFaceIDUsageDescription` only — no photo-library usage string needed given the PHPicker choice. No location, contacts, microphone, or network permissions. A Privacy Manifest (`PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`) declaring required-reason API usage (UserDefaults, file-timestamp APIs) is a concrete, tracked build task, not an afterthought discovered at submission. Root-level privacy overlay on `scenePhase` transitions, temp export files cleaned up after the share flow completes/dismisses, and widget/app-switcher surfaces never show raw values outside the same privacy discipline as the rest of the app.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Every requirement in this build is covered by first-party Apple frameworks, which makes the permissive-license check trivial — there's nothing added to review.

**Testing implications:** Pure-function unit tests (hand-built fixtures, no UI) for `effectiveState` precedence and active/locked partitioning, covering every locked edge case (tied timestamps, restore-while-at-cap, deleting a locked item promoting the next-oldest). Repository tests run against real SwiftData with `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)` plus temp file directories, so photo lifecycle and purge cleanup are verified against real engine behavior, not mocked into fiction. StoreKit purchase/restore/downgrade/offline-startup flows tested via `SKTestSession` against an injectable `EntitlementProvider`. A small set of end-to-end UI tests reserved only for genuine system-integration flows: capture-to-save timing, interrupted/backgrounded capture resuming correctly, PDF export producing a valid file at 0/1/many items, VoiceOver completing the full core loop, and Export Preview's page-position announcement specifically (since that's the one place a plausible-but-wrong implementation choice — `PDFView` — would silently fail an already-locked accessibility requirement).

---

CONSENSUS: YES

### Tech Specs

# provenance — Tech Specs

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:30:47._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide architecture, technology stack, data model, interfaces/APIs, and non-functional requirements with justified trade-offs.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

The simplest build that still survives the app’s edge cases is a four-lane split with very few cross-lane seams: `data_domain` owns the SwiftData models plus pure rules like active-vs-locked partitioning and effective display state; `services_utilities` owns file storage, Vision classification, PDF generation, widget summary computation, app-lock, and entitlements; `primary_ui` owns feature models and SwiftUI screens; `polish_resilience` owns privacy overlay behavior, downgrade/read-only copy states, export preview rasterization, accessibility labels, and test fixtures around failure paths. If those seams stay hard, parallel work can happen without type drift. If they don’t, the likely failure is UI code reaching straight into SwiftData or StoreKit and re-encoding business rules in three places.

I would lock one architecture decision right now because it kills a whole class of bugs: there is no persisted “locked” flag anywhere. The only stored facts are item data, timestamps, soft-delete state, incomplete/draft/completed status, and subscription snapshots. Whether an item is active, preserved/locked, deleted, or repairable is computed at read time from those facts plus current entitlement state. If anyone starts mutating stored records on downgrade or resubscribe, the app will desync and the widget/export/detail screens will disagree.

The file layout should be boring and explicit. `App/` for app entry, dependency container, scenePhase coordination, and App Group container setup. `DataDomain/Models/` for `Item`, `ConditionEntry`, `ValueEntry`, `AppSettings`, enums, and small value types like purchase facts and photo references. `DataDomain/Rules/` for `ItemDisplayState`, active-cap partitioning, restore/delete rules, collection summary computation, and export view data mappers. `Services/Storage/` for photo persistence, thumbnail generation, temp export cleanup, and repository implementations. `Services/Classification/` for Vision label mapping and confidence filtering. `Services/Export/` for PDF document rendering and preview page rasterization. `Services/Entitlements/` for StoreKit 2 listeners and cached capability state. `Services/Security/` for LocalAuthentication and privacy overlay triggers. `Features/Catalog/`, `Features/Capture/`, `Features/ItemDetail/`, `Features/Export/`, `Features/Paywall/`, `Features/Settings/`, each with one `@MainActor @Observable` feature model and supporting views. `Widgets/` reads only the shared `CollectionSummary`. `Tests/Unit/` for pure-rule tests, `Tests/Repository/` for real in-memory SwiftData plus temp files, and `Tests/UI/` for the few flows that genuinely need system integration.

The data model should stay flatter than people will want. `Item` needs stable identity, created/updated timestamps, `status` (`draft`, `incomplete`, `complete`), optional `deletedAt`, title, category enum, free-text subtype, current condition grade enum, acquisition date, optional purchase facts, cover-photo refs, and child relations to condition entries and value entries. `ConditionEntry` needs its own identity, item ID, date, note, optional provenance/source text, optional photo ref, and optional condition-change grade. `ValueEntry` needs identity, item ID, date, amount in minor units or `Decimal`, a hard value-type enum (`purchasePrice`, `appraisal`, `dealerOffer`, `marketObservation`, `insuredValue`), and a source note. `AppSettings` should persist fixed install currency code, app-lock preference, and recovery-window days. Photo references should be metadata only: original path, thumbnail path, pixel dimensions, and maybe a content hash for cleanup/debugging. I would not blob image data into SwiftData.

Persistence strategy needs three hard rules. First, the SwiftData store lives in the App Group container on day one because the widget is already in scope and retrofitting later is a migration project. Second, photos and thumbnails live on disk under stable item/entry-keyed paths; the database stores references only. Third, there is one main-actor mutation context for UI work, and any background work opens a fresh context from the shared `ModelContainer`. Reusing a `ModelContext` across actor boundaries is the fastest way to get invalidation crashes during export or thumbnail generation.

The capture workflow has to be specified as a real state machine, not hand-waved. The moment a photo is accepted from camera or picker, the repository creates a persisted draft `Item` row with placeholder metadata and a stable ID. Then the file service writes the original image, generates the thumbnail, and only after that does the classification service optionally produce a transient suggestion. That suggestion lives in feature-model state until save; it is not written through to the database unless it remains the chosen category when the user completes Quick Log. If the photo write fails after the row exists, the item stays persisted as `incomplete`; the UI never pretends it was fully saved. That one rule is what makes interruption recovery deterministic.

Export needs the same discipline. The real PDF file is generated off-main with `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer`. The in-app preview is not a `PDFView`; it is a paged SwiftUI preview over rasterized page images, because page-position accessibility needs to be reliable and controllable. Export also needs its own view-data mapping boundary so the renderer never reaches back into live SwiftUI or ad hoc model formatting. That mapper should take domain models plus settings and produce plain export sections with already-formatted strings, ordered entries, and resolved photo file URLs. If export formatting logic leaks into views and services at the same time, you will get mismatched dates and values between preview and shared file.

The biggest risk in this direction is not architectural complexity; it is false confidence that “local-first” means “simpler.” It does not. With no backend, the local store is the product. If purge cleanup, draft recovery, downgrade behavior, or temp export cleanup are even slightly sloppy, there is nothing upstream to reconcile against. My main objection to my own direction is that it introduces several protocols and mapping layers for an MVP, which can look heavier than necessary. I still think it is the right call because the app’s failure modes are trust failures, not styling failures, and these seams are what let us test the sharp edges directly.

Verification has to be named concretely. Pure function tests must cover active/locked partitioning, effective-state precedence, restore behavior at and above cap, deleting a locked item promoting the next-oldest, and value-entry sorting/grouping by type. Repository tests must run against real SwiftData with `isStoredInMemoryOnly: true` plus temp directories so photo writes, thumbnail generation, soft-delete, restore, purge, and temp export cleanup are verified against real file behavior. StoreKit tests must cover first purchase, restore, downgrade, offline launch with last-known entitlements, and resubscribe restoring edit access without any migration. UI tests should stay narrow: capture-to-save, interrupted capture recovery, app-lock on foreground, read-only preserved item behavior after downgrade, zero/one/many export generation, and VoiceOver page announcements in export preview. If those tests are not written, the team will think the app is stable because the happy path works once on one phone.

The detail the final phase output absolutely has to preserve is this: every parallel lane codes against shared, small interfaces and shared pure rules, not against shared assumptions. If `primary_ui` invents its own notion of “locked,” or `services_utilities` invents its own value summary math, or `polish_resilience` hardcodes a second date formatter for export preview, the app will look finished and still fail trust. This spec needs to freeze the cross-lane contract now.

```interfaces-json
      "kind": "enum",
      "language": "Swift",
      "signature": "enum CaptureWorkflowState: Equatable { case idle; case draftCreated(Item); case mediaProcessing(Item); case suggestionReady(Item, ClassificationSuggestion?); case saveReady(Item, ClassificationSuggestion?); case failedIncomplete(Item) }",
      "owning_lane": "primary_ui",
      "notes": "Single feature-model state machine for capture and quick log."
    },
    {
      "name": "CatalogRoute",
      "kind": "enum",
      "language": "Swift",
      "signature": "enum CatalogRoute: Hashable { case itemDetail(UUID); case preservedList; case recentlyDeleted; case exportPreview(ExportMode); case settings }",
      "owning_lane": "primary_ui",
      "notes": "Typed NavigationStack path contract across UI lanes."
    }
  ]
}
```

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Visionary) — Round 1**

Let me put down the architecture I'd actually want to build against, pushing hard on the pieces that determine whether this feels inevitable rather than merely competent.

The shape everyone converged on in ios_architecture_review — MVVM with `@Observable` `@MainActor` feature models, protocol boundaries in front of SwiftData/Vision/StoreKit, a named capture state machine, effective-state computed at read time — is right, and tech_specs' job is to turn that into types every build lane can code against without guessing. I want to push one level bolder than "solid MVVM" in three specific places, because this is the phase where being timid costs the most later: concurrency mode, the collection-summary/widget data path, and how rigorously the capture state machine is actually typed rather than just described in prose.

On concurrency: given we're targeting Xcode 26/Swift 6 tooling, I want to build this with Swift 6 strict concurrency checking turned on from day one, not bolted on after a data race surfaces in TestFlight. This app has real actor boundaries — main-actor UI, background PDF/thumbnail contexts, a StoreKit transaction listener, a widget extension reading the same store — and every one of those is exactly the kind of boundary Swift 6's compiler-enforced `Sendable` checking catches for free at compile time instead of via a crash report from a real device three weeks post-launch. This is the single highest-leverage "boldness" available here: it costs some upfront friction fixing warnings, but for a trust-first app where "did I lose your $9,000 record" is the actual stakes, compiler-verified concurrency safety is worth more than almost any UI polish we could spend that time on instead.

On the widget/summary path: I want `CollectionSummary` to not be a naive "fetch everything and sum" computed property recomputed on every access, because that's the kind of thing that looks fine at 20 items and stutters or drifts at 400. Concretely: one `CollectionSummaryProvider` service, backed by a lightweight cached struct (`totalActiveItemCount`, `totalCollectionValue`, `lastComputedAt`) that recomputes from a single repository query whenever a local write happens (item created/deleted/restored, value entry added) and is invalidated by the same debounce mechanism that already triggers the widget's `WidgetCenter.reloadTimelines`. Both the widget and any in-app value display read this one provider — never two independently-written sums. This directly extends what got flagged in design_handoff/detailed_discussion about avoiding two-sources-of-truth drift, but I want it typed as a real service now, not left as a principle someone has to remember.

On the capture state machine: this needs to be an actual typed enum with associated values, not an implied sequence of bools, because that's the one piece of this app doing real async orchestration under user pressure (photo lands, thumbnail generates, Vision resolves, user might background at any point). I'd type it as `CaptureState` — `.capturing`, `.processing(draftID:)`, `.awaitingDetails(draftID:, coverPhotoURL:, suggestion: ClassificationSuggestion?)`, `.saved(itemID:)`, `.incomplete(itemID:, reason:)` — owned by one `CaptureViewModel` that's the only thing allowed to write to the draft `Item` row. Vision's result flows into `.awaitingDetails`'s associated value, never into SwiftData directly; it only gets persisted if the user hasn't overridden the category by the time Save fires. This makes "what happens if the app is killed mid-capture" a resumability question answerable purely by re-deriving state from the persisted `Item.status` on next launch, rather than trying to reconstruct in-memory state that's already gone.

For file/module layout, I'd structure the Xcode project so parallel lanes can't collide on the same files: `App/` (entry point, root NavigationStack, scenePhase-driven lock/privacy modifiers, ModelContainer bootstrap), `Domain/` (the `@Model` types, enums, and every repository/service protocol — zero SwiftUI imports allowed here, this is the shared contract lane touches first), `Data/` (concrete SwiftData-backed repository implementations, on-disk photo store implementation), `Services/` (VisionClassificationService, PDFExportRenderer, StoreKitEntitlementProvider, LocalAuthenticationLockService — each a concrete conformance to a Domain protocol), `Features/Catalog`, `Features/Capture`, `Features/ItemDetail`, `Features/Export`, `Features/Paywall`, `Features/Settings` (each a self-contained folder of View + ViewModel + any feature-local subviews), `Widget/` (separate target, App-Group-shared read-only access via the same repository protocol), `Tests/` split into `DomainTests` (pure function/unit), `DataTests` (in-memory SwiftData), `ServiceTests` (StoreKit/Vision fakes), `UITests` (the narrow XCUITest set already agreed). The rule that keeps lanes from inventing incompatible types: nothing in `Features/*` defines its own version of a domain type or protocol — everything cross-cutting lives in `Domain/` and gets imported, never redeclared.

Data models, concretely: `Item` (`@Model`, `id: UUID`, `title: String`, `category: Category`, `subtype: String`, `status: ItemStatus`, `createdAt: Date`, `creationSequence: Int64` as the stable tiebreak nobody can accidentally omit, `deletedAt: Date?`, `coverPhotoPath: String?`, `conditionGrade: ConditionGrade?`, `purchaseDate: Date?`, `purchasePrice: Decimal?`, `purchaseSource: String?`, relationships to `[ConditionEntry]` and `[ValueEntry]` with cascade delete). `ConditionEntry` (`id`, `itemID`, `date`, `note: String`, `photoPath: String?`, `markedConditionChange: Bool`). `ValueEntry` (`id`, `itemID`, `date`, `amount: Decimal`, `valueType: ValueType`, `sourceNote: String?`). `AppSettings` (singleton row: `currencyCode: String`, `appLockEnabled: Bool`, installed at first launch from `Locale.current` once and never re-derived). `Category` is the fixed six-case enum plus `.other`; `ValueType` is the five-case enum locked in detailed_discussion; `ConditionGrade` I'd fix as a small ordered enum (`mint/excellent/good/fair/poor`) rather than free text, since an ordered grade is what makes a future "condition changed" visual affordance meaningful — free text can't be compared across entries. `ItemStatus` is `draft/incomplete/complete` per the shared-status-enum decision. None of these four enums are stored as raw strings anywhere outside the enum itself — SwiftData handles Codable enum storage natively, so there's no stringly-typed drift risk.

Persistence: one `ModelContainer` configured against the App Group container URL at `App/` launch, injected via environment into every feature; one main-actor `ModelContext` for UI mutation, fresh background `ModelContext` instances (from the same container) for `PDFExportRenderer` and thumbnail generation, per the already-agreed thread-safety plan. Schema versioning: `SchemaMigrationPlan` scaffolded now even with a single schema version, so the first real migration isn't the first time anyone's touched that API. Photos live on disk under the App Group container at `Photos/<itemID>/original.jpg` and `Photos/<itemID>/thumb.jpg` / `Photos/<itemID>/entries/<entryID>.jpg`, referenced by relative path string in the model, never absolute (App Group container paths can change between installs/updates).

Testing strategy: I'd actually adopt Swift Testing (`@Test`, `#expect`) for the `DomainTests` and `DataTests` tiers instead of XCTest — parametrized tests are a first-class feature there and `effectiveState`/active-locked-partition testing is exactly the shape of test that benefits (one `@Test(arguments:)` sweep across every locked edge case instead of a dozen hand-written XCTest methods). XCUITest stays XCTest-based since that's still the underlying UI automation API. This is a genuinely bolder-than-default pick but a safe one given the validated Xcode 26 toolchain, and it directly makes the highest-value test suite in the app (the pure effective-state functions) more exhaustive to write, which matters more here than almost anywhere else in the codebase.

The biggest risk in my own recommendation: turning on Swift 6 strict concurrency checking now, rather than after the MVP ships, will slow down the first two weeks of implementation while every actor-boundary violation gets surfaced and fixed. I think that's the correct trade for this specific app — given the trust thesis is "never lose or misstate a record," a class of bugs caught at compile time instead of discovered via a crash report against someone's real collection is worth the friction — but if a build lane is under real time pressure, this is the one place I'd expect pushback, and it should get resolved explicitly rather than quietly downgraded to minimal-checking by whoever hits the first hard error.

```interfaces-json
{"interfaces": [
{"name": "Item", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Model final class Item { @Attribute(.unique) var id: UUID; var title: String; var category: Category; var subtype: String; var status: ItemStatus; var createdAt: Date; var creationSequence: Int64; var deletedAt: Date?; var coverPhotoPath: String?; var conditionGrade: ConditionGrade?; var purchaseDate: Date?; var purchasePrice: Decimal?; var purchaseSource: String?; var acquisitionNote: String?; @Relationship(deleteRule: .cascade) var conditionEntries: [ConditionEntry]; @Relationship(deleteRule: .cascade) var valueEntries: [ValueEntry] }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "creationSequence is the mandatory stable tiebreak for the active-25 partition; never sort on createdAt alone."},
{"name": "ConditionEntry", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Model final class ConditionEntry { @Attribute(.unique) var id: UUID; var itemID: UUID; var date: Date; var note: String; var photoPath: String?; var markedConditionChange: Bool }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "One photo max per entry, per locked scope."},
{"name": "ValueEntry", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Model final class ValueEntry { @Attribute(.unique) var id: UUID; var itemID: UUID; var date: Date; var amount: Decimal; var valueType: ValueType; var sourceNote: String? }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "amount uses Decimal, never Double, to avoid float drift in a financial ledger."},
{"name": "AppSettings", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Model final class AppSettings { @Attribute(.unique) var id: Int; var currencyCode: String; var appLockEnabled: Bool; var installedAt: Date }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Singleton row (id fixed at 1); currencyCode stamped once at install from Locale.current, never re-derived."},
{"name": "Category", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum Category: String, Codable, CaseIterable { case watch, vinyl, card, wine, sneaker, other }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Fixed six-case enum, shared schema, free-text subtype field carries specificity."},
{"name": "ItemStatus", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum ItemStatus: String, Codable { case draft, incomplete, complete }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Shared status enum covers both never-finished drafts and photo-write failures."},
{"name": "ConditionGrade", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum ConditionGrade: String, Codable, CaseIterable, Comparable { case mint, excellent, good, fair, poor }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Ordered enum, not free text, so condition-change comparisons over time are meaningful."},
{"name": "ValueType", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum ValueType: String, Codable, CaseIterable { case purchasePrice, appraisal, dealerOffer, marketObservation, insuredValue }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Hard constrained enum; chart/ledger rendering must visually distinguish each case, never one undifferentiated line."},
{"name": "ItemDisplayState", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum ItemDisplayState { case normal; case incomplete(reason: String); case locked; case deleted(purgeDate: Date) }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Single enum every card/badge/detail view switches over; never independently computed per screen."},
{"name": "effectiveState", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "func effectiveState(for item: Item, subscriptionActive: Bool, activeItemIDs: Set<UUID>) -> ItemDisplayState", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Pure function, precedence: deleted > locked > incomplete > normal. Must be exhaustively unit tested including tied creationSequence values."},
{"name": "activeLockedPartition", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "func activeLockedPartition(items: [Item], freeCapActive: Bool, capSize: Int) -> (active: [Item], locked: [Item])", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "O(n) single-pass: filter non-deleted, sort by creationSequence, split at capSize when freeCapActive. Never O(n^2) per-item recomputation."},
{"name": "CollectionSummary", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct CollectionSummary: Sendable { var activeItemCount: Int; var totalCollectionValue: Decimal?; var lastComputedAt: Date }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "The one shared source of truth for widget and any in-app value display; never computed twice independently."},
{"name": "ItemRepository", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol ItemRepository: Sendable { func fetchAll(includeDeleted: Bool) async throws -> [Item]; func createDraft(coverPhotoPath: String?) async throws -> Item; func save(_ item: Item) async throws; func softDelete(_ itemID: UUID) async throws; func restore(_ itemID: UUID) async throws; func purgeExpired() async throws; func addConditionEntry(_ entry: ConditionEntry, to itemID: UUID) async throws; func addValueEntry(_ entry: ValueEntry, to itemID: UUID) async throws; func collectionSummary(subscriptionActive: Bool) async throws -> CollectionSummary }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Sole boundary between all feature/view models and SwiftData. Concrete impl lives in Data/."},
{"name": "ClassificationSuggestion", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct ClassificationSuggestion: Sendable, Equatable { var category: Category; var confidence: Double }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Nil/absent entirely below the confidence floor; never surfaced as a low-confidence guess."},
{"name": "ClassificationService", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol ClassificationService: Sendable { func classify(imageData: Data) async -> ClassificationSuggestion? }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "VNClassifyImageRequest-backed impl maps ~1000 Vision labels to the six-case Category via a reviewed lookup table artifact, not an inline switch."},
{"name": "EntitlementState", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum EntitlementState: Sendable, Equatable { case free; case subscribed(expiresAt: Date?); case unknownOffline }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "unknownOffline must resolve to the last cached verified state, never to free-tier lockout."},
{"name": "EntitlementProvider", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol EntitlementProvider: Sendable { var current: EntitlementState { get async }; func purchase(_ product: Product) async throws; func restorePurchases() async throws; func startTransactionListener() async }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Gates from Transaction.currentEntitlements (local cache), never a live network call. Listener started once at app launch."},
{"name": "PhotoStore", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol PhotoStore: Sendable { func saveOriginal(_ data: Data, itemID: UUID) async throws -> String; func generateThumbnail(itemID: UUID) async throws -> String; func saveEntryPhoto(_ data: Data, entryID: UUID) async throws -> String; func deletePhotos(itemID: UUID) async throws; func loadImage(relativePath: String) async throws -> Data }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "All paths relative to App Group container root; thumbnails generated off-main via byPreparingThumbnail."},
{"name": "PDFExportService", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol PDFExportService: Sendable { func renderSingleItem(_ itemID: UUID) async throws -> URL; func renderCollection() async throws -> URL; func rasterizedPreviewPages(for fileURL: URL) async throws -> [CGImage] }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "UIGraphicsPDFRenderer off-main for the real file; rasterizedPreviewPages feeds the native SwiftUI paged preview, never embed PDFView."},
{"name": "AppLockService", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol AppLockService: Sendable { func deviceAuthenticationAvailable() -> Bool; func authenticate() async -> Bool }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "Lock is skipped entirely (returns as if authenticated) when deviceAuthenticationAvailable() is false."},
{"name": "CaptureState", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum CaptureState { case capturing; case processing(draftID: UUID); case awaitingDetails(draftID: UUID, coverPhotoPath: String, suggestion: ClassificationSuggestion?); case saved(itemID: UUID); case incomplete(itemID: UUID, reason: String) }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Owned exclusively by CaptureViewModel; Vision result only persists to Item.category if still selected at Save."},
{"name": "CaptureViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class CaptureViewModel { private(set) var state: CaptureState; func photoCaptured(data: Data) async; func selectCategory(_ category: Category); func save(title: String) async; func resumeDraft(itemID: UUID) async }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Single feature model orchestrating draft creation, photo write, thumbnail gen, and classification request."},
{"name": "CatalogViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class CatalogViewModel { private(set) var activeItems: [Item]; private(set) var lockedCount: Int; private(set) var loadState: LoadState; func load() async; func softDelete(_ itemID: UUID) async; func undoDelete(_ itemID: UUID) async }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Renders ItemDisplayState per item via effectiveState; never its own ad hoc locked/incomplete checks."},
{"name": "LoadState", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum LoadState: Equatable { case loading; case loaded; case empty; case error(String) }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Shared four-state enum reused across Catalog, Export Preview, Preserved list, Recently Deleted."},
{"name": "ItemDetailViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class ItemDetailViewModel { private(set) var item: Item; private(set) var displayState: ItemDisplayState; func addConditionEntry(note: String, photoData: Data?, markedChange: Bool) async; func addValueEntry(amount: Decimal, type: ValueType, sourceNote: String?, date: Date) async }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Owns the three-module state; Value module renders LockedTeaserCard vs live ledger vs read-only-preserved based on displayState + subscription."},
{"name": "ExportPreviewViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class ExportPreviewViewModel { private(set) var pages: [CGImage]; private(set) var loadState: LoadState; func generate(mode: ExportMode) async; func share() -> URL? }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Backed by PDFExportService.rasterizedPreviewPages; drives the TabView(.page) preview with explicit page-position accessibility."},
{"name": "ExportMode", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum ExportMode { case singleItem(UUID); case collection }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Shared toggle state between the two export variants on one screen."},
{"name": "PaywallViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class PaywallViewModel { private(set) var products: [Product]; private(set) var loadState: LoadState; func purchase(_ product: Product) async; func restore() async }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Reads/writes only through EntitlementProvider; never mutates ItemRepository directly."},
{"name": "PrivacyOverlay", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct PrivacyOverlayModifier: ViewModifier { func body(content: Content) -> some View }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Root-level modifier applied once in App/, driven by scenePhase; covers app-switcher snapshot, not per-screen."},
{"name": "CollectionSummaryProvider", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "actor CollectionSummaryProvider { func current() async -> CollectionSummary; func invalidate() async }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Debounced recompute on write events; single source feeding both widget timeline and any in-app value display."},
{"name": "WidgetReloadCoordinator", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "actor WidgetReloadCoordinator { func scheduleReload() async }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Coalesces WidgetCenter.reloadTimelines calls within a short debounce window to respect WidgetKit's background budget."},
{"name": "RecoveryPurgeScheduler", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct RecoveryPurgeScheduler { func purgeEligibleItems(repository: any ItemRepository) async throws }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Runs at launch/foreground to purge Recently Deleted items past the 30-day window; never a background task API."}
]}
```

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where round 1 landed: Codex and I are fully aligned on the big architectural calls — MVVM with `@Observable`/`@MainActor` feature models, a protocol boundary in front of SwiftData/Vision/StoreKit, capture modeled as one owned, typed state machine where Vision's result stays transient until Save, effective/display state as a pure precedence function computed at read time (never stored), the App Group SwiftData container with main-actor-vs-background-context separation, photos on disk referenced by path rather than blobbed into the store, `UIGraphicsPDFRenderer` for the real file paired with a native paged SwiftUI preview instead of `PDFView`, one shared `CollectionSummary` feeding both widget and in-app display, zero third-party dependencies, and a tiered test strategy (pure-function, in-memory-SwiftData, StoreKit-test, narrow UI). I pushed this further with concrete additions that don't fight anything Codex said — a `creationSequence: Int64` field so the active-25 tiebreak is a real column instead of a hope, a debounced `CollectionSummaryProvider` actor and `WidgetReloadCoordinator` actor turning "avoid drift" into typed services, a concrete file/module layout, and two genuinely new proposals Codex hasn't reacted to yet: turning on Swift 6 strict concurrency checking from day one, and using Swift Testing (`@Test`/`#expect`) instead of XCTest for the domain/data test tiers.

But there's a real problem underneath the agreement: the two interfaces-json blocks we each produced don't actually match, and this is exactly the failure mode this phase exists to prevent — a lane building against one of these would break against the other's concrete implementation. Concretely: money is `amountMinor: Int` in Codex's version vs `Decimal` in mine; photo references are a `PhotoAssetRef` struct with pixel dimensions in Codex's version vs a bare `String` path in mine; `ItemDisplayState` is a flat enum (no associated data) in Codex's version vs one carrying `incomplete(reason:)`/`deleted(purgeDate:)` in mine; `ItemRepository` has two structurally different method sets; the capture state machine got declared twice under two different names and shapes (`CaptureWorkflowState` vs `CaptureState`); and Codex named an explicit export-formatting mapper boundary that my `ExportPreviewViewModel`/`PDFExportService` didn't account for. None of that is a style disagreement — it's the acceptance check itself ("parallel build lanes can work without inventing incompatible types") not yet being met.

CONSENSUS: NO

Round 2 needs Codex to react to and sign off on: (1) the Swift 6 strict-concurrency and Swift Testing proposals, and (2) a single reconciled interfaces-json — I've drafted a merged version below resolving the conflicts (Decimal for money, the richer `PhotoAssetRef` struct, an associated-value `ItemDisplayState`, one unified `CaptureState`, one `ItemRepository` shape, `ExportPagePreview` carrying page-index metadata so the VoiceOver requirement stays airtight, and an explicit export-mapping service) so this doesn't quietly get decided by whichever lane starts coding first.

## Final Output

Not final yet. Architecture philosophy is converged; the concrete shared-type contract is not. Round 2 must explicitly resolve: money representation (Decimal vs minor-unit Int), photo-reference shape, `ItemDisplayState` associated data, one canonical capture-state enum, one canonical `ItemRepository` signature, the export-mapping boundary, and whether Swift 6 strict concurrency + Swift Testing are adopted for this build.

```interfaces-json
{"interfaces": [
{"name": "Category", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum Category: String, Codable, CaseIterable, Sendable { case watch, vinyl, card, wine, sneaker, other }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Fixed six-case enum, shared schema, free-text subtype carries specificity."},
{"name": "ItemStatus", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum ItemStatus: String, Codable, Sendable { case draft, incomplete, complete }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Persisted creation/completion state; separate from computed ItemDisplayState."},
{"name": "ConditionGrade", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum ConditionGrade: String, Codable, CaseIterable, Comparable, Sendable { case poor, fair, good, veryGood, excellent, mint }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Ordered enum (not free text) so condition-change comparisons over time are meaningful."},
{"name": "ValueType", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum ValueType: String, Codable, CaseIterable, Sendable { case purchasePrice, appraisal, dealerOffer, marketObservation, insuredValue }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Hard constrained enum; ledger/chart rendering must visually distinguish each case, never one undifferentiated line."},
{"name": "PhotoAssetRef", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct PhotoAssetRef: Codable, Hashable, Sendable { let originalRelativePath: String; let thumbnailRelativePath: String?; let pixelWidth: Int; let pixelHeight: Int }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "RESOLVED (merge): richer struct wins over bare String path so export downsampling can reason about source dimensions without re-decoding. All photo fields on Item/ConditionEntry/ValueEntry use this type."},
{"name": "PurchaseFacts", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct PurchaseFacts: Codable, Hashable, Sendable { var purchaseDate: Date?; var purchaseAmount: Decimal?; var source: String?; var locationNote: String? }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "RESOLVED (merge): Decimal, not minor-unit Int, for all monetary values app-wide -- avoids a parallel minor-units convention every lane would need to remember."},
{"name": "Item", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Model final class Item { @Attribute(.unique) var id: UUID; var title: String; var category: Category; var subtype: String; var status: ItemStatus; var createdAt: Date; var creationSequence: Int64; var deletedAt: Date?; var coverPhoto: PhotoAssetRef?; var conditionGrade: ConditionGrade?; var purchaseFacts: PurchaseFacts?; @Relationship(deleteRule: .cascade) var conditionEntries: [ConditionEntry]; @Relationship(deleteRule: .cascade) var valueEntries: [ValueEntry] }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "creationSequence is the mandatory stable tiebreak for active-25 partition; never sort on createdAt alone."},
{"name": "ConditionEntry", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Model final class ConditionEntry { @Attribute(.unique) var id: UUID; var itemID: UUID; var entryDate: Date; var note: String; var provenanceNote: String?; var conditionChange: ConditionGrade?; var photo: PhotoAssetRef? }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "One photo max per entry, per locked scope."},
{"name": "ValueEntry", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Model final class ValueEntry { @Attribute(.unique) var id: UUID; var itemID: UUID; var entryDate: Date; var amount: Decimal; var valueType: ValueType; var sourceNote: String?; var photo: PhotoAssetRef? }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Decimal amount, never Double, to avoid float drift in a financial ledger."},
{"name": "AppSettings", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Model final class AppSettings { @Attribute(.unique) var id: Int; var installCurrencyCode: String; var appLockEnabled: Bool; var recentlyDeletedRetentionDays: Int; var installedAt: Date }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Singleton row (id fixed at 1); currencyCode stamped once at install from Locale.current, never re-derived."},
{"name": "ItemDisplayState", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum ItemDisplayState: Equatable, Sendable { case normal; case incomplete(reason: String); case locked; case deleted(purgeDate: Date) }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "RESOLVED (merge): associated-value shape wins so badges/VoiceOver strings can be built directly from the enum without a second lookup. Precedence: deleted > locked > incomplete > normal."},
{"name": "computeDisplayState", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "func computeDisplayState(item: Item, subscriptionActive: Bool, activeItemIDs: Set<UUID>) -> ItemDisplayState", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Pure function. Must be exhaustively unit tested including tied creationSequence values, restore-at-cap, and delete-while-locked."},
{"name": "partitionActiveAndLocked", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "func partitionActiveAndLocked(items: [Item], freeCapActive: Bool, capSize: Int) -> (active: [Item], locked: [Item])", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "O(n) single pass: filter non-deleted, sort by creationSequence, split at capSize when freeCapActive. Never per-item O(n^2) recomputation."},

{"name": "CaptureState", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum CaptureState: Sendable { case capturing; case draftCreated(itemID: UUID); case mediaProcessing(itemID: UUID); case awaitingDetails(itemID: UUID, coverPhoto: PhotoAssetRef, suggestion: ClassificationSuggestion?); case saveReady(itemID: UUID); case incomplete(itemID: UUID, reason: String) }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "RESOLVED (merge/rename): single canonical name replacing both 'CaptureWorkflowState' and 'CaptureState' proposals. Owned exclusively by CaptureViewModel; Vision result only persists to Item.category if still selected at Save."},
{"name": "CatalogRoute", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum CatalogRoute: Hashable, Sendable { case itemDetail(UUID); case preservedList; case recentlyDeleted; case exportPreview(ExportMode); case settings }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Typed NavigationStack path; pushes only, never used for sheet-presented focused tasks."},
{"name": "LoadState", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum LoadState: Equatable, Sendable { case loading; case loaded; case empty; case error(String) }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Shared four-state enum reused across Catalog, Export Preview, Preserved list, Recently Deleted."},
{"name": "CaptureViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class CaptureViewModel { private(set) var state: CaptureState; func photoCaptured(data: Data) async; func selectCategory(_ category: Category); func save(title: String) async; func resumeDraft(itemID: UUID) async }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Sole owner of capture state machine and draft mutation; nothing else writes to a draft Item."},
{"name": "CatalogViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class CatalogViewModel { private(set) var activeItems: [Item]; private(set) var lockedCount: Int; private(set) var loadState: LoadState; func load() async; func softDelete(_ itemID: UUID) async; func undoDelete(_ itemID: UUID) async }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Renders ItemDisplayState per item via computeDisplayState; never its own ad hoc locked/incomplete checks."},
{"name": "ItemDetailViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class ItemDetailViewModel { private(set) var item: Item; private(set) var displayState: ItemDisplayState; func addConditionEntry(note: String, photoData: Data?, conditionChange: ConditionGrade?) async; func addValueEntry(amount: Decimal, type: ValueType, sourceNote: String?, date: Date) async; func savePurchaseFacts(_ facts: PurchaseFacts?) async }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Owns the three-module state; Value module renders LockedTeaserCard vs live ledger vs read-only-preserved based on displayState + subscription."},
{"name": "ExportPreviewViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class ExportPreviewViewModel { private(set) var pages: [ExportPagePreview]; private(set) var loadState: LoadState; func generate(mode: ExportMode) async; func share() -> URL? }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Backed by ExportService.generatePreviewPages; drives TabView(.page) with page-position accessibility sourced directly from ExportPagePreview fields."},
{"name": "PaywallViewModel", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Observable @MainActor final class PaywallViewModel { private(set) var products: [Product]; private(set) var loadState: LoadState; func purchase(_ product: Product) async; func restore() async }", "owning_lane": "primary_ui", "notes": "Reads/writes only through EntitlementProvider; never touches ItemRepository directly."},
{"name": "ItemRepository", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol ItemRepository: Sendable { func createDraft(coverPhoto: PhotoAssetRef?) async throws -> Item; func updateQuickLog(itemID: UUID, title: String, category: Category, subtype: String) async throws -> Item; func markItemIncomplete(itemID: UUID, reason: String) async throws; func loadActiveItems() async throws -> [Item]; func loadLockedItems() async throws -> [Item]; func loadItemDetail(itemID: UUID) async throws -> Item; func savePurchaseFacts(itemID: UUID, facts: PurchaseFacts?) async throws; func addConditionEntry(_ entry: ConditionEntry) async throws; func addValueEntry(_ entry: ValueEntry) async throws; func softDelete(_ itemID: UUID, deletedAt: Date) async throws; func restore(_ itemID: UUID) async throws; func purgeExpiredDeletedItems(now: Date) async throws; func loadRecentlyDeletedItems(now: Date) async throws -> [Item]; func collectionSummary(subscriptionActive: Bool) async throws -> CollectionSummary }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "RESOLVED (merge): one canonical signature set. Repository owns persistence semantics only; no stored locked/active flags -- callers pass subscriptionActive, never receive a pre-baked EntitlementSnapshot object into storage calls."},
{"name": "ClassificationSuggestion", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct ClassificationSuggestion: Sendable, Equatable { let category: Category; let confidence: Double; let sourceLabel: String }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "sourceLabel retained for auditing the Vision-label-to-Category mapping table; nil/absent entirely below the confidence floor."},
{"name": "ClassificationService", "kind": "protocol", "language": "swift", "signature": "protocol ClassificationService: Sendable { func classify(imageData: Data) async -> ClassificationSuggestion? }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "VNClassifyImageRequest-backed impl maps Vision labels to Category via a reviewed lookup-table artifact, not an inline switch."},
{"name": "EntitlementState", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum EntitlementState: Sendable, Equatable, Codable { case free; case subscribed(expiresAt: Date?); case unknownOffline(lastKnown: EntitlementState?) }", "owning_lane": "services_utilities", "notes": "RESOLVED (merge): enum wins over a separate EntitlementSnapshot struct -- unknownOffline carries the last verified state inline so it can never resolve to free-tier lockout by omission."},

Returns PhotoAssetRef, never a bare path string."},

{"name": "CollectionSummaryProvider", "kind": "function", "language": "swift", "signature": "actor CollectionSummaryProvider { func current() async -> CollectionSummary; func invalidate() async }", "owning_lane": "polish_resilience", "notes": "Debounced recompute on repository write events; single source feeding both widget timeline and any in-app value display."},
```
