### Project Plan

# provenance — Project Plan

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:39:31._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce milestones, sequencing, estimates, dependencies, and a risk-managed delivery plan.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Delivery Lead · Visionary) — Round 1**

I’d sequence this as six build milestones with verification embedded inside each one, not bolted on at the end, because this app’s biggest failure mode is a technically “complete” build that users will not trust with valuable records.

Milestone one is the foundation slice: app shell, App Group SwiftData container, domain models, repository protocols, photo storage, app settings, entitlement abstraction, and the pure functions that define active vs locked and display-state precedence. Nothing visual should get built on top of ad hoc data access. The key deliverable here is a stable core contract plus tests for `partitionActiveAndLocked`, `computeDisplayState`, `isSubscriptionActive`, soft-delete retention math, and the creation-sequence tiebreak rule. If this slips, every later screen inherits ambiguity.

Milestone two is the capture-and-save spine, because this is the product’s make-or-break moment. Camera and library fallback, draft creation at photo capture time, thumbnail generation, draft resume after interruption, quick-log save, and catalog insertion all need to work end to end before enrichment screens start. I would keep the first visible flow brutally focused: take or choose photo, title, category, save, done. Vision classification should ship in this milestone only as a silent suggestion wired into transient capture state, because the app needs to prove “faster than a spreadsheet” early. Verification here is not just unit coverage; it is timed manual and UI automation around cold-start capture, permission denial, photo-write failure, and relaunch-after-interruption.

Milestone three is the trust layer on item detail: purchase facts, condition/provenance entries, value entries, locked teaser states, read-only preserved states, and the clean separation of the three modules. This is where Provenance stops being a generic inventory app and starts feeling like a real dossier. I would not treat the value module as optional polish even if the chart is still basic, because the whole premium promise depends on the record aging well over time. This milestone should also include the near-empty value state and the cover-photo vs entry-photo distinction in real UI, not just in the model. Verification needs repository-backed integration tests and VoiceOver passes on the module stack, because this screen is the densest place for state bugs and accessibility regressions.

Milestone four is the safety and privacy envelope: app lock, privacy overlay on background, Recently Deleted with restore and purge behavior, preserved/locked list, and downgrade/read-only behavior. This needs to happen before monetization and widget work, not after, because these rules change how every existing screen behaves. The most important detail to preserve in the final plan is that active vs locked is computed, not stored, and that restore is never blocked by the free cap. This milestone’s verification needs scenario testing, not just snapshots: downgrade with more than 25 items, delete a locked item and confirm promotion, restore at cap and confirm active-or-locked placement, device with no passcode, and app-switcher privacy behavior.

Milestone five is export and premium value delivery. Single-item and collection export, document-model mapping, PDF rendering off-main, preview-page generation, locked export upsell state, and a real redacted paywall preview should land together. I would treat this as the “remarkable” moment for the app: the export cannot feel like a debug printout. Even in MVP form it should look like something you would hand to a spouse, appraiser, or insurer without apology. Final typography can improve later, but structure, readability, and correctness cannot. Verification here is a full matrix: zero items, one sparse item, one dense item, many photos, temp file cleanup, generation while background work is happening, and VoiceOver page-position announcements in preview.

Milestone six is monetization, widget, and hardening. StoreKit 2 purchase/restore/downgrade flows, the value card’s paid/free behaviors, widget value vs count states, debounced widget reloads, collection summary caching, and broad regression repair all belong here because they depend on the underlying business rules already being stable. I would not bring StoreKit in earlier than this unless the build team is at risk of forgetting it entirely. The paywall is not the product; it is the gate on top of a product that already works. Verification here needs `SKTestSession`, offline-startup entitlement behavior, downgrade preservation, widget refresh after local writes, and a final full-core-loop regression.

The dependency chain is strict in a few places. The App Group container decision has to happen first because the widget and shared persistence depend on it. Photo storage and `PhotoAssetRef` must be settled before capture UI and export, or both will churn. The pure partition/display-state functions must be implemented and tested before catalog, detail, preserved, and deleted views, because those screens all depend on the same access logic. Export depends on item detail data being complete enough to render the dossier honestly. StoreKit depends on locked/read-only behavior already existing, otherwise subscription testing is meaningless.

The biggest risk is not Vision misclassification. That’s manageable because classification is non-blocking and humble by design. The real risk is cross-feature state inconsistency: draft vs incomplete vs deleted vs locked vs subscribed, with photos and exports layered on top. That’s where apps like this become subtly untrustworthy. The mitigation is to keep state derivation centralized and to force integration checkpoints after each milestone instead of accumulating five partially-finished systems that only meet each other at the end.

The second real risk is that the export ships as a mechanical feature instead of the emotional proof of value. If that happens, the paid tier weakens and the whole app starts to look like a dark-themed inventory tracker. To avoid that, the plan should explicitly reserve design and verification time inside the export milestone for page composition, section hierarchy, and redacted paywall preview quality. That is the bold part of the product. The chart, the classifier, and even the widget are secondary to whether the record feels credible.

If I were assigning lanes after this phase, I’d split them as core data/persistence first, capture flow second, detail/enrichment third, export/paywall fourth, and privacy/widget/testing as a horizontal lane that starts early and keeps cutting across the build. The thing I would insist the final phase output preserve verbatim is this: testing and repair time must be attached to every milestone, and the export milestone is product-critical, not end-stage polish. Without those two disciplines, the plan will look executable on paper and still produce the wrong app.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Looking at this from the storage/systems side, my biggest worry walking into this phase is that "four lanes can build in parallel" is true for typing signatures and false for actual execution order — there's a real dependency chain hiding under the lane labels, and if the plan doesn't sequence around it, we'll get integration breakage exactly where nobody was watching.

Here's the order I'd actually build in, and why. First, before any feature code: prove the App Group + SwiftData container wiring works end to end, including a bare-bones widget extension target that reads from the shared container and renders literally anything. The architecture decision explicitly says the widget needs the App Group from day one because retrofitting it is a real migration problem — but a design doc saying "set it up correctly" isn't the same as verifying it. I want a milestone that is nothing but: create the app target, create the widget extension target, share an App Group entitlement between them, boot a SwiftData ModelContainer at that shared URL from both targets, and confirm a write from the app shows up when the widget reads it. That's maybe an hour of "real" work but it's the single highest-blast-radius thing to get wrong, so it goes first and gets its own verification checkpoint, not a checkbox buried inside a later milestone.

Second, the domain layer — models, the four enums, `computeDisplayState`, `partitionActiveAndLocked`, `isSubscriptionActive` — gets built and fully unit-tested with zero UI and zero SwiftData involved where possible (these are pure functions by design specifically so they can be tested without a database). This is genuinely parallelizable with nothing else and should be treated as the fastest, highest-confidence milestone. I'd push hard for exhaustive parametrized tests here before anyone touches a view, because every later screen leans on this being correct.

Third, the data layer: the concrete `ItemRepository` against real SwiftData (in-memory config + temp file dirs for photos) and `PhotoStore`. Here's a concrete risk nobody has said out loud yet: SwiftData `@Model` classes are reference types bound to a `ModelContext`, and under Swift 6 strict concurrency they are not freely `Sendable` across actor boundaries. The plan says background work opens its own `ModelContext` from the same `ModelContainer` — fine — but that means `Item` instances fetched on the main actor cannot just be handed to a background export task. The mapping from `Item`/`ConditionEntry`/`ValueEntry` into the plain `Sendable` `ExportDocumentModel`/`ExportSection` structs has to happen while still on the main actor (or by refetching via `PersistentIdentifier` in the background context), not by passing model objects across the boundary. This needs to be an explicit implementation task with its own verification, because it's exactly the kind of thing that compiles fine in a quick prototype and then produces concurrency-checker errors (or worse, silent data races if someone weakens the strict-concurrency setting to make the errors go away) once the export pipeline is fully wired up.

Fourth, services: classification, entitlement, export, applock — but I want to flag classification specifically as a scheduling risk, not just an engineering task. The Vision label-to-Category mapping table can't be designed abstractly; it requires running `VNClassifyImageRequest` against real sample photos across all six categories and empirically tuning the confidence floor and the mapping. That's investigative work with an unknown number of iterations, not a fixed-size coding task. And it has a nasty testability property: the spec says show nothing below the confidence floor, which means a broken mapping table and a working-but-humble mapping table look identical during casual manual QA — both show "no suggestion" a lot. If we don't build a small fixture set of reference images and a repeatable test harness that asserts specific expected categories for specific images, we will ship silent breakage that nobody catches until a real user says "it never once suggested anything."

Fifth — and this is a sequencing correction I think the naive plan would get wrong — Export has to be built before or alongside Paywall, not after. The paywall's whole design already-agreed is a real redacted mock of the export layout, which means Paywall has a hard dependency on Export's document/rendering pipeline existing in a demonstrable form. If someone schedules screens roughly alphabetically or by "importance," Paywall would get built with a fake mockup image instead of the real pipeline, and that fake would need to be thrown away and redone later.

On the UI features themselves, my skeptical flag is about the state-coverage requirement. Every named screen needing all four states isn't a detail, it's the largest actual surface area in this build — six feature screens times four states each, several with sub-states (locked Value teaser, incomplete-with-repair, deleted, tied-timestamp edge cases). I want that tracked as an explicit matrix artifact that gets checked off screen-by-state, not "the screen looks done so it's done." A screen that only demonstrably works in its happy path should not count as complete in any milestone.

On StoreKit: purchase/restore/downgrade/offline-startup via `SKTestSession` is more fiddly in practice than the one line in tech_specs suggests — simulating an expired transaction and confirming `unknownOffline` behaves correctly (never silently collapsing to free) takes deliberate setup, and I'd rather that be a named milestone with its own time than something squeezed in during a general "polish" pass at the end.

Last thing: the recovery-purge scheduler and the privacy overlay/applock gate are both root-level, cross-cutting concerns that should be wired early — applock especially, because if it's the very last thing added, every earlier manual verification pass will have been done against an unlocked app, and lock behavior interacting with navigation state (mid-capture, mid-export) won't have been exercised at all. I'd rather have a boring, ugly lock screen in place from very early on and refine its look later than bolt it on at the end and discover it fights the navigation stack.

So: my sequencing is infra-proof → domain (pure, tested) → data layer (with the Sendable-crossing discipline made explicit) → services (with classification flagged as open-ended, not fixed-size) → export-before-paywall → feature screens tracked against a real state matrix → widget completion → StoreKit hardening → a dedicated end-to-end verification pass (relaunch persistence, interrupted capture, VoiceOver full loop, PDF at 0/1/many, purge-scheduler with injected `now`) with repair time budgeted in, not assumed away.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where the two of them landed after round one — and it's a good round, but not a done one.

**What they agree on solidly:** Build the App Group + SwiftData wiring (with a bare-bones widget target reading from it) before anything else — that's the highest-blast-radius risk in the whole plan and both want it proven first, not assumed correct. Both want the pure functions (`computeDisplayState`, `partitionActiveAndLocked`, `isSubscriptionActive`, retention math) built and exhaustively tested with zero UI involved before any screen leans on them. Both insist Export must be built before or alongside Paywall, since the paywall's whole design is a real redacted mock of the export layout — building Paywall first would mean throwing away a fake mockup later. Both want testing and repair time treated as inline work inside each milestone, not a polish pass tacked on at the end. And both flagged Vision classification as something that needs real deliberate handling (not a checkbox), plus StoreKit purchase/restore/downgrade/offline testing as something that needs its own real time, not a squeeze at the finish.

**Where they actually diverge, and it's a real fork:** Codex's plan is organized around six user-facing capability milestones in sequence — foundation, then capture-and-save (with real screens), then item-detail trust layer, then a safety/privacy envelope (app-lock, Recently Deleted, preserved/locked), then export/paywall, then monetization/widget/hardening. Claude's plan is organized more around architecture layers — get domain and data and services fully built and tested *before* touching feature screens at all — and explicitly argues app-lock should be wired in early (even ugly) rather than sitting in a "milestone 4" that comes after capture and detail screens are already built and manually verified unlocked. That's not a small wording difference: it changes whether app-lock and privacy behavior get exercised against navigation/mid-capture/mid-export states from the start, or get bolted on afterward and risk a state-interaction bug nobody caught. They haven't reconciled these into one shared milestone list yet.

There's also a concrete technical risk Claude raised that Codex hasn't had a chance to react to: SwiftData `@Model` objects aren't freely `Sendable` under Swift 6 strict concurrency, so the export pipeline can't just hand `Item`/`ConditionEntry`/`ValueEntry` instances across the main-actor/background-context boundary — the mapping into the plain `Sendable` `ExportDocumentModel` has to happen on the main actor (or via `PersistentIdentifier` refetch), and that needs to be called out as its own implementation task with its own verification, not discovered mid-build.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex to respond to three things: (1) reconcile the capability-milestone structure against Claude's layer-first sequencing — specifically, do feature screens (capture, detail) get built before the full domain/data/services stack is proven, or after; (2) where app-lock actually belongs in the sequence — a dedicated milestone 4, or wired in early as a rough cross-cutting concern from day one; (3) explicit sign-off (or pushback) on the Sendable-crossing export-mapping risk as a named, verified implementation task. Once those are settled, this phase can converge on one canonical milestone list with dependencies and a verification plan attached to each.

### Round 2


**Codex (Delivery Lead · Visionary) — Round 2**

Claude’s right that the unresolved part is not “do we have the right pieces,” it’s whether we’re sequencing them in a way that exposes the dangerous interactions early enough. I do want to adjust my original milestone framing in his direction, but not all the way to “finish every layer before any feature screen exists,” because that tends to produce a technically tidy build that hasn’t proved the actual product loop. The right reconciliation is: keep the milestones user-legible, but put hard infrastructure gates in front of them and wire the cross-cutting safety pieces earlier than I first placed them. So yes, App Group + SwiftData + a trivial widget read has to be a standalone proof before anything else. Yes, pure domain rules and repository/photo-store work need to be real and tested before the first meaningful UI milestone. And yes, the export-mapping boundary Claude called out needs to become an explicit task, not an implementation detail somebody “handles later,” because with Swift 6 strict concurrency it is absolutely a real dependency: `@Model` objects are not a bag of Sendable structs you can casually shuttle into a background export renderer.

Where I’m pushing back a little is on the implication that capture/detail screens should wait until the entire services layer is rounded out. They shouldn’t. This app still lives or dies on whether capture-to-save feels faster than a spreadsheet, and we need that proven before we’ve spent a week perfecting peripheral services. The sequence I’d lock is: first, infrastructure proof and shared container verification. Second, domain rules and tests. Third, concrete data layer plus photo storage plus the export-safe mapping seam from model objects into plain Sendable export inputs. Fourth, the first vertical slice: capture, quick log, draft resume, catalog insertion, with app-lock/privacy overlay already wired in at a rough level so every manual pass from this point on is happening inside the real trust envelope, not a fake unlocked dev mode. That’s the place I’m changing my mind most from round one: Claude is right that app-lock and privacy can’t sit in a late “safety milestone” if they affect launch flow, backgrounding, mid-capture behavior, and app-switcher exposure. They should be ugly-but-real early, then refined. After that, item detail and enrichment; then export and paywall together; then StoreKit, preserved/locked and Recently Deleted edge behavior, widget, and full hardening. So the plan becomes dependency-first at the bottom, but still organized around vertical proofs of product value rather than finishing every abstraction before the product breathes.

On the Sendable issue specifically, I want that written into the plan in concrete terms: “export document assembly happens before the background render boundary.” Either `ExportService.buildDocumentModel` runs on the actor/context that safely owns the fetched SwiftData models and returns a plain `ExportDocumentModel`, or the background export task refetches by identifier inside its own context and never receives model instances directly. I don’t care which implementation wins later, but I do care that the milestone says we verify it under strict concurrency rather than letting someone weaken compiler settings to quiet warnings. Same story with Vision: I agree with Claude that the mapping table is not a fixed-size coding task, it’s empirical tuning, and the milestone should explicitly budget a fixture set and a repeatable check so “no suggestion” doesn’t hide a broken pipeline. And I want one more strong delivery opinion on top of both of your plans: export is not just “before paywall,” it is the emotional center of the product, so the milestone for it needs design review time inside the estimate, not after. If that PDF looks like a system dump, the app is dead even if every test passes. So the canonical plan I’d defend is: infra proof, domain truth, persistence/export-safe data seam, capture vertical slice with early lock/privacy, detail/enrichment, export-plus-paywall, then monetization/widget/hardening with scenario-based regression after each step. That gives Claude the dependency discipline he’s asking for without losing the ruthless focus on proving the core user moment early.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Codex, I want to push back gently on the idea that our two plans are actually a deep disagreement, because rereading your milestone one — "app shell, App Group SwiftData container, domain models, repository protocols, photo storage, app settings, entitlement abstraction, and the pure functions" — that's basically my domain-layer-and-data-layer argument already folded into your milestone one. The real fork isn't "layers first vs capabilities first," it's narrower and more specific than the coordinator's framing suggests: it's specifically about app-lock, and I think I can make a sharper case for why it can't wait until milestone four without costing us real rework, not just tidiness.

Here's the concrete failure mode I'm worried about: your milestone two verification list includes "relaunch-after-interruption" and "cold-start capture." Those are scenePhase-boundary behaviors — background, foreground, relaunch. If app-lock and the privacy overlay aren't wired in yet when that verification happens, you're testing scenePhase transitions in an app that doesn't yet have the exact lifecycle hooks (`PrivacyOverlayModifier`, the lock gate on foreground-return) that will exist in the shipped app. Once milestone four adds those hooks, they insert themselves into precisely the transitions milestone two already verified — foreground-return now triggers an auth challenge before the draft-resume logic even gets a chance to run. So either milestone four's app-lock work silently changes the behavior milestone two already signed off on (and nobody re-checks it, because "capture" was already marked verified), or someone has to redo the capture-interruption verification pass after milestone four lands. That's not a hypothetical — draft-resume-after-backgrounding and applock-on-foreground are the same trigger, competing for the same moment, and the spec explicitly requires the lock to never trap a user, which means there's real logic (skip-if-no-passcode, don't block resuming a draft, etc.) that has to interact correctly with capture state. I'd rather write that interaction once, correctly, from the start, than write it twice.

So my concrete proposal, and I think this actually resolves the fork rather than just restating my side: keep your milestone four exactly as you scoped it — Recently Deleted, preserved/locked list, downgrade read-only behavior, the full scenario-testing matrix you listed ([REDACTED:high_entropy], delete-a-locked-item-promotes-next-oldest, restore-at-cap, no-passcode device) — none of that needs to move, it's genuinely substantial standalone work and doesn't block earlier milestones. What I want pulled forward into milestone one is much smaller than the full feature: just the `AppLockService` gate at the root of navigation and the `PrivacyOverlayModifier` wired to `scenePhase`, both already fully specified as protocols in tech_specs, rendering something deliberately ugly — a plain "Unlock to continue" screen, no design polish. That's maybe half a day of work sitting on top of infra we're already building in milestone one, and it means every subsequent milestone's manual verification happens against the actual lifecycle shape the shipped app will have, instead of a lifecycle shape we later mutate out from under already-verified work.

On the Sendable-crossing risk — since you haven't reacted to it yet, let me sharpen it into something concrete enough to just accept or reject rather than a vague warning. The rule I want written into the plan as a named, testable acceptance criterion is: `ExportService.buildDocumentModel` must complete all reads of `Item`/`ConditionEntry`/`ValueEntry` and finish mapping into `ExportSection`/`ExportDocumentModel` while still running on the main actor, before any hand-off to the background rendering step. That's not just a concurrency-compiler-appeasement detail — it actually buys us something worth naming explicitly in the verification plan: it means an export is a point-in-time snapshot, immune to concurrent mutation from a user still editing the item elsewhere while the PDF renders. I'd want a real test for that specifically — start generating a collection export, mutate a value entry mid-render, assert the exported document reflects the pre-mutation state. If we don't write that test now, we won't find out this works by accident; we'll find out it doesn't work when someone edits an item during a slow multi-photo collection export and the export corrupts or crashes.

Last thing, since we're converging: I want to explicitly confirm milestone one's "photo storage" and "repository protocols" mean the concrete SwiftData-backed implementation and the concrete disk-backed `PhotoStore`, tested against real `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)` — not just protocol shells that get faked until later. If milestone two's capture-to-save timing verification is going to mean anything, it needs to run against the real persistence path, not a mock standing in for it. And separately, the Vision label-mapping work you and I both flagged as a real risk should start as an exploratory side-thread from milestone one onward, in parallel with the infra work, precisely because it's open-ended investigative effort with an unknown number of iterations — if we wait to start it until "services" comes up in sequence, we're gambling that it'll fit in whatever time is left, and I don't trust that gamble on the one piece of this build that's genuinely research, not engineering.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Round 2 actually closed the fork. Codex moved a real distance toward Claude's position — he explicitly said app-lock and privacy "can't sit in a late safety milestone" and should be "ugly-but-real early, then refined," which is the same conclusion Claude was arguing for, just less specific about the boundary. Claude then sharpened it into something concrete and uncontested: don't move the whole safety feature set (Recently Deleted, preserved/locked list, downgrade scenarios) forward — that's still substantial standalone work that belongs later — just pull the bare `AppLockService` gate and `PrivacyOverlayModifier` into the first milestone, rough and unstyled, so every later milestone's manual verification (backgrounding, relaunch, mid-capture) happens against the real lifecycle shape instead of one that gets mutated out from under already-signed-off work. Nobody's arguing against that split; it's a direct consequence of what Codex already conceded.

Same story on the other two threads: Codex explicitly signed off on the Sendable-crossing risk in concrete terms ("export document assembly happens before the background render boundary"), and Claude's round-2 addition — a specific test where a value entry gets mutated mid-export and the exported document must still reflect the pre-mutation snapshot — is just the natural verification instance of that same rule, not a new dispute. And both now agree the Vision label-mapping work is investigative, not a fixed coding task, and should start as a parallel fixture-driven side-thread from the very first milestone rather than waiting for its turn in sequence.

The one thing that never got contested and should just be locked in: milestone one's "repository protocols" and "photo storage" mean the real SwiftData-backed and disk-backed implementations, tested against real (in-memory) persistence — not protocol shells faked until later — because the capture-to-save timing verification in milestone two is meaningless against a mock.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Milestones:**

1. **Infrastructure proof + domain truth + data seam.** App Group container wired for both the app and a trivial widget target, verified end-to-end (app writes, widget reads). Domain models, the four fixed enums, and the pure functions (`computeDisplayState`, `partitionActiveAndLocked`, `isSubscriptionActive`, retention math) built and exhaustively unit-tested with zero UI. Concrete SwiftData-backed `ItemRepository` and disk-backed `PhotoStore`, tested against real `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)` plus temp file dirs — not protocol shells. `AppSettings` singleton. A rough-but-real `AppLockService` gate at the root of navigation plus `PrivacyOverlayModifier` wired to `scenePhase` (plain "Unlock to continue," no polish) — pulled forward here specifically so every later milestone's manual verification happens against the real lifecycle shape instead of a fake unlocked dev mode. In parallel, kick off the Vision label-mapping investigative side-thread: a fixture photo set across all six categories plus a repeatable harness asserting expected category per fixture, since this is open-ended empirical tuning with an unknown iteration count, not a sized coding task.
   *Verification:* parametrized tests covering tied `creationSequence`, restore-at-cap, delete-while-locked, delete-of-locked-item-promotes-next-oldest; repository/photo-store integration tests against real persistence; App-Group write-then-widget-read smoke test; app-lock skip-when-no-passcode test.

2. **Capture vertical slice.** Camera + library fallback, draft creation at photo-capture time, thumbnail generation, draft resume after interruption, quick-log save, catalog insertion, Vision suggestion wired as silent transient capture state. Built and manually verified against the real lock/privacy lifecycle from milestone one, not a bypassed one.
   *Verification:* cold-start capture, permission-denied fallback, photo-write failure marks item incomplete, interrupted/backgrounded/force-quit resume — tested specifically alongside the app-lock/foreground-return interaction (draft-resume must never be blocked by an auth challenge), timed capture-to-save automation.

3. **Item detail trust layer.** Purchase facts, condition/provenance timeline, value-entry ledger, the three-module separation, cover-vs-entry-photo distinction, locked Value teaser, read-only preserved state, near-empty value state.
   *Verification:* repository-backed integration tests, VoiceOver pass across the module stack, display-state precedence rendering correctly per item state.

4. **Safety and data-integrity feature set.** Recently Deleted (restore/purge + undo toast), full preserved/locked read-only list, downgrade read-only behavior end-to-end. (The base lock/privacy gate already exists from milestone one — this is the remaining feature scope, and can run in parallel with milestone three since both depend only on milestone one.)
   *Verification:* downgrade with more than 25 items, restore-at-cap lands active-or-locked correctly, delete-a-locked-item promotes the next-oldest, purge scheduler exercised with an injected `now`, no-passcode device.

5. **Export + Paywall together.** Document-model mapping, PDF rendering off-main, paged in-app preview, and a real redacted paywall mock built from the actual pipeline (never a placeholder image). The Sendable-crossing rule is a named acceptance criterion: `buildDocumentModel` must complete all SwiftData reads and mapping into `ExportDocumentModel`/`ExportSection` on the main actor before any hand-off to the background renderer. Design-review time is explicitly reserved inside this milestone's estimate — this is the product's emotional proof point, not mechanical output.
   *Verification:* full matrix at 0/1-sparse/1-dense/many-photo item counts, temp-file cleanup, VoiceOver "Page 2 of 5" announcement, and a concurrency-safety test: start a collection export, mutate a value entry mid-render, assert the exported document reflects the pre-mutation snapshot.

6. **Monetization, widget, and hardening.** Real StoreKit 2 wiring (purchase/restore/downgrade/offline via `SKTestSession`), `CollectionSummaryProvider` + `WidgetReloadCoordinator` debounced reload, widget free/paid states, full regression across every prior milestone.
   *Verification:* `SKTestSession` scenarios including offline-startup `unknownOffline` never silently collapsing to free, widget refresh after local writes, and a final end-to-end pass (relaunch persistence, interrupted capture, full VoiceOver core loop, PDF at 0/1/many, purge scheduler).

**Dependencies:** App Group before widget/persistence work of any kind. Photo storage/`PhotoAssetRef` settled before both capture UI and export. Pure functions proven before any screen that reads display state. The main-actor export-mapping seam settled before the export renderer is built. A real (not mocked) export pipeline exists before paywall is built against it. The app-lock/privacy gate exists before any scenePhase-transition verification in milestone two or later. Milestones three and four are mutually independent and can run in parallel once milestone one is done.

**Risk mitigations:** Cross-feature state inconsistency (draft/incomplete/deleted/locked/subscribed) is mitigated by centralizing all derivation in the pure functions, proven before any UI touches them. Data races in export are mitigated by the main-actor-only mapping rule plus a concurrent-mutation snapshot test. Vision mapping-table risk is mitigated by starting the fixture-based investigation in milestone one and running it in parallel throughout, never treated as a fixed-size task. The risk of export feeling mechanical rather than premium is mitigated by reserved design-review time inside milestone five. StoreKit fragility is mitigated by a dedicated milestone with real `SKTestSession` scenarios, never a last-minute squeeze. App-lock/privacy interaction bugs are mitigated by wiring the rough gate in milestone one so every later milestone's verification already happens inside the real trust envelope.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: FAIL

## Feedback

Everything else here is strong: six milestones sequenced correctly by dependency (App Group → domain/data seam → capture → detail/safety in parallel → export+paywall → monetization/widget), the round 2 disagreements are genuinely resolved rather than papered over (app-lock split into "rough gate now, full feature set in milestone 4," the Sendable-crossing rule turned into a named acceptance criterion with a concrete mid-export-mutation test, Vision mapping treated as a parallel investigative side-thread from milestone one), dependencies are explicit, and verification is embedded per-milestone rather than swept into a final polish pass — which directly satisfies the phase rule "testing and integration are not left to the end as vague polish."

The gap: this phase's own stated purpose is "Produce milestones, sequencing, **estimates**, dependencies, and a risk-managed delivery plan," and the Final Output has zero estimates anywhere — no day/week ranges, no relative sizing (S/M/L), nothing that lets the task-assignment phase size effort or staff lanes against a timeline. Six milestones, several explicitly called out as parallelizable (3 and 4), with no indication of which is bigger or how long the whole build is expected to take, leaves the next phase without a usable input for scheduling.

Repair instruction for the next round: keep the milestone list, dependencies, risk mitigations, and verification plan exactly as they are — do not reopen any resolved disagreement. Just add a size/estimate to each of the 6 milestones (relative sizing like S/M/L/XL is sufficient, or day ranges if the group prefers) and note the estimated critical-path length given the milestone-3/4 parallelization already agreed. This is additive scaffolding on top of already-converged content, not a new debate.

_Quality gate requested another round before this phase can close._

### Round 3


**Codex (Delivery Lead · Visionary) — Round 3**

Claude’s fail note is right, and I don’t think this needs another philosophical round. The plan is already good; it’s just missing scheduling truth. So I’d lock the milestone list exactly as-is and add estimates in a way the task-assignment phase can actually use without pretending we know the hour count to the decimal. I’d size them as: milestone one is `L` at about 4-5 working days because it’s not just scaffolding, it’s shared-container proof, real repository/photo-store implementations, pure-rule tests, the rough lock/privacy gate, and the Vision fixture harness starting in parallel. Milestone two is `M` at about 3-4 days because the capture loop is narrow but touches camera/library fallback, draft persistence, thumbnailing, interruption recovery, and timing verification. Milestone three is `M/L` at about 4 days because the item-detail surface is dense and stateful even if it’s only one screen family. Milestone four is `M` at about 3 days because the logic is subtle but the UI footprint is smaller than detail. Milestone five is `L` at about 4-5 days because export plus paywall is the most product-defining milestone and needs real design review time inside it, not after it. Milestone six is `M` at about 3-4 days because StoreKit, widget reload behavior, entitlement edge cases, and full regression always take longer than they look.

On critical path, the important thing is not to sum everything mechanically and accidentally hide the only real parallelism we have. Milestones one and two are sequential. After one, the Vision fixture work keeps running as a side thread, but it shouldn’t define the path unless it’s blocking category suggestions from shipping at all. Three and four can run in parallel once one is complete, but five effectively depends on three more than four, because export/paywall needs the item-detail data shape and real dossier content, while Recently Deleted/preserved flows mostly matter for hardening and entitlement behavior. So the critical path is really one → two → three → five → six, with four overlapping three and the tail of five. Using the estimates above, that puts the likely critical-path length around 18-22 working days, call it roughly four to four-and-a-half weeks for one strong builder or one lead lane plus support, and closer to three weeks if the team truly staffs parallel lanes instead of just talking about them. I would explicitly write that milestone four has about 1-2 days of slack because it can overlap milestone three, but only if the pure-state logic from milestone one is already locked and tested.

The other thing I’d insist we preserve is that these estimates already include verification and repair, not just first-pass implementation. That matters because otherwise people will turn “4 days for export” into “2 days to code, 2 days magically appear later,” and that is exactly how the PDF becomes mechanically correct but emotionally weak. Same for milestone six: if StoreKit and widget get estimated as “a couple of integration tasks,” the team will underbudget downgrade/offline-startup behavior and then spend the last week discovering entitlement edge cases. So if we’re closing this cleanly, I’d say the final delivery plan should literally name the milestone sizes, the 18-22 day critical path, the fact that three and four are the only meaningful overlap, and that the estimates are inclusive of verification/repair. That makes the plan schedulable without reopening a single settled product or architecture decision.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 3**

Fair cop on the quality gate — we converged on a genuinely good sequence and then forgot the thing the phase actually asked for. Let me fix that without reopening anything, and I'll use the skeptic lens specifically on the estimates themselves, because "add some numbers" is exactly the kind of task where people quietly lowball the two milestones that are hardest to size honestly.

Milestone one — infra proof, domain truth, concrete data seam, rough lock gate, Vision fixture kickoff — I'm calling this a solid L, roughly 4-5 days of focused work, and I want to defend why it's not smaller than that. It's tempting to look at "App Group wiring plus some enums plus a repository" and think it's a warm-up milestone, but we already agreed the concrete SwiftData repository and PhotoStore have to be real and tested against actual in-memory persistence, not stubs — that's full implementation work, not scaffolding. And Swift 6 strict concurrency is exactly the kind of thing where the estimate is fine right up until you hit the first `@Model`-crossing-an-actor-boundary error that the compiler surfaces three files away from where you thought the problem was. I'd rather size this generously now than have it eat into milestone two's schedule later.

Milestone two, the capture vertical slice, I'd put at M, 3-4 days. Camera/library capture is well-trodden Apple-framework territory, but draft-resume-after-interruption plus the interaction with the app-lock foreground-return gate we just agreed to wire in early is genuinely new integration surface, not boilerplate — that's the exact seam I was worried about last round, and now it has to actually get built and verified, which costs real time even though the two pieces are individually simple.

Milestones three and four I'd size at M-L (4-5 days) and M (3-4 days) respectively, running in parallel as already agreed — so the parallel window is bounded by milestone three's length, and milestone four's time effectively hides underneath it rather than adding to the critical path. That's the one place parallelization actually saves us calendar time in this plan, and I want to flag explicitly that we shouldn't try to manufacture more parallelization than that — Codex's own dependency chain point still holds, export can't start early just because "it's a different feature," since it needs milestone three's detail data to be complete enough to render an honest dossier.

Milestone five is where I want to push hardest, because I think this is the one most likely to get lowballed if we're not careful: I'd call it XL, 5-7 days, bigger than any other milestone in the plan. Codex said it himself — "if that PDF looks like a system dump, the app is dead even if every test passes" — and that's precisely the combination that blows past estimates: real off-main PDF rendering, paginated native preview with page-position accessibility, a full 0/1-sparse/1-dense/many-photo verification matrix, the concurrency-safety mutation test we just locked in, and reserved design-review time that is by nature open-ended because "does this feel premium" isn't a binary pass/fail the way a unit test is. If anyone treats this as a mechanical "hook up UIGraphicsPDFRenderer" task sized at 2-3 days, we will blow through it, and it's the worst possible milestone to run over on, since it's also gating the paywall.

Milestone six I'd put at M-L, 4-5 days — StoreKit sandbox testing is fiddlier in practice than the tech-specs one-liner suggests, as I flagged back in round one, and "full regression across every prior milestone" is real verification debt, not a rubber stamp at the end.

Adding it up along the critical path — M1 through M2 through the parallel M3/M4 window through M5 through M6 — that's roughly 5 + 4 + 5 + 7 + 5, call it 26 days, so somewhere around five calendar weeks if this is one lane working straight through, with the M3/M4 overlap already baked in as the only real parallelization gain. I'd rather hand the task-assignment phase that range with the reasoning attached than a single confident number, because the two soft spots — milestone one's concurrency friction and milestone five's design-quality ceiling — are exactly the kind of thing that's unknowable until you're inside them, and I'd rather the next phase budget explicit buffer there than discover the overrun by surprise.

**Gemini — Round 3 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

Round three did what the quality gate asked without reopening anything structural — same six milestones, same dependencies, same risk mitigations, same verification plan, just estimates layered on top. Both Codex and Claude sized milestones one through four almost identically (M1 large at 4-5 days because the concrete SwiftData/PhotoStore work and the rough lock gate are real implementation, not scaffolding; M2 medium at 3-4 days; M3 and M4 running in parallel with M3 as the longer one, so M4's time hides underneath it rather than adding to the schedule).

Where they land in different places is milestone five and six. Codex sizes export+paywall as `L` (4-5 days) and monetization/widget/hardening as `M` (3-4 days), landing on an 18-22 day critical path — about 4 to 4.5 weeks solo, closer to 3 weeks with real parallel staffing. Claude pushes back specifically on those two, arguing export is the single easiest milestone to lowball because "does this feel premium" isn't a pass/fail check the way a unit test is, and sizes it `XL` at 5-7 days, with monetization/hardening at `M-L` (4-5 days) because StoreKit sandbox testing is fiddlier in practice than it looks on paper — landing on roughly 26 days, about 5 calendar weeks. Neither is disputing the other's reasoning, they're just landing on different amounts of built-in buffer for the two milestones that are genuinely hardest to size honestly (concurrency friction in M1, design-quality ceiling in M5). That's not a structural fork worth another round — it's a judgment call about how much schedule risk to bank up front, and I think the honest move is to hand the next phase a range with the reasoning attached rather than force a fake single number.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Milestones, sizes, and estimates** (structure, dependencies, risk mitigations, and verification plan carried forward unchanged from round 2):

1. **Infrastructure proof + domain truth + data seam** — `L`, 4-5 days. App Group + widget read/write proof, pure functions fully tested, concrete (not stubbed) SwiftData repository and disk-backed PhotoStore, rough app-lock/privacy gate, Vision fixture-harness kickoff. Sized generously on purpose — Swift 6 strict-concurrency friction on `@Model` boundaries tends to surface late and eat into the next milestone if underestimated here.
2. **Capture vertical slice** — `M`, 3-4 days. Camera/library capture is well-trodden; the real cost is draft-resume-after-interruption interacting correctly with the app-lock foreground-return gate.
3. **Item detail trust layer** — `M/L`, 4-5 days.
4. **Safety and data-integrity feature set** — `M`, 3-4 days, running in parallel with milestone 3 — its time is absorbed under milestone 3's length rather than adding to the critical path, provided milestone 1's pure-state logic is already locked.
5. **Export + Paywall together** — `L` to `XL`, 4-7 days. Both agree this is the single highest-uncertainty milestone: real off-main PDF rendering, paginated accessible preview, the full 0/1/many-item verification matrix, the concurrency-safety mutation test, and open-ended design-review time to make sure the export reads as a dossier and not a system dump. Budget toward the high end of this range rather than the low end.
6. **Monetization, widget, hardening** — `M` to `M/L`, 3-5 days. StoreKit sandbox testing (purchase/restore/downgrade/offline `unknownOffline` behavior) and full cross-milestone regression consistently take longer than they look on paper.

**Critical path:** Milestone 1 → 2 → 3 → 5 → 6, with milestone 4 overlapping milestone 3 as the plan's one real parallelization gain — don't try to manufacture more overlap than that, since export genuinely can't start until item-detail data is complete enough to render honestly. Total critical-path length: roughly **18-26 working days**, i.e. about **4 to 5 calendar weeks** for one strong builder or lead lane, trending toward the shorter end only if the team genuinely staffs milestones 3 and 4 as separate parallel lanes rather than sequentially. Estimates already include verification and repair time, not just first-pass implementation — that's a deliberate choice so nobody quietly turns "4 days for export" into "2 days to code, 2 days that mysteriously vanish later." Milestones 1 (concurrency risk) and 5 (design-quality ceiling) are flagged as the two places most likely to run over; the next phase should carry explicit schedule buffer there rather than be surprised by it.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 3**

QUALITY: FAIL

## Feedback

The estimate gap from the previous FAIL is now fixed — milestones are sized, a critical-path range is given, and the reasoning for the two riskiest sizing calls (M1 concurrency friction, M5 design-quality ceiling) is preserved. Good, minimal, non-reopening repair on that front.

But this round's `## Final Output` dropped two of the four required output categories from its own text. The phase rules require **Milestones, Dependencies, Risk mitigations, Verification plan** as the deliverable. This round's Final Output contains milestones-with-estimates and a critical-path paragraph, but no explicit **Dependencies** section and no explicit **Risk mitigations** section — it only says they're "carried forward unchanged from round 2" without restating them. Round 2's Final Output had a concrete `Dependencies:` paragraph (App Group before widget/persistence, photo storage before capture/export, pure functions before any display-state screen, the main-actor export seam before the renderer, app-lock/privacy gate before scenePhase verification, M3/M4 parallel) and a concrete `Risk mitigations:` paragraph (state-consistency, export data races, Vision mapping-table risk, export-quality risk, StoreKit fragility, app-lock interaction bugs) — neither appears in this round's message.

If the task-assignment phase works from this phase's final coordinator message as the authoritative artifact, it would be missing two of its four required sections. This is a copy-forward, not a new debate — nothing here needs re-litigation.

**Repair instruction for the next round:** Reprint the full Final Output as one self-contained block: keep the milestone list with sizes/estimates and the critical-path paragraph exactly as written this round, and append the unchanged `Dependencies:` and `Risk mitigations:` paragraphs from round 2's Final Output verbatim (per-milestone verification detail from round 2 can also be folded back in, since this round's milestone bullets dropped the "*Verification:*" lines too — the phase rule explicitly requires verification not be left implicit). This is pure assembly of already-agreed content, not a new round of discussion.

_Quality gate requested another round before this phase can close._

### Round 4


**Codex (Delivery Lead · Visionary) — Round 4**

Claude’s last fail note is right, and at this point the only bad move would be to treat an assembly problem like a strategy problem. We already did the hard part. The milestone structure is settled, the sequencing is settled, the app-lock split is settled, the export Sendable boundary is settled, the only thing missing is that the final artifact has to be self-contained instead of relying on “carried forward from round 2.” So I’m in favor of closing this right now by restating the whole delivery plan in one place, with the estimates we just added and the unchanged dependencies, risk mitigations, and verification plan spelled out explicitly.

The plan I’d stand behind is still the same one. Milestone 1 is large, about 4 to 5 working days: App Group container proof for app and widget, domain models and pure rule functions fully tested, real SwiftData repository and disk-backed PhotoStore, AppSettings, rough-but-real app-lock gate and privacy overlay wired to scenePhase, plus the Vision fixture harness starting as a parallel investigation from day one. Milestone 2 is medium, about 3 to 4 days: camera and library fallback, draft creation at photo capture, thumbnailing, quick log save, catalog insertion, interrupted capture recovery, all exercised inside the real lock/privacy lifecycle. Milestone 3 is medium-to-large, about 4 to 5 days: item detail as the actual trust surface, with purchase facts, condition/provenance timeline, value ledger, near-empty states, locked teaser behavior, cover-versus-entry photo distinctions. Milestone 4 is medium, about 3 to 4 days, and it runs in parallel with milestone 3: Recently Deleted, restore and purge flows, preserved/locked list, downgrade read-only behavior, and the subtle promotion/restore edge cases. Milestone 5 is the one I want us to budget honestly, not optimistically: export plus paywall is 4 to 7 days. I’m fine with calling that L-to-XL because that’s where design quality and engineering correctness collide: main-actor snapshot mapping, off-main PDF rendering, native paged preview, the full 0/1/many verification matrix, concurrency-snapshot testing, and time to make the export feel like a dossier instead of a dump. Milestone 6 is 3 to 5 days: StoreKit 2 purchase/restore/downgrade/offline behavior, CollectionSummaryProvider, WidgetReloadCoordinator, free versus paid widget states, and full hardening/regression. The critical path is still 1 to 2 to 3 to 5 to 6, with 4 overlapping 3. That puts us at roughly 18 to 26 working days, so call it 4 to 5 calendar weeks solo, shorter only if we actually staff 3 and 4 as separate lanes instead of pretending.

The dependencies need to be said plainly in the same artifact. App Group setup comes before any real widget or shared persistence work. Photo storage and PhotoAssetRef have to be settled before both capture UI and export. The pure functions for display state, active/locked partitioning, and entitlement conversion have to be proven before any screen depends on them. The export-mapping seam has to be settled before the renderer exists at all: build the Sendable ExportDocumentModel snapshot before the background render boundary, never pass SwiftData model instances across it. The rough app-lock and privacy overlay have to exist before we verify any scenePhase-sensitive behavior like draft resume, backgrounding, or relaunch. Milestones 3 and 4 can overlap once milestone 1 is done, but export really depends on milestone 3 because without honest detail data there is nothing worth rendering. And the paywall comes after a real export pipeline exists, because selling a fake mock instead of the actual artifact would be a product mistake, not just a sequencing mistake.

The risk mitigations are also already agreed, so we should stop hiding them behind references. Cross-feature state inconsistency is handled by centralizing derivation in pure tested functions instead of letting screens invent their own rules. Export race and snapshot bugs are handled by the main-actor document assembly rule plus an explicit test where data mutates during render and the PDF still reflects the pre-mutation snapshot. Vision risk is handled by treating mapping-table quality as an empirical side-thread from milestone 1 onward, with fixtures and repeatable checks, not as a neat little implementation task at the end. Export quality risk is handled by reserving design review time inside milestone 5 instead of calling it “polish later.” StoreKit fragility is handled by giving it its own milestone with real SKTestSession scenarios, including offline startup and downgrade behavior. App-lock and privacy interaction bugs are handled by wiring the rough versions in from milestone 1 so every later verification pass already happens inside the shipped lifecycle shape. And concurrency friction in milestone 1 is why that estimate is large in the first place, not because we’re being lazy with sizing.

And the verification plan should be explicit enough that task assignment can turn it into work without guesswork. Milestone 1 verifies tied creationSequence handling, restore-at-cap, locked-plus-incomplete precedence, delete-of-locked-item-promotes-next-oldest, repository and PhotoStore integration against real in-memory persistence, App Group app-write/widget-read proof, and skip-lock-when-no-passcode behavior. Milestone 2 verifies cold-start capture, permission denial fallback, photo-write failure becoming incomplete instead of lost, force-quit/background resume, and capture timing, all with the lock gate in place. Milestone 3 verifies repository-backed detail behavior, VoiceOver across the module stack, and correct rendering of display state. Milestone 4 verifies downgrade over 25 items, restore landing active or locked correctly, purge scheduling with injected time, locked-item deletion promotion, and no-passcode edge behavior. Milestone 5 verifies export at zero items, one sparse item, one dense item, many-photo collection export, temp-file cleanup, page-position accessibility, and the mid-render mutation snapshot test. Milestone 6 verifies purchase, restore, downgrade, offline unknownOffline continuity, widget refresh after local writes, relaunch persistence, interrupted capture, full VoiceOver core loop, and final PDF sanity across the item-count matrix. That’s the full plan. I don’t think we need another round; we need to publish that as the authoritative artifact and move on.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 4**

The gate's right and there's nothing to argue about here — we let the per-milestone verification lines and the dependencies/risk paragraphs quietly fall out of the message when we were heads-down on sizing, and if the task-assignment phase only sees this round's text as authoritative, it'd be working from an incomplete artifact. Codex, I don't think you and I have anything left to debate; this is just making sure the full thing is in one place instead of scattered across three rounds of "carried forward unchanged."

So let me just say the whole thing back in full, sizes and verification and dependencies and risks all together, so whoever reads this next doesn't have to go spelunking through round 2 to reconstruct it. Milestone one, infrastructure proof plus domain truth plus the concrete data seam plus the rough app-lock gate plus the Vision fixture kickoff, is an L at 4-5 days, and it still needs to be checked against the same things we named in round two: parametrized tests hitting tied creation-sequence values, restore-at-cap, delete-while-locked, and delete-of-a-locked-item promoting the next-oldest; repository and photo-store integration tests running against real persistence, not fakes; the App-Group write-then-widget-read smoke test; and the app-lock skip-when-no-passcode case. Milestone two, the capture vertical slice, stays an M at 3-4 days, verified with cold-start capture, the permission-denied fallback, photo-write failure correctly marking an item incomplete rather than pretending it's whole, interrupted/backgrounded/force-quit resume tested specifically alongside the app-lock foreground-return interaction we both flagged as the real risk, and timed capture-to-save automation. Milestone three, the item-detail trust layer, is M/L at 4-5 days, verified with repository-backed integration tests, a full VoiceOver pass across the three-module stack, and confirmation that display-state precedence renders correctly per item state. Milestone four, the safety and data-integrity set, is M at 3-4 days and still runs in parallel under milestone three's window rather than adding to the schedule, verified with the downgrade-at-more-than-25-items scenario, restore-at-cap landing active-or-locked correctly, delete-of-a-locked-item promoting the next-oldest, the purge scheduler exercised against an injected `now` rather than real wall-clock waiting, and the no-passcode device case. Milestone five, export plus paywall together, is the L-to-XL 4-7 day range we both landed near, verified with the full 0/1-sparse/1-dense/many-photo matrix, temp-file cleanup, the VoiceOver "Page 2 of 5" announcement, and the concurrency-safety test where a value entry gets mutated mid-render and the exported document has to still reflect the pre-mutation snapshot. Milestone six, monetization/widget/hardening, is M to M/L at 3-5 days, verified with `SKTestSession` scenarios including offline-startup `unknownOffline` never silently collapsing to free, widget refresh firing off local writes, and a final end-to-end regression covering relaunch persistence, interrupted capture, the full VoiceOver core loop, PDF correctness at 0/1/many items, and the purge scheduler.

On top of that, the dependency chain from round two stands exactly as written: App Group has to exist before any widget or persistence work happens at all; photo storage and `PhotoAssetRef` have to be settled before either capture UI or export gets built, since both would churn otherwise; the pure functions have to be proven before any screen that reads display state touches them; the main-actor export-mapping seam has to be settled before the export renderer exists; a real, non-mocked export pipeline has to exist before paywall gets built against it, or we end up throwing away a fake mockup like we discussed; the app-lock and privacy gate has to exist before any scenePhase-transition verification happens in milestone two or later, which is the whole reason we pulled it forward; and milestones three and four are mutually independent, running in parallel once milestone one is done, which is the one place this plan actually saves calendar time. And the risk register carries forward untouched too: cross-feature state inconsistency across draft/incomplete/deleted/locked/subscribed is mitigated by centralizing every bit of that logic in the pure functions and proving them before any UI depends on them; data races in export are mitigated by the main-actor-only mapping rule plus the concurrent-mutation snapshot test; the Vision mapping-table risk is mitigated by treating it as an open-ended fixture-driven investigation starting in milestone one and running throughout, never a fixed-size task; the risk of the export reading as mechanical rather than premium is mitigated by the design-review time we insisted on reserving inside milestone five specifically; StoreKit fragility is mitigated by giving it its own dedicated milestone with real `SKTestSession` scenarios instead of a last-minute squeeze; and the app-lock/privacy interaction bugs we were most worried about are mitigated by wiring the rough gate into milestone one so that every later milestone's manual verification already happens inside the real trust envelope instead of one we'd have to retrofit and re-verify against.

Critical path is unchanged too: one through two through the parallel three/four window through five through six, landing at roughly 18-26 working days, call it four to five calendar weeks for a single strong lane, with milestones one and five flagged as the two places most likely to run past their estimate — one because Swift 6 strict-concurrency friction on `@Model` boundaries tends to surface late, five because "does this feel premium" has no unit test. Nothing here is new; it's the same plan, just written down as one complete thing instead of three partial ones stitched together by reference.

## Final Output

**Milestones (with sizes, estimates, and verification):**

1. **Infrastructure proof + domain truth + data seam** — `L`, 4-5 days. App Group container proof for app and widget (write from app, read from widget). Domain models and the four fixed enums. Pure rule functions (`computeDisplayState`, `partitionActiveAndLocked`, `isSubscriptionActive`, retention math) fully unit-tested with zero UI. Concrete SwiftData-backed `ItemRepository` and disk-backed `PhotoStore` — real implementations tested against actual in-memory persistence, not stubs. `AppSettings` singleton. A rough-but-real `AppLockService` gate and `PrivacyOverlayModifier` wired to `scenePhase` from day one (plain, unstyled). The Vision label-mapping fixture harness kicked off as a parallel, open-ended investigation, not a scheduled coding task.
   *Verification:* tied `creationSequence` handling, restore-at-cap, locked-plus-incomplete precedence, delete-of-locked-item-promotes-next-oldest; repository/PhotoStore integration against real persistence; App-Group write-then-widget-read smoke test; skip-lock-when-no-passcode behavior.

2. **Capture vertical slice** — `M`, 3-4 days. Camera + library fallback, draft creation at photo-capture time, thumbnailing, quick-log save, catalog insertion, interrupted-capture recovery — all exercised inside the real lock/privacy lifecycle from milestone one, not a bypassed dev mode.
   *Verification:* cold-start capture, permission-denied fallback, photo-write failure marking an item incomplete rather than lost, force-quit/backgrounded resume tested specifically alongside the app-lock foreground-return interaction, timed capture-to-save automation.

3. **Item detail trust layer** — `M/L`, 4-5 days. Purchase facts, condition/provenance timeline, value ledger, three-module separation, cover-vs-entry-photo distinction, locked Value teaser, near-empty states.
   *Verification:* repository-backed integration tests, full VoiceOver pass across the module stack, correct display-state precedence rendering per item.

4. **Safety and data-integrity feature set** — `M`, 3-4 days, running in parallel with milestone three (its time is absorbed under milestone three's window, not additive to the schedule). Recently Deleted (restore/purge, undo toast), full preserved/locked read-only list, downgrade read-only behavior end-to-end.
   *Verification:* downgrade with more than 25 items, restore-at-cap landing active-or-locked correctly, delete-of-locked-item promotion, purge scheduler exercised against an injected `now`, no-passcode device case.

5. **Export + Paywall together** — `L`–`XL`, 4-7 days, budgeted toward the high end. Main-actor snapshot mapping into `ExportDocumentModel`/`ExportSection`, off-main PDF rendering, native paged accessible preview, real redacted paywall mock built from the actual pipeline, reserved design-review time so the export reads as a dossier rather than a dump.
   *Verification:* full 0/1-sparse/1-dense/many-photo matrix, temp-file cleanup, VoiceOver "Page 2 of 5" announcement, concurrency-safety test (mutate a value entry mid-render, assert the export reflects the pre-mutation snapshot).

6. **Monetization, widget, hardening** — `M`–`M/L`, 3-5 days. StoreKit 2 purchase/restore/downgrade/offline flows, `CollectionSummaryProvider` + `WidgetReloadCoordinator`, free/paid widget states, full cross-milestone regression.
   *Verification:* `SKTestSession` scenarios including offline-startup `unknownOffline` never silently collapsing to free, widget refresh after local writes, final end-to-end pass (relaunch persistence, interrupted capture, full VoiceOver core loop, PDF correctness at 0/1/many items, purge scheduler).

**Dependencies:** App Group exists before any widget or shared-persistence work. Photo storage/`PhotoAssetRef` settled before both capture UI and export. Pure functions proven before any screen reads display state. The main-actor export-mapping seam settled before the export renderer exists. A real, non-mocked export pipeline exists before paywall is built against it. The app-lock/privacy gate exists before any scenePhase-transition verification in milestone two or later. Milestones three and four are mutually independent and run in parallel once milestone one is done — the plan's one genuine calendar-time savings.

**Risk mitigations:** Cross-feature state inconsistency (draft/incomplete/deleted/locked/subscribed) is mitigated by centralizing all derivation in the pure functions, proven before any UI depends on them. Export data races are mitigated by the main-actor-only mapping rule plus the mid-render mutation snapshot test. Vision mapping-table risk is mitigated by treating it as an open-ended fixture-driven investigation from milestone one onward, never a fixed-size task. Export-quality risk (feeling mechanical rather than premium) is mitigated by reserved design-review time inside milestone five. StoreKit fragility is mitigated by its own dedicated milestone with real `SKTestSession` scenarios, not a last-minute squeeze. App-lock/privacy interaction bugs are mitigated by wiring the rough gate in from milestone one so every later milestone's verification already happens inside the real trust envelope.

**Critical path:** Milestone 1 → 2 → 3 → 5 → 6, with milestone 4 overlapping milestone 3. Total: roughly **18-26 working days**, about **4 to 5 calendar weeks** for one strong lane, shorter only if milestones 3 and 4 are genuinely staffed as separate parallel lanes. Estimates include verification and repair time throughout, not just first-pass implementation. Milestones one (Swift 6 concurrency friction surfacing late) and five (design-quality ceiling with no unit-test equivalent) are the two most likely to run past estimate and should carry explicit schedule buffer in the next phase.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 4**

QUALITY: PASS

All four required outputs (Milestones, Dependencies, Risk mitigations, Verification plan) are present as distinct, locatable content in this single message — a task-assignment phase reading only this output has everything it needs.

Substantively, the plan remains sound: sequencing follows real dependency structure (App Group → domain/data seam → capture → parallel detail/safety → export+paywall → monetization/hardening), the one genuine parallelization opportunity (milestones 3/4) is called out without overclaiming additional overlap, and the two hardest-to-size milestones (1's concurrency friction, 5's design-quality ceiling) are flagged with reasoning rather than smoothed over. The round 3 sizing disagreement between Codex (18-22 days) and Claude (26 days) was handled honestly — reported as a range with the underlying judgment-call reasoning attached, not forced into a fake single number. Verification is embedded per-milestone throughout, not deferred to a final polish pass, satisfying the phase's explicit anti-pattern check. Ready to close.
