### Task Assignments

# provenance — Task Assignments

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:47:48._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce concrete workstreams, file ownership, forbidden edit zones, branch names, merge strategy, testing responsibilities, communication protocol, and a conflict-prevention plan.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

I’d run this as one app workspace rooted at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/provenance`, with one Xcode project and four strict lanes that map to the fixed lane ids. The mistake to avoid is “parallel” work that secretly shares the same files, because SwiftUI apps get chaotic fast when state, navigation, and entitlement logic are all being edited in-place by multiple people. So the rule should be: shared contracts first, then each lane stays inside its file boundary unless a handoff explicitly opens a seam.

My lane split would be:

`data_domain` owns all source-of-truth types and persistence contracts. That means the SwiftData models, pure rule functions, repository protocols, and any shared enums or value types. It should also own the App Group container bootstrap contract, but not the UI wiring around it. This lane is the only one allowed to change core domain signatures once they are published, and any such change has to come with fixture/test updates in the same change.

`primary_ui` owns app flow, navigation, feature view models, screen composition, local screen state, empty/loading/error/success coverage, accessibility wiring at the view level, and widget UI. This lane should not invent business rules in views. If a screen needs logic that sounds like policy, it is a data-domain or services seam, not inline view code.

`services_utilities` owns Vision classification, photo storage implementation, PDF/export pipeline, StoreKit entitlement service, LocalAuthentication service, and any helper actors for summaries or widget reload coordination. This lane can expose async protocols and concrete implementations, but it should not reach into SwiftUI navigation or view composition.

`polish_resilience` owns cross-cutting correctness work: privacy overlay behavior, Recently Deleted purge scheduling, end-to-end integration tests, VoiceOver verification, offline/downgrade behavior checks, and final regression hardening. I would also give this lane integrator rights over app entry files and test plans, because otherwise nobody owns the final “does this whole thing still work together” pass.

I would keep file ownership brutally explicit. Something like:

`data_domain`
- `Provenance/App/Bootstrap/ModelContainerFactory.swift`
- `Provenance/Domain/Models/*`
- `Provenance/Domain/Enums/*`
- `Provenance/Domain/Rules/*`
- `Provenance/Domain/Protocols/ItemRepository.swift`
- `Provenance/Domain/Protocols/*`
- `Provenance/Tests/DomainTests/*`
- `Provenance/Tests/DataTests/*`

`primary_ui`
- `Provenance/App/Navigation/*`
- `Provenance/Features/Catalog/*`
- `Provenance/Features/Capture/*`
- `Provenance/Features/ItemDetail/*`
- `Provenance/Features/ExportPreview/*`
- `Provenance/Features/Paywall/*`
- `Provenance/Features/Settings/*`
- `Provenance/Widget/UI/*`
- `Provenance/Tests/UITests/*` for feature-flow cases, but not system regression plans

`services_utilities`
- `Provenance/Services/Vision/*`
- `Provenance/Services/Photos/*`
- `Provenance/Services/Export/*`
- `Provenance/Services/Entitlements/*`
- `Provenance/Services/AppLock/*`
- `Provenance/Services/Summaries/*`
- `Provenance/Services/WidgetReload/*`
- `Provenance/Tests/ServiceTests/*`

`polish_resilience`
- `Provenance/App/ProvenanceApp.swift`
- `Provenance/App/Root/*`
- `Provenance/App/Privacy/*`
- `Provenance/Resilience/*`
- `Provenance/Tests/IntegrationTests/*`
- shared QA docs under `Provenance/Docs/QA/*`

Forbidden edit zones matter more than branch names. `primary_ui` should be forbidden from editing `Domain/Rules`, `Domain/Models`, and service implementations. `services_utilities` should be forbidden from editing feature views and navigation. `data_domain` should not edit screen files. `polish_resilience` gets narrow integrator exceptions in app entry and test harness files, but not free rein over feature code. If a task needs a new seam, the owner of the seam adds it; consumers do not patch around it locally.

I’d use branch names that encode lane and task, for example `provenance/data-domain/T-001-domain-contracts`, `[REDACTED:high_entropy]`, and so on. Merge strategy should be short-lived branches merged to a shared integration branch `provenance/integration` first, not directly to `main`. Rebase before merge, no long-lived divergence, no “fix conflicts in Xcode project files later.” If `.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` changes are needed, one lane at a time, preferably the integrator lane batching target membership changes, because that file is where fake parallelism goes to die.

The handoff order should be hard-gated. `data_domain` publishes contracts and pure rules first. `services_utilities` can start concrete implementations once those protocols/types exist. `primary_ui` starts with stubbed protocol-backed feature models as soon as the interfaces are frozen, but should not hardcode business behavior while waiting. `polish_resilience` starts as soon as the app boots, wiring privacy/lock lifecycle and building regression scaffolding early rather than waiting for “polish.” The important detail to preserve is that app-lock and privacy overlay are not late cleanup work; they must exist early so capture interruption, foreground return, and export preview are tested against the real lifecycle.

Biggest objection to my own plan: the `polish_resilience` lane is easy to under-define and turn into “cleanup later,” which would be a failure here. If that lane does not own concrete files, concrete tests, and app-entry integration, it becomes a dumping ground instead of a build lane. The final phase output needs to preserve that this lane owns root integration and regression, not just vague QA.

How we know it works is not “the screens render.” Each lane needs acceptance tied to observable behavior: domain rules with deterministic tests, services with real fixture-backed behavior, UI with full state coverage, and resilience with downgrade/offline/background/export regression. Communication should be lightweight but strict: when a lane changes a published interface, it posts the file list, the signature diff, and which downstream tasks are affected. No silent shape changes. Conflict prevention should be one shared contract file set, one owner per directory, integration merges at least daily, and a standing rule that if two lanes think they need the same file, ownership was defined badly and must be corrected before coding continues.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Let me ground this in the systems/data reality before anyone starts writing Swift, because the biggest risk in this phase isn't the feature list — it's four people editing one Xcode project without stepping on each other's `.pbxproj`, and a shared `Domain/Protocols/` folder that six different protocols all need to live in without collision.

First, location: I can't browse `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/` from this sandbox (only `.agent_cwd` is visible to me here), but per the parent portfolio's "one folder per app" rule and the established convention, the actual Xcode project belongs at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/provenance/Provenance/` — sibling to this `.agent_cwd` orchestration scratch dir, not inside it. That's `Provenance.xcodeproj` (or generated project), a `Provenance/` app target, a `ProvenanceWidget/` extension target, and `ProvenanceTests`/`ProvenanceUITests` bundles. Whoever executes the build tasks needs to actually create that folder — I'm stating it as the agreed location, not confirming it exists yet.

Second, and this is the concrete pragmatist objection: four lanes cannot hand-edit one `project.pbxproj` in parallel without constant merge pain — that file is a giant ordered plist that git treats as one blob. My strong recommendation is to generate the project with XcodeGen from a `project.yml` (or Tuist, but XcodeGen is lighter for one app) so target membership is derived from folder structure, not hand-maintained — new files just need to exist under the right directory and a `xcodegen generate` regenerates the project deterministically. If we're on Xcode 16+, synchronized root groups (`fileSystemSynchronizedGroups`) give the same effect natively. Either way, nobody should be hand-editing the project file as their day-to-day workflow. I want this decided now because retrofitting it after four lanes have already made ad hoc edits is exactly the kind of "discovered under pressure" problem earlier phases kept trying to avoid.

Third, file ownership. The tech_specs interfaces-json already assigned every type an `owning_lane` — I'm not re-litigating that, just turning it into folders:

- **data_domain** owns `Domain/Models/` (Item, ConditionEntry, ValueEntry, AppSettings, the four enums, PhotoAssetRef, PurchaseFacts), `Domain/DisplayState/` (ItemDisplayState, computeDisplayState, partitionActiveAndLocked, CollectionSummary, ExportMode), `Domain/Protocols/ItemRepository.swift`, `Data/SwiftDataItemRepository.swift`, `Tests/DomainTests/`, `Tests/DataTests/`.
- **services_utilities** owns `Domain/Protocols/ClassificationService.swift`, `Domain/Protocols/EntitlementProvider.swift`, `Domain/Protocols/PhotoStore.swift`, `Domain/Protocols/ExportService.swift`, `Domain/Protocols/AppLockService.swift` (yes, these physically sit in the same `Domain/Protocols/` directory as data_domain's `ItemRepository.swift` — that's fine, ownership is per-file not per-directory, nobody edits a file outside their list), plus `Services/` for every concrete implementation, `Tests/ServiceTests/`, and the Vision fixture/label-mapping harness.
- **primary_ui** owns `App/ProvenanceApp.swift` and `App/RootView.swift` (composes the lock gate + privacy overlay, doesn't implement them), all of `Features/` (Catalog, Capture, ItemDetail, Export, Paywall, Settings — view + view model per folder), the shared component set (PhotoCard, CategoryChip, EffectiveStateBadge, TimelineEntryRow, LockedTeaserCard, UndoToast, EmptyStateView, PaywallPreviewCard), and the Widget UI/TimelineProvider (data comes from polish_resilience's provider, rendering is primary_ui's).
- **polish_resilience** owns `Polish/CollectionSummaryProvider.swift`, `Polish/WidgetReloadCoordinator.swift`, `Polish/RecoveryPurgeScheduler.swift`, `Polish/PrivacyOverlayModifier.swift`, both `.entitlements` files, `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`, the `.storekit` config, and `Tests/UITests/` (the narrow XCUITest set already agreed: capture timing, interrupted capture, PDF matrix, VoiceOver full loop).

Forbidden zones: nobody edits another lane's files directly. If primary_ui needs a repository method that doesn't exist, that's a blocking dependency to flag, not a reason to reach into `Data/SwiftDataItemRepository.swift` themselves — the interfaces-json method set is already complete, so this should rarely happen, but it's the rule. `App/ProvenanceApp.swift` and the generated project file are the two "integrator" touchpoints — small, boring, and reviewed at each milestone boundary rather than owned exclusively by one lane.

Fourth, sequencing has to follow the already-agreed critical path — I'm not reopening milestone order, just turning it into a dependency graph so build workers know what blocks what: infra/domain/data-seam first (unblocks everyone), then capture, then detail + safety in parallel, then export+paywall, then monetization/widget/hardening. Tasks are sized to fit inside a lane's milestone window, not across it.

Fifth, how we know it works: each lane's tests are the acceptance gate for its own tasks (DomainTests/DataTests for data_domain, ServiceTests for services_utilities), and the cross-cutting XCUITest suite in polish_resilience is the integration gate that nobody merges past without. A shared, append-only `INTEGRATION_NOTES.md` at the project root is where any lane logs a shared-type change or a cross-lane blocker — nobody edits it destructively, only appends, so it can't itself become a merge-conflict file.

```tasks-json

{"id": "T-001", "title": "Project scaffold: XcodeGen project.yml, multi-target layout (app + widget extension + unit/UI test bundles), folder skeleton matching lane ownership", "owner_lane": "data_domain", "files": ["project.yml", "Provenance.xcodeproj", "Domain/", "Data/", "Services/", "Features/", "Polish/", "Widget/", "Tests/"], "depends_on": [], "acceptance_criteria": ["xcodegen generate produces a project that builds an empty app + empty widget target", "Folder skeleton matches the four-lane ownership map exactly, no stray top-level files", "project.pbxproj is generated, never hand-edited going forward"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-002", "title": "Domain models and fixed enums (Item, ConditionEntry, ValueEntry, AppSettings, Category, ItemStatus, ConditionGrade, ValueType, PhotoAssetRef, PurchaseFacts)", "owner_lane": "data_domain", "files": ["Domain/Models/Item.swift", "Domain/Models/ConditionEntry.swift", "Domain/Models/ValueEntry.swift", "Domain/Models/AppSettings.swift", "Domain/Models/Enums.swift", "Domain/Models/PhotoAssetRef.swift", "Domain/Models/PurchaseFacts.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-001"], "acceptance_criteria": ["All @Model types compile under Swift 6 strict concurrency", "creationSequence exists as the mandatory active-25 tiebreak", "amount/purchaseAmount fields are Decimal, never Double or Int", "Category/ItemStatus/ConditionGrade/ValueType match the locked fixed enum cases exactly"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-003", "title": "Pure display-state and partition functions with exhaustive unit tests", "owner_lane": "data_domain", "files": ["Domain/DisplayState/ItemDisplayState.swift", "Domain/DisplayState/ComputeDisplayState.swift", "Domain/DisplayState/PartitionActiveAndLocked.swift", "Domain/CollectionSummary.swift", "Domain/ExportMode.swift", "Tests/DomainTests/DisplayStateTests.swift", "Tests/DomainTests/PartitionTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-002"], "acceptance_criteria": ["computeDisplayState precedence verified: deleted > locked > incomplete > normal", "partitionActiveAndLocked is O(n), tested with tied creationSequence values", "restore-at-cap and delete-of-locked-item-promotes-next-oldest covered by parametrized tests", "zero UI or SwiftData dependency in these files"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-004", "title": "Concrete SwiftData-backed ItemRepository (full method set) tested against real in-memory persistence", "owner_lane": "data_domain", "files": ["Domain/Protocols/ItemRepository.swift", "Data/SwiftDataItemRepository.swift", "Tests/DataTests/ItemRepositoryTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-003"], "acceptance_criteria": ["loadActiveAndLocked delegates to partitionActiveAndLocked, never reimplements the split", "All ItemRepository methods from interfaces-json implemented and tested against ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)", "softDelete/restore/purgeExpiredDeletedItems verified with injected dates, not wall-clock waiting", "App Group container path used for the ModelContainer, confirmed via a write-then-read-from-widget-target smoke test"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-005", "title": "Disk-backed PhotoStore protocol + implementation", "owner_lane": "services_utilities", "files": ["Domain/Protocols/PhotoStore.swift", "Services/DiskPhotoStore.swift", "Tests/ServiceTests/PhotoStoreTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-001"], "acceptance_criteria": ["All paths relative to App Group container root", "Thumbnails generated off-main via byPreparingThumbnail", "Returns PhotoAssetRef with real pixel dimensions, never a bare path string", "removeAssets tested for both item-level and entry-level cleanup"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-006", "title": "AppLockService (LocalAuthentication) — rough, unstyled gate", "owner_lane": "services_utilities", "files": ["Domain/Protocols/AppLockService.swift", "Services/LocalAuthenticationAppLockService.swift", "Tests/ServiceTests/AppLockServiceTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-001"], "acceptance_criteria": ["deviceAuthenticationAvailable() returns false and access is allowed (never trapped) when no device passcode is set", "authenticate() tested via a fake for both success and failure paths"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-007", "title": "App Group entitlements, PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy, PrivacyOverlayModifier, widget target skeleton, App-Group write/read smoke test", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["Provenance.entitlements", "ProvenanceWidget.entitlements", "PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy", "Polish/PrivacyOverlayModifier.swift", "Widget/ProvenanceWidgetBundle.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-001"], "acceptance_criteria": ["App writes to the shared App Group container and the widget target reads the same data in a smoke test", "PrivacyOverlayModifier swaps in on scenePhase background transition, verified in isolation", "Required-reason API usage declared in the privacy manifest"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-008", "title": "App entry point + RootView composing lock gate and privacy overlay", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["App/ProvenanceApp.swift", "App/RootView.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-004", "T-006", "T-007"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Cold launch and return-from-background show the lock screen only when device auth is available", "PrivacyOverlayModifier applied at root, not per-screen", "ModelContainer bootstrapped once from the App Group path and injected via environment"], "status": "pending"},

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Both of them independently arrived at the same four-lane skeleton — `data_domain` for models/enums/pure rules/repository protocol, `services_utilities` for Vision/photos/export/entitlements/app-lock implementations, `primary_ui` for every feature screen plus widget UI, `polish_resilience` for root app lifecycle, privacy overlay, purge scheduling, and the cross-cutting integration/regression test suite. Nobody disagrees on that division, on the "app-lock and privacy wire in early, not late" carryover from earlier phases, or on the idea that `Domain/Protocols/` can hold files from two different owning lanes as long as file-level (not folder-level) ownership is respected.

But there are a few real gaps that haven't been reconciled yet, not just restated differently:

1. **Project tooling fork Codex hasn't seen yet.** Claude proposed generating the Xcode project via XcodeGen (or Xcode 16 synchronized root groups) specifically so nobody hand-edits `project.pbxproj` day-to-day. Codex's round assumed hand-editing the pbxproj is fine as long as it's "one lane at a time." That's a real, concrete difference in how new files get added to targets, and Codex needs to react to it.
2. **Task granularity mismatch.** Codex submitted 6 broad tasks (roughly one per milestone/lane), Claude submitted 24 granular tasks that map tightly to the tech_specs interfaces-json and the milestone plan's verification items. These aren't contradictory, but they can't both be "the" backlog — round 2 needs to converge on one canonical tasks-json rather than have two competing ones.
3. **Branch/merge mechanics only came from one side.** Codex proposed branch naming (`provenance/lane/task-id`) and a shared `provenance/integration` branch merged-to-before-main; Claude didn't address this at all. Needs explicit agreement or a counter-proposal.
4. **Exact project path.** Claude proposed `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/provenance/Provenance/` as the concrete Xcode project location; Codex didn't commit to a specific path. Small, but should be nailed down explicitly rather than assumed.

None of this is a deep disagreement — it's mostly one side having proposed something concrete that the other hasn't confirmed or pushed back on yet. Next round should have Codex react to the XcodeGen proposal and the exact path, and the two of them should merge into one backlog (probably closer to Claude's granularity, since it ties directly to already-locked interfaces-json/milestone content, but Codex should get to weigh in on that call) plus settle branch/merge naming as a pair.

CONSENSUS: NO

## Final Output (provisional — pending round 2 reconciliation)

Four lanes (`data_domain`, `primary_ui`, `services_utilities`, `polish_resilience`) with file ownership matching the tech_specs `owning_lane` assignments; forbidden cross-lane edits except at named integrator seams (`App/ProvenanceApp.swift`, the generated project file); app-lock/privacy wired in from lane one, not deferred. Still open: project-generation tooling (XcodeGen vs. hand-edited pbxproj), one merged tasks-json at agreed granularity, and explicit branch/merge conventions.

### Round 2


**Codex — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex returned empty output — refusing to fabricate a response. See logs/._

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Coordinator's read of round one is right, and I want to hit the open items directly rather than re-litigate ground we already agree on. Codex — you and I landed on the same four lanes independently, which is a good signal, but there are three concrete places where your list actually says something different from mine, not just phrased differently, and I want to call them out by name instead of hand-waving past them.

First, the pbxproj fight. You said "if `.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` changes are needed, one lane at a time, preferably the integrator lane batching target membership changes." I get the instinct, but think about what that actually means operationally: every single new file, in every lane, for the entire build, funnels through one serialization point that isn't even a real lock — it's a social convention ("don't touch it if someone else is touching it") enforced by agents that can't literally see each other's live edits. That's not "one lane at a time," that's "whoever commits last wins and everyone else rebases a giant ordered plist by hand and prays." I'm not proposing XcodeGen as a nice-to-have, I'm proposing it because it deletes the entire class of conflict instead of managing it — a `project.yml` that lists target names and folder globs, and `xcodegen generate` regenerates the pbxproj deterministically from whatever files exist on disk. Nobody edits the pbxproj, ever, by hand. New file appears in `Features/Capture/` → next generate picks it up → done. Given this costs us maybe twenty minutes of one-time setup in T-001 and removes a recurring merge headache for the rest of the build, I don't think this is a close call, and I'd like you to just say yes to it rather than us defaulting to the riskier manual-serialization plan by omission.

Second — and this is a real error, not a style difference — your T-001 file list puts `Provenance/Domain/Protocols/ExportContracts.swift` under `data_domain`, and your prose gives `data_domain` a wildcard claim over `Provenance/Domain/Protocols/*`. That directly contradicts the interfaces-json we both inherited from tech_specs: `ExportService`, `ExportSection`, `ExportDocumentModel`, and `ExportPagePreview` are all explicitly `owning_lane: services_utilities`, same as `ClassificationService`, `EntitlementProvider`, `PhotoStore`, and `AppLockService`. Only `ItemRepository` is data_domain's protocol. If we let data_domain claim the whole `Domain/Protocols/` directory by wildcard, we've recreated exactly the ownership ambiguity I was trying to kill — two lanes both thinking they own the same folder. My fix, which I already had in my round-1 list and want to reconfirm explicitly now: file-level ownership only, never directory wildcards for content, even when multiple lanes' files physically sit in the same folder. `data_domain` gets `Domain/Protocols/ItemRepository.swift` and nothing else in that folder; `services_utilities` gets the other five protocol files there. I'll take the scaffold task's use of `Domain/`, `Services/`, etc. as directory placeholders — that's only for creating the empty skeleton in T-001, not a content claim, and I want that distinction explicit so nobody reads it as blanket ownership later.

Third, you're right about something I missed: splitting `Tests/UITests/` so both primary_ui and polish_resilience write into the same folder name is exactly the kind of soft collision I was warning about — even though the underlying files would differ, it's needlessly fragile. I like your instinct to give primary_ui its own lightweight feature-flow test files, separate from the deep integration/regression suite. So let's split it cleanly: primary_ui owns `Tests/FeatureUITests/` for basic per-screen smoke checks ("can add an item and see it in the list," not timing/lock-interaction/export-matrix depth), and polish_resilience owns `Tests/IntegrationTests/` for everything that requires cross-lane, scenario-based verification — capture timing, downgrade/restore scenarios, the export matrix, StoreKit sandbox, VoiceOver, final regression. That's a real improvement over what I had, credit where it's due.

On your `ModelContainerFactory.swift` under data_domain — I actually think that's correct and I should have had it in my list. The factory needs to know the full model schema (every `@Model` type) and the App Group container path, both of which are data_domain's concern; primary_ui's `RootView.swift` should call it, not reimplement it. I'm folding that file into T-004 as a data_domain deliverable, consumed by T-008.

On branch/merge mechanics, since I didn't address them last round and you did — I'll take your proposal and firm up the parts you left loose: `provenance/<lane>/T-XXX-<slug>` branch names, merged into a shared `provenance/integration` branch (never straight to `main`), rebase before merge so history stays linear, and because the pbxproj is now generated rather than hand-maintained, a "conflict" there just means regenerate-and-recommit, not manual reconciliation. Integration branch promotes to `main` at milestone boundaries, gated on that milestone's `Tests/IntegrationTests/` suite passing — which lines up cleanly with the critical path already locked in project_plan (M1 → M2 → M3/M4 parallel → M5 → M6).

And to close the loop on location: nobody's actually disputing `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/provenance/Provenance/` as the project root, Codex just didn't commit to a specific path in his message — I'll take that as tacit agreement unless he says otherwise next round, since restating it isn't a real disagreement.

{"id": "T-004", "title": "Concrete SwiftData-backed ItemRepository (full method set) + App Group ModelContainer factory, tested against real in-memory persistence", "owner_lane": "data_domain", "files": ["Domain/Protocols/ItemRepository.swift", "Data/SwiftDataItemRepository.swift", "Data/ModelContainerFactory.swift", "Tests/DataTests/ItemRepositoryTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-003"], "acceptance_criteria": ["loadActiveAndLocked delegates to partitionActiveAndLocked, never reimplements the split", "All ItemRepository methods from interfaces-json implemented and tested against ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)", "softDelete/restore/purgeExpiredDeletedItems verified with injected dates, not wall-clock waiting", "ModelContainerFactory builds the container at the App Group path from the full model schema, confirmed via a write-then-read-from-widget-target smoke test"], "status": "pending"},

{"id": "T-006", "title": "AppLockService (LocalAuthentication) — rough, unstyled gate", "owner_lane": "services_utilities", "files": ["Domain/Protocols/AppLockService.swift", "Services/LocalAuthenticationAppLockService.swift", "Tests/ServiceTests/AppLockServiceTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-001"], "acceptance_criteria": ["deviceAuthenticationAvailable() returns false and access is allowed (never trapped) when no device passcode is set", "authenticate() tested via a fake for both success and failure paths"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-007", "title": "App Group entitlements, PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy, PrivacyOverlayModifier, widget target skeleton, App-Group write/read smoke test", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["Provenance.entitlements", "ProvenanceWidget.entitlements", "PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy", "Polish/PrivacyOverlayModifier.swift", "Widget/ProvenanceWidgetBundle.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-001"], "acceptance_criteria": ["App writes to the shared App Group container and the widget target reads the same data in a smoke test", "PrivacyOverlayModifier swaps in on scenePhase background transition, verified in isolation", "Required-reason API usage declared in the privacy manifest"], "status": "pending"},

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Codex's CLI was down again this round, so it's just Claude talking to herself — but importantly, what she did wasn't restate her own position, it was go through the three gaps I flagged and actually resolve them, and two of the three aren't really "one side's opinion vs another's" anymore.

The `ExportContracts.swift`/`Domain/Protocols/*` wildcard issue is a real bug fix, not a preference: Codex's round-1 file list gave `data_domain` a wildcard claim over the whole `Domain/Protocols/` folder, but the interfaces-json locked back in tech_specs explicitly assigns `ExportService`, `PhotoStore`, `EntitlementProvider`, `ClassificationService`, and `AppLockService` to `services_utilities` — only `ItemRepository` belongs to `data_domain`. Claude caught that this would recreate the exact two-lanes-own-one-folder ambiguity the whole plan is trying to avoid, and fixed it by going file-level instead of folder-level. That's not a new opinion, it's correcting Codex's list against a contract he himself helped write earlier in the process.

The XcodeGen proposal is a genuine one-sided ask still waiting on Codex, but the reasoning for it is concrete enough (pbxproj as a giant ordered plist with no real lock, vs. a generated file nobody touches by hand) that I don't think it's a coin-flip call — and it costs about twenty minutes of setup with no real downside anyone's identified. Branch/merge naming, the Tests split (`Tests/FeatureUITests/` for primary_ui's smoke checks vs `Tests/IntegrationTests/` for polish_resilience's cross-lane scenario suite), and the exact project path are all things Codex either proposed or left unaddressed and Claude adopted/filled in without contradiction.

Given Codex has now missed two rounds in a row and nothing in Claude's round-2 output overrides a position Codex actually defended (it either fixes an objective error against the locked interfaces-json or fills a gap he left open), I'm treating this as converged rather than stalling the phase further waiting on a tool that isn't coming back.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Four lanes, file-level (never folder-level) ownership: `data_domain` owns domain models/enums, the pure display-state/partition functions, the `ItemRepository` protocol + SwiftData implementation, and the `ModelContainerFactory`. `services_utilities` owns every other protocol in `Domain/Protocols/` (`PhotoStore`, `AppLockService`, `ClassificationService`, `ExportService`, `EntitlementProvider`) plus their concrete implementations. `primary_ui` owns all feature screens/view models, shared UI components, widget UI, and a lightweight `Tests/FeatureUITests/` smoke suite per screen. `polish_resilience` owns app entry/root composition, privacy overlay, purge scheduler, App Group/entitlements/privacy-manifest setup, and the deep `Tests/IntegrationTests/` suite (timing, downgrade/restore scenarios, export matrix, StoreKit sandbox, VoiceOver, final regression).

Project lives at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/provenance/Provenance/`, generated via XcodeGen from `project.yml` — nobody hand-edits `project.pbxproj`; new files just need to land in the right folder and a regenerate picks them up. Branches follow `provenance/<lane>/T-XXX-<slug>`, merged into a shared `provenance/integration` branch (never straight to `main`) via rebase, promoted to `main` at milestone boundaries gated on that milestone's integration-test suite passing. App-lock and privacy overlay are wired in rough-but-real from the very first task, not deferred to a later "safety" milestone, so every later milestone's manual verification happens inside the real trust envelope. Sequencing follows the already-locked critical path: infra/domain/data seam first, then capture, then detail/safety in parallel, then export+paywall, then monetization/widget/hardening.

{"id": "T-011", "title": "Catalog Home: active list, Preserved/Locked shelf, empty/loading/success/error states", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["Features/Catalog/CatalogView.swift", "Features/Catalog/CatalogViewModel.swift", "Features/Shared/EffectiveStateBadge.swift", "Features/Shared/EmptyStateView.swift", "Tests/FeatureUITests/CatalogSmokeUITests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-004", "T-008"], "acceptance_criteria": ["All four states reachable and visually distinct", "Preserved/Locked section always visible, never interleaved as dimmed rows", "Every card's badge driven by ItemDisplayState, no ad hoc per-screen state checks"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-012", "title": "Capture-to-save timing + interrupted-capture integration test (with app-lock foreground-return interaction)", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["Tests/IntegrationTests/CaptureFlowIntegrationTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-010"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Automated timing assertion: tap-Add to saved-item well under 60s", "Backgrounded/force-quit capture verified resumable, tested specifically alongside the app-lock foreground-return interaction"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-013", "title": "Item Detail trust layer: purchase facts, condition/provenance timeline, value ledger, locked teaser, near-empty states", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["Features/ItemDetail/ItemDetailView.swift", "Features/ItemDetail/ItemDetailViewModel.swift", "Features/ItemDetail/PurchaseFactsEditorView.swift", "Features/ItemDetail/ConditionEntrySheet.swift", "Features/ItemDetail/ValueEntrySheet.swift", "Features/Shared/TimelineEntryRow.swift", "Features/Shared/LockedTeaserCard.swift", "Tests/FeatureUITests/ItemDetailSmokeUITests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-004", "T-011"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Three modules render as visually separate, never merged into one feed", "Cover photo distinct from timeline-entry photos", "Value module renders locked-teaser vs live vs read-only-preserved correctly per subscription/downgrade state", "Full VoiceOver pass across the module stack"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-014", "title": "Recently Deleted + Preserved/Locked read-only list + undo toast", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["Features/Catalog/RecentlyDeletedView.swift", "Features/Catalog/PreservedLockedListView.swift", "Features/Shared/UndoToast.swift", "Tests/FeatureUITests/RecentlyDeletedSmokeUITests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-011"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Delete fires an inline Undo toast immediately, independent of navigating to Recently Deleted", "Recently Deleted reached via overflow/toolbar affordance, not a home-screen scroll section", "Attempted edit on a locked item shows the upgrade prompt, never the repair flow"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-015", "title": "RecoveryPurgeScheduler + downgrade/restore-at-cap/promotion scenario integration tests", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["Polish/RecoveryPurgeScheduler.swift", "Tests/IntegrationTests/SafetyIntegrityIntegrationTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-004"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Purge runs only at launch/foreground with an injected now, never a background task scheduler", "Downgrade-with->25-items, restore-at-cap, and delete-of-locked-item-promotes-next-oldest all verified end to end"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-016", "title": "ExportService: main-actor document snapshot mapping, off-main PDF rendering, native paged preview generation", "owner_lane": "services_utilities", "files": ["Domain/Protocols/ExportService.swift", "Services/PDFExportService.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-004"], "acceptance_criteria": ["buildDocumentModel completes all SwiftData reads/mapping on the main actor before handing off to the background renderer", "renderPDF never touches PDFView; preview pages generated as rasterized images with explicit page-position metadata", "Concurrency-safety test: mutate a value entry mid-render, exported document reflects the pre-mutation snapshot"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-017", "title": "Export Preview screen: mode toggle, paged accessible preview, all named states", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["Features/Export/ExportPreviewView.swift", "Features/Export/ExportPreviewViewModel.swift", "Features/Shared/ExportPagePlaceholder.swift", "Tests/FeatureUITests/ExportPreviewSmokeUITests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-016"], "acceptance_criteria": ["TabView(.page) preview announces Page N of M correctly to VoiceOver", "Zero-item collection export renders a real sparse page, never blank", "Generating/failed/locked-upsell states all reachable"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-018", "title": "EntitlementProvider (StoreKit 2): CachedEntitlement, EntitlementState, isSubscriptionActive, transaction listener", "owner_lane": "services_utilities", "files": ["Domain/Protocols/EntitlementProvider.swift", "Services/StoreKitEntitlementProvider.swift", "Tests/ServiceTests/EntitlementProviderTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-001"], "acceptance_criteria": ["isSubscriptionActive is the only conversion point from EntitlementState to Bool, verified no other lane inlines its own switch", "unknownOffline never silently collapses to free by omission", "Listener task started once at launch"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-019", "title": "Paywall screen with real redacted export mock built from the actual export pipeline", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["Features/Paywall/PaywallView.swift", "Features/Paywall/PaywallViewModel.swift", "Features/Shared/PaywallPreviewCard.swift", "Tests/FeatureUITests/PaywallSmokeUITests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-016", "T-018"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Redacted mock uses real section headers/photo placement/typography from ExportDocumentModel, never a placeholder image", "StoreKit product-fetch failure shows a retry state, never a silently broken paywall", "Restore-purchases link present and functional against EntitlementProvider"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-020", "title": "Full export verification matrix + VoiceOver page-position integration test", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["Tests/IntegrationTests/ExportVerificationIntegrationTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-017"], "acceptance_criteria": ["0/1-sparse/1-dense/many-photo item counts all verified structurally correct", "Temp export files confirmed cleaned up after share-sheet completion or dismissal"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-021", "title": "CollectionSummaryProvider + WidgetReloadCoordinator", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["Polish/CollectionSummaryProvider.swift", "Polish/WidgetReloadCoordinator.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-004", "T-018"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Single source of truth for widget and any in-app aggregate value display, never computed twice independently", "Widget reload debounced/coalesced on local write events, not fired per-save"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-022", "title": "Widget UI: free (item count) vs paid (collection value) states", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["Widget/ProvenanceWidget.swift", "Widget/WidgetViews.swift", "Widget/WidgetTimelineProvider.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-021"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Free tier shows item count, paid tier shows collection value, no other content", "Tap-to-open only, no deep-linking or in-widget actions", "Respects the same privacy-overlay discipline as the rest of the app"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-023", "title": "StoreKit SKTestSession scenario suite: purchase/restore/downgrade/offline-startup", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["Tests/IntegrationTests/StoreKitScenarioIntegrationTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-018", "T-019"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Offline-startup unknownOffline verified to never silently collapse to free", "Downgrade preserves data (hides, never deletes) and resubscribe restores full access with no migration step"], "status": "pending"},
{"id": "T-024", "title": "Final end-to-end regression pass", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["Tests/IntegrationTests/FullRegressionIntegrationTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-012", "T-015", "T-020", "T-022", "T-023"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Relaunch persistence, interrupted capture, full VoiceOver core loop, PDF correctness at 0/1/many items, and purge scheduler all verified together in one pass", "No regression in any earlier milestone's verified behavior"], "status": "pending"}
]}
```

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase. All four required outputs are present in substance, even though not every one carries a literal heading — per the rubric, that's not a failing reason on its own.

- **Lane-by-lane responsibilities**: explicitly stated for all four lanes (`data_domain` = models/pure functions/`ItemRepository`; `services_utilities` = every other `Domain/Protocols/*` conformance; `primary_ui` = feature screens/view models/widget UI/smoke tests; `polish_resilience` = app entry/root composition, privacy overlay, purge scheduler, App Group setup, and the deep integration-test suite).
- **Owned files**: resolved at file level, not folder level — the round-1 bug where `data_domain` held a wildcard claim over all of `Domain/Protocols/` was caught and fixed against the already-locked interfaces-json (`ItemRepository` stays `data_domain`; `PhotoStore`/`AppLockService`/`ClassificationService`/`ExportService`/`EntitlementProvider` correctly move to `services_utilities`). Each task's `files` list is now a disjoint set with no cross-task collisions, and `T-001`'s directory skeletons are explicitly disclaimed as non-content-owning so they don't recreate the ambiguity.
- **Handoffs and forbidden edit zones**: functionally covered — the file-level ownership map is itself the forbidden-zone definition (a file not listed under your task is off-limits), and the branch/merge protocol (`provenance/<lane>/T-XXX-<slug>` → `provenance/integration` via rebase → `main` at milestone boundaries gated on integration tests) defines the handoff mechanism and where lane work reconverges.
- **tasks-json backlog**: present, well-formed, and satisfies the acceptance checks — 24 tasks, every task has a valid `owner_lane` from the fixed set, no ownerless tasks, dependencies reference only existing task ids, and the graph is acyclic (topological order is consistent with ascending ids; e.g. T-024 depends only on earlier T-012/T-015/T-020/T-022/T-023).

Sequencing correctly reflects the project_plan critical path (infra/domain/data seam → capture → detail/safety in parallel → export+paywall → monetization/widget/hardening), and the app-lock/privacy-overlay-early decision from earlier phases is preserved in task ordering (T-006/T-007 before T-008, and T-008 gating the capture/catalog UI tasks). Ready to close.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Codex's CLI was down again this round, so it's just Claude talking to herself — but importantly, what she did wasn't restate her own position, it was go through the three gaps I flagged and actually resolve them, and two of the three aren't really "one side's opinion vs another's" anymore.

The `ExportContracts.swift`/`Domain/Protocols/*` wildcard issue is a real bug fix, not a preference: Codex's round-1 file list gave `data_domain` a wildcard claim over the whole `Domain/Protocols/` folder, but the interfaces-json locked back in tech_specs explicitly assigns `ExportService`, `PhotoStore`, `EntitlementProvider`, `ClassificationService`, and `AppLockService` to `services_utilities` — only `ItemRepository` belongs to `data_domain`. Claude caught that this would recreate the exact two-lanes-own-one-folder ambiguity the whole plan is trying to avoid, and fixed it by going file-level instead of folder-level. That's not a new opinion, it's correcting Codex's list against a contract he himself helped write earlier in the process.

The XcodeGen proposal is a genuine one-sided ask still waiting on Codex, but the reasoning for it is concrete enough (pbxproj as a giant ordered plist with no real lock, vs. a generated file nobody touches by hand) that I don't think it's a coin-flip call — and it costs about twenty minutes of setup with no real downside anyone's identified. Branch/merge naming, the Tests split (`Tests/FeatureUITests/` for primary_ui's smoke checks vs `Tests/IntegrationTests/` for polish_resilience's cross-lane scenario suite), and the exact project path are all things Codex either proposed or left unaddressed and Claude adopted/filled in without contradiction.

Given Codex has now missed two rounds in a row and nothing in Claude's round-2 output overrides a position Codex actually defended (it either fixes an objective error against the locked interfaces-json or fills a gap he left open), I'm treating this as converged rather than stalling the phase further waiting on a tool that isn't coming back.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Four lanes, file-level (never folder-level) ownership: `data_domain` owns domain models/enums, the pure display-state/partition functions, the `ItemRepository` protocol + SwiftData implementation, and the `ModelContainerFactory`. `services_utilities` owns every other protocol in `Domain/Protocols/` (`PhotoStore`, `AppLockService`, `ClassificationService`, `ExportService`, `EntitlementProvider`) plus their concrete implementations. `primary_ui` owns all feature screens/view models, shared UI components, widget UI, and a lightweight `Tests/FeatureUITests/` smoke suite per screen. `polish_resilience` owns app entry/root composition, privacy overlay, purge scheduler, App Group/entitlements/privacy-manifest setup, and the deep `Tests/IntegrationTests/` suite (timing, downgrade/restore scenarios, export matrix, StoreKit sandbox, VoiceOver, final regression).

Project lives at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/provenance/Provenance/`, generated via XcodeGen from `project.yml` — nobody hand-edits `project.pbxproj`; new files just need to land in the right folder and a regenerate picks them up. Branches follow `provenance/<lane>/T-XXX-<slug>`, merged into a shared `provenance/integration` branch (never straight to `main`) via rebase, promoted to `main` at milestone boundaries gated on that milestone's integration-test suite passing. App-lock and privacy overlay are wired in rough-but-real from the very first task, not deferred to a later "safety" milestone, so every later milestone's manual verification happens inside the real trust envelope. Sequencing follows the already-locked critical path: infra/domain/data seam first, then capture, then detail/safety in parallel, then export+paywall, then monetization/widget/hardening.

### Implementation Readiness Gate

# provenance — Implementation Readiness Gate

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 21:56:14._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Before any code is written, audit whether the product brief, design handoff, iOS architecture, tech specs, project plan, and task contracts are coherent enough for a one-shot production build. Resolve blockers or explicitly downgrade scope before build starts.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex returned empty output — refusing to fabricate a response. See logs/._

**Claude (Delivery Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Walking through this as the person who has to actually watch a real collector open this app for the first time, and as the person who has to sequence twenty-four tasks across four lanes without inventing anything mid-build — here's where I think this stack of decisions is genuinely solid, and where it's not.

The core loop is in good shape. Capture-to-save under a minute, Vision as a silent non-blocking suggestion, the three-module detail screen, soft-delete with an undo toast, the deleted/locked/incomplete precedence rule, the App Group container from day one — all of that is consistent from prompt_contract straight through to task_assignments, and I don't see a version of any of these decisions that contradicts an earlier one. That's the hard part of a spec like this and it's actually done.

But I found one real contradiction, not just a stylistic gap, and it's in the value chart. app_features locks it as a must-have: "Value chart — correct and type-differentiated, not yet interactive... correctness and honesty are must-have now." That's an explicit commitment to a rendered chart existing in this build. But ios_architecture_review and tech_specs both describe Swift Charts as "reserved for the (deferred) value chart" — language that reads as "not built now." And when I check the actual task backlog, there is no task anywhere in the 24 that builds a chart view — T-013's Item Detail trust layer lists `TimelineEntryRow.swift` and the value ledger sheet, but no `ValueChartView.swift` or equivalent. next_steps_small explicitly cut "rendered value-over-time chart" and substituted a plain list. So three different phases point in two different directions, and the task list actually implements the "no chart" version while a locked feature doc claims a chart is a must-have. If build starts today, whoever picks up T-013 has to guess which is true — that's exactly the kind of decision this gate exists to catch before code, not during code. My take: resolve this by downgrading app_features' item 4 to match what's actually buildable and already tasked — a correct, type-differentiated value ledger list is the v1 deliverable, the rendered Swift Charts view is explicitly future work. That's consistent with "smaller finished over larger half-built," and it means zero new tasks, just a correction to which document is authoritative.

Second gap, smaller but concrete: nobody owns creating the actual StoreKit testing configuration — the `.storekit` file with the two real subscription products (~$6.99/mo, $59/yr) in a subscription group. T-018 (EntitlementProvider) and T-023 (SKTestSession scenario suite) both depend on this existing, but it appears nowhere in any task's file list. Claude's very first lane description in this same phase's round 1 mentioned a `.storekit` config file under polish_resilience, and then it silently disappeared from every later draft. This needs a named owner and a task, or T-018/T-023 can't actually be verified against anything.

Third, smaller still but worth nailing down now rather than letting a build agent invent it: T-001's XcodeGen `project.yml` needs a concrete bundle identifier and App Group identifier before it can be generated. Nobody has proposed actual values. This is implementation detail, not a product decision, but "invent it during build" is exactly the failure mode this gate is supposed to prevent, so I'd rather see a placeholder convention agreed now (something like `com.[org].provenance` / `group.com.[org].provenance`) than have it improvised silently inside T-001.

One process note, not a blocker: the XcodeGen-over-hand-edited-pbxproj decision only ever got argued by one side — Codex's tool was down for both of the rounds where it mattered. I think the reasoning holds up on its own merits (a generated project file removes an entire class of merge conflict for close to zero cost), so I'm not treating it as unresolved, but it's fair to flag that it was never actually contested, just uncontested.

Everything else I checked lines up: every core workflow from the original prompt (capture, classify-assist, condition/provenance logging, value entries, export) has a corresponding task with a testable acceptance criterion; testing ownership is explicit and non-overlapping (DomainTests/DataTests/ServiceTests per producing lane, FeatureUITests for primary_ui's smoke checks, IntegrationTests for polish_resilience's cross-cutting scenarios); the "insurance-grade" language from the original prompt was correctly walked back everywhere downstream to "structurally professional, not legally certified," and I don't see it creeping back into any locked copy. The milestone-3/milestone-4 "parallel" framing in project_plan is a little generous — T-013 and T-014 are both owned by primary_ui, so within that lane they're still serial; the real parallelism is primary_ui's UI work against polish_resilience's scheduler/integration-test work, not two lanes both hitting milestone 4 simultaneously. Not a blocker, just don't oversell the calendar savings when the same lane is now not literally splitting itself, in whatever we tell the person running the build.

So: not ready to greenlight as-is, but the fixes are narrow and don't reopen any real product debate — correct the value-chart status to match what's actually tasked, name an owner and add a task for the StoreKit config file, and pin down a concrete bundle ID / App Group ID convention before T-001 runs. Everything else checks out clean enough that I'd call this close to done, not back to the drawing board.

But `ios_architecture_review` and `tech_specs` both talk about Swift Charts as reserved for later, and the actual 24-task backlog never builds a chart view at all — `next_steps_small` explicitly cut it in favor of a plain list. Codex still hasn't come back — two rounds down now — so I'm going to stop waiting and just resolve the three things I flagged, since they're mine to resolve and letting this phase stall on a silent CLI would be worse for the user than me making the calls. None of these are close judgment calls where I'd want a second opinion before committing; they're "pick the option that matches everything already agreed" decisions.

On the chart: I'm downgrading app_features item 4's language, not adding a task. The honest read of the paper trail is that `next_steps_small` cut the rendered chart first, `ios_architecture_review` and `tech_specs` both treated Swift Charts as "reserved for later" — meaning neither engineering-facing document ever actually committed to building it — and it was only `app_features`, written in between, that drifted back into calling it a must-have. Given two later, more technical documents and the actual 24-task backlog all agree on "list now, chart later," and given the global rule to prefer smaller finished scope, the fix is to correct app_features' wording to: the value ledger renders correctly with type-differentiated entries (purchase/appraisal/dealer offer/market observation/insured value visually distinguished, never one connected line) as the v1 must-have, and the rendered Swift Charts visualization is explicitly deferred, consistent with what T-013 already builds. I don't think this needs a second opinion to close — it's not really a 50/50 product call, it's noticing that the majority of the paper trail already agreed and one document hadn't caught up.

On the StoreKit config file — I said last round this needed an owner and it just vanished from every draft after my very first lane sketch, and that's still true, so I'm adding it as a real task rather than leaving it as a footnote. New task, call it T-025: "StoreKit configuration: Configuration.storekit file defining the Provenance+ subscription group with two products (monthly and annual) and matching local pricing," owned by `services_utilities` since it's the natural extension of T-018's entitlement work — whoever builds `StoreKitEntitlementProvider` needs the product identifiers to exist before they can even compile a `Product.products(for:)` call against them. I'm making T-018 depend on T-025 (swapping its current `depends_on: ["T-001"]` to include T-025), and T-023's SKTestSession suite already depends on T-018 so it inherits the config transitively — no other task needs to change. Concrete identifiers, so nobody invents them mid-build: subscription group "Provenance+", product IDs `com.pchordia.provenance.plus.monthly` and `com.pchordia.provenance.plus.annual`, priced to match the ~$6.99/mo and $59/yr already locked in the product brief.

On bundle ID and App Group — same logic, pick a concrete placeholder now instead of letting whoever runs T-001 improvise one. Bundle ID `com.pchordia.provenance`, App Group `group.com.pchordia.provenance`. That's a reasonable reverse-DNS convention given this is a personal/indie build under the iOS-App-Factory portfolio; if there's an existing Apple Developer Team ID or an established convention from the other apps in the portfolio (Waylay, TrueScale) that should take precedence, but absent that information, a named placeholder that's actually consistent (used identically in the app target, the widget target, and the entitlements files) beats each of those three surfaces getting a slightly different guess from three different lane workers.

With those three resolved, here's where I land on the actual gate output the phase needs. Readiness verdict: conditionally ready — the product, design, architecture, and task layers agree with each other everywhere except the three points above, and all three now have concrete fixes that require no new product debate, so build can start as soon as those fixes are folded into app_features and task_assignments. Cross-phase consistency check: core loop, capture timing, Vision-as-suggestion-never-gate, three-module detail screen, soft-delete/recovery, locked/incomplete/deleted precedence, App Group-from-day-one, no-PDFView-preview, file-level (not folder-level) lane ownership, and the "insurance-ready in structure, not legal status" language all check out clean end-to-end from prompt_contract through task_assignments — I re-verified this round rather than just asserting it, specifically re-reading task_assignments' file lists against the interfaces-json owning_lane assignments again, and I don't find a second instance of the Domain/Protocols-wildcard-style bug. Build blockers and fixes: the value-chart wording mismatch (fix: correct app_features to ledger-now/chart-later, matching what's tasked), the missing StoreKit config ownership (fix: new task T-025 under services_utilities, T-018 now depends on it), and the undefined bundle ID/App Group convention (fix: `com.pchordia.provenance` / `group.com.pchordia.provenance`, applied uniformly at T-001). Final scope for build is exactly the 24-task backlog plus T-025, with the corrected chart language — nothing added, nothing else cut. Acceptance criteria checklist is the union of every task's acceptance_criteria in task_assignments plus T-025's (StoreKit config loads successfully in a sandbox test session with both products resolvable by identifier), which collectively already cover every core workflow, every named screen's four states, and the cross-cutting trust guarantees (soft-delete, downgrade-preserves-data, offline entitlement continuity, export correctness at 0/1/many items, VoiceOver end-to-end).

## Final Output

**Readiness verdict:** Ready to build, conditional on three small fixes being folded into the docs before the first line of code — none of which require new product decisions or reopen anything already locked.

**Cross-phase consistency check:** Verified clean end-to-end from `prompt_contract` through `task_assignments` — the core loop (capture-to-save under a minute, Vision as a silent never-blocking suggestion, three separated detail modules, soft-delete with recovery, deleted>locked>incomplete precedence), the App-Group-from-day-one architecture, the no-`PDFView`-in-preview decision, file-level (never folder-level) lane ownership, and the "insurance-ready in structure, not legal status" language all agree with each other across every phase. Re-checked specifically for a repeat of the round-1 `Domain/Protocols` wildcard-ownership bug — none found.

**Build blockers and fixes (all resolved this phase):**
1. *Value chart says "must-have" in `app_features` but no task builds it and two later technical docs already treated it as deferred.* Fix: correct `app_features` item 4 to read as "value ledger renders correctly with type-differentiated entries (purchase/appraisal/dealer offer/market observation/insured value visually distinct, never one connected line) — v1 must-have; rendered Swift Charts visualization is explicitly deferred." Matches what T-013 already builds. No task changes.
2. *No task owned creating the `.storekit` test configuration that T-018 and T-023 both depend on.* Fix: new task **T-025** — "StoreKit configuration: `Configuration.storekit` file defining the Provenance+ subscription group with two products (monthly and annual)," owned by `services_utilities`, subscription group "Provenance+", product IDs `com.pchordia.provenance.plus.monthly` and `com.pchordia.provenance.plus.annual`, priced to match ~$6.99/mo and $59/yr. T-018's `depends_on` now includes T-025.
3. *No concrete bundle ID / App Group identifier for T-001's XcodeGen scaffold.* Fix: bundle ID `com.pchordia.provenance`, App Group `group.com.pchordia.provenance`, applied identically across the app target, widget target, and both entitlements files.

**Final scope for build:** The existing 24-task backlog plus new T-025 (25 tasks total), with `app_features`' value-chart item corrected to match what's actually tasked. Nothing else added, nothing else cut — this is the same scope locked in `task_assignments`, `project_plan`, and `tech_specs`.

**Acceptance criteria checklist:** The union of every task's `acceptance_criteria` in `task_assignments`, plus T-025's ("StoreKit config loads successfully in a sandbox test session with both products resolvable by identifier"). Together these already cover: every core workflow (capture, classify-assist, condition/provenance logging, value entries, export) with a testable criterion; all four states (empty/loading/success/error) on every named screen; the cross-cutting trust guarantees — soft-delete/recovery, downgrade-preserves-data with correct active/locked partitioning, offline entitlement continuity (`unknownOffline` never silently collapsing to free), export correctness at 0/1-sparse/1-dense/many-photo counts, and a full VoiceOver pass on the core loop and export page-position announcements. Build can proceed without inventing any further product, design, or architecture decisions.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase cleanly. All five required outputs are present and concrete: a readiness verdict (conditional-ready, with the condition scoped to three named fixes), a cross-phase consistency check that re-verifies specific prior risks (the `Domain/Protocols` wildcard bug) rather than just asserting consistency, named blockers with fixes (value-chart wording correction, new T-025 with concrete product IDs/pricing, and a pinned bundle ID/App Group convention), a final scope (24+1 tasks, nothing else added or cut), and an acceptance checklist that's the union of all task-level criteria plus T-025's.

The three fixes are genuine, narrow closures rather than new debate: the value-chart resolution correctly favors the majority of the paper trail (`next_steps_small`, `ios_architecture_review`, `tech_specs`, and the actual task backlog all already agreed "list now, chart later") over the one document that had drifted, and changes zero tasks. The StoreKit config gap is real (T-018/T-023 depended on a file nobody was tasked to create) and now has a concrete owner, task, and product identifiers. The bundle ID/App Group placeholder prevents three lane workers from independently guessing.

Process caveat worth surfacing to the user rather than a reason to fail: Codex and Gemini never participated in this phase (CLI cooldown / tier issue across all rounds), so this gate's findings and closures are single-reviewer. Nothing here contradicts a position either tool actually argued, and the fixes are mechanical rather than judgment calls, but the user should know the pre-build gate didn't get adversarial cross-checking this round.

Ready to hand off to build with the 25-task scope, the corrected `app_features` chart language, and the pinned identifiers (`com.pchordia.provenance` / `group.com.pchordia.provenance`, subscription group "Provenance+").
