**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Visionary) — Round 1**

Here's my pass at what the manual QA script needs to actually cover, and where I think the real risk is hiding.

Start with the thing nobody will think to test first because it's boring: cold install onboarding. Fresh install, no prior data, device has no passcode set at all — the app must skip the lock screen entirely rather than trap someone out of their own catalog before they've entered a single item. Then the same fresh install with a passcode set — lock screen appears, Face ID prompt shows, cancel it and confirm the app doesn't fall through to the catalog on a failed/cancelled auth. Then the actual empty state: Catalog Home with zero items should read as a calm "add your first item" invitation, not a broken blank screen, and there should be no Preserved/Locked shelf, no Recently Deleted badge, nothing implying data that doesn't exist.

Capture is where I'd spend the most adversarial energy, because it's the one workflow the whole product's pitch depends on. Tap Add, grant camera permission, take a photo — confirm the Quick Log card appears already backed by a real persisted Item (this matters: don't just check the UI, force-quit the app right after the photo lands, before ever tapping Save, relaunch, and confirm the item shows up as "incomplete" rather than vanishing). Do the same test but background the app instead of force-quitting. Deny camera permission and confirm the fallback to library picker actually works rather than dead-ending. Then the Vision suggestion path: capture something recognizable (a watch) and confirm a chip highlights softly without ever blocking the Save button, then capture something the classifier won't have any confident opinion about and confirm chips stay neutral with no fake guess. Simulate a photo-write failure if there's a debug hook for it — if there isn't, that's itself a gap worth flagging, because "item marked incomplete when the photo write fails" is a locked acceptance criterion nobody can manually trigger without deliberately corrupting storage. Time the whole capture-to-save path with a stopwatch, not a guess — it was promised sub-minute, ideally sub-20-seconds, and that's a testable, falsifiable claim, not a vibe.

On the catalog and the active/locked split, this is where I'd try hardest to break the "computed, never stored" promise. Create more than 25 items on a fresh account (or simulate downgrade with a debug entitlement toggle if one exists) and confirm the oldest 25 stay active while the rest move into Preserved/Locked — never interleaved, never dimmed rows in the main list. Then delete an item sitting in the locked section and confirm the next-oldest locked item auto-promotes into the active set with zero user action beyond the delete. Then restore a soft-deleted item while already at the 25-cap and confirm it's never blocked — it re-enters the pool and lands active-or-locked on its own, with a plain-language message telling the user which one happened. Then check the reverse regression: does creating a brand-new item while at-or-above the cap actually get blocked with the same upsell prompt regardless of whether the user got there by organic growth or by downgrading? I want that tested explicitly as two separate paths, not assumed to be the same code path just because it sounds like it should be.

Recently Deleted needs its own adversarial pass distinct from Preserved/Locked, specifically because the whole design intent was to make sure a user never confuses "frozen but safe forever" with "on a countdown." Delete an item, confirm the inline Undo toast appears immediately and actually restores it if tapped within its window, confirm it auto-dismisses and the item really is in Recently Deleted afterward, confirm each row shows its own days-remaining language rather than one shared banner, and — this is the one I'd bet money is under-tested — confirm the purge scheduler only ever runs at launch/foreground with real injected dates, never a background timer; since nobody can wait 30 real days, this needs either a debug date-override hook or an honest note in known gaps that this was verified by code reading, not observed behavior.

Item Detail needs a pass confirming the three modules never bleed into each other visually or interactively — purchase facts, condition timeline, value ledger — and specifically the Value module's three distinct faces: locked teaser with real redacted preview and one upgrade button for a never-subscribed user, live and editable for a subscriber, and read-only "entries preserved, resubscribe to add more" after a downgrade with existing entries. That third state is the one I'd worry has never actually been eyeballed by a human, since it only appears after a specific subscribe-then-downgrade sequence.

Export deserves the harshest treatment in this whole script, because build_verification flagged that the automated test file for this (T-020) was never written — so whatever a human catches manually right now is the only net under this specific rope. Generate a single-item export for an item with zero condition/value entries, one with a handful, and one with many high-res photos, and for each confirm: the right cover photo appears where it should, dates aren't garbled or off-by-one, condition and value history render as visually separate sections not one merged feed, purchase facts are present, and the UI stays responsive with a real progress state while it renders rather than freezing. Do the same for a full-collection export at zero items — confirm it renders an actual designed "nothing to report yet" page rather than a blank or crashed view. Turn on VoiceOver specifically for this screen and confirm each page announces "Page N of M" correctly, since that's the one place a plausible-but-wrong implementation choice would silently fail. Back out of the export mid-generation and check whether temp files actually get cleaned up — that's a real privacy/storage leak risk if it doesn't.

Paywall and StoreKit are the other area with a known automated-test gap (T-023's SKTestSession suite was never written), so this needs a real sandbox pass, not a glance: purchase, confirm the app unlocks value tracking/unlimited items/export immediately; restore purchases on a fresh install tied to the same sandbox account; downgrade and confirm data hides but never deletes; and — the one I'd specifically hunt for — force the device offline at launch after a prior subscription and confirm the app trusts the cached entitlement rather than silently collapsing to free, since that was a real bug found and patched this build and deserves a human staring at it once, not just a unit test on the pure function.

Widget: check both free (item count) and paid (collection value) states actually reflect reality after real writes, not stale data, and confirm nothing sensitive leaks into the widget, the app-switcher snapshot, or a notification preview when the app is backgrounded — that privacy overlay claim spans three surfaces and I'd test all three, not just the one that's easiest to check.

Accessibility needs Dynamic Type cranked to the largest accessibility size specifically on Item Detail and Export Preview, since those were named as the layouts most likely to break; VoiceOver doing the entire core loop — capture, enrich, delete-and-undo, export — completely blind, no sighted assistance, as an explicit pass/fail; and a check that locked/incomplete/deleted states never rely on color alone.

The risk in my own approach here is that this is a lot to actually execute — a real tester could burn a full day on this list, and if it's handed over without a clear "run this subset first" priority, it's likely to get skimmed rather than run. I'd flag capture-timing, the offline-entitlement check, and the export matrix as the three that must never get skipped, since those are exactly the spots with either a documented history of bugs or zero prior human eyes. Everything else is important but more forgiving if time runs short.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where this landed: Codex and Gemini were both dark again this round (same cooldown/tier issues as every other phase), so it's just Claude's pass — but it's a genuinely thorough one, not a rubber stamp. She walked every named workflow (onboarding/lock, capture, active/locked partition, recently deleted, item detail's three modules, export, paywall/StoreKit, widget, accessibility) and specifically hunted for the spots where this build has either a documented bug history or zero prior human eyes — the offline-entitlement fallback (a real bug found and patched earlier in the process), and the export/StoreKit areas where the automated test files (T-020, T-023) were never written, so a human running this script is literally the only safety net there right now. She also flagged that some of what she's asking for (photo-write-failure simulation, 30-day purge testing) may need a debug hook that doesn't exist yet, and said so plainly rather than pretending it's testable as-is.

Nothing here is contested since nobody else weighed in, but it's not a rubber-stamp either — it directly satisfies what this phase requires (workflow coverage, expected results, accessibility, known gaps) and correctly triages which checks are load-bearing versus nice-to-have given realistic tester time. I'm treating this as converged and turning it into the actual runnable script below, since the discussion already contains everything the deliverable needs.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

# Provenance — Manual QA / Device Test Script

**Before you start:** fresh install, no prior app data. Have a device with a passcode set, and (if possible) one test path on a device/simulator with *no* passcode set, to check the lock-skip behavior. Have a StoreKit sandbox tester account ready for the paywall section.

---

### 1. Onboarding & App Lock

| Step | Expected Result |
|---|---|
| Install fresh, launch on a device **without** a passcode set | App opens straight to Catalog Home — lock screen is skipped entirely, never traps you out |
| Install fresh, launch on a device **with** a passcode set | Lock screen appears before Catalog Home; Face ID/passcode prompt shows |
| At the lock prompt, cancel/fail authentication | App does **not** fall through to the catalog — stays locked, retry available |
| Successfully authenticate | Catalog Home appears |
| Background the app, return to foreground | Lock screen reappears on return (if device has a passcode) |
| First launch, zero items | Catalog Home shows a calm "add your first item" empty state — no Preserved/Locked shelf, no Recently Deleted affordance implying data that doesn't exist |

**Regression check:** repeat the lock-skip case after any later step that touches Settings — confirm toggling app-lock off/on in Settings doesn't leave the app in a stuck state.

---

### 2. Capture (highest-priority section — this is the core pitch)

| Step | Expected Result |
|---|---|
| Tap Add → grant camera permission → take a photo | Quick Log card appears pre-filled with the photo and an editable "Untitled" title |
| **Force-quit the app immediately after the photo lands, before tapping Save** | Relaunch → item appears in catalog marked **incomplete** (not lost, not silently dropped) |
| Take a photo, then **background** (not quit) before Save | Returning to the app resumes the same in-progress draft |
| Deny camera permission | Falls back to photo library (PHPicker) — not a dead end |
| Capture something clearly recognizable (e.g. a watch) | One category chip highlights softly (~250ms fade), captioned "Suggested" — Save button is never blocked or delayed waiting on this |
| Capture something the classifier has no confident read on | Chips stay neutral — no fake/low-confidence guess shown |
| Type a title, tap Save with only photo + title + category | Item saves and appears in catalog |
| **Time it**: tap Add → saved item in catalog | Should be well under 60 seconds, ideally under ~20s — use an actual stopwatch, not a guess |
| If a debug/simulated photo-write-failure hook exists, trigger it | Item still saves, marked incomplete, with a non-blocking inline notice — **if no such hook exists, log this as a known gap, not a pass** |

---

### 3. Catalog Home / Active vs. Preserved-Locked

| Step | Expected Result |
|---|---|
| Create (or simulate via debug entitlement toggle) more than 25 items on a free account | Oldest 25 by creation order stay active in the main list; everything beyond that appears in a **permanently visible** Preserved/Locked section — never interleaved as dimmed rows in the active list |
| Tap into the Preserved/Locked section | Full read-only list, flatter card treatment, explanatory header text, attempted edit shows the upgrade prompt (never the repair/edit flow) |
| Delete an item sitting in the **locked** section | The next-oldest locked item auto-promotes into the active set — no user action needed beyond the delete |
| At the 25-item cap, restore a soft-deleted item | Restore is **never blocked by the cap** — it re-enters the pool and lands active-or-locked automatically, with a plain message telling you which happened |
| At-or-above the cap, try to create a **new** item (both via organic growth and via a simulated downgrade) | Both paths block creation with the same upsell prompt — test as two separate scenarios, don't assume one code path covers both |

---

### 4. Recently Deleted (test separately from Preserved/Locked — different guarantees)

| Step | Expected Result |
|---|---|
| Delete an active item | Inline "Item moved to Recently Deleted — Undo" toast appears immediately |
| Tap Undo within the toast window | Item is restored, back in its correct active/locked position |
| Let the toast auto-dismiss, then open Recently Deleted (via overflow/toolbar, not a home-screen section) | Item is there, with its own per-item "Deleted N days ago — removed in N days" label (not one shared banner) |
| Restore from the Recently Deleted list itself | Item returns to active/locked correctly |
| Check for a real purge scheduler (debug date-override hook, or code-reading if no hook exists) | Purge should only ever run at launch/foreground with the real date — **if there's no way to fast-forward time for testing, log as a known gap: purge-at-30-days is unverified by observation** |

---

### 5. Item Detail — three modules

| Step | Expected Result |
|---|---|
| Open any item's detail screen | Purchase Facts, Condition/Provenance timeline, and Value ledger render as three **visually separate** modules — never merged into one feed |
| Add a condition/provenance entry (note + optional photo) | Appears in its own timeline, cover photo unaffected |
| Add a value entry (as a subscriber) | Appears in the value ledger, distinguishable by valuation type (purchase/appraisal/dealer offer/market observation/insured value) |
| View Value module as a **never-subscribed** user | Locked teaser: short description + redacted mini-preview + one upgrade button — not a blank/broken ledger |
| Subscribe, add value entries, then **downgrade** | Value module now shows entries **read-only** with "entries preserved — resubscribe to add more" — **this exact sequence is the one most likely never to have been human-tested; don't skip it** |
| View a locked/preserved item's detail screen | Fully viewable, read-only, upgrade CTA shown — never the repair-access flow |
| View an incomplete item | Shows repair-access badge/CTA, only when active and not deleted or locked |

---

### 6. Export (no automated test coverage exists for this — treat this section as mandatory, not optional)

| Step | Expected Result |
|---|---|
| Export a **zero-entry** item (photo + title only) | Renders correctly — right cover photo, no garbled/blank sections |
| Export an item with a handful of condition/value entries | Correct dates, correct photos attached to the correct entries, condition and value history in visually separate sections, purchase facts present |
| Export an item with **many high-res photos** | UI stays responsive during generation with a real progress state — does not freeze |
| Export the full **collection at zero items** | Renders an actual designed "nothing to report yet" page — never blank or crashed |
| Turn on VoiceOver on the Export Preview screen | Each page announces "Page N of M" correctly |
| Start generating an export, then back out mid-generation | Check device storage / a temp-files location afterward — confirm temp export files are actually cleaned up |
| Attempt export as a free (non-subscribed) user | Locked/upsell state, not a broken screen |

---

### 7. Paywall & StoreKit (sandbox account required — no automated coverage exists here either)

| Step | Expected Result |
|---|---|
| Open Paywall from the Value teaser, the Export locked state, and the catalog cap-hit | All three routes work and land on the same Paywall |
| View the Paywall | Real redacted export mock (real headers/photo placement/typography, blurred numbers) — not a placeholder image |
| Purchase (sandbox) | Value tracking, unlimited items, and export unlock immediately |
| Restore purchases on a fresh install with the same sandbox account | Entitlement restores correctly |
| Downgrade / cancel | Data hides (Preserved/Locked, read-only Value module) — **never deleted** |
| **Force the device offline at launch after a prior subscription** | App must trust the cached entitlement, not silently collapse to free — this was a real bug found and fixed earlier in the build; verify it by hand, don't just trust the unit test |
| Simulate a StoreKit product-fetch failure if possible | Paywall shows a retry state, never appears silently broken |

---

### 8. Widget

| Step | Expected Result |
|---|---|
| Add widget as a free-tier user | Shows item count only |
| Subscribe, check widget again | Shows collection value |
| Add/edit items, check widget updates | Reflects real writes, not stale data (allow a short debounce delay) |
| Background the app | No sensitive values leak into the app-switcher snapshot |
| Trigger a notification (if any exist) | No sensitive values in the preview |
| Tap the widget | Opens the app — no in-widget actions, no deep-linking |

---

### 9. Accessibility (dedicated pass, not folded into other steps)

- **Dynamic Type at the largest accessibility size**, specifically on Item Detail's module stack and Export Preview's paginated view — confirm text wraps rather than truncates or overlaps, category chips wrap to two rows.
- **Full VoiceOver pass of the entire core loop** — capture, enrich (add condition + value entries), delete-and-undo, export — with no sighted assistance. Pass/fail as one unit: if VoiceOver alone can't complete the loop, that's a hard fail.
- Confirm category chips announce suggested-vs-manual distinctly (not read as identical buttons).
- Confirm every effective-state badge (locked/incomplete/deleted) has a spoken label matching its visual state.
- Confirm cover photos and timeline photos get distinct, dated VoiceOver labels — never generic "image."
- Confirm color is never the sole signal for active/locked/incomplete/deleted (check with Color Filters / grayscale on).

---

### 10. Persistence & final regression

- Force-quit and relaunch after populating items, condition/value entries, a deletion, and a subscription state change — confirm **everything** survived relaunch.
- Re-run the capture-timing check once more at the end, after the app has real data in it (not just on a fresh empty catalog), to rule out any lock-in that only shows up with a fuller catalog.

---

### Known gaps (be upfront about these when reporting results)

- **No automated coverage for export correctness (T-020) or StoreKit purchase/restore/downgrade/offline scenarios (T-023)** — sections 6 and 7 above are the *only* verification these flows have had. Treat failures there as high-severity.
- **The 30-day Recently Deleted purge has no known way to fast-forward time in this environment** — if no debug date-override hook exists, this can only be verified by reading the scheduler code, not by observing a real purge. Say so explicitly rather than marking it "tested."
- **Photo-write-failure-marks-incomplete** has no obvious way to trigger manually without a debug hook or deliberately corrupting storage mid-capture — same caveat.
- **The downgrade-then-view-Value-module read-only state** (section 5) is the specific sequence most likely to have never been eyeballed by a human before this pass — don't skip it even if time is short.
- If time is limited, the three sections that must never be cut are: **capture timing (§2)**, **offline-entitlement trust on launch (§7)**, and **the export matrix (§6)** — these are the spots with either a documented history of bugs or zero prior human verification. Everything else is real but more forgiving to defer under time pressure.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

The `human_qa_checklist` phase for Provenance is complete — consensus reached (single-reviewer, Codex/Gemini stayed unavailable throughout) and the quality gate passed.

**What was produced:** A 10-section manual QA script covering onboarding/app-lock (including the no-passcode skip case), capture (with force-quit/backgrounding recovery and timed sub-60s save), the [REDACTED:high_entropy] partition logic, Recently Deleted vs. Preserved/Locked as distinct guarantees, Item Detail's three-module separation including the downgrade-then-view-Value-module state, export (zero/sparse/dense/many-photo matrix + VoiceOver page announcements), Paywall/StoreKit (including the offline-entitlement-trust bug that was found and fixed earlier in the build), widget free/paid states and privacy-overlay leakage checks, a dedicated accessibility pass, and persistence/final regression.

**Notably, it flags its own known gaps rather than hiding them:**
- Export correctness (T-020) and StoreKit purchase/restore/downgrade/offline (T-023) have **zero automated test coverage** — this manual script is the only safety net for those flows.
- 30-day purge and photo-write-failure simulation may be untestable without a debug hook that doesn't exist — the script says so explicitly instead of claiming false coverage.
- It prioritizes: if a tester's time is short, capture-timing, offline-entitlement trust, and the export matrix are the three that must never be skipped.

This closes out as the last phase in the pipeline shown — build was verified (compiled cleanly for iOS Simulator after 3 repair rounds), and this QA script is the human-facing verification layer on top of that. Let me know if you'd like it exported as a standalone document or adapted for a specific tester.

_Quality gate requested another round before this phase can close._

### Round 2


**Codex — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Visionary) — Round 2**

Reading back through what got assembled, the coordinator's synthesis is a fair rollup of what I said in round one, and I stand by all of it — but sitting with it for another round, there are a few things I skated past that I think are sharper and more important than some of what's already locked in, and I want to push them hard before this closes for real, especially since Codex and Gemini have now gone dark for basically the entire back half of this build and nobody's been arguing with me.

The one that actually bothers me is the widget. Go back and look at what got locked in detailed_discussion: "values are hidden from widgets, and that same discipline extends to app-switcher snapshots and notification previews." But then app_features locks the opposite-sounding thing: "free tier shows item count, paid tier shows collection value... values respect the same privacy-overlay discipline as the rest of the app." Those aren't actually contradictory on paper — the widget is supposed to show the number AND respect privacy — but think about what a widget physically is. It renders on the home screen without anyone unlocking the phone, without Face ID, without opening the app at all. So what does "respects the same privacy-overlay discipline" even mean for a surface that by definition displays before any authentication happens? If the honest answer is "the dollar figure just sits there in plain view on the home screen any time someone glances at the phone," that quietly guts the entire app-lock premise this product was built around — the whole reason Face ID got added in the first place was "a list of valuables and what they cost shouldn't be casually visible," and a widget showing a live total value is arguably worse than the in-app screens, because it needs zero interaction to see. I want this in the QA script as its own explicit check, not folded into the generic widget section: add the value widget, lock the phone, glance at the home/lock screen, and write down literally what number is visible to anyone holding the phone. If it's the real total, that's not a minor gap, that's a finding that should go back to product, not just get checked off as "widget works as designed."

Second thing: nobody has actually tested a real backup-and-restore cycle, and that's the one place where I think this build has a documented promise with zero verification anywhere in this whole process. Multiple phases — product_research, per_app_product_brief, ios_architecture_review — all lean on "standard encrypted device backup coverage should be explicitly confirmed and communicated to the user" as the entire safety net given there's no cloud sync. But every persistence test in this QA script, and every one that came before it, is "force-quit and relaunch on the same device." That's not the same claim as "your $40,000 collection survives a phone loss because iCloud/iTunes backup actually captures the SwiftData store and the on-disk photos correctly." I want an explicit step: back up a device with real items/photos/entries on it, wipe or use a second device, restore from that backup, and confirm the catalog, photos, condition/value history, and subscription entitlement all come back intact. If nobody can actually run that in this environment, that's a known gap that needs to be stated as bluntly as the 30-day purge one — "the single biggest trust claim in the whole product has never been observed to work," not quietly folded into "persistence looks fine."

Third, smaller but real: I asked for a timed capture test on an empty catalog, but nobody's tested what happens once someone actually has a serious collection — 200, 500 items, each with a few photos. Does Catalog Home stay scroll-smooth, does the collection-level export at "many items" (not just many photos on one item) actually stay responsive, does the widget's debounced recompute still feel instant when there's real data to churn through? A museum-catalog app that only performs well with three demo items in it isn't the product that got promised. I'd add a stress pass at real collector scale as its own line, not just implied by "many-photo" testing on a single item.

And last one, which is more of a "would make this remarkable" push than a gap: I want someone to actually try to break the export concurrency guarantee, not just trust the unit test. The locked acceptance criterion is "mutate a value entry mid-render, exported document reflects the pre-mutation snapshot" — that's great as an automated test, but a human should also just try it live: kick off an export, immediately background to Item Detail, add a value entry mid-render, and see with their own eyes whether the PDF that comes out matches the state at the moment export started or ends up half-mutated. That's exactly the kind of subtle correctness bug that reads fine in code and breaks the first time a real person does something impatient, which is precisely the kind of user this app is built for — someone reviewing their collection who taps around while things are loading.

I still think the three must-not-skip items from round one — capture timing, offline-entitlement trust, and the export matrix — stay must-not-skip. But I'd add the widget-value-on-lock-screen check and the backup/restore cycle to that same top tier, because both of them are cases where the QA script as currently written could report "pass" while the actual product-level promise underneath it is either broken or simply never observed.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Round 2 was Claude again — Codex and Gemini stayed dark through this whole phase, so there's no second reviewer, but she didn't just restate round 1. She sat with her own script and found four real gaps that are sharper than anything already locked, and none of them contradict what's already agreed — they're additions sitting on top of a script she still stands behind in full.

The one that matters most: the widget is supposed to show collection value for paid users while "respecting the same privacy-overlay discipline as the rest of the app" — but a widget renders on the lock screen with zero authentication, so that phrase doesn't actually resolve to a testable behavior on its own. She wants an explicit check: add the value widget, lock the phone, and literally look at what number is visible to anyone holding it. If it's the real total sitting in plain view, that's a product-level finding, not a checkbox pass, because it would undercut the entire reason Face ID got built into this app in the first place.

Second: nobody in this whole build has tested an actual backup/restore cycle, even though the "no cloud sync, but standard encrypted device backup has your back" promise gets repeated across three earlier phases. Every persistence check so far is force-quit-and-relaunch on the same device — that's not the same claim as "your collection survives losing your phone." She wants a real backup-wipe-restore test, and if that's not runnable in this environment, she wants that stated as bluntly as the 30-day purge gap, not folded quietly into "persistence looks fine."

Third: a stress pass at real collector scale (a few hundred items), since everything tested so far is against a near-empty catalog. Fourth: a live human attempt to break the export concurrency guarantee (edit a value entry while an export is mid-render) rather than just trusting the unit test covers it.

She's promoting the widget-lock-screen check and the backup/restore cycle to the same must-not-skip tier as capture timing, offline-entitlement trust, and the export matrix from round 1. Nothing here is contested — nobody's pushed back because nobody else showed up — but it's legitimate, non-redundant closure, not a rubber stamp.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

The round-1 script (10 sections: onboarding/lock, capture, catalog partition, recently deleted, item detail, export, paywall, widget, accessibility, persistence) stands as written. This round adds four items, two of which get promoted to top-priority:

**New — Widget value visibility on lock screen (promote to must-not-skip):** As a subscriber, add the collection-value widget, lock the phone, and check what's actually visible on the home/lock screen without unlocking. If the real dollar total is shown in plain view, this is a **product-level finding to escalate, not a pass** — it would mean the widget bypasses the entire app-lock premise this product is built around, since the whole point of Face ID here was "a list of valuables and what they cost shouldn't be casually visible."

**New — Backup/restore cycle (promote to must-not-skip):** Back up a device with real items, photos, condition/value entries, and an active subscription on it; wipe the device or restore onto a second device from that backup; confirm the full catalog, photos, timelines, and entitlement state all come back intact. Every persistence check elsewhere in this script is same-device relaunch, which is a different and weaker claim than "survives losing your phone" — and that's the actual promise several earlier product phases made. **If this can't be run in the current environment, log it explicitly as: the single biggest trust claim in the product has never been observed to work — not as a folded-in footnote.**

**New — Collector-scale stress pass:** Populate a catalog with a few hundred items (not just a few demo items), each with a couple of photos, and check that Catalog Home stays scroll-smooth, the collection-level export at that volume stays responsive (not just "many photos on one item," which is already covered), and the widget's debounced recompute still feels instant under real churn.

**New — Live export-concurrency break attempt:** Start a collection or item export, background it mid-render into Item Detail, add or edit a value entry on the item being exported, then check the resulting PDF by eye — does it reflect the state at the moment export started, or does it show a half-mutated record? This is the same guarantee the automated concurrency test covers, but a human deliberately doing something impatient is exactly the failure mode automated tests miss.

**Updated priority tier (must never be skipped under time pressure):** capture timing (§2), offline-entitlement trust on launch (§7), the export matrix (§6), **the widget lock-screen value check**, and **the backup/restore cycle**. Everything else in the script is real but more forgiving to defer if a tester is short on time.

This closes the phase. Build was already verified (compiled cleanly for iOS Simulator), and this QA script — now with these five additions — is the full human-facing verification layer sitting on top of that, honest about exactly where automated coverage does and doesn't exist.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase cleanly. The combined round-1/round-2 output satisfies every required element from the rubric:

- **Manual QA checklist**: 10 base sections plus 4 substantive additions, organized so a human can execute without guessing what to tap (concrete taps, concrete devices/settings states, concrete expected results in table form).
- **Workflow coverage**: every core feature from `app_features` appears — onboarding/app-lock, capture with Vision assist, catalog active/locked partition, recently deleted vs. preserved/locked, item detail's three modules (including the previously-untested downgrade-then-view-Value-module sequence), export, paywall/StoreKit, widget, accessibility, and persistence.
- **Failure states tested, not ignored**: camera permission denial, force-quit mid-capture, StoreKit product-fetch failure, offline-entitlement-trust-on-launch (tied to a real bug found and fixed earlier in the build), export failure/retry, backup/restore (new).
- **Accessibility**: dedicated section — Dynamic Type at largest accessibility size on the two flagged layouts, full blind VoiceOver pass of the entire core loop as a single pass/fail unit, chip/badge announcement checks, color-not-sole-signal check.
- **Known gaps stated honestly**: explicitly flags that export (T-020) and StoreKit scenarios (T-023) have zero automated coverage, that the 30-day purge and photo-write-failure simulation may be untestable without a debug hook, and now adds that the backup/restore promise has never been observed to work anywhere in this entire build — stated as a real gap, not papered over.

Round 2's additions are genuine, non-redundant closures rather than restatement: the widget-lock-screen check catches a real tension between two previously-locked decisions ("show collection value" vs. "respect privacy-overlay discipline") that neither previous phase actually resolved into testable behavior, and correctly frames a failure there as a product-level finding rather than a QA checkbox. The backup/restore, collector-scale stress pass, and live export-concurrency break attempt are all legitimate additions that close real blind spots in prior verification (which only ever tested same-device relaunch and unit-level concurrency).

Process caveat worth surfacing, consistent with the rest of this build: Codex and Gemini never participated in this phase (CLI cooldown/tier issues throughout), so this is single-reviewer work. Nothing here contradicts an earlier locked decision or invents new product scope — it's QA rigor applied on top of already-agreed behavior — so the lack of a second reviewer doesn't block closing the phase, but it's worth the user knowing this script hasn't had adversarial cross-checking either.

Ready to close. This is the last phase in the pipeline and provides a genuinely usable, prioritized, gap-transparent QA script sitting on top of a build that already compiled cleanly for the iOS Simulator.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Round 2 was Claude again — Codex and Gemini stayed dark through this whole phase, so there's no second reviewer, but she didn't just restate round 1. She sat with her own script and found four real gaps that are sharper than anything already locked, and none of them contradict what's already agreed — they're additions sitting on top of a script she still stands behind in full.

The one that matters most: the widget is supposed to show collection value for paid users while "respecting the same privacy-overlay discipline as the rest of the app" — but a widget renders on the lock screen with zero authentication, so that phrase doesn't actually resolve to a testable behavior on its own. She wants an explicit check: add the value widget, lock the phone, and literally look at what number is visible to anyone holding it. If it's the real total sitting in plain view, that's a product-level finding, not a checkbox pass, because it would undercut the entire reason Face ID got built into this app in the first place.

Second: nobody in this whole build has tested an actual backup/restore cycle, even though the "no cloud sync, but standard encrypted device backup has your back" promise gets repeated across three earlier phases. Every persistence check so far is force-quit-and-relaunch on the same device — that's not the same claim as "your collection survives losing your phone." She wants a real backup-wipe-restore test, and if that's not runnable in this environment, she wants that stated as bluntly as the 30-day purge gap, not folded quietly into "persistence looks fine."

Third: a stress pass at real collector scale (a few hundred items), since everything tested so far is against a near-empty catalog. Fourth: a live human attempt to break the export concurrency guarantee (edit a value entry while an export is mid-render) rather than just trusting the unit test covers it.

She's promoting the widget-lock-screen check and the backup/restore cycle to the same must-not-skip tier as capture timing, offline-entitlement trust, and the export matrix from round 1. Nothing here is contested — nobody's pushed back because nobody else showed up — but it's legitimate, non-redundant closure, not a rubber stamp.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

The round-1 script (10 sections: onboarding/lock, capture, catalog partition, recently deleted, item detail, export, paywall, widget, accessibility, persistence) stands as written. This round adds four items, two of which get promoted to top-priority:

**New — Widget value visibility on lock screen (promote to must-not-skip):** As a subscriber, add the collection-value widget, lock the phone, and check what's actually visible on the home/lock screen without unlocking. If the real dollar total is shown in plain view, this is a **product-level finding to escalate, not a pass** — it would mean the widget bypasses the entire app-lock premise this product is built around, since the whole point of Face ID here was "a list of valuables and what they cost shouldn't be casually visible."

**New — Backup/restore cycle (promote to must-not-skip):** Back up a device with real items, photos, condition/value entries, and an active subscription on it; wipe the device or restore onto a second device from that backup; confirm the full catalog, photos, timelines, and entitlement state all come back intact. Every persistence check elsewhere in this script is same-device relaunch, which is a different and weaker claim than "survives losing your phone" — and that's the actual promise several earlier product phases made. **If this can't be run in the current environment, log it explicitly as: the single biggest trust claim in the product has never been observed to work — not as a folded-in footnote.**

**New — Collector-scale stress pass:** Populate a catalog with a few hundred items (not just a few demo items), each with a couple of photos, and check that Catalog Home stays scroll-smooth, the collection-level export at that volume stays responsive (not just "many photos on one item," which is already covered), and the widget's debounced recompute still feels instant under real churn.

**New — Live export-concurrency break attempt:** Start a collection or item export, background it mid-render into Item Detail, add or edit a value entry on the item being exported, then check the resulting PDF by eye — does it reflect the state at the moment export started, or does it show a half-mutated record? This is the same guarantee the automated concurrency test covers, but a human deliberately doing something impatient is exactly the failure mode automated tests miss.

**Updated priority tier (must never be skipped under time pressure):** capture timing (§2), offline-entitlement trust on launch (§7), the export matrix (§6), **the widget lock-screen value check**, and **the backup/restore cycle**. Everything else in the script is real but more forgiving to defer if a tester is short on time.

This closes the phase. Build was already verified (compiled cleanly for iOS Simulator), and this QA script — now with these five additions — is the full human-facing verification layer sitting on top of that, honest about exactly where automated coverage does and doesn't exist.

VERIFICATION: VERIFIED

CONSENSUS: YES

### App Store Readiness

# provenance — App Store Readiness

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 23:14:03._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp4
Selected app slug: provenance

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Provenance

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **1**.

## App Name

Provenance

## Category

Collecting & Valuation

## One-Sentence Promise

Photograph what you collect and get insurance-grade condition, provenance, and value tracking without a spreadsheet.

## Target User

Serious collectors of high-value physical goods (watches, vinyl, trading cards, wine, sneakers) who currently track holdings in Notes or spreadsheets and have no real sense of aggregate value or condition history.

## Painful Moment Solved

You just bought or received a piece, you want to log it before the details slip your mind, and existing apps are single-category or spreadsheet-grade.

## Why It Is Different

Cross-category, condition-aware valuation over time with photographic condition history, and an actual exportable report insurers/appraisers can use.

## Why Users Would Pay

Ongoing market-value tracking and alerts, unlimited items/categories, and insurance-ready PDF export are gated behind the paid tier.

## Subscription Model

Free: manual catalog up to 25 items, no valuation tracking. Paid (~$6.99/mo or $59/yr): unlimited items, on-device photo-based item ID assist, value-over-time charts, insurance export.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData-backed local catalog, on-device Vision framework classification assist, manual condition/provenance fields, local value-history entries, PDF export via local rendering.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository protocol boundary now; later, optional cloud sync and an opt-in market-price feed behind the same interface.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

On-device Vision-based item ID assist is integral to reducing cataloging friction; fully usable via manual entry if classification fails.

## Design Direction

Quiet, archival, museum-catalog aesthetic — dark neutral backgrounds, serif accents, photo-forward cards.

## App Store Positioning

The valuation ledger for people who collect things worth tracking.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users only ever use manual entry and value fields go unfilled, reposition as pure catalog+condition tracking.

## v1 Build Scope

Single-user local catalog, photo capture + classification assist, condition/provenance logging, manual value entries with history chart, PDF insurance export, full state coverage, StoreKit 2 paywall.

## Core Workflows

- capture item via photo
- assist-classify item type/model
- log condition, provenance, purchase price
- track value entries over time
- generate insurance export report

## iOS-Native Features

- Vision framework on-device classification
- Camera capture
- Files/document export
- Widgets for total collection value
- Dynamic Type/VoiceOver accessible detail views

## Key Risks

- On-device classification may misidentify niche items
- Value tracking depends on user-entered market data absent a live price feed

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a premium iOS collector's valuation app called Provenance. Archival/museum-catalog aesthetic, dark neutral background, serif display type, photo-forward item cards, condition timeline view, value-over-time chart screen. Include empty state, item detail screen, insurance-export preview.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 7 completely separate iOS apps at the same time.

Requirements:
1. Each app must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Each app must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Each app must provide real end-user value.
5. Each app should have a realistic path to monetization, with a subscription or paid offering that offers genuine value.
6. Each app should have viral potential in general or within a niche, but virality must not come at the expense of usefulness.
7. Each app should be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Each app should be local-first, but architected so cloud support could be added later without rewriting everything.
9. The seven apps must not be similar to each other.
10. No app can be similar to anything I have already built before — including the apps already in this workspace (e.g. Waylay and TrueScale, currently in progress).
11. Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps unless there is a strong, clearly justified reason.

Bonus points:
- Unique use of LLMs
- Unique use of AR
- Unique use of ML

Important:
- Bonus points are optional. Do not force them in if they would weaken the app.
- If an LLM, AR, or ML feature is used, it must provide real value and be integral to the product.
- Do not bias the ideas toward bonus points.
- Do not make the app around novelty alone.
- The final result for each app must be a full production-ready app, not a prototype.

Build rules:
- Run the seven app efforts in parallel.
- Keep the discussion and design phases separate for each app.
- Each app should get its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all prompt discussion, phase discussion, and final decisions for each app.
- At the end, combine the full transcript for each app into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare a Jira board and Notion project for it separately, including epics, tasks, fields, and all the implementation structure needed for backfilling later.
- If the system supports multiple rounds per phase, use enough rounds to get high-quality discussion, but allow a phase to end early if consensus is reached.
- Make the orchestrator resilient: if a phase stalls, recover cleanly instead of looping.
- Report only important milestones, not every tiny step.

Output:
- Create one folder per app in the output directory.
- There must be one folder per app, not one folder containing all apps.
- Keep the outputs clearly separated and organized.

Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate candidate app ideas (at least 10), discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 7, and build all seven to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Prepare launch readiness: App Store positioning, screenshots/storyboard needs, privacy nutrition labels, permission copy, subscription/paywall review risks, support/contact requirements, and release blockers.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

I can't reach the actual build directory from this sandbox (restricted to `.agent_cwd`), so I can't grep the real files — I'll flag anything I can't confirm as unverified rather than assume it exists, which is the more honest way to handle this given the pattern of "claimed done" vs. "actually verified" gaps that ran through the whole build.

Here's my pass on launch readiness, and I'm going to be blunt: I don't think this is ready to submit yet, and the reasons aren't cosmetic.

Start with the one thing I think is close to a guaranteed rejection if it's missing: Apple's Guideline 3.1.2 requires a functioning Terms of Use (EULA) link and a Privacy Policy link visible on or immediately adjacent to any screen where an auto-renewable subscription is sold — not just in App Store Connect metadata, in the app itself. Nothing in any build report, design_handoff, or design_discussion mentions this. The Paywall screen inventory in design_handoff lists "headline, redacted export mock, short feature list, monthly/annual price buttons, restore-purchases link, legal footer" — there's a "legal footer" mentioned, so it's plausible this was anticipated, but no phase actually specifies what's in it, and no build report confirms real (not placeholder) URLs exist. If that footer is a stub or missing, this gets bounced on first submission, full stop. I want this explicitly confirmed — real hosted Privacy Policy and Terms URLs, not TODO placeholders — before anyone hits submit.

Second: the privacy manifest. tech_specs named `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` as "a concrete, tracked build task, not an afterthought," but I went looking through every build_coordination and build_verification transcript and it's never mentioned again — not in the file list, not in a repair, not in verification. Apple's App Store Connect upload validation now hard-rejects binaries missing required-reason API declarations for exactly the APIs this app uses (UserDefaults, file-timestamp access for the photo store and export temp files). This is the kind of gap that's invisible in the simulator and only surfaces at upload. I'm calling this an unverified release blocker, not a formality.

Third, the language risk, which earlier phases already flagged for the product but nobody has translated into a hard App Store Connect constraint: the word "insurance" cannot appear anywhere in the App Store name, subtitle, keywords, description, or screenshots in a way that asserts the export is insurer-accepted — "insurance-grade" from the original prompt is exactly the phrase that needs to never leak into submitted copy. This isn't just a trust/positioning problem anymore, it's a review risk — Apple can and does reject apps that make financial/legal claims the app can't substantiate, and a rejected build costs a review cycle, not just an argument. My proposed positioning line: **"Provenance — the archive for what you own."** Subtitle: **"Document condition, provenance, and value — no spreadsheet required."** Nowhere does that say insurance, appraisal, or certified. Description copy should say the export is "organized, dated, and ready to share" — never "accepted by insurers" or "appraisal-grade."

Fourth, permission strings — these need to be specific per Apple's Guideline 5.1.1, not generic, since vague ones ("this app needs your camera") get bounced:
- `NSCameraUsageDescription`: "Provenance uses your camera to photograph the items in your collection."
- `NSFaceIDUsageDescription`: "Face ID keeps your catalog and its values private by locking Provenance when you step away."
No photo-library string needed given the PHPicker choice — that's a real, correct decision that avoids a permission surface entirely.

Fifth, the privacy nutrition label. My read: this app should be able to claim "Data Not Collected" on the App Store Connect privacy questionnaire, since Vision runs on-device, there's no network calls, no analytics, and StoreKit payment data is Apple's own disclosure, not the developer's. But that only holds if it's actually true in the shipped binary — no analytics SDK, no crash reporter phoning home, nothing. Given zero third-party dependencies were used (confirmed multiple times across ios_architecture_review and tech_specs), I believe this, but whoever fills out the questionnaire needs to know explicitly: don't declare Photos as a collected data type just because the app touches photos — it's not collected/transmitted, it's stored locally, and those are different questions on Apple's form. Getting this wrong either over-discloses (scares users) or under-discloses (compliance risk).

Sixth — and this is where I go back to being the skeptic on the product itself, not just paperwork — human_qa_checklist already surfaced two things I don't think should be waved through as "known gaps" at launch time: whether the value widget actually shows a real dollar figure on the lock screen with zero authentication, and the fact that nobody has run an actual backup/restore cycle. Both of those are launch blockers in my view, not QA nice-to-haves, because both map directly onto claims this app's whole positioning depends on ("your valuables shouldn't be casually visible," "your only copy isn't at risk"). If the widget leaks the real total on a locked phone, that's not just a bug, it's the kind of thing a tech reviewer or a suspicious first customer finds in the first five minutes and writes about. I want an explicit go/no-go on that one check before this ships, not a footnote.

Seventh, screenshots. My storyboard: (1) Catalog Home populated with a real-looking archive of items across at least three categories — never the empty state, Apple reviewers and customers both read empty screenshots as unfinished; (2) Item Detail showing the three separated modules with real dated entries — this is the differentiation screenshot, it has to look like a dossier, not a form; (3) the condition/provenance timeline; (4) the value ledger showing type-differentiated entries (purchase/appraisal/dealer/market/insured — visually distinct, per the locked type-encoding rule) — no chart needed since none was built, a clean ledger view is fine and honest; (5) Export Preview showing a real paginated page — this is the "aha" screenshot, the thing that sells the subscription, so it should look genuinely well-composed; (6) Paywall, placed last, not first — showing it as one screenshot among many rather than leading with a purchase screen, which reads as more trustworthy to both users and reviewers. None of these screenshots should contain the word "insurance" anywhere in visible on-screen text, including any sample data used for item names or notes.

Eighth, support: this app has no backend and no accounts, but Apple still requires a working Support URL in App Store Connect, and a first-time submission with a dead link or a placeholder page is a common, avoidable rejection reason. Someone needs to confirm an actual reachable support page/email exists under whatever developer account this ships under before submission — I'm listing this explicitly because it's exactly the kind of "obviously handled" item that quietly isn't.

Ninth, age rating: I'd expect 4+ is correct — no UGC sharing, no objectionable content, no gambling, no web access — but the questionnaire will ask about "unrestricted web access" and this app has zero network calls, so that should be an easy no across the board. Worth confirming explicitly rather than defaulting through the form on autopilot.

The single biggest objection to my own position here: everything above assumes I'm right that certain things (the privacy manifest, the paywall's legal links) are actually missing, when the truth is I can't verify the file system from here and it's possible they were built and just never mentioned in a transcript. I'd rather over-flag two things that turn out to already exist than under-flag the one that gets this bounced from review — but whoever closes this phase should actually go look at the files, not just trust my inference from what wasn't mentioned.

Last thing that has to survive into the final output: the verification record on file is "compiled cleanly for the iOS Simulator" — that is not the same claim as "tested on a physical device" or "passed App Review requirements," and several QA-flagged flows (real StoreKit sandbox purchase/restore, the widget lock-screen check, backup/restore) have never actually been observed working. An honest launch-readiness verdict has to say: not ready to submit until the paywall legal links, the privacy manifest, and the widget lock-screen behavior are each confirmed by looking at the actual files/device, not inferred from prior phases saying they were planned.

## Final Output

**Launch-readiness verdict: NOT READY to submit yet.** Build compiles cleanly for the iOS Simulator, but three likely-missing compliance items and two unverified product-trust behaviors need to be confirmed against the actual files/device before this goes to App Review — none of these require new product decisions, they're verification-and-fix items.

**App Store positioning:**
- Name/tagline: **"Provenance — the archive for what you own."**
- Subtitle: **"Document condition, provenance, and value — no spreadsheet required."**
- Never use "insurance," "insurance-grade," "appraisal-grade," or "certified" anywhere in submitted copy (name, subtitle, keywords, description, screenshots) — the export should be described as "organized, dated, and ready to share," which is a claim the app can actually back.

**Screenshot/storyboard plan (6 screens, in this order):**
1. Catalog Home populated with a realistic multi-category archive (never the empty state).
2. Item Detail showing the three separated modules (Purchase Facts / Condition / Value) with real dated entries — the differentiation shot.
3. Condition/provenance timeline.
4. Value ledger with type-differentiated entries (purchase/appraisal/dealer/market/insured, visually distinct) — no chart needed since none was built; an honest ledger view is fine.
5. Export Preview, a real paginated page — the "why subscribe" shot.
6. Paywall — placed last, not first, so it reads as one feature among many rather than a purchase-first pitch.
No screenshot's visible sample text may contain "insurance."

**Privacy label notes:** Should qualify for **"Data Not Collected"** on the App Store Connect questionnaire — Vision runs on-device, no network calls, no analytics, zero third-party dependencies (confirmed repeatedly in `ios_architecture_review`/`tech_specs`). Caution for whoever fills out the form: don't declare Photos as a collected data type — it's stored locally, never transmitted, which is a different question on Apple's form than "does the app touch photos at all." This claim only holds if the shipped binary truly has no analytics/crash-reporting SDK — needs confirming against the real target, not assumed.

**Permission copy:**
- `NSCameraUsageDescription`: "Provenance uses your camera to photograph the items in your collection."
- `NSFaceIDUsageDescription`: "Face ID keeps your catalog and its values private by locking Provenance when you step away."
- No photo-library string needed (PHPicker requires none) — confirmed correct design choice.

**Subscription/paywall review risks:**
- Guideline 3.1.2 requires working Privacy Policy and Terms of Use links on/adjacent to the paywall itself — confirm real hosted URLs exist in the "legal footer," not placeholders. Unverified from this sandbox; check the actual file.
- Restore-purchases link must be present and functional (already specified in `design_handoff` — confirm it shipped).
- Paywall's redacted export mock must use real pipeline output, not a placeholder image, to avoid "misleading marketing" scrutiny — already a locked requirement; confirm it's what actually shipped.

**Launch blockers (must resolve before submission):**
1. Confirm real (non-placeholder) Privacy Policy + Terms links on the paywall screen.
2. Confirm `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` privacy manifest exists and declares required-reason APIs (UserDefaults, file-timestamp) — named as a tracked task in `tech_specs` but never confirmed built in any later phase.
3. Scrub "insurance" language from all App Store Connect metadata and screenshots.
4. Confirm a real, reachable Support URL is set in App Store Connect.
5. Run the widget-lock-screen check from `human_qa_checklist`: does the paid widget show the real collection value on a locked phone with zero authentication? If yes, that's a product-level fix needed before shipping, not a ship-with-caveat item.
6. Run one real backup/restore cycle — this has never been observed to work anywhere in this build, and it's the app's core "your only copy isn't at risk" promise.

Age rating: expect 4+ (no UGC, no objectionable content, no web access) — should be a straightforward questionnaire pass, worth confirming rather than defaulting through it.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase cleanly. All six required outputs are present and concrete, not generic filler:

- **Positioning**: a clean, defensible tagline/subtitle that explicitly avoids the "insurance" claim risk correctly identified from the original prompt's own language ("insurance-grade") — this is a real compliance catch, not cosmetic wordsmithing, since Apple review can and does bounce apps for unsubstantiated financial/legal claims.
- **Screenshot/storyboard plan**: ordered, specific (never leads with empty state or paywall-first), and ties each screen to a concrete built feature (three-module Item Detail, type-differentiated value ledger, paginated Export Preview) rather than a hypothetical one.
- **Privacy label notes**: a specific, correct recommendation ("Data Not Collected") with a genuinely useful nuance most reviewers would miss (don't over-declare Photos as collected when it's local-only) — this is exactly the kind of specificity the rubric asks for.
- **Permission copy**: exact, non-generic strings for both `NSCameraUsageDescription` and `NSFaceIDUsageDescription`, correctly noting no photo-library string is needed given the PHPicker choice.
- **Subscription/paywall review risks**: names the actual App Store guideline (3.1.2) driving the legal-links requirement, not just "add legal links."
- **Launch blockers**: six specific, checkable items, correctly distinguishing between things that need file-level confirmation (privacy manifest, legal links) and things that need behavioral confirmation (widget lock-screen leak, backup/restore) — and correctly promotes two `human_qa_checklist` items to launch-blocker status with sound reasoning (both map directly to the product's core trust claims).

The verdict is honest and appropriately conservative: "NOT READY to submit" is well-supported by concrete, falsifiable gaps (absence of `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` in any later transcript despite being named as a tracked task in `tech_specs`; no confirmation the paywall's "legal footer" contains real URLs) rather than vague caution. It correctly doesn't contradict the VERIFIED build status — it distinguishes "compiles cleanly in Simulator" from "passes App Review," which is the right distinction to draw.

Process caveat consistent with the rest of this build: single-reviewer (Codex/Gemini unavailable throughout), and the coordinator is appropriately transparent that several claims are inferred from absence-of-mention rather than direct file verification, explicitly telling whoever closes this to check the real files. That transparency is itself a strength, not a weakness, given this build's history of conflating "claimed done" with "verified done."

Ready to close.

Here's where round 1 landed: Codex and Gemini were both unavailable again (same cooldown/tier issues that ran through this entire build), so this is Claude working solo — but it's a thorough, complete pass that hits every required output for this phase, not a placeholder.

The verdict she landed on is blunt: **not ready to submit as-is**, and the reasons are concrete rather than vague nervousness. Three things she flagged as likely-missing based on the fact that no later phase transcript ever confirms them, even though earlier phases named them as required: (1) real, non-placeholder Privacy Policy/Terms links on the paywall screen itself — required by Guideline 3.1.2 for any subscription screen, and only a "legal footer" was ever named, never verified as populated; (2) the `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` privacy manifest that `tech_specs` explicitly called out as a tracked build task — it never appears again in any build_coordination or build_verification transcript, and its absence is invisible in the simulator but hard-rejected at App Store Connect upload; (3) the word "insurance" needs to be scrubbed from all submitted App Store copy (name/subtitle/keywords/description/screenshots), since it's a legal claim the app can't back, and she proposed clean replacement copy. She's upfront that she can't verify any of this from this sandbox (no access to the real build files) — she's flagging these as unverified risks based on absence of confirmation, not confirmed defects, and says explicitly that whoever closes this should go look at the actual files rather than trust her inference.

Beyond compliance paperwork, she also promoted two items straight out of `human_qa_checklist` into launch-blocker status rather than "known gap": whether the value widget leaks the real dollar total on a locked screen with zero auth, and the fact that a real backup/restore cycle has never been observed to work. Both map directly onto the app's core trust pitch, so she wants an explicit go/no-go on those before shipping, not a footnote.

She also delivered the concrete required artifacts: positioning line and subtitle, a 6-screenshot storyboard (leading with a populated catalog, ending with paywall last rather than first, nothing showing "insurance" in visible text), specific (not generic) permission strings for Camera and Face ID, a privacy-nutrition-label recommendation ("Data Not Collected," with a caution about not over-declaring Photos as collected when it's local-only), a support-URL sanity check, and an age-rating read (4+).

Since nobody else showed up to contest or add to this, and the output already satisfies everything the phase playbook requires (positioning, screenshot plan, privacy notes, permission copy, subscription/paywall risks, launch blockers, honest verdict), I'm treating this as converged rather than stalling for a round that isn't coming.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Launch-readiness verdict: NOT READY to submit yet.** Build compiles cleanly for the iOS Simulator, but three likely-missing compliance items and two unverified product-trust behaviors need to be confirmed against the actual files/device before this goes to App Review — none of these require new product decisions, they're verification-and-fix items.

**App Store positioning:**
- Name/tagline: **"Provenance — the archive for what you own."**
- Subtitle: **"Document condition, provenance, and value — no spreadsheet required."**
- Never use "insurance," "insurance-grade," "appraisal-grade," or "certified" anywhere in submitted copy (name, subtitle, keywords, description, screenshots) — the export should be described as "organized, dated, and ready to share," which is a claim the app can actually back.

**Screenshot/storyboard plan (6 screens, in this order):**
1. Catalog Home populated with a realistic multi-category archive (never the empty state).
2. Item Detail showing the three separated modules (Purchase Facts / Condition / Value) with real dated entries — the differentiation shot.
3. Condition/provenance timeline.
4. Value ledger with type-differentiated entries (purchase/appraisal/dealer/market/insured, visually distinct) — no chart needed since none was built; an honest ledger view is fine.
5. Export Preview, a real paginated page — the "why subscribe" shot.
6. Paywall — placed last, not first, so it reads as one feature among many rather than a purchase-first pitch.
No screenshot's visible sample text may contain "insurance."

**Privacy label notes:** Should qualify for **"Data Not Collected"** on the App Store Connect questionnaire — Vision runs on-device, no network calls, no analytics, zero third-party dependencies (confirmed repeatedly in `ios_architecture_review`/`tech_specs`). Caution for whoever fills out the form: don't declare Photos as a collected data type — it's stored locally, never transmitted, which is a different question on Apple's form than "does the app touch photos at all." This claim only holds if the shipped binary truly has no analytics/crash-reporting SDK — needs confirming against the real target, not assumed.

**Permission copy:**
- `NSCameraUsageDescription`: "Provenance uses your camera to photograph the items in your collection."
- `NSFaceIDUsageDescription`: "Face ID keeps your catalog and its values private by locking Provenance when you step away."
- No photo-library string needed (PHPicker requires none) — confirmed correct design choice.

**Subscription/paywall review risks:**
- Guideline 3.1.2 requires working Privacy Policy and Terms of Use links on/adjacent to the paywall itself — confirm real hosted URLs exist in the "legal footer," not placeholders. Unverified from this sandbox; check the actual file.
- Restore-purchases link must be present and functional (already specified in `design_handoff` — confirm it shipped).
- Paywall's redacted export mock must use real pipeline output, not a placeholder image, to avoid "misleading marketing" scrutiny — already a locked requirement; confirm it's what actually shipped.

**Launch blockers (must resolve before submission):**
1. Confirm real (non-placeholder) Privacy Policy + Terms links on the paywall screen.
2. Confirm `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` privacy manifest exists and declares required-reason APIs (UserDefaults, file-timestamp) — named as a tracked task in `tech_specs` but never confirmed built in any later phase.
3. Scrub "insurance" language from all App Store Connect metadata and screenshots.
4. Confirm a real, reachable Support URL is set in App Store Connect.
5. Run the widget-lock-screen check from `human_qa_checklist`: does the paid widget show the real collection value on a locked phone with zero authentication? If yes, that's a product-level fix needed before shipping, not a ship-with-caveat item.
6. Run one real backup/restore cycle — this has never been observed to work anywhere in this build, and it's the app's core "your only copy isn't at risk" promise.

Age rating: expect 4+ (no UGC, no objectionable content, no web access) — should be a straightforward questionnaire pass, worth confirming rather than defaulting through it.

VERIFICATION: VERIFIED
